A pneumatic cylinder exhausting to atmosphere: effective force = gauge pressure × area, not absolute. > 3. **Listing advantages without justification** — KTU 2024 scheme uses Outcome-Based Education; a bare list earns ≤ 60 % marks. Always append a one-line engineering reason. > 4. **Writing the comparison as a paragraph** — always use a **two-column table**; this is the fastest way to score full 7 marks. > 5. **Omitting the verification step** in derivations — the energy-conservation check ($W_{in} = W_{out}$) is a hallmark of a top-grade answer and is rewarded with 0.5–1 bonus mark by most KTU examiners. > 6. **Wrong direction of mechanical advantage:** Students sometimes write $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_1/A_2)$, inverting the ratio. Memorise: **larger area → larger force.** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - [ ] Fluid power = transmission of energy using pressurised fluid (liquid or gas). - [ ] Two branches: **Hydraulics** (oil, incompressible, high force) and **Pneumatics** (air, compressible, low force, high speed). - [ ] **Pascal's Law** is the governing principle: $P_1 = P_2$ in an enclosed, incompressible fluid. - [ ] Force equation: $F = P \cdot A$; Velocity equation: $v = Q/A$. - [ ] Force is multiplied; displacement is divided by the same area ratio (energy is conserved). - [ ] **Hydraulic power:** $P = p \cdot Q$ (in SI: p in Pa, Q in m³/s → W). - [ ] Standard pressures: Hydraulics 100–350 bar; Pneumatics 4–8 bar. - [ ] Key advantages to memorise: force multiplication, stepless control, overload protection, self-lubrication, smooth motion, hold-pressure. - [ ] Key disadvantages to acknowledge: leakage, contamination, noise, fire hazard, heat. - [ ] Top 3 industries per medium: Hydraulics → heavy engineering, aerospace, mining. Pneumatics → packaging, food, automation. - [ ] Always use a **two-column comparison table** for hydraulic-vs-pneumatic questions. - [ ] Always **state the working principle first**, then derive quantities — KTU examiners reward a structured, principle → formula → substitution → result flow. - [ ] Memorise: 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa; cylinder area $A = \pi D^2/4$; annulus area $A_a = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$. - [ ] For any KTU 14-mark question, plan: **2 pages for theory + 1 page for derivation/numerical + 0.5 page for diagram/table** — this is the typical "excellent answer" template.

topics.title: Fluid Properties: Specific Weight, Density, and Specific Gravity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Properties: Specific Weight, Density, and Specific Gravity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of a Fluid
A **fluid** is a substance that continuously deforms (flows) under the application of a shear stress. In Industrial Hydraulics & Pneumatics, we deal with two primary categories of working fluids:
- **Hydraulic fluids** (typically mineral oils, e.g., ISO VG 32/46/68) used to transmit power in incompressible systems.
- **Pneumatic fluids** (compressed air and gases) used in compressible systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** The very first step before analyzing any hydraulic circuit, pump, or actuator is to understand the **physical properties** of the working fluid. The three most fundamental properties are **Density (ρ)**, **Specific Weight (γ)**, and **Specific Gravity (S)**.

### 1.2 Formal Academic Definitions

**A. Mass Density (ρ)**
Mass density is defined as the mass of the fluid per unit volume. It quantifies how much matter is packed into a given space.

$$ \rho = \lim_{\Delta V \to 0} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V} = \frac{dm}{dV} $$

- **SI Unit:** $\text{kg/m}^3$
- **CGS Unit:** $\text{g/cm}^3$ or $\text{g/cc}$

**B. Specific Weight (γ)**
Specific weight is the weight (force due to gravity) of the fluid per unit volume. It is mathematically related to density by the acceleration due to gravity.

$$ \gamma = \rho \cdot g $$

- **SI Unit:** $\text{N/m}^3$
- **CGS Unit:** $\text{dyne/cm}^3$

**C. Specific Gravity (S) or Relative Density (RD)**
Specific gravity is a **dimensionless** ratio that compares the density (or specific weight) of a given fluid to a reference fluid. For liquids, the reference is **pure water at 4°C**; for gases, it is **air at standard conditions** (typically 0°C and 1 atm).

$$ S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4^\circ\text{C}}} = \frac{\gamma_{\text{fluid}}}{\gamma_{\text{water at } 4^\circ\text{C}}} $$

- **Unit:** Dimensionless (no unit)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Luggage Analogy" for Density:**
> Imagine two identical suitcases. One is stuffed with cotton, the other with iron bars. Both suitcases have the *same volume*, but the iron-filled one is *much heavier*. We say iron has a higher **density** — it packs more mass into the same space.
>
> **The "Earth's Pull" for Specific Weight:**
> Now, take both suitcases to the Moon. They will weigh less because the Moon's gravitational pull is weaker. So **weight** depends on *where* you are, but **mass** is constant. **Specific weight (γ = ρg)** captures this locality, which is why engineers in Kerala (g ≈ 9.81 m/s²) and engineers in Delhi (g ≈ 9.79 m/s²) will get slightly different γ values for the same oil.
>
> **The "Comparison Shopping" for Specific Gravity:**
> Specific gravity is like a benchmarked review. When a hydraulic oil datasheet says *"Specific Gravity = 0.86 at 15°C"*, it simply means: *"This oil is 0.86 times as heavy as water."* You instantly know it floats on water without doing any math.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Values (MUST MEMORIZE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Fluid (Water at 4°C):**
> - $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3 = 1 \; \text{g/cm}^3$
> - $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 9810 \; \text{N/m}^3 = 9.81 \; \text{kN/m}^3$
> - $S_{\text{water}} = 1$ (dimensionless)
>
> **Standard Reference Gas (Air at Standard Conditions):**
> - $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.293 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
> - $S_{\text{air}} = 1$ (dimensionless)

### 1.5 Typical Hydraulic Oil Properties
For KTU practicals, you will frequently encounter petroleum-based hydraulic oils with the following standard values:

| Hydraulic Oil Grade | Density (kg/m³) | Specific Gravity |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| ISO VG 32 | ~870 | 0.87 |
| ISO VG 46 | ~875 | 0.875 |
| ISO VG 68 | ~885 | 0.885 |
| Water-Glycol | ~1070 | 1.07 |
| Synthetic Ester | ~920 | 0.92 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Chain Connecting the Three Properties

The three properties are not independent — they form a tightly coupled family governed by **Newton's Second Law** (F = ma) and the **Universal Law of Gravitation**.

**Step 1: Mass Density (ρ) — The "Local Concentration"**
- *Why:* Fluids are continuous media. We cannot count individual molecules, so we measure how much mass is contained within a small representative elemental volume (REV).
- *How:* As the elemental volume $\Delta V$ shrinks to an infinitesimally small point, the ratio $\Delta m / \Delta V$ stabilizes to a finite value called the *point density*.

**Step 2: Specific Weight (γ) — The "Gravitational Intensity"**
- *Why:* In nearly all engineering applications (reservoirs, tanks, pipelines), fluids are subjected to Earth's gravity. A column of oil sitting in a tank exerts pressure *because* of its weight.
- *How:* Since Weight $W = m \cdot g$, dividing by volume $V$ gives $\gamma = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot g / V = \rho \cdot g$. The constant $g$ is the bridge between mass-based and force-based properties.

**Step 3: Specific Gravity (S) — The "Normalized Benchmark"**
- *Why:* Absolute numbers (e.g., 875 kg/m³) are hard to remember and compare across regions. A *ratio* is universal.
- *How:* We divide the fluid's property by that of pure water at 4°C (the temperature where water attains its maximum density of exactly 1000 kg/m³ — a convenient SI standard).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | SI Unit | Typical Value (Oil) | Typical Value (Water) |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **Mass Density (ρ)** | $\rho = m/V$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | 870 | 1000 |
| 2 | **Specific Weight (γ)** | $\gamma = \rho g$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | 8533 | 9810 |
| 3 | **Specific Gravity (S)** | $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$ | Dimensionless | 0.87 | 1.00 |
| 4 | **Density from S** | $\rho_{\text{fluid}} = S \times 1000$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | 870 | 1000 |
| 5 | **Specific Wt from S** | $\gamma_{\text{fluid}} = S \times 9810$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | 8535 | 9810 |
| 6 | **Mass from Density** | $m = \rho \times V$ | kg | — | — |
| 7 | **Weight from Sp. Wt** | $W = \gamma \times V$ | N | — | — |
| 8 | **Inverse Relation** | $\gamma = S \times \gamma_{\text{water}}$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Tip for CGS Problems:**
> - $1 \; \text{g/cm}^3 = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ (Density)
> - $1 \; \text{dyne/cm}^3 = 0.1 \; \text{N/m}^3$ (Specific Weight)
> - To convert S (same for both) → no conversion needed, it is dimensionless.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Pump Sizing:** A pump's weight depends on the *specific weight* of the oil it contains. A 200-litre tank of oil (ρ = 875 kg/m³) weighs $W = \gamma \cdot V = 8583.75 \times 0.2 = 1716.75$ N, which dictates structural support design.
- **Manometer & Pressure Calculations:** Hydrostatic pressure $P = \gamma \cdot h$ directly uses specific weight. Without γ, we cannot compute the pressure head developed in a vertical hydraulic cylinder.
- **Hydraulic Oil Selection:** Specific gravity tells a maintenance engineer whether the oil will stratify from water (water contamination sinks because $S_{\text{water}} = 1 > S_{\text{oil}} = 0.86$).
- **Buoyancy & Cavitation Analysis:** Cavitation in pumps occurs when local pressure drops below vapor pressure, and buoyancy forces on submerged components depend entirely on the density difference.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Newton's Second Law to Specific Weight

**Given:** A fluid element of mass $m$ and volume $V$, subjected to Earth's gravity $g$.

**Step 1 — Start with the weight of the fluid (Newton's Law).**
The weight $W$ of a body is the gravitational force acting on its mass.
$$ W = m \cdot g $$

**Step 2 — Express mass in terms of density and volume.**
By the definition of mass density, $m = \rho \cdot V$. Substituting:
$$ W = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot g $$

**Step 3 — Derive specific weight by dividing both sides by volume.**
Specific weight is defined as *weight per unit volume*. Divide the entire equation by $V$:
$$ \frac{W}{V} = \rho \cdot g $$

**Step 4 — Recognize the left side as the definition of γ.**
The left-hand side is exactly $\gamma$ (weight per unit volume). Therefore:
$$ \boxed{\gamma = \rho \cdot g} $$

This is the **fundamental link** between mass-based and force-based fluid properties.

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Gravity as a Density Ratio

**Given:** Two fluids — the test fluid and the reference fluid (water at 4°C).

**Step 1 — Write the density of the test fluid.**
$$ \rho_{\text{fluid}} = \frac{m_{\text{fluid}}}{V_{\text{fluid}}} $$

**Step 2 — Write the density of the reference (water).**
$$ \rho_{\text{water}} = \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{V_{\text{water}}} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — Form the dimensionless ratio.**
$$ S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} $$

**Step 4 — Verify dimensionless nature.**
The kg/m³ units cancel, leaving a pure number:
$$ S = \frac{\text{kg/m}^3}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \text{dimensionless} $$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to express density in terms of S (the most-used KTU working formula).**
$$ \rho_{\text{fluid}} = S \times \rho_{\text{water}} = S \times 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3 $$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Property Variation Logic

| Scenario | ρ (Density) | γ (Specific Weight) | S (Specific Gravity) | Example Fluid |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Heavier than water | $\rho > 1000$ | $\gamma > 9810$ | $S > 1$ | Mercury (S = 13.6), Glycerine (S = 1.26) |
| Lighter than water | $\rho < 1000$ | $\gamma < 9810$ | $S < 1$ | Hydraulic oil (S ≈ 0.86), Petrol (S ≈ 0.72) |
| Equal to water | $\rho = 1000$ | $\gamma = 9810$ | $S = 1$ | Pure water at 4°C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Logic Trap:** If a problem says "oil of specific gravity 0.86," you must NOT use 0.86 as the density. Density = $0.86 \times 1000 = 860 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. This is the single most common error reported in KTU valuation audits.

### 3.4 Detailed Numerical Walkthrough (For Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A hydraulic reservoir holds 250 litres of ISO VG 46 hydraulic oil. Given the specific gravity is 0.875 and $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$, find:
(a) The mass of the oil in the tank.
(b) The weight of the oil in the tank.
(c) The specific weight of the oil.

**Given Data:**
- Volume: $V = 250 \; \text{L} = 250 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3 = 0.25 \; \text{m}^3$
- Specific Gravity: $S = 0.875$
- Gravity: $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- Reference density of water: $\rho_w = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mass of oil:**
First, find the density of the oil using the specific gravity:
$$ \rho_{\text{oil}} = S \times \rho_w = 0.875 \times 1000 = 875 \; \text{kg/m}^3 $$

Now, compute the mass:
$$ m = \rho_{\text{oil}} \times V = 875 \times 0.25 = 218.75 \; \text{kg} $$

**Part (b) — Weight of oil:**
$$ W = m \times g = 218.75 \times 9.81 = 2145.94 \; \text{N} \approx 2.146 \; \text{kN} $$

**Part (c) — Specific weight of oil:**
Method 1 (Direct from density):
$$ \gamma = \rho_{\text{oil}} \times g = 875 \times 9.81 = 8583.75 \; \text{N/m}^3 $$

Method 2 (Verification using S):
$$ \gamma = S \times \gamma_w = 0.875 \times 9810 = 8583.75 \; \text{N/m}^3 \;\checkmark $$

Both methods agree, confirming the chain of relations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Relationship Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

This diagram maps the interconnections between Mass, Weight, Density, Specific Weight, and Specific Gravity — the exact cognitive map examiners expect you to reproduce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MassM["Mass m kg"] -->|divide by V| DensityRho["Density rho kg per m3"]
    DensityRho -->|multiply by g| SpecificWeightGamma["Specific Weight gamma N per m3"]
    SpecificWeightGamma -->|divide by gamma water| SpecificGravityS["Specific Gravity S dimensionless"]
    DensityRho -->|divide by rho water| SpecificGravityS
    WeightW["Weight W N"] -->|divide by V| SpecificWeightGamma
    MassM -->|multiply by g| WeightW
    Vref["V olume m3"] --> DensityRho
    Vref --> SpecificWeightGamma
```

### 4.2 Property Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT VARIABLES"]
        mA["Mass m kg"]
        vA["Volume V m3"]
        gA["Gravity g m per s2"]
    end
    
    subgraph PROPERTIES["FLUID PROPERTIES"]
        rA["Density rho"]
        gG["Specific Weight gamma"]
        sA["Specific Gravity S"]
    end
    
    subgraph REFERENCE["REFERENCE STANDARDS"]
        rwA["Water at 4 C rho 1000"]
        gwA["Water gamma 9810"]
    end
    
    mA --> rA
    vA --> rA
    rA --> gG
    gA --> gG
    rA --> sA
    rwA --> sA
    gG --> sA
    gwA --> sA
```

### 4.3 Real-World Application Flow — Hydraulic Reservoir

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Tank["Hydraulic Reservoir 250 L"] --> Oil["ISO VG 46 Oil S 0.875"]
    Oil --> Calc1["rho = S x 1000 = 875 kg per m3"]
    Calc1 --> Calc2["m = rho x V = 218.75 kg"]
    Calc2 --> Calc3["W = m x g = 2145.94 N"]
    Calc1 --> Calc4["gamma = rho x g = 8583.75 N per m3"]
    Calc4 --> Output["Pressure Head P gamma h"]
```

### 4.4 Classification of Fluids by Specific Gravity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["All Fluids"] --> Check{"S ?"}
    Check -->|S less than 1| Lighter["LIGHTER than water n floats on water Examples Hydraulic oil S 0.86 Petrol S 0.72"]
    Check -->|S equal 1| Equal["EQUAL to water Examples Pure water at 4 C"]
    Check -->|S greater than 1| Heavier["HEAVIER than water n sinks in water Examples Mercury S 13.6 Glycerine S 1.26 Brine S 1.20"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight from the Diagrams:** The diagrams visually confirm that **S is the master "switch"** that connects a fluid to engineering reality. Once you know S, you can quickly derive ρ, γ, m (if V is given), and W (if V and g are given) — all without re-deriving Newton's laws.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following fluid properties with their SI units:**
(a) Mass Density
(b) Specific Weight
(c) Specific Gravity

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Mass Density (ρ):** It is defined as the mass of the fluid per unit volume.
$$ \rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad ; \quad \text{SI Unit: kg/m}^3 $$

**(b) Specific Weight (γ):** It is defined as the weight of the fluid per unit volume. It is the product of density and acceleration due to gravity.
$$ \gamma = \rho \cdot g \quad ; \quad \text{SI Unit: N/m}^3 $$

**(c) Specific Gravity (S):** It is the dimensionless ratio of the density of the given fluid to the density of pure water at 4°C (1000 kg/m³).
$$ S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4^\circ\text{C}}} \quad ; \quad \text{SI Unit: Dimensionless} $$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**A hydraulic oil has a specific gravity of 0.86. Find its (a) density, and (b) specific weight. Take g = 9.81 m/s².**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $S = 0.86$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 9810 \; \text{N/m}^3$.

**(a) Density of oil:**
$$ \rho_{\text{oil}} = S \times \rho_{\text{water}} = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860 \; \text{kg/m}^3 $$

**(b) Specific weight of oil:**
$$ \gamma_{\text{oil}} = \rho_{\text{oil}} \times g = 860 \times 9.81 = 8436.6 \; \text{N/m}^3 $$
(Or equivalently: $\gamma_{\text{oil}} = S \times \gamma_{\text{water}} = 0.86 \times 9810 = 8436.6 \; \text{N/m}^3$)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a)** Derive the relationship between specific weight (γ), mass density (ρ), and acceleration due to gravity (g). Explain the physical significance of each property with one real-world hydraulic application.
**(b)** A hydraulic tank contains 500 litres of oil. If the specific gravity of the oil is 0.88, determine:
   (i) The mass of the oil.
   (ii) The weight of the oil.
   (iii) The specific weight of the oil in $\text{N/m}^3$ and $\text{kN/m}^3$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define weight from Newton's Second Law:**
The weight $W$ of a fluid body of mass $m$ is the gravitational force on it:
$$ W = m \cdot g $$

**Step 2 — Substitute mass using density:**
By definition, mass density $\rho = m/V \Rightarrow m = \rho \cdot V$. Substituting:
$$ W = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot g $$

**Step 3 — Divide by volume to obtain specific weight:**
$$ \frac{W}{V} = \rho \cdot g $$

By definition, the left side is specific weight $\gamma$. Therefore:
$$ \boxed{\gamma = \rho \cdot g} $$

**Physical Significance:**
- **ρ** is intrinsic to the fluid (does not depend on location).
- **γ** is location-dependent (changes with g).
- *Application:* In a hydraulic cylinder, the pressure generated by a vertical oil column of height h is $P = \gamma h$. A heavier oil (higher γ) generates more pressure head for the same column height.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V = 500 \; \text{L} = 0.5 \; \text{m}^3$, $S = 0.88$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$.

**(i) Mass of oil:**
$$ \rho_{\text{oil}} = S \times 1000 = 0.88 \times 1000 = 880 \; \text{kg/m}^3 $$
$$ m = \rho_{\text{oil}} \times V = 880 \times 0.5 = 440 \; \text{kg} $$

**(ii) Weight of oil:**
$$ W = m \cdot g = 440 \times 9.81 = 4316.4 \; \text{N} \approx 4.32 \; \text{kN} $$

**(iii) Specific weight of oil:**
$$ \gamma = \rho_{\text{oil}} \times g = 880 \times 9.81 = 8632.8 \; \text{N/m}^3 = 8.6328 \; \text{kN/m}^3 $$

---

### **OR (Internal Choice — Attempt Any ONE)**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Alternative Pattern]**
**(a)** Define specific gravity. Why is water chosen as the reference fluid at 4°C? What would happen if the reference were chosen at 25°C? Explain with numerical justification.
**(b)** A cylindrical hydraulic actuator has a piston of diameter 100 mm. The cylinder is filled with oil of specific gravity 0.87 to a depth of 0.5 m above the piston. Calculate the hydrostatic pressure exerted on the piston due to the oil column. Take $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical Discussion (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Specific gravity is the dimensionless ratio of the density of a fluid to the density of pure water at 4°C.
$$ S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water, 4°C}}} $$

**Why water at 4°C?**
Water exhibits an anomalous behavior: it attains its **maximum density** of exactly **1000 kg/m³** at **4°C**. This makes 1000 kg/m³ a clean, round SI number — easy to remember and standardize globally. It is the SI reference temperature for density tables.

**What if the reference were 25°C?**
At 25°C, the density of water is approximately **997 kg/m³**. If we used this as the reference:
- Specific gravity values would no longer be "clean" decimals.
- Old textbooks and ISO standards would be incompatible with new references.
- A hydraulic oil with $\rho = 870 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ would have $S_{25°C} = 870/997 = 0.8726$, not 0.87. All datasheets would need re-scaling.
**Conclusion:** 4°C is the universally accepted standard (per ISO 18421) to maintain consistency in engineering and scientific literature.

**(b) Hydrostatic Pressure Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Piston diameter: $D = 100 \; \text{mm} = 0.1 \; \text{m}$ *(not needed for pressure, included as distractor)*
- Oil depth above piston: $h = 0.5 \; \text{m}$
- Specific gravity of oil: $S = 0.87$
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Find density of oil:**
$$ \rho_{\text{oil}} = S \times \rho_w = 0.87 \times 1000 = 870 \; \text{kg/m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Find specific weight of oil:**
$$ \gamma_{\text{oil}} = \rho_{\text{oil}} \times g = 870 \times 9.81 = 8534.7 \; \text{N/m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — Apply hydrostatic pressure equation:**
$$ P = \gamma_{\text{oil}} \times h = 8534.7 \times 0.5 = 4267.35 \; \text{N/m}^2 = 4267.35 \; \text{Pa} \approx 4.27 \; \text{kPa} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Writing $V = 500$ (treating litres as m³) — always convert litres to m³: divide by 1000.
> 2. **Specific Gravity Misuse:** Using 0.88 directly as the density. **Remember:** $S$ is dimensionless; $\rho = S \times 1000$ is the mandatory step.
> 3. **Forgetting g:** Computing "weight" as just $m$ (numerically) without multiplying by g. Weight is a *force*, expressed in **Newtons**, not kg.
> 4. **Mixing Reference Fluids:** Using density of *seawater* (1025 kg/m³) or *water at 25°C* (997 kg/m³) as the reference. **Always use 1000 kg/m³ unless explicitly told otherwise.**
> 5. **Pressure Formula Misuse:** Writing $P = \rho h$ (a classic dimensional error) instead of $P = \gamma h = \rho g h$. The unit must be Pa = N/m².
> 6. **Skipping the "Given Data" Step:** Examiners allocate 1 mark for systematic presentation. Always start with a "Given:" block.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Density (ρ)** = mass per unit volume → $\rho = m/V$ → Unit: **kg/m³**
- **Specific Weight (γ)** = weight per unit volume → $\gamma = \rho g$ → Unit: **N/m³**
- **Specific Gravity (S)** = ratio to water at 4°C → $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}} / 1000$ → **Dimensionless**
- **The Master Chain:** $S \xrightarrow{\times 1000} \rho \xrightarrow{\times g} \gamma \xrightarrow{\times V} W$
- **Water at 4°C:** $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\gamma = 9810 \; \text{N/m}^3$, $S = 1$
- **Air at STP:** $\rho = 1.293 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $S = 1$ (relative to air)
- **Lighter fluids** ($S < 1$): oils, petrol, alcohol — *float on water*
- **Heavier fluids** ($S > 1$): mercury, glycerine, brine — *sink in water*
- **Hydraulic oil S range:** 0.85 to 0.92 (always less than water — critical for water-separation in tanks)
- **Volume conversion:** 1 litre = $10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3$ = $10^3 \; \text{cm}^3$ (cc)
- **Pressure head formula (used in next sub-topics):** $P = \gamma h = \rho g h$
- **KTU Board Tip:** When in doubt, show the chain $S \to \rho \to \gamma \to W$ explicitly — partial marks are awarded for each correct step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pascal's Law and Its Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pascal's Law and Its Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (Pressure Transmission Law):**
> *"Pressure applied at any point to a confined static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surface of the containing vessel."*
> — Formally articulated by **Blaise Pascal (1653 A.D.)**

In rigorous vector-tensor notation used in fluid statics, for a static fluid element:

$$P_x = P_y = P_z = P$$

This means the pressure at a point inside a static fluid is a **scalar quantity** with the same magnitude in every direction, irrespective of the orientation of the surface on which it acts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balloon Squeeze" Intuition

Imagine a completely sealed, water-filled balloon connected to several smaller balloons through thin tubes. The moment you squeeze the main balloon at **one spot**, **all** the smaller balloons puff out **simultaneously and equally** — no matter how far away they are.

- Your finger = applied force
- The water inside = confined incompressible fluid
- The simultaneous puffing = pressure transmission in all directions
- The thin tubes = hydraulic lines

This is precisely what happens inside a hydraulic jack when the operator pumps the handle.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa} = 1.01325\ \text{bar}$
> - Density of water (at $4^\circ\text{C}$): $\rho_{water} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Density of standard hydraulic oil: $\rho_{oil} \approx 870\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
> - SI unit of pressure: **Pascal (Pa)** = $1\ \text{N/m}^2$
> - $1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 100\ \text{kPa}$

## 1.4 Conditions for Validity

| S.No. | Mandatory Condition | Reason |
|:-----:|:--------------------|:-------|
| 1 | Fluid must be **confined** (enclosed in a sealed boundary) | Otherwise pressure dissipates to atmosphere |
| 2 | Fluid must be in **static equilibrium** | The law fails under dynamic/turbulent flow |
| 3 | Fluid should be **incompressible** (or nearly so) | Liquids obey this; gases only approximately |
| 4 | **No shear stress** can exist in static fluid | Viscosity effects are neglected in the idealized statement |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Pascal's Law — A Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Molecular Model:** A liquid in static equilibrium has molecules that are tightly packed. They cannot be compressed further under normal pressures.
- **Step 2 — Force Equilibrium on a Fluid Particle:** Consider an infinitesimally small fluid cube. For the cube to be in static equilibrium, the net force on every face must sum to zero.
- **Step 3 — Symmetry Argument:** Because the fluid is continuous, homogeneous, and isotropic, there is no preferred direction. Hence, the pressure must be the same in every direction — otherwise the fluid would accelerate.
- **Step 4 — Consequence — Instantaneous Transmission:** When external pressure is applied, the molecules push their neighbors, which push the next, and the disturbance (pressure wave) propagates at the **speed of sound** in the medium — essentially instantaneous for engineering purposes.
- **Step 5 — Engineering Exploitation — Force Multiplication:** Since the same pressure acts on pistons of different cross-sectional areas, forces can be multiplied.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|:-:|:------------------|:---------------|:------------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $P$ = Pressure (Pa), $F$ = Force (N), $A$ = Area (m²) | Basic pressure definition |
| 2 | $P_1 = P_2$ (for same horizontal level in static fluid) | Equality of pressure at same elevation | Pascal's Law core statement |
| 3 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | $P_0$ = atmospheric / surface pressure, $\rho$ = density, $h$ = depth | Hydrostatic pressure variation with depth |
| 4 | $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ | $F_1, F_2$ = forces, $A_1, A_2$ = piston areas | Hydraulic press / jack force equation |
| 5 | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = F_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$ | $D_1, D_2$ = piston diameters | Force multiplication in circular pistons |
| 6 | $V_1 = V_2$ (incompressibility) | $V_1 = A_1 \cdot x_1$, $V_2 = A_2 \cdot x_2$ | Conservation of volume — displacement ratio |
| 7 | $x_2 = x_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$ | $x_1, x_2$ = piston displacements | Mechanical disadvantage — load moves slowly |
| 8 | Mechanical Advantage (MA) $= \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Ratio of output to input force | Quick MA calculation |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pascal's Law is the **foundational principle** behind virtually every hydraulic machine in industry:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Hydraulic Press** — forging, stamping, sheet metal forming
> - **Hydraulic Jack** — vehicle lifting in service stations
> - **Hydraulic Lift** — elevators in workshops, aircraft carriers
> - **Hydraulic Brake** — automotive and aircraft braking systems
> - **Hydraulic Accumulator** — energy storage in hydraulic circuits
> - **Hydraulic Ram** — water pumping without external power
> - **Die Casting & Injection Moulding Machines** — high pressure generation
> - **Earth-moving Equipment** — JCB, excavators, loaders, dump trucks
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Variation with Depth (Hydrostatic Equation)

### Setup

Consider a small fluid element in the shape of a **right rectangular prism** (cuboid) immersed in a static incompressible fluid of density $\rho$.

- Width along $x$-axis = $dx$
- Depth along $y$-axis = $dy$
- Height along $z$-axis = $dz$
- Let the $z$-axis point **upward** (positive direction = against gravity)

### Force Balance in the $z$-direction

The forces acting on the element in the vertical direction are:

**(a) Pressure force on the bottom face** (acts upward, positive $z$):

$$F_{bottom} = P \cdot dA = P \cdot (dx \cdot dy)$$

**(b) Pressure force on the top face** (acts downward, negative $z$):

$$F_{top} = -\left(P + \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z}\,dz\right) \cdot (dx \cdot dy)$$

**(c) Weight of the element** (acts downward, negative $z$):

$$W = -m \cdot g = -(\rho \cdot dV) \cdot g = -\rho \cdot (dx \cdot dy \cdot dz) \cdot g$$

### Equilibrium Condition

Since the fluid is static, the net force on the element is zero:

$$F_{bottom} + F_{top} + W = 0$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$P(dx\,dy) - \left(P + \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z}\,dz\right)(dx\,dy) - \rho \cdot g \cdot (dx\,dy\,dz) = 0$$

### Simplification

Divide throughout by $(dx\,dy\,dz)$:

$$P - P - \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z}\,dz - \rho \cdot g = 0$$

The $P$ terms cancel out:

$$-\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z}\,dz - \rho g = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$$

### Integration

Integrating both sides with respect to $z$ from the free surface (where $P = P_0$ and $z = 0$) to a depth $h$ below the surface (where $z = -h$):

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = -\int_{0}^{-h} \rho g\, dz$$

$$P - P_0 = -\rho g (-h) = \rho g h$$

### Final Hydrostatic Equation

$$\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}$$

This is the **fundamental equation of fluid statics** — the foundation of Pascal's Law and all hydraulic engineering.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force Multiplication in a Hydraulic Press

### System Description

A hydraulic press consists of two connected cylinders of cross-sectional areas $A_1$ (small) and $A_2$ (large), filled with an incompressible fluid and fitted with frictionless pistons.

### Application of Pascal's Law

A small input force $F_1$ is applied on the small piston, generating pressure $P_1$:

$$P_1 = \dfrac{F_1}{A_1}$$

By Pascal's Law, this pressure is transmitted undiminished throughout the fluid. Hence the pressure on the large piston is:

$$P_2 = P_1$$

$$\dfrac{F_2}{A_2} = \dfrac{F_1}{A_1}$$

### Force Multiplication Formula

$$\boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = F_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2}$$

For circular pistons of diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$, since $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$:

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{(\pi D_2^2 / 4)}{(\pi D_1^2 / 4)} = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{D_2^2}{D_1^2}$$

### Work Conservation Check (Sanity Verification)

By conservation of energy (assuming no losses), work input = work output:

$$F_1 \cdot x_1 = F_2 \cdot x_2$$

Since the volume of incompressible fluid displaced must be equal on both sides:

$$A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = x_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$$

Substituting:

$$F_1 \cdot x_1 = F_2 \cdot x_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms that while force is **multiplied**, displacement is **reduced** by the same factor — the principle of virtual work is preserved.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A hydraulic press has a plunger of diameter $D_1 = 50\ \text{mm}$ and a ram of diameter $D_2 = 500\ \text{mm}$. A force of $F_1 = 200\ \text{N}$ is applied on the plunger. Find:
(i) The pressure generated in the fluid
(ii) The force exerted by the ram
(iii) The distance moved by the ram if the plunger moves $x_1 = 100\ \text{mm}$

### Solution Table

| Step | Quantity | Formula | Calculation | Result |
|:----:|:---------|:--------|:------------|:-------|
| 1 | Area of plunger $A_1$ | $\dfrac{\pi D_1^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4}$ | $1.963 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$ |
| 2 | Area of ram $A_2$ | $\dfrac{\pi D_2^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi (0.50)^2}{4}$ | $1.963 \times 10^{-1}\ \text{m}^2$ |
| 3 | Pressure $P$ | $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1}$ | $\dfrac{200}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}}$ | $1.019 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} \approx 1.019\ \text{bar}$ |
| 4 | Force on ram $F_2$ | $F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | $200 \times \dfrac{0.1963}{0.001963}$ | $20{,}000\ \text{N} = 20\ \text{kN}$ |
| 5 | Distance moved by ram $x_2$ | $x_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$ | $100 \times \dfrac{1.963 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-1}}$ | $1\ \text{mm}$ |
| 6 | Mechanical Advantage | $\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | $\dfrac{0.1963}{0.001963}$ | $100$ |

### Key Observation

A small input of $200\ \text{N}$ (about the weight of a $20\ \text{kg}$ bag) produces an output of $20\ \text{kN}$ — enough to lift a small car. The ram, however, moves only $1\ \text{mm}$ when the plunger moves $100\ \text{mm}$. **Force is multiplied; distance is divided; power is conserved.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pascal's Law Transmission in a Hydraulic System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of force multiplication via Pascal's Law in a generic hydraulic press/jack system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator Input Force F1] --> B[Small Piston Area A1]
    B --> C[Pressure P = F1 divided by A1]
    C --> D[Confined Incompressible Fluid]
    D --> E[Pressure Transmitted Equally in All Directions]
    E --> F[Large Piston Area A2]
    F --> G[Output Force F2 = F1 x A2 divided by A1]
    G --> H[Load Lifted Compressed Forged Stamped]
    D --> I[Sealed Rigid Boundary]
    I -.-> D
    J[Volume Displaced A1 x x1] -.-> D
    D -.-> K[Equal Volume Rises A2 x x2]
    K -.-> H
```

### Reading the Block Diagram

| Node | Function |
|:-----|:---------|
| `A` Operator Input Force $F_1$ | The manual or mechanical effort applied |
| `B` Small Piston Area $A_1$ | Convert force into fluid pressure |
| `C` Generated Pressure $P$ | Computed as $F_1 / A_1$ |
| `D` Confined Fluid | The hydraulic medium (typically oil) |
| `E` Equal Pressure Transmission | The application of Pascal's Law |
| `F` Large Piston Area $A_2$ | Receives the transmitted pressure |
| `G` Output Force $F_2$ | The multiplied force delivered to the load |
| `H` Load Application | Forging, lifting, braking, etc. |
| `I` Sealed Boundary | Rigid container required for pressure containment |
| `J`, `K` Volume Conservation | Verifies incompressibility and displacement ratio |

## 4.2 Physical Schematic — Hydraulic Press (ASCII Representation)

```
                OUTPUT SIDE (Large Ram)
                =====================
                |                   |
   LOAD (W) --> |    [====]        |
                |   [  P2  ]        |
                |   [  F2  ]        |  D2 = 500 mm
                |   [  A2  ]        |
                =====================
                       |   |
                       |   |   Hydraulic Oil
                       |   |   (Confined, Static)
                       |   |
                =====================
                |                   |
   INPUT        |   [=]            |
   FORCE (F1)-> |  [P1]            |  D1 = 50 mm
   Hand Pump    |  [A1]            |
                |  [F1]            |
                =====================
                INPUT SIDE (Small Plunger)
```

**Working Principle (Read the diagram top-down and left-to-right):**
- The operator applies $F_1$ on the small plunger (diameter $D_1$)
- This generates pressure $P = F_1 / A_1$ in the oil
- By Pascal's Law, the same $P$ acts on the large ram (diameter $D_2$)
- The ram lifts a load $F_2 = P \cdot A_2$, where $F_2 >> F_1$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. State Pascal's law. Mention any two of its industrial applications.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 1]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pascal's Law (1.5 marks):** Pascal's law states that *the pressure applied to a confined static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surface of the containing vessel.*

**Two industrial applications (0.75 marks each):**
1. **Hydraulic press** — used in forging, stamping, and metal forming operations.
2. **Hydraulic jack** — used in automobile service stations to lift vehicles for tyre/wheel servicing.

*(Any other valid application like hydraulic brake, hydraulic lift, hydraulic accumulator also acceptable.)*

---

### Q2. Define pressure. State its SI unit and write the expression for the pressure at a depth `h` in a static fluid of density `ρ`.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sessional Exam Reference]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pressure is defined as the normal force exerted per unit area on a surface. Mathematically, $P = F/A$.

**SI Unit (1 mark):** The SI unit of pressure is **Pascal (Pa)**, where $1\ \text{Pa} = 1\ \text{N/m}^2$. Other practical units include bar ($1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa}$) and $N/mm^2$.

**Expression (1 mark):** The pressure at a depth $h$ in a static fluid of density $\rho$ is given by:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

where $P_0$ is the atmospheric / surface pressure acting on the free surface of the fluid.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, full Module 1 carries 14 marks with internal choice. The marks are split into sub-parts (a) — 7 marks and (b) — 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the pressure at a depth $h$ in a static incompressible fluid. Show that pressure is independent of the shape of the container. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hydraulic press has a plunger diameter of $60\ \text{mm}$ and a ram diameter of $450\ \text{mm}$. If a force of $350\ \text{N}$ is applied on the plunger, find: (i) the pressure developed in the fluid, (ii) the load that can be lifted, and (iii) the distance moved by the ram when the plunger moves through $80\ \text{mm}$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

### Part (a) — Derivation of Hydrostatic Pressure (7 marks)

Consider a static fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and height $dh$ at depth $h$ below the free surface. The forces acting on the element are:

- Pressure on the top face: $P_0 \cdot dA$ (downward)
- Pressure on the bottom face: $P \cdot dA$ (upward)
- Weight of the element: $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dh$ (downward)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** For equilibrium in the vertical direction:

$$P \cdot dA = P_0 \cdot dA + \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dh$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Simplifying (cancel $dA$):

$$P - P_0 = \rho g \, dh$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Integrating from the free surface ($h = 0$, $P = P_0$) to depth $h$:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = \rho g \int_{0}^{h} dh \quad \Rightarrow \quad P - P_0 = \rho g h$$

**Final Result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}$$

**Independence of container shape (1 mark):** The derived expression contains **no term involving the cross-sectional area** $dA$ or the geometry of the container. Hence, pressure at a given depth depends **only on depth, fluid density, and surface pressure** — known as the **hydrostatic paradox**.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical on Hydraulic Press (7 marks)

**Given Data:**

$$D_1 = 60\ \text{mm} = 0.060\ \text{m}, \quad D_2 = 450\ \text{mm} = 0.450\ \text{m}, \quad F_1 = 350\ \text{N}, \quad x_1 = 80\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 1 — Calculate areas (1 mark):**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.060)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.450)^2}{4} = 1.590 \times 10^{-1}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Pressure developed (1 mark):**

$$P = \dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{350}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.238 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 1.238\ \text{bar}$$

**Step 3 — Load that can be lifted (2 marks):**

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 1.238 \times 10^5 \times 1.590 \times 10^{-1} = 19{,}684\ \text{N} \approx 19.68\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Distance moved by ram (2 marks):**

By conservation of volume, $A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$:

$$x_2 = x_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} = 80 \times \dfrac{2.827 \times 10^{-3}}{1.590 \times 10^{-1}} = 1.422\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical Advantage (1 mark):**

$$MA = \dfrac{F_2}{F_1} = \dfrac{19{,}684}{350} \approx 56.24$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Slightly Modified Pattern]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a hydraulic jack. State the relationship between input and output forces. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the working of a hydraulic brake system used in automobiles with a neat diagram. State Pascal's law application in this system. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

### Part (a) — Hydraulic Jack (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):** A hydraulic jack consists of two cylinders of different diameters connected at the bottom and filled with hydraulic oil. The small cylinder houses a **plunger (piston) P₁** of area $A_1$, and the large cylinder houses a **ram (piston) P₂** of area $A_2$. A **pump handle** with check valves (one-way valves) is used to pressurize the fluid.

**Working (4 marks):**
1. The operator pumps the handle, which forces the small piston $P_1$ down.
2. This pressurizes the oil, generating pressure $P = F_1 / A_1$.
3. By **Pascal's Law**, this pressure is transmitted undiminished to the large piston $P_2$.
4. The pressure on the large piston produces a force $F_2 = P \cdot A_2$.
5. Since $A_2 \gg A_1$, the force $F_2$ is large enough to lift a heavy load (e.g., a vehicle).
6. A **release valve** is opened to lower the load, allowing oil to flow back to the reservoir.

**Force Relationship (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = F_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2}$$

---

### Part (b) — Hydraulic Brake System (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):** In an automobile hydraulic brake system, a **master cylinder** is connected via brake lines (filled with brake fluid) to **wheel cylinders** at each wheel. The master cylinder has a small piston connected to the brake pedal. Each wheel cylinder has a larger piston that pushes the brake shoes/pads against the rotating disc or drum.

**Working (4 marks):**
1. When the driver presses the brake pedal, a small force $F_1$ acts on the master cylinder piston (area $A_1$).
2. This generates pressure $P = F_1 / A_1$ in the brake fluid.
3. By **Pascal's Law**, the same pressure $P$ is transmitted equally to all four wheel cylinders.
4. The pressure on each wheel cylinder piston (area $A_2$) produces a force $F_2 = P \cdot A_2$ that pushes the brake pads against the wheel disc.
5. Friction between pads and disc slows down the vehicle.
6. The system is **self-equalizing** — equal braking force is automatically applied to all wheels.

**Pascal's Law Application (1 mark):** Pressure generated in the master cylinder is transmitted undiminished through the brake fluid to all wheel cylinders, ensuring simultaneous and uniform braking at all wheels — critical for vehicle stability.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Pascal's Law Problems:**
> 
> **Trap 1 (–1 mark):** Students write Pascal's law as *"pressure is transmitted equally"* without specifying **"in a confined static fluid"** — this is incomplete. Always mention **confinement** and **static condition**.
> 
> **Trap 2 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert diameters from **mm to m** before computing area. Area must be in $m^2$ for pressure to come out in Pa.
> 
> **Trap 3 (–1 mark):** Confusing force multiplication ratio with area ratio. The correct relation is $F_2 / F_1 = A_2 / A_1$, **NOT** $A_1 / A_2$.
> 
> **Trap 4 (–1 mark):** In hydraulic brake questions, students often forget to mention **"equal braking force on all wheels"** — this is a key application point examiners look for.
> 
> **Trap 5 (–1 mark):** Writing $P = \rho g h$ without $P_0$. When the surface is open to atmosphere, **always include $P_0$** in the final expression.
> 
> **Trap 6 (–2 marks):** Skipping the **diagram** in 7-mark questions. KTU evaluators routinely deduct 1–2 marks for missing neat sketches in descriptive questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Pascal's law applies **only to confined, static, incompressible fluids**.
- ✅ Pressure at a point in a static fluid is a **scalar**, same in all directions.
- ✅ Hydrostatic equation: $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ — independent of container shape.
- ✅ Force multiplication in hydraulic systems: $F_2 = F_1 \times (A_2 / A_1) = F_1 \times (D_2 / D_1)^2$.
- ✅ Displacement ratio: $x_2 = x_1 \times (A_1 / A_2)$ — inverse of force ratio.
- ✅ Energy is **conserved** — force × distance on input side = force × distance on output side.
- ✅ SI unit of pressure: **Pascal (Pa)** = $N/m^2$; $1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa}$.
- ✅ Standard $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$; density of water = $1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$.
- ✅ Key applications: hydraulic press, jack, lift, brake, accumulator, ram, injection moulding.
- ✅ Hydraulic brake: master cylinder → brake fluid → wheel cylinders (Pascal's law in action).
- ✅ Hydraulic jack: small plunger pumps oil → large ram lifts heavy loads.
- ✅ **Always draw a neat labelled diagram** for 7-mark questions.
- ✅ **Always convert mm → m** when computing area from diameter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuity Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Continuity Equation** is a mathematical statement of the **conservation of mass** applied to a fluid in motion. In the context of Industrial Hydraulics and Pneumatics, it is the foundational governing equation that links the **flow velocity**, the **cross-sectional area** of flow, and the **density** of the fluid at different points along a streamline or within a control volume.

For a steady, one-dimensional flow passing through a stream tube, the Continuity Equation is formally written as:

$$\rho_1 \cdot A_1 \cdot V_1 = \rho_2 \cdot A_2 \cdot V_2 = \text{constant}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = mass density of the fluid ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of flow ($\text{m}^2$)
- $V$ = average velocity of flow ($\text{m/s}$)

For an **incompressible fluid** (which is the standard assumption for hydraulic oils used in industrial systems, since their density remains practically constant), the equation simplifies to the **Volumetric Continuity Equation**:

$$A_1 \cdot V_1 = A_2 \cdot V_2 = Q = \text{constant}$$

Where $Q$ is the **volumetric flow rate** ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ or LPM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Continuity Equation is the single most frequently tested topic in Module 1. It forms the mathematical basis for derivations of cylinder velocities, ram speeds in hydraulic presses, and piston travel times — all classic 7-mark and 14-mark problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **garden hose connected to a tap**. When you partially block the hose opening with your thumb, what do you observe?

The water **shoots out faster** and travels a much **longer distance**. Here, the amount (mass) of water entering the hose from the tap per second is exactly equal to the amount leaving the hose per second — **no water is created or destroyed inside the hose**. But because you reduced the outlet area ($A_2 < A_1$), the velocity at the outlet had to increase ($V_2 > V_1$) to push the same volume of water through the smaller opening in the same time.

This is the **Continuity Equation in action**:
> *"The smaller the pipe, the faster the fluid must flow to deliver the same quantity."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Example:** In a hydraulic system, when oil flows from a **large-bore pipe (50 mm diameter)** into a **hydraulic cylinder of smaller bore (25 mm)**, the fluid velocity inside the cylinder automatically becomes **4 times faster** to maintain the same flow rate. This is why hydraulic actuators are designed as inverse functions of their piston areas relative to the supply line.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Engineering Unit (KTU) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Density of hydraulic oil | $\rho$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Typically **850 – 900 kg/m³** at 40 °C |
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | **LPM** (Litres Per Minute) or **CC/REV** |
| Velocity of flow | $V$ | $\text{m/s}$ | **m/s** |
| Cross-sectional area | $A$ | $\text{m}^2$ | **mm²** or **cm²** |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}$ | $\text{kg/s}$ | **kg/s** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ | $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles

The Continuity Equation is built on **two fundamental pillars** of physics:

1. **Conservation of Mass** — Mass can neither be created nor destroyed within a closed system (excluding nuclear reactions). What flows in must flow out.
2. **Steady-State Assumption** — At any given instant, the flow conditions (velocity, density, pressure) at a point do not change with time. Mathematically: $\frac{\partial (\rho V)}{\partial t} = 0$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of the Concept

Consider a **stream tube** (an imaginary bundle of streamlines) carrying a fluid:

- **Step 1 — Mass entering Section 1 in time $dt$:**

The mass of fluid crossing section 1 in time $dt$ equals density $\times$ volume swept:

$$dm_1 = \rho_1 \cdot (A_1 \cdot V_1 \cdot dt)$$

- **Step 2 — Mass leaving Section 2 in time $dt$:**

Similarly, the mass leaving section 2 in the same time $dt$:

$$dm_2 = \rho_2 \cdot (A_2 \cdot V_2 \cdot dt)$$

- **Step 3 — Apply Conservation of Mass:**

By the principle of conservation, the mass entering must equal the mass leaving (no accumulation in steady flow):

$$dm_1 = dm_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \rho_1 A_1 V_1 \, dt = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 \, dt$$

- **Step 4 — Cancel the common time element $dt$:**

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m} \;\; (\text{constant})$$

- **Step 5 — Incompressible flow assumption ($\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$):**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \;\; (\text{volumetric flow rate})$$

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Industrial Systems

The Continuity Equation is the **backbone calculation** for:

- **Sizing hydraulic cylinders** — predicting piston extension/retraction speeds.
- **Valve sizing** — selecting the correct $C_v$ (flow coefficient) for control valves.
- **Pump displacement calculations** — relating pump RPM to LPM output.
- **Hydraulic press operations** — calculating ram velocities for forming and forging.
- **Pneumatic circuit design** — actuator timing and flow requirement calculations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM GOLD:** The following table consolidates every Continuity Equation variant that has appeared in past KTU University Examinations.

| # | Equation | Applicability | Common KTU Use |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Compressible flow (general) | Pneumatic systems, gas flow |
| 2 | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ | Incompressible flow (hydraulic oil) | Hydraulic circuits, oil flow |
| 3 | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Single-section flow rate | Cylinder speed calculations |
| 4 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Velocity from flow and area | Pipe velocity problems |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | Circular pipe/cylinder bore area | Diameter-based problems |
| 6 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate conversion | Pump mass output |
| 7 | $Q \; (\text{LPM}) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \, V \times 60000$ | LPM conversion with $d$ in m, $V$ in m/s | Numerical problem conversion |
| 8 | $V_p = \dfrac{Q}{A_p} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Piston/ram velocity | Hydraulic press, actuator problems |

## 2.5 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why must velocity increase when area decreases?**
> Because the fluid is **incompressible** — its molecules cannot be squeezed into a smaller space. The only way to push the same volume of fluid per second through a smaller cross-section is to **accelerate** the fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How does this relate to hydraulic systems?**
> When a pump delivers a fixed $Q$, a **larger cylinder bore** produces a **slower piston speed** but **higher force**, while a **smaller cylinder bore** produces a **faster piston speed** but **lower force**. This inverse Area–Velocity relationship (via continuity) and the direct Area–Pressure relationship (via Pascal's law) together define the **force-velocity trade-off** in every hydraulic system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Continuity Equation (Integral Form)

### Step 1: Define a Fixed Control Volume (CV)

We define a control volume bounded by an inlet surface (Section 1) and an outlet surface (Section 2), with impermeable side walls. The fluid is the system, and we observe it over a small time interval $dt$.

### Step 2: Express Mass Flow Rate at Inlet

The mass flow rate entering the CV at section 1 is:

$$\dot{m}_1 = \rho_1 \cdot A_1 \cdot V_1 \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \right]$$

**Reasoning:** In time $dt$, a fluid column of length $V_1 \cdot dt$ and cross-section $A_1$ crosses section 1. Its mass is density $\times$ volume $= \rho_1 \cdot A_1 \cdot V_1 \cdot dt$. Dividing by $dt$ gives the rate.

### Step 3: Express Mass Flow Rate at Outlet

Similarly, at section 2:

$$\dot{m}_2 = \rho_2 \cdot A_2 \cdot V_2$$

### Step 4: Apply Conservation of Mass (General Form)

The net mass accumulation inside the CV equals the difference between mass entering and mass leaving:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$$

### Step 5: Apply Steady-State Condition

For **steady flow**, there is no accumulation inside the control volume:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$$

### Step 6: Final General Form

$$\boxed{\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \text{constant}}$$

### Step 7: Specialization for Incompressible Flow

For hydraulic oils, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho = \text{constant}$. Dividing both sides by $\rho$:

$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Continuity Equation Variants

| Case | Fluid Type | Equation Form | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Case A | Incompressible, circular pipe | $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot V$ | Hydraulic line sizing |
| Case B | Incompressible, cylinder piston | $V_p = \dfrac{Q}{A_p} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Actuator speed |
| Case C | Compressible, isothermal gas | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Pneumatic circuits |
| Case D | Compressible, ideal gas (isothermal) | $P_1 A_1 V_1 = P_2 A_2 V_2$ (since $\rho \propto P$) | Compressed air networks |
| Case E | Branching pipe (multiple outlets) | $Q_1 = Q_2 + Q_3 + \ldots + Q_n$ | Manifold flow distribution |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Model Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked example illustrates the **exact KTU valuation method** — every algebraic step is written explicitly.

**Problem:** Oil (density $\rho = 880 \; \text{kg/m}^3$) flows through a 50 mm diameter pipe and enters a hydraulic cylinder of bore diameter 100 mm. If the average velocity in the pipe is $4 \; \text{m/s}$, find:
1. The volumetric flow rate $Q$.
2. The velocity of the piston in the cylinder.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the cross-sectional area of the pipe:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the volumetric flow rate $Q$:**

$$Q = A_1 \cdot V_1 = (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) \times 4 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to **LPM** (multiply by $60 \times 1000$):

$$Q = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 1000 = 471.24 \; \text{LPM}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the cross-sectional area of the cylinder bore:**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Continuity Equation to find piston velocity:**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{Q}{A_2}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Verification:** Since $d_2 = 2 d_1$, the area ratio is $4:1$, so velocity should drop by factor 4: $V_2 = 4/4 = 1 \; \text{m/s}$. ✓

## 3.4 Derivation of Piston Travel Time (KTU Favourite)

For a hydraulic cylinder with bore area $A_p$ and stroke length $L$, the time $t$ for the piston to complete one full stroke is:

$$V_{cylinder} = A_p \cdot L \quad \text{(volume of oil required)}$$

$$t = \frac{V_{cylinder}}{Q} = \frac{A_p \cdot L}{Q}$$

Substituting $Q = A_p \cdot V_p$:

$$\boxed{t = \frac{L}{V_p} = \frac{A_p \cdot L}{Q}}$$

This is the **second most tested equation** in Module 1 (after the main continuity equation itself).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Continuity Principle in a Hydraulic Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Reservoir Tank] --> B1[Suction Strainer]
    B1 --> C1[Hydraulic Pump]
    C1 --> D1[Pressure Relief Valve]
    C1 --> E1[Flow Control Valve]
    E1 --> F1[Directional Control Valve DCV]
    F1 --> G1[Inlet Pipe Section 1]
    G1 --> H1[Hydraulic Cylinder Section 2]
    H1 --> I1[Piston Rod Extension]
    H1 --> J1[Return Line to Tank]
    J1 --> A1
    G1 --- K1[Q1 equals A1 times V1]
    H1 --- L1[Q2 equals A2 times V2]
    K1 --- M1[Continuity Equation Q1 equals Q2]
    L1 --- M1
    M1 --> N1[Constant Volumetric Flow Rate]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Representation — Continuity Across a Variable Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Inlet Section Large Diameter D1] --> B0[Stream Tube Convergence]
    B0 --> C0[Throat Section Small Diameter D2]
    C0 --> D0[Outlet Section]
    A0 --- A1[A1 equals pi by 4 times D1 squared]
    C0 --- C1[A2 equals pi by 4 times D2 squared]
    A1 --- E1[Continuity Principle]
    C1 --- E1
    E1 --> F1[A1 times V1 equals A2 times V2]
    F1 --> G1[If D1 greater than D2 then V1 less than V2]
    F1 --> H1[Mass flow rate mdot is constant]
```

## 4.3 Flow Distribution in a Branching Manifold (Continuity for Multi-Outlet)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Main Supply Line Q total] --> J1[Junction Node 1]
    J1 --> B1[Branch 1 Flow Q1]
    J1 --> B2[Branch 2 Flow Q2]
    J1 --> B3[Branch 3 Flow Q3]
    B1 --> W1[Actuator 1]
    B2 --> W2[Actuator 2]
    B3 --> W3[Actuator 3]
    S --- EQ1[Continuity at Junction]
    EQ1 --> R1[Q total equals Q1 plus Q2 plus Q3]
    R1 --> R2[Conservation of Mass Applied]
```

## 4.4 Concept Map — How the Continuity Equation Interlinks with Other Module 1 Topics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CO[Continuity Equation A1V1 equals A2V2] --> PASC[Pascal Law Force equals Pressure times Area]
    CO --> VEL[Velocity of Piston V equals Q by A]
    CO --> TIME[Stroke Time t equals L by V]
    CO --> FLOW[Volumetric Flow Rate Q equals A times V]
    CO --> MASS[Mass Flow mdot equals rho times Q]
    PASC --> FF[Force Velocity Trade off in Hydraulics]
    VEL --> FF
    FLOW --> PUMP[Pump Displacement Q equals Vdisp times N]
    TIME --> ACT[Actuator Sizing Problems]
    MASS --> COMP[Compressible Flow in Pneumatics]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

> **State the Continuity Equation for a steady incompressible fluid flow. Mention the physical principle on which it is based.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Mark Distribution):**
- **(1 Mark)** Physical principle: *Law of Conservation of Mass*.
- **(1 Mark)** Statement: *The mass of fluid passing through any cross-section of a stream tube per unit time is constant.*
- **(1 Mark)** Equation:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \;\; (\text{for incompressible flow})$$

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series II]`

> **A hydraulic oil of density $880 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ flows through a pipe of diameter 60 mm at a velocity of 3 m/s. Calculate the (a) volumetric flow rate in LPM and (b) mass flow rate in kg/s.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Volumetric Flow Rate (1.5 Marks):**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.060)^2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$$

$$Q = A \cdot V = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times 3 = 8.482 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 8.482 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 1000 = 508.94 \; \text{LPM}$$

**(b) Mass Flow Rate (1.5 Marks):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 880 \times 8.482 \times 10^{-3} = 7.464 \; \text{kg/s}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Most common error (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert diameter from mm to m before applying $A = \pi d^2 / 4$. Always check units FIRST.
> - **Second most common error (–1.5 marks):** Mixing LPM with m³/s in the same equation. Use **one consistent unit system** throughout.
> - **Third most common error (–1 mark):** Stating the continuity equation for compressible flow in a problem that explicitly states "incompressible oil" — read the problem carefully.
> - **Valuation Tip:** Showing the principle/assumption before writing the equation fetches **1 extra mark** in KTU board exams.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Continuity Equation for a one-dimensional steady flow of an incompressible fluid through a variable cross-section pipe, starting from the principle of conservation of mass.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks)** A hydraulic pump delivers oil at a flow rate of 40 LPM to a hydraulic cylinder of bore diameter 80 mm and rod diameter 45 mm. Calculate: (i) The extension velocity of the piston. (ii) The retraction velocity of the piston. (iii) The time required for a stroke of 300 mm during extension.

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Step | Content | Marks |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Define control volume between sections 1 and 2 | 1 |
| 2 | Express mass entering in time $dt$ as $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 \, dt$ | 1.5 |
| 3 | Express mass leaving in time $dt$ as $\rho_2 A_2 V_2 \, dt$ | 1.5 |
| 4 | Apply steady-state conservation: $dm_{in} = dm_{out}$ | 1 |
| 5 | Cancel $dt$ and obtain general form $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | 1 |
| 6 | Apply incompressible condition and write final form $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ | 1 |

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert flow rate to SI units (1 Mark):**

$$Q = 40 \; \text{LPM} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Piston area on full bore side (1 Mark):**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$$

**(i) Extension velocity (2 Marks):** During extension, oil acts on the full piston area:

$$V_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \; \text{m/s} \;\; (\approx 7.96 \; \text{m/min})$$

**Step 3 — Annulus area on rod side (1 Mark):**

$$A_{annulus} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} [(0.080)^2 - (0.045)^2] = 3.437 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Retraction velocity (1 Mark):** During retraction, oil acts on the annular area:

$$V_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_{annulus}} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{3.437 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1940 \; \text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Stroke time during extension (1 Mark):**

$$t = \frac{L}{V_{ext}} = \frac{0.300}{0.1326} = 2.263 \; \text{s}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the principle of continuity in a hydraulic system. How is the Continuity Equation modified for compressible fluid flow? Give two industrial applications of the Continuity Equation.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** In a hydraulic press, oil flows from a 25 mm diameter pipe into a ram cylinder of 150 mm bore diameter. The flow rate is 30 LPM. Determine: (i) Velocity in the pipe. (ii) Velocity of the ram. (iii) Mass flow rate, given oil density is 870 kg/m³. (iv) Verify the Continuity Equation numerically.

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **(2 Marks)** Sketch description: Show a pipe with section 1 (large) converging into a smaller section 2, with arrow markings for $V_1$ and $V_2$, and label $A_1 > A_2$ and $V_1 < V_2$.
- **(2 Marks)** Continuity equation for incompressible flow: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$.
- **(1.5 Marks)** Modified form for compressible flow: $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ — density must be retained because gas compressibility changes the mass per unit volume.
- **(1.5 Marks)** Two industrial applications:
  1. Calculation of cylinder piston speed in hydraulic actuators.
  2. Sizing of flow control valves and orifices in hydraulic circuits.

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert flow rate (0.5 Mark):**

$$Q = 30 \; \text{LPM} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Pipe area (0.5 Mark):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2$$

**(i) Velocity in the pipe (1.5 Marks):**

$$V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.0185 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Ram cylinder area (0.5 Mark):**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Velocity of the ram (1.5 Marks):**

$$V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.0283 \; \text{m/s} \;\; (\approx 1.7 \; \text{m/min})$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate (1 Mark):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 870 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4} = 0.435 \; \text{kg/s}$$

**(iv) Verification of Continuity Equation (1.5 Marks):**

$$A_1 V_1 = (4.909 \times 10^{-4})(1.0185) = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$A_2 V_2 = (1.767 \times 10^{-2})(0.0283) = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\therefore A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Continuity Equation (Module 1, PCMRT503)**

- 🔹 **Foundation principle:** Conservation of Mass (NOT conservation of energy — that is Bernoulli's).
- 🔹 **General form:** $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m} = \text{constant}$.
- 🔹 **Incompressible form (use this for hydraulic oil):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$.
- 🔹 **Compressible form (use this for air/gas):** Keep $\rho$ in the equation; for isothermal ideal gas, $\rho \propto P$, so $P_1 A_1 V_1 = P_2 A_2 V_2$.
- 🔹 **Key derived relation:** $V_{piston} = Q / A_{piston}$ — single most used formula in KTU numericals.
- 🔹 **Piston travel time:** $t = L / V_p = A_p L / Q$ — used for hydraulic press and actuator timing problems.
- 🔹 **Area for double-acting cylinder retraction:** $A_{annulus} = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$ — the **retraction speed is always higher** than extension speed for the same flow rate.
- 🔹 **Branching pipe continuity:** $Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out}$ — useful in manifold problems.
- 🔹 **Always convert LPM → m³/s** by dividing by $60{,}000$ before plugging into SI equations.
- 🔹 **Always convert mm → m** for diameters (forget this and lose 2 marks).
- 🔹 **Flow rate units used in KTU:** LPM, m³/s, CC/REV, GPM (US).
- 🔹 **Typical density of hydraulic oil:** $850$–$900 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ at $40\;°\text{C}$.
- 🔹 **KTU's favourite trick:** They will ask you to verify the continuity equation numerically after computing $V_1$ and $V_2$ — do NOT skip the verification step; it carries **1–1.5 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conservation of Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Conservation of Energy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Industrial Hydraulics & Pneumatics**, the **Conservation of Energy** is the foundational principle that governs all fluid power systems. It is a direct consequence of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a steady, flowing fluid stream. The principle mathematically asserts that for an ideal fluid (inviscid and incompressible) flowing along a streamline, the **sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, potential energy, and any shaft work done on or by the fluid remains constant** between any two points in the flow field. This principle is universally expressed in fluid power engineering as **Bernoulli's Equation** (Daniel Bernoulli, 1738), which is the cornerstone equation of hydraulic circuit analysis. In real hydraulic circuits, however, mechanical energy is continuously converted into **thermal energy** due to viscous friction, turbulent eddies, and component throttling, leading to **head loss** ($h_L$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (CO1 – Mapped):** > The *Conservation of Energy* in hydraulic systems states that the total mechanical energy of a fluid particle — comprising **pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head** — is conserved in the absence of friction, and is reduced by the frictional loss term $h_L$ in a real industrial circuit. Mathematically, the **General Energy Equation** between any two sections (1) and (2) of a hydraulic line is written as: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ where every term has units of **meters (m)** — i.e., expressed as fluid head. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are standing at the top of a water slide holding a sealed plastic bottle. As you release it: - At the **top (Point 1)**: The bottle has lots of **potential energy** (elevation) but is barely moving. The pressure inside the bottle equals atmospheric pressure. - As it **slides down (transitions)**: The bottle speeds up (gains **kinetic energy**), drops in height (loses **potential energy**), and the water inside experiences a pressure change. - At the **bottom (Point 2)**: The bottle is moving fast (kinetic energy is high), elevation is low (potential energy is low), and pressure may differ. If the slide were **perfectly smooth and frictionless**, the *total energy* at the top would exactly equal the total energy at the bottom. But in reality, some energy is "lost" as heat from the rubbing of the bottle against the slide — this is the **head loss** $h_L$. > [!NOTE] > **Hydraulic Analogy:** In an industrial hydraulic circuit, the **pump** acts like the person lifting water to the top. The **actuator (cylinder/motor)** acts like the bottom of the slide, where stored fluid energy is converted back to useful mechanical work. The **valves, bends, and pipe walls** cause the friction — analogous to the slide's roughness. ## 1.3 Standard Constants & Metrics (Bold Highlighted) The following physical quantities and constants govern the conservation equation in hydraulics: | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) | |---|---|---| | $p$ | Fluid pressure | **Pa (N/m²)** or **bar (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa)** | | $\rho$ | Mass density of hydraulic oil | **kg/m³** (typically **860–890 kg/m³** for mineral oils) | | $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | | $v$ | Flow velocity | **m/s** | | $z$ | Elevation above datum | **m** | | $Q$ | Volumetric flow rate | **m³/s** or **L/min (lpm)** | | $h_L$ | Head loss (frictional) | **m** | | $P$ | Hydraulic power | **W (J/s)** or **kW** | | $\gamma$ | Specific weight of fluid | **N/m³** ($\gamma = \rho g$) | | $\eta$ | Volumetric / overall efficiency | **dimensionless (0 < η ≤ 1)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Specific weight of standard hydraulic mineral oil** is approximately **$\gamma \approx 8700$ N/m³ to 8800 N/m³** at 40°C. This conversion is critical for KTU numerical problems where pressure must be converted to head in meters. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Forms of Fluid Energy in a Hydraulic Line For any control volume of fluid in a hydraulic circuit, mechanical energy manifests in three distinct forms. These must be enumerated clearly for the KTU examiner. ### 2.1.1 Pressure Energy (Flow Energy / Flow Work) - This is the energy a fluid possesses by virtue of being pressurized. - It is the work done by external pressure forces to push the fluid into and through the control volume. - **Per unit weight**, this is called the **pressure head**: $h_p = \dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ (units: m of fluid). - It is the *most dominant* form of energy in a typical industrial hydraulic circuit (often **hundreds of meters of head**, equivalent to **100–350 bar**). ### 2.1.2 Kinetic Energy (Velocity Energy) - This is the energy a fluid possesses by virtue of its bulk motion. - **Per unit weight**, this is the **velocity head** or **kinetic head**: $h_v = \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ (units: m of fluid). - In most industrial hydraulics, flow velocities are kept low (typically **1–6 m/s**) to minimize losses, so this term is **usually small** (often **0.05 m to 2 m** of head) compared to the pressure head. ### 2.1.3 Potential Energy (Elevation Energy) - This is the energy a fluid possesses by virtue of its position relative to a reference datum. - **Per unit weight**, this is the **elevation head** or **datum head**: $h_z = z$ (units: m). - In most machine-tool hydraulic circuits, the elevation differences are small (a few meters), so this term is often **neglected** for short piping runs. ## 2.2 The Logic of Conservation (Step-by-Step) The derivation of Bernoulli's equation (which embodies conservation of energy) proceeds from Euler's equation of motion along a streamline, integrated from point 1 to point 2. Conceptually, the logic is: 1. **Apply Newton's Second Law** ($F = ma$) to an elemental fluid parcel moving along a streamline. 2. **Identify the three force components** acting on the parcel: pressure force, gravity (weight) force, and (in the extended form) pump/ turbine shaft force. 3. **Integrate** the equation of motion along the streamline path from section 1 to section 2. 4. **Recognize** that the integrated form is the **Work-Energy Theorem**, where the net work done on the fluid equals its change in kinetic energy. 5. **Introduce a loss term** $h_L$ to account for irreversible conversion of mechanical energy into thermal energy (friction, turbulence). 6. **Rearrange** into the standard head-form to obtain the General Energy Equation. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Conservation of Energy principle is not merely academic — it is the working equation behind every component selection in a hydraulic circuit: - **Pump Sizing**: A pump must supply enough head to overcome the sum of pressure head required at the actuator, velocity head differences, elevation head, and frictional head loss. Pump rating is directly computed from the energy equation. - **Cylinder Force Prediction**: The thrust produced by a hydraulic cylinder is $F = p \times A - F_{friction}$, derivable from the pressure-energy component. - **Valve Sizing**: Pressure drop across a directional/flow control valve (which is a deliberate "head loss") is computed as $\Delta p = \rho g \, h_L$. - **Heat Exchanger Sizing**: The total frictional head loss $h_L$ multiplied by $\rho g Q$ gives the **heat dissipation rate** in watts — the basis of reservoir cooling design. - **Pipe Diameter Selection**: For a given flow rate, the velocity head $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ dictates the minimum pipe bore to avoid excessive turbulence and cavitation. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains the **must-know equations** for any KTU 2024 question on the Conservation of Energy in hydraulic circuits. All equations are in **SI units** unless otherwise noted. | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bernoulli (Ideal, head form) | $\frac{p}{\rho
