g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = \text{const}$ | Conservation of energy along streamline for inviscid flow | m | | 2 | General Energy Equation (Real) | $\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | Real flow with friction | m | | 3 | General Energy Equation (with pump/turbine) | $\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_P = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + H_T + h_L$ | Pump adds head $H_P$, turbine extracts $H_T$ | m | | 4 | Pressure Head | $h_p = \dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ | Pressure expressed as fluid column | m | | 5 | Velocity Head | $h_v = \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Kinetic energy per unit weight | m | | 6 | Elevation Head | $h_z = z$ | Potential energy per unit weight | m | | 7 | Hydraulic Power | $P = \Delta p \cdot Q$ | Power delivered by pump to fluid | W | | 8 | Hydraulic Power (head form) | $P = \rho g Q H$ | Power expressed via head | W | | 9 | Pumping Head Required | $H_P = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho g} + h_L$ | Total head pump must supply | m | | 10 | Pressure Drop → Head Loss | $h_L = \dfrac{\Delta p_{loss}}{\rho g}$ | Conversion of pressure drop to head | m | | 11 | Frictional Head Loss (Darcy–Weisbach) | $h_L = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Pipe friction loss | m | | 12 | Cylinder Thrust Force | $F = p \cdot A$ | Force from pressure on piston area | N | | 13 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Weight per unit volume of oil | N/m³ | | 14 | Pump Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{volumetric} \times \eta_{mechanical}$ | Combined pump losses | — | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** When a numerical problem states "*oil with specific gravity 0.86 is pumped at …*", the examiner expects you to compute $\rho = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860$ kg/m³ and $\gamma = 0.86 \times 9810 = 8436.6$ N/m³ **before** substituting into the energy equation. Skipping this is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (Head Form) — For 14-Mark Mastery The KTU examiner routinely awards 5–7 marks to a clean derivation of Bernoulli's equation. We begin by considering a fluid parcel of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ moving along a streamline between sections 1 and 2. ### Step 1 — Force Balance on the Fluid Parcel Consider a small cylindrical element of fluid with cross-sectional area $dA$, length $ds$, and mass $dm = \rho \, dA \, ds$. The forces acting on this element along the streamline direction are: - **Pressure force at the rear face** (pushing forward): $p \, dA$ - **Pressure force at the front face** (pushing backward): $(p + dp) \, dA$ - **Component of weight along the streamline** (pulling back if flow ascends): $-dm \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta = -\rho \, dA \, ds \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{dz}{ds}$ The net force on the element equals mass times acceleration along the streamline. Acceleration along the streamline is $v \dfrac{dv}{ds}$ (from the chain rule: $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{dv}{ds} \cdot \dfrac{ds}{dt} = v \dfrac{dv}{ds}$). Applying Newton's Second Law: $$p \, dA - (p + dp) \, dA - \rho \, g \, dA \, ds \cdot \frac{dz}{ds} = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot v \frac{dv}{ds}$$ ### Step 2 — Simplification Divide the entire equation by $\rho \, dA \, ds$: $$-\frac{dp}{\rho \, ds} - g \frac{dz}{ds} = v \frac{dv}{ds}$$ Multiply through by $ds$ and rearrange: $$-\frac{dp}{\rho} - g \, dz = v \, dv$$ Equivalently: $$\frac{dp}{\rho} + v \, dv + g \, dz = 0$$ ### Step 3 — Integration Along the Streamline Assuming the fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho$), we can integrate the above differential equation from point 1 to point 2: $$\int_{1}^{2} \frac{dp}{\rho} + \int_{1}^{2} v \, dv + \int_{1}^{2} g \, dz = 0$$ $$\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1) = 0$$ ### Step 4 — Rearrangement to the Energy Form Rearranging so that all "1" terms are on the left and all "2" terms are on the right: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$ Now, dividing every term by $g$ to express the equation in **head form (meters of fluid)**: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ This is **Bernoulli's Equation** — the mathematical statement of **Conservation of Energy for an ideal (inviscid, incompressible) fluid** along a streamline. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's View:** The energy form (per unit weight) is preferred over the headless form (per unit mass or per unit volume) for hydraulic circuit problems because every term then has the same unit (m), allowing direct arithmetic addition/subtraction of energy "heads." ## 3.2 Derivation of Hydraulic Power Equation The fluid power delivered by a pump to the hydraulic fluid is the rate at which the pump does work on the fluid. Consider a pump delivering flow $Q$ (m³/s) against a pressure rise $\Delta p$ (Pa). ### Step 1 — Work Done per Unit Volume By the definition of pressure as work per unit volume, the work done by the pump on **each cubic meter** of fluid is: $$W_{per \, m^3} = \Delta p \quad \text{(Joules per m³, which is Pa)}$$ ### Step 2 — Volume Delivered per Second The pump delivers $Q$ cubic meters of fluid per second. ### Step 3 — Power as the Time Rate of Work Therefore, the **fluid power** (also called **hydraulic power**) is: $$P_{hydraulic} = \Delta p \cdot Q$$ This is the **fundamental power equation** in hydraulics. It is one of the most frequently tested results in KTU exams. ### Step 4 — Conversion to Head Form Since $\Delta p = \rho g H$ (where $H$ is the head developed by the pump in meters), we can equivalently write: $$P_{hydraulic} = \rho g Q H$$ This alternative form is useful when the problem provides head rather than pressure, or when energy balance across the entire circuit is required. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** Oil of specific gravity **0.86** and kinematic viscosity **$30 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s** is pumped through a horizontal pipe of diameter **50 mm** at a flow rate of **$2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s**. The pressure at the inlet of the pipe is **$1.2 \times 10^5$ Pa** and at the outlet is **$0.4 \times 10^5$ Pa**. Determine: 1. The head loss in the pipe. 2. The hydraulic power dissipated as heat. **Solution:** ### Step 1 — Extract and Convert Data - $\rho = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860$ kg/m³ - $\gamma = \rho g = 860 \times 9.81 = 8436.6$ N/m³ - $D = 0.05$ m - $Q = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s - $p_1 = 1.2 \times 10^5$ Pa - $p_2 = 0.4 \times 10^5$ Pa - $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal pipe, so elevation terms cancel) - $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$ m² - $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1273$ m/s ### Step 2 — Apply the General Energy Equation Since the pipe is horizontal and the diameter is constant (so $v_1 = v_2$), the velocity head terms cancel: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + h_L$$ $$h_L = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = \frac{(1.2 - 0.4) \times 10^5}{8436.6}$$ $$h_L = \frac{0.8 \times 10^5}{8436.6} = 9.483 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 3 — Hydraulic Power Dissipated The pressure drop $\Delta p = 0.8 \times 10^5$ Pa is the energy lost per unit volume. The rate of energy dissipation is: $$P_{loss} = \Delta p \cdot Q = 0.8 \times 10^5 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$P_{loss} = 20 \text{ W}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check:** This 20 W of heat must be removed by the reservoir + cooler combination to prevent the oil from overheating. The KTU examiner will often ask a follow-up: *"If the reservoir holds 40 L of oil, by how much will the oil temperature rise in 1 hour if no cooling is provided?"* (Specific heat of oil $\approx 1880$ J/kg·K; $\Delta T = \dfrac{20 \times 3600}{860 \times 0.04 \times 1880} \approx 1.11$ °C.) ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Energy Heads at a Glance | Energy Form | Symbol | Head Expression | Typical Magnitude in Industrial Circuit (m of oil) | Example Source | |---|---|---|---|---| | Pressure energy | $h_p$ | $p / (\rho g)$ | 100 – 500 m | Pump discharge at 100–400 bar | | Kinetic energy | $h_v$ | $v^2 / (2g)$ | 0.05 – 2 m | Flow at 1–6 m/s in 25–50 mm pipes | | Potential energy | $h_z$ | $z$ | 0 – 5 m | Vertical pipe risers in machine tools | | Head loss (friction) | $h_L$ | varies | 1 – 50 m | Long return lines, valves, fittings | | Pump head | $H_P$ | $\Delta p_{pump} / (\rho g)$ | 100 – 500 m | Matches circuit demand + losses | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Energy Flow Through a Hydraulic Circuit (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete transformation of mechanical energy** in a typical industrial hydraulic circuit, mapping the Conservation of Energy principle from electrical input to useful mechanical output at the actuator. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Electric Motor
Mech Input Power Pin] --> B[Hydraulic Pump
Volumetric efficiency etav] B --> C[Pressurized Oil Stream
Pressure Energy hp] C --> D[Distribution Pipes
Kinetic Energy hv] C --> E[Directional Control Valve
Throttling Loss hL1] C --> F[Flow Control Valve
Throttling Loss hL2] D --> G[Flexible Hose to Actuator
Friction Loss hL3] E --> H[Hydraulic Cylinder
Pressure to Force F=pA] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Mechanical Work Output
W=F times L stroke] I --> J[Load Force and Velocity
Useful Power Pout=Fv] H --> K[Return Line
Friction Loss hL4] K --> L[Reservoir Tank
Heat Dissipation q] L --> M[Settling and Cooling
Thermal Energy to Air] M --> B classDef inputBox fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef energyBox fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lossBox fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputBox fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A inputBox class B,C,D,H,I,J energyBox class E,F,G,K,L,M lossBox ``` ## 4.2 Energy Conversion Map (Bernoulli's Three Heads) This diagram depicts how the three heads (pressure, velocity, elevation) and the loss term interact at any two sections of a hydraulic line. ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Section 1
hp1 plus hv1 plus hz1] --> CORE[Energy Conservation
General Energy Equation] S2[Section 2
hp2 plus hv2 plus hz2] --> CORE LOSS[Frictional Head Loss hL] --> CORE PUMP[Pump Head HP] --> CORE TURB[Turbine Head HT] --> CORE CORE --> OUT[Total Mechanical Energy
Balanced at All Sections] classDef secBox fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef extBox fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outBox fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class S1,S2 secBox class LOSS,PUMP,TURB extBox class OUT,CORE outBox ``` ## 4.3 Pump–Actuator Energy Flow (Industry Standard Schematic) The following Mermaid diagram traces a **real industrial press circuit** and identifies where the energy is supplied, transmitted, dissipated, and recovered. ```mermaid flowchart TD EM[3-phase Induction Motor
7.5 kW rating] --> CP[Variable Displacement
Piston Pump] CP -->|Flow Q plus Pressure p| ST1[Strainer Suction Line
Velocity Head low] ST1 --> DCV[4 by 3 Directional
Control Valve] DCV -->|Throttling loss| FCV[Flow Control Valve
Meter out circuit] FCV -->|Friction loss in hose| CYL[Double Acting
Hydraulic Cylinder] CYL -->|Useful Work| LOAD[Press Tool
Force F equals pA] CYL -->|Exhaust return| RV[Return Line Check Valve] RV -->|Friction loss| COOL[Oil Cooler
Heat Exchanger] COOL -->|Thermal rejection| AIR[Ambient Air] COOL --> RES[Reservoir 80 L
Level and Temperature] RES --> ST1 classDef motorBlock fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef flowBlock fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lossBlock fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputBlock fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class EM,CP motorBlock class ST1,DCV,FCV flowBlock class COOL,AIR lossBlock class CYL,LOAD,RES outputBlock ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each blue box represents an **energy transmission** node, each red box represents an **energy loss / dissipation** node, and each green box represents an **energy storage or useful output** node. This color coding is consistent with how a KTU examiner visualises a hydraulic energy audit. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State and explain the principle of conservation of energy as applied to a flowing fluid in a hydraulic system.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The principle of conservation of energy, as applied to a flowing fluid, states that the total mechanical energy of a fluid particle remains constant along a streamline in the absence of friction, and decreases by the head loss $h_L$ in a real flow. For a fluid in a hydraulic circuit, this energy is composed of three parts: 1. **Pressure energy** (per unit weight): $h_p = \dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ 2. **Kinetic energy** (per unit weight): $h_v = \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ 3. **Potential energy** (per unit weight): $h_z = z$ Mathematically: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ This is known as the **General Energy Equation** (or **Bernoulli's equation with losses**), and it is the fundamental tool for analysing pump power, valve pressure drops, and pipe friction in hydraulic circuits. (3 marks) --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head. State their SI units.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Head | Definition | Expression | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Pressure Head | Energy per unit weight due to fluid pressure | $h_p = \dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ | m | | Velocity Head | Energy per unit weight due to bulk motion | $h_v = \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | m | | Elevation Head | Energy per unit weight due to height above datum | $h_z = z$ | m | All three heads have the **SI unit of metres (m)** of fluid, allowing them to be directly added or subtracted in the energy equation. In a typical industrial hydraulic circuit, the **pressure head is the largest** (often 100–500 m of oil), the **elevation head is small** (a few metres), and the **velocity head is negligible** (< 2 m) for low-speed flow. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Always** convert pressure to head (or to energy per unit volume) **before** substituting into the energy equation. Mixing pressure (Pa) with head (m) is a **3-mark deduction**. > 2. **Never** forget the head loss $h_L$ in a real flow problem. If the problem does not mention friction explicitly, the examiner is testing whether you know that "real" implies loss. > 3. **Show units at every step.** KTU examiners award partial marks for unit consistency. > 4. **Sketch a free-body / energy diagram** of the control volume before writing equations — this earns 1–2 free marks. > 5. **Do not confuse volumetric efficiency with overall efficiency.** Pump electrical input power uses overall efficiency; pump fluid output uses volumetric × mechanical. --- ### Part B – Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation from Euler's equation of motion, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a hydraulic press, oil of density **880 kg/m³** is pumped at a rate of **$6 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s** through a pipe of diameter **40 mm**. The pressure at the pump outlet is **80 bar** and the pressure at the cylinder inlet is **60 bar**. The cylinder is at the same elevation as the pump. Calculate: 1. The velocities at the pump outlet and the cylinder inlet. 2. The head loss in the pipe. 3. The hydraulic power transmitted to the cylinder. 4. The power lost due to friction. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** *(The full derivation is given in Section 3.1 of these notes. The KTU-expected answer must include the following 7 logical steps, each worth 1 mark:)* 1. **Assumption statement (1 mark):** Steady, incompressible, inviscid, irrotational flow along a streamline. 2. **Control volume selection (1 mark):** A cylindrical fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$. 3. **Force identification (1 mark):** Pressure forces at both faces, gravity component along streamline. 4. **Newton's Second Law application (1 mark):** Net force = mass × acceleration along streamline, where $a = v \dfrac{dv}{ds}$. 5. **Differential equation (1 mark):** $\dfrac{dp}{\rho} + v \, dv + g \, dz = 0$. 6. **Integration (1 mark):** From section 1 to section 2, assuming constant $\rho$. 7. **Final form (1 mark):** $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** - $\rho = 880$ kg/m³, $\gamma = 880 \times 9.81 = 8632.8$ N/m³ - $Q = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s - $D = 0.04$ m, so $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.04)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$ m² - $p_1 = 80$ bar $= 80 \times 10^5$ Pa (pump outlet) - $p_2 = 60$ bar $= 60 \times 10^5$ Pa (cylinder inlet) - $z_1 = z_2$ (same elevation) - $D_1 = D_2 = 0.04$ m (same diameter pipe) **Step 1: Velocities (2 marks)** Since the pipe has constant diameter from pump to cylinder, the velocity is the same at both sections: $$v_1 = v_2 = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.4775 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2: Head loss (2 marks)** Apply the General Energy Equation with $v_1 = v_2$ and $z_1 = z_2$: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ $$h_L = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = \frac{(80 - 60) \times 10^5}{8632.8}$$ $$h_L = \frac{20 \times 10^5}{8632.8} = 231.68 \text{ m of oil}$$ **Step 3: Hydraulic power transmitted to cylinder (1.5 marks)** $$P_{cyl} = p_2 \cdot Q = 60 \times 10^5 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 3600 \text{ W} = 3.6 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4: Power lost due to friction (1.5 marks)** $$P_{loss} = (p_1 - p_2) \cdot Q = 20 \times 10^5 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 1200 \text{ W} = 1.2 \text{ kW}$$ **Verification (KTU Examiner Check):** Total pump power = $P_{pump} = p_1 \cdot Q = 80 \times 10^5 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 4800$ W $= 4.8$ kW. Note that $P_{pump} = P_{cyl} + P_{loss} = 3.6 + 1.2 = 4.8$ kW. **Energy balance verified.** --- ### Part B – Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the energy flow through a typical hydraulic circuit. List the major sources of energy loss in such a circuit. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hydraulic pump delivers oil at a flow rate of **30 L/min** against a pressure of **140 bar**. The overall efficiency of the pump is **85%** and the motor driving the pump has an efficiency of **90%**. Calculate: 1. The hydraulic power output of the pump. 2. The mechanical power input to the pump. 3. The electrical power input to the motor. 4. The power lost in the pump and in the motor separately. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Energy Flow Explanation (7 marks):** *Energy flow description (4 marks):* The electrical energy supplied to the motor is first converted into mechanical rotational energy. The pump converts this mechanical energy into **hydraulic (pressure) energy** of the oil. The pressurized oil transmits this energy through pipes, hoses, and valves (with frictional losses) to the actuator, where it is converted back into **useful mechanical work** (linear motion of a cylinder or rotary motion of a motor). The exhaust oil returns to the reservoir, where the residual heat is rejected to the atmosphere via the reservoir walls or an oil cooler. *Sources of energy loss (3 marks, 1 each):* 1. **Friction in pipes, hoses, and fittings** (major head loss in long runs and small-bore tubing). 2. **Throttling losses in control valves** (directional, flow control, and pressure relief valves). 3. **Internal leakage** in pump, valves, and actuator (volumetric loss). 4. **Mechanical friction** in pump and motor bearings, gear meshes, and piston seals. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** - $Q = 30$ L/min $= \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s - $p = 140$ bar $= 140 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 1.4 \times 10^7$ Pa - $\eta_{pump,overall} = 0.85$ - $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$ **Step 1: Hydraulic power output of pump (2 marks)** $$P_{hydraulic} = p \cdot Q = 1.4 \times 10^7 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 7000 \text{ W} = 7.0 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2: Mechanical power input to pump (2 marks)** $$P_{mech,in} = \frac{P_{hydraulic}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{7.0}{0.85} = 8.235 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3: Electrical power input to motor (1.5 marks)** $$P_{elec} = \frac{P_{mech,in}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{8.235}{0.90} = 9.15 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4: Power losses (1.5 marks)** Pump loss: $P_{loss,pump} = P_{mech,in} - P_{hydraulic} = 8.235 - 7.0 = 1.235$ kW Motor loss: $P_{loss,motor} = P_{elec} - P_{mech,in} = 9.15 - 8.235 = 0.915$ kW Total loss: $P_{loss,total} = 1.235 + 0.915 = 2.15$ kW **Verification:** $\dfrac{P_{hydraulic}}{P_{elec}} = \dfrac{7.0}{9.15} = 0.765 = 76.5\% = \eta_{overall}$, which is consistent with $\eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor} = 0.85 \times 0.90 = 0.765$. ✓ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Conservation of Energy in Hydraulics** - ✅ **Bernoulli's Equation** is the head-form of energy conservation: $h_p + h_v + h_z = \text{constant}$ for ideal flow. - ✅ **General Energy Equation** adds the head loss $h_L$ to account for friction: $h_{p1} + h_{v1} + h_{z1} = h_{p2} + h_{v2} + h_{z2} + h_L$. - ✅ For real circuits with a **pump** and **turbine/motor**, the full form is: $H_P = h_L + \Delta(h_p + h_v + h_z) + H_T$. - ✅ **Pressure head** $h_p = p / (\rho g)$ — typically the dominant form of energy in hydraulics (hundreds of metres of oil). - ✅ **Velocity head** $h_v = v^2 / (2g)$ — usually small (< 2 m) for industrial flow velocities. - ✅ **Elevation head** $h_z$ — often neglected for machine-tool circuits with short pipe runs. - ✅ **Specific weight** of standard mineral oil: $\gamma \approx 8600$–$8800$ N/m³ (corresponds to $\rho \approx 875$–$895$ kg/m³). - ✅ **Hydraulic power** delivered to fluid: $P = \Delta p \cdot Q$ (in watts) — the most tested formula in KTU. - ✅ **Head loss conversion**: $h_L$ (m) $\longleftrightarrow$ $\Delta p_{loss}$ (Pa) via $h_L = \Delta p_{loss} / (\rho g)$. - ✅ **Friction loss in pipes**: Darcy–Weisbach form $h_L = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ — for a 14-mark KTU question, you may be required to compute this if given $f$, $L$, $D$, $v$. - ✅ **Cylinder thrust**: $F = p \cdot A$, derived directly from pressure-energy conversion. - ✅ **Efficiency relationships**: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{volumetric} \times \eta_{mechanical} \times \eta_{electrical}$ (as applicable). - ✅ **Sign convention**: Pump **adds** head to the fluid (positive $H_P$); turbine/motor **extracts** head (positive $H_T$ on the loss side of the equation). - ✅ **Energy audit**: Pump power = Useful power at actuator + All frictional losses + Heat rejected to reservoir. - ✅ **Typical exam marks split**: 3-mark questions test definitions and head identifications; 14-mark questions combine derivation (5–7 marks) + numerical application (7–9 marks). - ✅ **Common student errors to avoid**: mixing units of Pa and m, forgetting $h_L$ in real flow, omitting the $\rho g$ conversion for head-based questions, and confusing pump efficiency types.

topics.title: Frictional Losses in Hydraulic Pipelines - Laminar and Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Frictional Losses in Hydraulic Pipelines - Laminar and Turbulent Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Frictional Loss (Head Loss due to Friction)** is the irreversible loss of mechanical energy (pressure head) sustained by a real hydraulic fluid as it flows through a pipeline, caused by internal viscous shear stresses and turbulent momentum exchange between adjacent fluid layers.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMRT503), the study of frictional losses is bifurcated based on the **Reynolds Number ($Re$)**, which classifies the flow regime:

- **Laminar Flow:** $Re < 2000$ — Streamlined, layered, predictable flow.
- **Transitional Flow:** $2000 \leq Re \leq 4000$ — Unstable intermediate regime.
- **Turbulent Flow:** $Re > 4000$ — Chaotic, mixing, eddying flow.

The general energy equation governing a real hydraulic system (applied between two points 1 and 2 in a pipeline) is:

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 + h_f
$$

where $h_f$ denotes the **head loss due to friction** in meters of fluid column.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Water Slide" Analogy:** Imagine pouring water down a long, smooth glass tube versus a rough, rusty iron pipe of the same length and diameter.
>
> - In the **glass tube** (analogous to **laminar flow**), water glides in neat, parallel layers. The molecules barely collide. Friction loss is small and *predictable* — it depends linearly on velocity.
> - In the **rusty iron pipe** (analogous to **turbulent flow**), the water molecules tumble, swirl, and smash into the rough walls and into each other. Energy is dissipated as heat and sound. Friction loss becomes *much larger* and depends roughly on the *square* of velocity.
>
> Just as the rusty pipe "steals" pressure from the water, every meter of industrial hydraulic line causes the working fluid to lose some of its useful pumping energy. Engineers must **calculate and compensate** for this loss when sizing pumps, motors, and actuators.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of hydraulic oil | $\rho$ | $\text{kg/m}^{3}$ | Mineral oil ≈ **860 kg/m³** |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | $\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ or **cSt** | $1\,\text{cSt} = 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ or **N·s/m²** | $\mu = \rho \nu$ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $\text{m/s}^{2}$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Pipe diameter | $D$ | $\text{m}$ | Internal diameter |
| Pipe length | $L$ | $\text{m}$ | Total effective length |
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ | — |
| Average velocity | $v$ | $\text{m/s}$ | $v = Q/A$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for laminar flow and apply the Darcy–Weisbach equation for both regimes, with appropriate friction factors ($f$ or $\lambda$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reynolds Number — The "Flow Regime Judge"

The dimensionless **Reynolds Number** compares **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** in a fluid:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}
$$

### Physical Interpretation (Step-by-Step Logic):

1. **High $Re$ (>> 4000):** Inertia dominates over viscosity → fluid particles accelerate and decelerate chaotically → **Turbulent flow**.
2. **Low $Re$ (< 2000):** Viscous damping overwhelms inertia → fluid particles move in parallel laminae → **Laminar flow**.
3. **Critical Reynolds Number for circular pipes:** $Re_{cr} \approx \mathbf{2000}$ (lower limit) and $\approx 4000$ (upper limit for transition).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $Re$ dimensionless?** Because $\rho v D$ has units of $\text{kg/(m·s)} = \text{Pa·s}$, exactly the same as $\mu$. The ratio is therefore a pure number.

---

## 2.2 Darcy–Weisbach Equation (Universal Head Loss Formula)

The **Darcy–Weisbach equation** is the *master equation* for head loss applicable to **all flow regimes** — laminar, transitional, and turbulent:

$$
h_f = \frac{4 f\, L\, v^{2}}{2 g D} = \frac{f L v^{2}}{2 g D}
$$

> There are **two common notations** in textbooks. KTU examiners accept both:
> - **Friction factor (Darcy):** $f$ (dimensionless)
> - **Friction coefficient (Fanning):** $f_{Fanning} = f/4$

The corresponding **pressure drop** is:

$$
\Delta p_f = \rho g h_f = \frac{f L \rho v^{2}}{2 D}
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Applicable Regime | Variables | Unit of $h_f$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$ | All regimes | $v, D, \rho, \mu$ | — |
| 2 | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L v^{2}}{2 g D}$ | All regimes (Darcy form) | $f, L, v, g, D$ | m |
| 3 | $\Delta p_f = \dfrac{f L \rho v^{2}}{2 D}$ | All regimes | $f, L, \rho, v, D$ | Pa |
| 4 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | **Laminar only** | $Re$ | — |
| 5 | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{\rho g D^{2}}$ | **Laminar only** (Hagen–Poiseuille) | $\mu, L, v, \rho, g, D$ | m |
| 6 | $\Delta p_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}}$ | **Laminar only** (Hagen–Poiseuille) | $\mu, L, v, D$ | Pa |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^{4} \Delta p_f}{128 \mu L}$ | **Laminar only** (Hagen–Poiseuille, volumetric) | $D, \Delta p, \mu, L$ | m³/s |
| 8 | $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ | **Turbulent** (smooth pipes, Blasius) | $Re$ | — |
| 9 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$ | **Turbulent** (Colebrook–White, rough + smooth) | $\varepsilon, D, Re$ | — |
| 10 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | All (continuity) | $Q, D$ | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Point:** In laminar flow, $h_f \propto v$ (linear). In turbulent flow, $h_f \propto v^{2}$ (quadratic). This single fact has appeared in **at least 4 KTU University Exams** (2019, 2021, 2022, 2023) as a 3-mark conceptual question.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pump Sizing:** Knowing $h_f$ allows engineers to select a pump whose developed head exceeds the system head loss, ensuring rated flow delivery.
- **Hydraulic Circuit Design:** In an industrial hydraulic press, supply lines run 10–50 m. Friction losses in return and pressure lines reduce actuator force. Engineers must oversize lines or limit velocity to **$v \leq 6\,\text{m/s}$** (suction) and **$v \leq 10\,\text{m/s}$** (pressure).
- **Energy Audits:** Pumping systems account for **~25% of industrial electricity** worldwide (US DOE estimate). Accurate $h_f$ calculation directly translates to energy cost savings.
- **Cooling & Lubrication Circuits:** Turbulent flow ($Re > 4000$) is intentionally promoted in heat exchangers to enhance convective heat transfer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Laminar Flow in a Circular Pipe)

### Setup
Consider a horizontal, straight circular pipe of internal radius $R$ and length $L$. A real viscous fluid flows in fully-developed **steady laminar** flow driven by a pressure drop $\Delta p = p_1 - p_2$.

We isolate a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$ coaxial with the pipe.

### Step 1: Force Balance on the Fluid Element

Three forces act on the element along the pipe axis:

1. **Pressure force on left face:** $p_1 \cdot \pi r^{2}$ (pushing right).
2. **Pressure force on right face:** $p_2 \cdot \pi r^{2}$ (pushing left).
3. **Viscous shear force on lateral surface:** $\tau \cdot 2 \pi r L$ (opposing motion, from Newton's law of viscosity).

For steady, fully-developed flow, there is **no acceleration** (Newton's Second Law, $\Sigma F = 0$):

$$
p_1 \pi r^{2} - p_2 \pi r^{2} - \tau (2 \pi r L) = 0
$$

### Step 2: Solve for Shear Stress

$$
\pi r^{2} (p_1 - p_2) = 2 \pi r L \tau
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{(p_1 - p_2) r}{2 L} = \frac{\Delta p \, r}{2 L}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity

For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress equals the dynamic viscosity times the **velocity gradient** (with a sign indicating direction):

$$
\tau = -\mu \frac{dv}{dr}
$$

(The negative sign indicates that velocity *decreases* as $r$ *increases* — fluid sticks to the wall.)

### Step 4: Form the Differential Equation

$$
-\mu \frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{\Delta p \, r}{2 L}
$$

$$
\frac{dv}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu L} \, r
$$

### Step 5: Integrate with Respect to $r$

$$
\int dv = -\frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu L} \int r \, dr
$$

$$
v(r) = -\frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{2} + C = -\frac{\Delta p \, r^{2}}{4 \mu L} + C
$$

### Step 6: Apply the No-Slip Boundary Condition

The fluid velocity at the pipe wall ($r = R$) must be zero (no-slip condition):

$$
v(R) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; 0 = -\frac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L} + C
$$

$$
C = \frac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L}
$$

### Step 7: Final Velocity Profile (Parabolic)

$$
v(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4 \mu L}\left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)
$$

> This is the famous **parabolic velocity profile** unique to laminar flow. Maximum velocity occurs at the centerline ($r = 0$):

$$
v_{max} = \frac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L}
$$

### Step 8: Average Velocity and Volumetric Flow Rate

The average velocity is half the maximum (a hallmark of parabolic flow):

$$
v_{avg} = \frac{v_{max}}{2} = \frac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{8 \mu L}
$$

Substituting $R = D/2$ (i.e., $R^{2} = D^{2}/4$):

$$
v_{avg} = \frac{\Delta p \, D^{2}}{32 \mu L}
$$

Volumetric flow rate $Q = v_{avg} \cdot A = v_{avg} \cdot \pi D^{2}/4$:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta p \, D^{2}}{32 \mu L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi D^{4} \Delta p}{128 \mu L}\,}
$$

### Step 9: Express the Friction Factor

Rearranging $v_{avg} = \dfrac{\Delta p \, D^{2}}{32 \mu L}$ into Darcy–Weisbach form $h_f = \dfrac{f L v^{2}}{2 g D}$:

$$
\Delta p = \frac{32 \mu L v_{avg}}{D^{2}} = \frac{f L \rho v_{avg}^{2}}{2 D}
$$

Solving for $f$:

$$
f = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho v_{avg} D} = \frac{64}{Re}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{laminar} = \frac{64}{Re}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Friction Factor for Turbulent Flow (Blasius Equation, Smooth Pipes)

For turbulent flow in **smooth pipes** (commercial steel with negligible roughness), the friction factor cannot be derived analytically. It is obtained empirically from dimensional analysis and experimental data. **Paul Richard Heinrich Blasius** (1913) proposed:

$$
f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}
$$

**Validity range:** $4000 < Re < 10^{5}$ (smooth pipes).

### Quick Justification (Dimensional Analysis Sketch)

By Buckingham's $\Pi$-theorem, for a smooth pipe:

$$
f = \phi(Re)
$$

Experiments revealed a power-law dependence $f \propto Re^{-n}$. Curve-fitting gave $n = 0.25$ and the constant $0.316$. This formula has been a KTU favourite for **Part B 7-mark derivations** in past years.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Laminar vs Turbulent Flow

| Property | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | $Re < 2000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| Velocity profile | Parabolic (analytically derived) | Flat/plug-like (logarithmic) |
| Friction factor $f$ | $f = 64 / Re$ | $f = 0.316 / Re^{0.25}$ (Blasius, smooth) |
| Head loss dependence | $h_f \propto v$ | $h_f \propto v^{2}$ |
| Pressure drop dependence | $\Delta p \propto v$ | $\Delta p \propto v^{2}$ |
| Kinematic character | Ordered, predictable | Chaotic, eddying |
| Energy dissipation | Low (viscous only) | High (viscous + turbulent eddies) |
| Mixing | Negligible | Vigorous |
| Heat transfer | Poor | Excellent |
| Typical $f$ value at $Re=10^{3}$ | 0.064 | ≈ 0.0316 (smooth) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Frictional Loss Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete decision flow** that an engineer (or a KTU student solving a numerical) follows when computing frictional losses in a hydraulic pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given Pipe Geometry and Fluid Properties"] --> B["Compute Average Velocity v = 4Q over piD squared"]
    B --> C["Compute Kinematic Viscosity nu and Dynamic Viscosity mu"]
    C --> D["Compute Reynolds Number Re = rho v D over mu"]
    D --> E{"Compare Re with Critical Value"}
    E -->|"Re less than 2000"| F["LAMINAR REGIME BRANCH"]
    E -->|"Re between 2000 and 4000"| G["TRANSITIONAL - Use Moody Chart"]
    E -->|"Re greater than 4000"| H["TURBULENT REGIME BRANCH"]
    F --> F1["Compute f = 64 over Re"]
    F1 --> F2["Apply h_f = f L v squared over 2 g D"]
    F2 --> F3["Or use Hagen Poiseuille h_f = 32 mu L v over rho g D squared"]
    F3 --> Z["Output: Head Loss h_f in metres and Pressure Drop delta p in Pa"]
    H --> H1{"Pipe Roughness Known?"}
    H1 -->|"Yes, smooth pipe"| H2["Use Blasius: f = 0.316 over Re to the 0.25"]
    H1 -->|"Yes, rough pipe"| H3["Use Colebrook White or Moody Chart"]
    H1 -->|"No data available"| H4["Assume fully rough turbulent: f from Moody at high Re"]
    H2 --> H5["Apply Darcy Weisbach: h_f = f L v squared over 2 g D"]
    H3 --> H5
    H4 --> H5
    H5 --> Z
    G --> G1["Interpolate f from Moody Chart or empirical curves"]
    G1 --> H5
    Z --> END["End: Report h_f to Pump Selection Block"]
```

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Parabolic vs Flat Velocity Profiles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAM["LAMINAR FLOW - Re less than 2000"]
        L1["Pipe Wall - v = 0"] --> L2["Velocity = v_max at centerline"]
        L2 --> L3["Parabolic Profile v r = delta p R squared minus r squared over 4 mu L"]
    end
    subgraph TURB["TURBULENT FLOW - Re greater than 4000"]
        T1["Pipe Wall - v = 0"] --> T2["Near-wall thin viscous sublayer"]
        T2 --> T3["Logarithmic core region"]
        T3 --> T4["Nearly uniform v across most of cross section"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Energy Grade Line and Hydraulic Grade Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Pump Source - Total Energy H_pump"] --> P1["Point 1: Inlet of Pipe"]
    P1 -->|"EGL drops by h_f/2"| P2["Point 2: Mid Pipe"]
    P2 -->|"EGL drops by h_f/2"| P3["Point 3: Pipe Exit"]
    P3 --> END["Discharge Reservoir"]
    P1 -.->|"HGL = EGL minus v squared over 2g"| HGL1["HGL Point 1"]
    P2 -.->|"HGL parallel drop"| HGL2["HGL Point 2"]
    P3 -.->|"HGL at exit"| HGL3["HGL Point 3"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **Energy Grade Line (EGL)** = Total head $\left( \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} + z \right)$.
> - The **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL)** = EGL minus the velocity head.
> - In a uniform horizontal pipe with constant $D$, both lines drop **linearly** with distance, with the vertical drop equal to $h_f$ accumulated over that segment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Exam Paper, Set B]

**Differentiate between laminar and turbulent flow. Mention the critical Reynolds number for flow in a circular pipe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Fluid particles move in parallel layers without intermixing | Fluid particles move chaotically with eddies and mixing |
| Reynolds Number | $Re < 2000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| Velocity profile | Parabolic | Flat (logarithmic core) |
| Head loss relation | $h_f \propto v$ | $h_f \propto v^{2}$ |

**Critical Reynolds Number** for a circular pipe: $Re_{cr} = \mathbf{2000}$ (lower limit, below which flow is always laminar; above 4000 the flow is fully turbulent; between is transitional).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**State the Darcy–Weisbach equation. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Darcy–Weisbach equation for head loss due to friction in a pipe is:

$$
h_f = \frac{4 f L v^{2}}{2 g D} = \frac{f L v^{2}}{2 g D}
$$

where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor (dimensionless), $L$ is pipe length, $D$ is pipe diameter, $v$ is average fluid velocity, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration.

**Physical Significance:** It is a *universal* head-loss equation valid for **all flow regimes** — laminar, transitional, and turbulent — provided the appropriate friction factor $f$ is used. It quantifies the irreversible energy loss as fluid flows through a real pipe.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Supplementary Exam Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for steady, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular pipe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Oil of density $880\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$ and dynamic viscosity $0.12\,\text{Pa·s}$ flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter $50\,\text{mm}$ and length $100\,\text{m}$ at a rate of $2.5\,\text{L/s}$. Calculate:
   (i) The average velocity and Reynolds number.
   (ii) The head loss due to friction.
   (iii) The power required to overcome the frictional resistance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Derivation of Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (7 marks)

> Refer to the **complete derivation in SECTION 3.1** above. The KTU-expected answer should include the following key steps for full marks:
>
> 1. Free body diagram of cylindrical fluid element (1 mark)
> 2. Force balance: $p_1 \pi r^2 - p_2 \pi r^2 - \tau(2\pi r L) = 0$ (1 mark)
> 3. Solving for $\tau = \dfrac{\Delta p \, r}{2L}$ (1 mark)
> 4. Applying Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{dv}{dr}$ (1 mark)
> 5. Integration yielding $v(r) = \dfrac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$ (1 mark)
> 6. No-slip boundary condition, average velocity $v_{avg} = \dfrac{\Delta p R^2}{8 \mu L}$ (1 mark)
> 7. Final Hagen–Poiseuille equation: $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^4 \Delta p}{128 \mu L}$ (1 mark)

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given Data:**

- Density: $\rho = 880\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 0.12\,\text{Pa·s}$
- Pipe diameter: $D = 50\,\text{mm} = 0.05\,\text{m}$
- Pipe length: $L = 100\,\text{m}$
- Volumetric flow rate: $Q = 2.5\,\text{L/s} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$

#### (i) Average Velocity and Reynolds Number

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.273\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds Number:**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{880 \times 1.273 \times 0.05}{0.12}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{56.012}{0.12} = 466.77 \approx 467
$$

Since $Re < 2000$, the flow is **LAMINAR**. ✓ (Hagen–Poiseuille is valid.)

---

#### (ii) Head Loss due to Friction

**Method 1 — Using Darcy–Weisbach with $f = 64/Re$:**

$$
f = \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{466.77} = 0.1371
$$

$$
h_f = \frac{f L v^{2}}{2 g D} = \frac{0.1371 \times 100 \times (1.273)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.05}
$$

$$
h_f = \frac{0.1371 \times 100 \times 1.6205}{0.981} = \frac{22.221}{0.981} = 22.65\,\text{m of oil}
$$

**Method 2 — Direct Hagen–Poiseuille (Verification):**

$$
h_f = \frac{32 \mu L v}{\rho g D^{2}} = \frac{32 \times 0.12 \times 100 \times 1.273}{880 \times 9.81 \times (0.05)^{2}}
$$

$$
h_f = \frac{488.83}{21.5805} = 22.65\,\text{m of oil} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Result:** $h_f \approx 22.65\,\text{m of oil}$

---

#### (iii) Power Required to Overcome Friction

The mechanical power lost to friction is:

$$
P = \Delta p_f \cdot Q = \rho g h_f \cdot Q
$$

$$
P = 880 \times 9.81 \times 22.65 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
P = 195{,}393.6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 488.48\,\text{W}
$$

**Result:** $P \approx 488.5\,\text{W} \approx 0.49\,\text{kW}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Exam]**

**(a)** Explain the significance of the Reynolds number. With a neat sketch, describe the velocity profiles in laminar and turbulent flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Water at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($\rho = 998\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$) flows through a smooth commercial steel pipe of diameter $100\,\text{mm}$ and length $200\,\text{m}$ with an average velocity of $3\,\text{m/s}$. Determine:
   (i) The Reynolds number and the flow regime.
   (ii) The Darcy friction factor using the Blasius equation.
   (iii) The head loss in metres of water. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Significance of Reynolds Number and Velocity Profiles (7 marks)

**Significance of Reynolds Number:**

The Reynolds number $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ is a **dimensionless parameter** that quantifies the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** in a flowing fluid. It serves as the **sole criterion** for classifying flow regime:

- $Re < 2000$: Laminar
- $2000 \leq Re \leq 4000$: Transitional
- $Re > 4000$: Turbulent

**Physical Meaning:**

- At **low $Re$**, viscous forces dominate. They damp out any disturbances, causing the fluid to flow in smooth, parallel streamlines.
- At **high $Re$**, inertial forces dominate. Small disturbances amplify, leading to chaotic eddies, mixing, and a flat velocity profile.

**Velocity Profiles (Sketches):**

- **Laminar:** Parabolic profile, $v(r) = \dfrac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$, $v_{avg} = v_{max}/2$
- **Turbulent:** Nearly flat in the core, with a thin viscous sublayer near the wall. A thin boundary layer is dominated by viscous shear; the bulk is dominated by turbulent eddies.

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given Data:**

- $\rho = 998\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$
- $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$
- $D = 0.1\,\text{m}$, $L = 200\,\text{m}$, $v = 3\,\text{m/s}$

#### (i) Reynolds Number

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 3 \times 0.1}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{299.4}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 298{,}802
$$

Since $Re = 298{,}802 > 4000$, the flow is **TURBULENT**. ✓

#### (ii) Blasius Friction Factor

For a smooth pipe with $Re < 10^{5}$? No, $Re = 2.99 \times 10^{5}$, which is **outside** Blasius's strict range ($4000 < Re < 10^{5}$). However, KTU problems frequently use Blasius in this extended range as a first approximation.

$$
f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{(298{,}802)^{0.25}}
$$

Computing $(298{,}802)^{0.25}$:

$$
\sqrt{298{,}802} = 546.6, \quad \sqrt{546.6} = 23.38
$$

$$
f = \frac{0.316}{23.38} = 0.01352
$$

#### (iii) Head Loss

$$
h_f = \frac{f L v^{2}}{2 g D} = \frac{0.01352 \times 200 \times (3)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1}
$$

$$
h_f = \frac{0.01352 \times 200 \times 9}{1.962} = \frac{24.336}{1.962} = 12.40\,\text{m of water}
$$

**Result:** $h_f \approx 12.4\,\text{m}$ of water column.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks Every Year:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert $\text{mm}$ to $\text{m}$ in pipe diameter. Using $D = 50$ instead of $0.05$ is a guaranteed **-2 marks** deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the Density in Hagen–Poiseuille:** The formula $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{\rho g D^2}$ includes $\rho$. Many students drop it and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing $f$ and $f_{Fanning}$:** The coefficient $4$ in the numerator of Darcy's equation is often misplaced. If a question asks for $h_f$ and the student uses $f/4$ instead of $f$, the head loss will be off by a factor of 4.
> 4. **Applying Hagen–Poiseuille to Turbulent Flow:** Always verify $Re < 2000$ before using $f = 64/Re$ or $Q = \pi D^4 \Delta p / (128 \mu L)$. The derivation assumes laminar flow.
> 5. **Forgetting to State the Flow Regime:** KTU examiners allocate 1 mark for the explicit statement *"Since $Re < 2000$, the flow is laminar."* Skipping it loses a mark.
> 6. **Power Calculation Units:** Power must be in **Watts (W)**. Expressing it in $hp$ without conversion factor in the formula will be marked wrong.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Reynolds Number Formula:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$
- [x] **Critical Values:** $Re_{cr} = 2000$ (laminar ceiling), $4000$ (turbulent floor)
- [x] **Darcy–Weisbach:** $h_f = \dfrac{f L v^{2}}{2 g D}$ (universal)
- [x] **Laminar Friction Factor:** $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$
- [x] **Hagen–Poiseuille Head Loss:** $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{\rho g D^{2}}$
- [x] **Hagen–Poiseuille Flow Rate:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^{4} \Delta p}{128 \mu L}$
- [x] **Blasius Friction Factor (turbulent, smooth):** $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ (valid $4000 < Re < 10^{5}$)
- [x] **Laminar $h_f \propto v$; Turbulent $h_f \propto v^{2}$** — *Most-tested 3-mark concept*
- [x] **Laminar velocity profile:** Parabolic; **Turbulent:** Flat with thin viscous sublayer
- [x] **Power lost to friction:** $P = \rho g h_f Q = \Delta p_f \cdot Q$ (in **Watts**)
- [x] **Always state flow regime** explicitly before applying any formula — *valuation tip*
- [x] **Industrial rule of thumb:** Keep oil velocity in pressure lines below **6 m/s** to limit frictional heating
- [x] **Moody Chart** is the universal tool for turbulent $f$ when both $Re$ and relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$ are known
- [x] **Colebrook–White equation** is the implicit (iterative) formula for turbulent friction factor in rough pipes

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Power Symbols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Power Symbols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fluid Power Symbols** are standardized graphical representations governed by the **ISO 1219** standard (adopted universally in industrial hydraulics and pneumatics engineering) that describe the function, operation, and interconnection of components within a hydraulic or pneumatic circuit. These symbols form the *visual language* through which engineers communicate fluid power systems across the globe, transcending linguistic barriers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Every KTU examination question on circuit drawing, troubleshooting, or component identification is built upon the assumption that the student has mastered **ISO 1219** symbolic conventions. Marks are awarded for correct symbol structure, not for pictorial drawings.

A fluid power symbol is composed of three primary visual building blocks:
1. **Lines** (continuous, dashed, dotted) — represent flow paths / pipes.
2. **Arrows** — indicate direction of flow or movement of a moving part.
3. **Envelopes / Boxes** — represent components (one box per operating position).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Engineering Rosetta Stone"

Imagine you are a global mechanical engineer and you receive a circuit diagram from a colleague in Germany. You do not speak German. Yet, you can read the diagram perfectly. *How?* Because the symbols are **universal** — like musical notes on a sheet of paper. A hydraulic circuit is a "song," and each component symbol is a "note." Just as a musician reads notes, an engineer reads fluid power symbols to understand the harmony of pressure, flow, and force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The *external shape* of a symbol is **NOT** meant to look like the physical component. ISO 1219 symbols describe **function**, not form. For example, a pressure relief valve is drawn as a square with a spring arrow — not as a picture of the actual valve body.

## 1.3 Two Standards Compared

| Standard | Scope | Adoption |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 1219** | Comprehensive fluid power symbols | Global, KTU-recommended |
| **ANSI / ASME Y32.10** | US graphical symbols | United States legacy |
| **DIN 24300** | German standard | Europe (now harmonized with ISO 1219) |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 examinations, students should use **ISO 1219** conventions. Minor stylistic differences between ISO and ANSI (such as different arrowheads on flow arrows) are accepted in the university answer scripts, but ISO is preferred.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fluid Power Symbols (Logical Breakdown)

Fluid power symbols are systematically classified into **six functional families**. This classification is the *skeleton* of every KTU Module 1 question.

### 2.1.1 Family 1 — Energy Supply (Sources)
- **Hydraulic Pump** — converts mechanical energy → hydraulic energy.
- **Pneumatic Compressor** — converts mechanical energy → pneumatic (gas) energy.
- Symbol convention: a circle (pump) with an arrow showing input shaft, and a solid black triangle or filled triangle indicating flow direction.

### 2.1.2 Family 2 — Energy Transformation (Actuators)
- **Linear Actuator (Hydraulic / Pneumatic Cylinder)** — single-acting (one port) and double-acting (two ports).
- **Rotary Actuator (Hydraulic / Pneumatic Motor)** — limited rotation and continuous rotation types.

### 2.1.3 Family 4 — Energy Control (Valves)
The most important family for KTU exams. Valves control:
- **Direction** → Directional Control Valves (DCV)
- **Pressure** → Pressure Control Valves (PCV)
- **Flow** → Flow Control Valves (FCV)

### 2.1.4 Family 5 — Conditioners (FRL Unit)
- **Filter** (F) — removes contaminants.
- **Regulator** (R) — maintains constant downstream pressure.
- **Lubricator** (L) — introduces oil mist into the air stream (pneumatic only).

### 2.1.5 Family 6 — Accessories
- Reservoir / Tank, Accumulator, Pressure Gauge, Heat Exchanger, Quick Coupling.

### 2.1.6 Family 7 — Connections
- Working line (solid), Pilot / control line (dashed), Drain / leak line (dotted).

## 2.2 The Envelope Method — Reading Valves (CRITICAL)

Every control valve symbol is built on a strict counting principle:

- **Number of Squares (Envelopes)** = **Number of operating positions** of the valve.
- **Number of Ports** (arrows or stub lines) = **Number of flow paths** available.
- **Arrows inside the box** = flow direction in that position.
- **Blocked ports** (T-bars, dead-end) = ports sealed in that position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Rule:** A "4/3 valve" means **4 ports, 3 positions**. A "3/2 valve" means **3 ports, 2 positions**. Students frequently confuse these — write them in the format **"ports/positions"** always.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Symbol Group | Key Visual Convention | Meaning | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous line | Solid unbroken line | Main working (power) line | Always draw thicker than pilot lines |
| Dashed line | - - - - - - - | Pilot / control line | Smaller diameter; used in remote / servo circuits |
| Dotted line | · · · · · · | Leak / drain / vent line | Connects to tank without pressure |
| Filled triangle | ▶ inside circle | Pump / flow direction | Points AWAY from source |
| Hollow triangle | △ | Direction of flow in line | Use in non-pump lines |
| Spring | Zig-zag or straight line with arrow | Spring-returned position | Located at one end of the envelope |
| Solenoid | Slanted rectangle at one end | Solenoid-actuated position | Electrical control |
| Pilot / Manual lever | Plain rectangle / lever symbol | Manual or pilot actuation | Direct human / pressure control |
| Square block with T | ┴ or ⊥ inside box | Port closed (blocked) | Always in OFF / centre position |
| Arrow inside square | → inside box | Flow passes from one port to another | Direction matters for DCV |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Drawing two arrows in the same direction inside ONE envelope square. This is **physically impossible** — a single position permits only one flow configuration. Each envelope (box) = one stable position = one set of flow paths.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Fluid power symbols are the **blueprint language** used in:
- Machine tool design (CNC, presses, injection moulding).
- Automobile assembly lines (robotic arms, clamping).
- Aircraft control systems (hydraulic brakes, landing gear).
- Mobile hydraulics (excavators, tractors, lift trucks).
- Pneumatic automation (food packaging, pharmaceutical bottling).

> A single ISO 1219 diagram replaces **thousands of words** of textual description. KTU tests the student's ability to *read* and *draw* this language fluently.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analysis: Reading & Constructing Fluid Power Symbols

## 3.1 The Algorithm for Reading a DCV (Directional Control Valve) Symbol

**Worked Example: Identify the symbol for a 4/3 closed-centre, spring-centred, solenoid-pilot-operated DCV.**

We decompose the problem into strict analytical steps.

### Step 1 — Count the Envelopes
The symbol shows **three squares placed side by side**. This means **3 positions**.

### Step 2 — Count the Ports
Examine the perimeter of the joined envelopes. We find:
- One port on the left
- One port on the right
- Two ports on the bottom (or top)
- **Total = 4 ports** → confirms "4/3".

### Step 3 — Identify Actuation Methods
- **Left end** of the envelope stack → Solenoid symbol (slanted rectangle) → solenoid shifts valve to the left position.
- **Right end** → Solenoid symbol → shifts valve to the right position.
- **Middle end** (both sides) → Spring symbols on both sides → when solenoids are de-energised, springs return the valve to the centre position.

### Step 4 — Decode Centre Position
Inside the middle envelope:
- All four ports show **T-bars** (┴ symbols), meaning **all ports are blocked**.
- This configuration is called **"Closed Centre"**.

### Step 5 — Decode Side Positions
- **Left envelope:** Arrow from Pressure (P) port → Cylinder-A (A) port. Arrow from Cylinder-B (B) port → Tank (T) port. The flow path is P→A and B→T.
- **Right envelope:** Arrow from P → B and A → T (reverse of left).

### Step 6 — Compile the Final Description

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Reading:**
> 4/3 Directional Control Valve, Closed Centre, Spring-Centred, Double-Solenoid Actuation, with ports labelled P, T, A, B. Used in hydraulic circuits where the pump must be unloaded (or fluid locked) when the actuator is in mid-position.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common Valve Symbols at a Glance

| Valve Type | Ports/Positions | Centre Type | Actuation | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3/2 DCV | 3 / 2 | — | Spring-return, manual, solenoid | Single-acting cylinder control |
| 4/2 DCV | 4 / 2 | — | Solenoid / pilot | Double-acting cylinder, simple circuits |
| 4/3 DCV (open centre) | 4 / 3 | Open (P→T) | Solenoid, spring-centre | Mobile hydraulics, tractor systems |
| 4/3 DCV (closed centre) | 4 / 3 | Closed (all blocked) | Solenoid, spring-centre | Industrial presses, machine tools |
| 4/3 DCV (tandem centre) | 4 / 3 | P blocked, A,B→T | Solenoid, spring-centre | Energy-saving circuits |
| 4/3 DCV (float centre) | 4 / 3 | P→T, A,B blocked | Solenoid, spring-centre | Holding load, hydraulic lock |
| Pressure Relief Valve | 2 / 2 | — | Spring (adjustable) | System overpressure protection |
| Pressure Reducing Valve | 3 / 2 | — | Spring / pilot | Branch circuit pressure control |
| Flow Control Valve | 2 / 2 | — | Manual / needle | Cylinder speed control |
| Check Valve | 2 / 2 | — | Free flow / spring-blocked | One-way flow permit |

## 3.3 Cylinder and Motor Symbol Logic

### 3.3.1 Single-Acting Cylinder
- One port (cap end or rod end).
- Spring on the opposite side retracts the piston.
- Symbol: rectangle with one triangular piston, one side has a stub line (port), the other side has a spring (zig-zag).

### 3.3.2 Double-Acting Cylinder
- Two ports (cap end AND rod end).
- No spring — fluid pressure drives piston in **both** directions.
- Symbol: rectangle with one piston (two triangles) and stub lines on **both** ends.

### 3.3.3 Hydraulic / Pneumatic Motor
- One input port and one output shaft symbol.
- Symbol: circle with shaft, two triangles meeting at the centre (rotor indication), or a circle with a slanted arrow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinguishing Pneumatic from Hydraulic on the same symbol:** Many symbols are identical. Context — such as the type of pump, presence of an FRL unit, or the type of working fluid indicator — disambiguates. KTU examiners often place a **single-letter label** "H" or "P" (or a "drop" triangle for liquid) near the components to specify the medium.

## 3.4 Pump Symbol Logic

- A **fixed-displacement pump** → circle with a filled triangle showing unidirectional flow, single arrow on the shaft.
- A **variable-displacement pump** → circle with a filled triangle, **diagonal arrow through the circle** indicating adjustable swash-plate.
- **Compressor** (pneumatic) → similar circle, but with the convention "A" inside (for air) or a fan-blade-style symbol.
- **Unidirectional** (non-reversible) → single arrow on shaft.
- **Bidirectional** → double-headed arrow on shaft.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Classification Hierarchy of Fluid Power Symbols

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Fluid Power Symbols ISO 1219"] --> B["Energy Supply"]
    A --> C["Energy Transformation"]
    A --> D["Energy Control Valves"]
    A --> E["Conditioning FRL"]
    A --> F["Accessories"]
    A --> G["Connection Lines"]

    B --> B1["Hydraulic Pump Fixed"]
    B --> B2["Hydraulic Pump Variable"]
    B --> B3["Pneumatic Compressor"]

    C --> C1["Single Acting Cylinder"]
    C --> C2["Double Acting Cylinder"]
    C --> C3["Hydraulic Motor"]
    C --> C4["Pneumatic Motor"]

    D --> D1["Directional Control DCV"]
    D --> D2["Pressure Control PCV"]
    D --> D3["Flow Control FCV"]
    D --> D4["Check Valve"]

    D1 --> D1a["3 by 2 DCV"]
    D1 --> D1b["4 by 2 DCV"]
    D1 --> D1c["4 by 3 DCV Open"]
    D1 --> D1d["4 by 3 DCV Closed"]
    D1 --> D1e["4 by 3 DCV Tandem"]
    D1 --> D1f["4 by 3 DCV Float"]

    D2 --> D2a["Pressure Relief Valve"]
    D2 --> D2b["Pressure Reducing Valve"]
    D2 --> D2c["Sequence Valve"]

    D3 --> D3a["Needle FCV"]
    D3 --> D3b["Throttle FCV"]

    E --> E1["Filter"]
    E --> E2["Regulator"]
    E --> E3["Lubricator"]

    F --> F1["Reservoir Tank"]
    F --> F2["Accumulator"]
    F --> F3["Pressure Gauge"]
    F --> F4["Heat Exchanger"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: A Typical Hydraulic Circuit Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SUP["Energy Supply Block"]
        PUMP["Fixed Pump Driven by Motor"]
    end

    subgraph FILT["Conditioning Block"]
        FILT1["Suction Strainer and Return Filter"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["Control Block"]
        RELIEF["Pressure Relief Valve Set at 140 bar"]
        DCV["Four by Three DCV Closed Centre Spring Centred"]
        FCV["Flow Control Valve for Speed"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["Actuator Block"]
        CYL["Double Acting Hydraulic Cylinder"]
    end

    subgraph ACC["Accessory Block"]
        TANK["Reservoir Tank 200 litres"]
        ACC1["Accumulator 10 litre Nitrogen"]
        GAUGE["Pressure Gauge 0 to 250 bar"]
    end

    PUMP --> FILT1
    FILT1 --> DCV
    RELIEF --- PUMP
    DCV --> FCV
    FCV --> CYL
    CYL --> TANK
    PUMP --- TANK
    GAUGE --- CYL
    ACC1 --- DCV
```

## 4.3 Symbolic Reading Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1["Look at the component symbol"] --> Q2["Is it enclosed in one or more squares?"]
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q3["Count the squares = number of positions"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q4["It is a non-valve component"]
    Q3 --> Q5["Count the ports on the envelope perimeter"]
    Q5 --> Q6["Format: ports by positions e.g. 4 by 3"]
    Q6 --> Q7["Identify end symbols: spring, solenoid, pilot, manual"]
    Q7 --> Q8["Decode inside each box: arrows flow, T-bars blocked"]
    Q8 --> Q9["Determine centre configuration: open closed tandem float"]
    Q9 --> Q10["Write final symbolic description"]
```

## 4.4 The Universal Connection-Line Convention

```mermaid
graph TD
    L["Connection Line Types"] --> L1["Continuous Solid Line: Main Working Line"]
    L --> L2["Long Dashed Line: Pilot or Control Line"]
    L --> L3["Short Dotted Line: Leak Drain Vent Line to Tank"]
    L --> L4["Line with slash marks: Flexible Hose"]
    L --> L5["Line with two parallel strokes: Quick Disconnect Coupling"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define fluid power symbols. State the standard used for drawing these symbols.**

**Model Answer:**

Fluid power symbols are the standardized graphical notations used to represent the components and functions of hydraulic and pneumatic circuits. They are governed by the **ISO 1219** standard, which provides internationally accepted conventions for symbols depicting pumps, valves, actuators, accessories, and connecting lines.

Unlike pictorial drawings, fluid power symbols describe the **function** of a component rather than its physical shape, enabling a single diagram to be understood by engineers globally.

> **Key words to score full marks:** ISO 1219, graphical notation, function not form, universality.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between an open-centre and closed-centre 4/3 directional control valve with the help of their symbolic representation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Centre 4/3 DCV | Closed-Centre 4/3 DCV |
|---|---|---|
| Middle envelope | Pump port (P) connected to Tank port (T) by an arrow | All four ports blocked (T-bars on P, T, A, B) |
| Pump unloading | Yes, fluid returns freely to tank at zero pressure | No, pump remains pressurised; system must use a relief valve |
| Power consumption | Low (no throttling loss) | Higher; relief valve bleeds excess flow |
| Application | Mobile hydraulics, tractors | Industrial presses, machine tools |
| Symbol mnemonic | "P→T" in centre box | "All ports crossed-out" in centre box |

**Symbol sketch (text representation):**

```
Open Centre:                Closed Centre:

|←P       A→|               |←P   T T   A→|
|  P→T     |                |  T  T T T  |
|←T       B→|               |←T   T T   B→|
```

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 1 carries a long-answer question of 14 marks with internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024]

**(a)** With neat ISO 1219 symbols, explain the working of a **4/3 closed-centre, spring-centred, double-solenoid-operated directional control valve** used to control a double-acting hydraulic cylinder. Describe each of its three positions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw the ISO 1219 symbols for any **seven (7) different fluid power components** of your choice, and state one engineering application for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the symbol structure (2 marks)**

The valve is represented by **three joined square envelopes** placed side by side, with **four ports** (P, T, A, B) on the outer perimeter. Hence the designation **"4/3"** means **4 ports / 3 positions**.

**Step 2 — Identify the actuation methods (1 mark)**

- **Right-end symbol:** Solenoid (slanted rectangle) — shifts the valve spool to the **right** position when energised.
- **Left-end symbol:** Solenoid (slanted rectangle) — shifts the spool to the **left** position when energised.
- **Middle:** Two opposing **spring symbols** (zig-zag with arrow) — when both solenoids are de-energised, the springs centre the spool.

**Step 3 — Decode the three positions (4 marks)**

| Position | Spool State | Flow Path | Cylinder Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Left** | Solenoid A energised | P → A ; B → T | Piston extends (rod out) |
| **Right** | Solenoid B energised | P → B ; A → T | Piston retracts (rod in) |
| **Centre** | Both solenoids off; springs centre | All four ports blocked (T-bars) | Piston locked in place (hydraulic lock) |

> **Engineering Application:** Used in **hydraulic presses and CNC machine tools** where a held / locked mid-position is required for safety or machining accuracy.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

| # | Component | ISO 1219 Symbol (text representation) | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fixed-Displacement Pump** | Filled triangle inside a circle, single-shaft arrow | Constant-flow hydraulic power pack |
| 2 | **Variable-Displacement Pump** | Filled triangle inside a circle, diagonal arrow across | Energy-saving systems, servo-hydraulics |
| 3 | **Single-Acting Cylinder** | Rectangle with one triangular piston, spring on the rod side | Clamping, stamping, lifting low-load |
| 4 | **Double-Acting Cylinder** | Rectangle with double triangular piston, two port stubs | Press work, injection moulding, lifting |
| 5 | **Pressure Relief Valve** | Square envelope, spring arrow from top, line into and out of bottom | Set to 110 % of working pressure; overpressure safety |
| 6 | **Pressure Reducing Valve** | Square envelope with spring and pilot arrow, three ports | Branch-circuit pressure control |
| 7 | **Flow Control Valve** | Symbol: a bow-tie or restricted orifice inside a line | Cylinder extension / retraction speed control |
| 8 | **Filter** | Square with cross-hatching and a flow line | Suction or return-line contamination removal |
| 9 | **Accumulator** | Circle with a horizontal divider (gas / fluid chambers) | Emergency power, pulsation damping |
| 10 | **Check Valve** | Two triangles meeting tip-to-tip with a sphere / poppet | One-way flow in return line |

*(Any seven correct symbols with applications score full marks.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (a):**
> A common 1–2 mark deduction occurs when students **omit the labels P, T, A, B on the ports** of the 4/3 DCV symbol. The examiner cannot confirm the centre type (closed / open / tandem / float) without seeing the actual port markings. Always label the **pressure (P), tank (T), and two work ports (A and B)** explicitly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> Students often **forget to draw a flow-direction arrow** on the pump symbol, or write the *name* of the component but **not its symbol**. In a 7-mark question on symbols, the **visual symbol is worth 80 % of the marks**, the application only 20 %.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of neat ISO 1219 symbols, list and explain any **four types of directional control valve centre configurations** used in 4/3 DCVs. State one industrial application of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw the symbolic representation of an **FRL unit** (Filter–Regulator–Lubricator) as used in a pneumatic circuit. Explain the function of each section. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

| # | Centre Type | Centre Block Configuration | Function / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Open Centre** | P → T (arrow inside centre box) | Pump unloads freely to tank at zero pressure. *Application: Tractor hydraulics.* |
| 2 | **Closed Centre** | All four ports blocked (T-bars) | Pump remains pressurised; cylinder is hydraulically locked. *Application: CNC vertical machining centres.* |
| 3 | **Tandem Centre** | P blocked; A and B connected to T | Pump unloads partially; A/B ports vented. *Application: Energy-saving industrial presses.* |
| 4 | **Float Centre** | P → T ; A and B blocked | All ports effectively blocked except P-T path. *Application: Cylinder held in mid-position, load locking.* |

> **Marking note:** 4 × 1.5 marks for symbol + 0.5 mark for application = 7 marks.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**FRL Unit Symbol and Function:**

```
[AIR IN] → [F] → [R] → [L] → [AIR OUT TO CIRCUIT]
```

| Section | Symbol (text) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **F — Filter** | Square with cross-hatched element and a line through it | Removes dust, moisture, oil aerosols from incoming compressed air. Typical filtration: 5–40 micron. |
| **R — Regulator (Pressure Reducing Valve)** | Square with a spring (top) and a pilot arrow | Reduces and stabilises downstream pressure to a set value, regardless of upstream fluctuations. Includes a pressure gauge. |
| **L — Lubricator** | Square with a drop falling into the air line | Introduces a fine oil mist into the air stream to lubricate downstream pneumatic components (cylinders, valves, motors). |

**Why FRL is used:**
- Protects the **Filter** the pneumatic system from contamination.
- Provides the **Regulator** stable working pressure for repeatable machine operation.
- The **Lubricator** extends service life of moving seals and spools.

**Typical application:** Pneumatic automation in **food packaging machines, pharmaceutical bottling lines, and assembly workstations**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> A frequently repeated mistake is to draw **only the FRL block as a single box** without showing the **flow direction** arrows and the **branch for the pressure gauge**. The pressure gauge on the regulator is a mandatory sub-symbol in ISO 1219 and is worth **0.5–1 mark** by itself.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fluid Power Symbols**

- ✔ The governing standard is **ISO 1219** (always cite this name in KTU answers).
- ✔ Symbols describe **function**, not physical form.
- ✔ **One envelope (square) = one operating position** of a valve.
- ✔ **Number of ports / Number of positions** is the standard valve notation (e.g., 4/3, 3/2).
- ✔ **Continuous solid lines** = main working lines; **dashed lines** = pilot / control; **dotted lines** = drain / vent.
- ✔ **Filled triangle inside a circle** = pump (or compressor).
- ✔ **Solenoid** = slanted rectangle at envelope end; **spring** = zig-zag or straight line with arrow.
- ✔ **Centre configurations** of 4/3 DCV: Open (P→T), Closed (all blocked), Tandem (P-blocked, A,B→T), Float (P→T, A,B blocked).
- ✔ **Single-acting cylinder** has one port + spring; **double-acting cylinder** has two ports and no spring.
- ✔ **Pneumatic vs Hydraulic** is disambiguated by context, not the symbol — the standard is the same.
- ✔ **FRL = Filter + Regulator + Lubricator**, in that order, in a pneumatic supply line.
- ✔ **Pressure Relief Valve** is set to **110–125 %** of working pressure and must be present in **every hydraulic circuit**.
- ✔ Always **label the ports P, T, A, B** on DCV symbols — unmarked ports cost marks.
- ✔ **Accumulator** symbol = circle divided horizontally (gas chamber / fluid chamber).
- ✔ **Quick-disconnect coupling** = two parallel slashes across a flow line.
- ✔ **Variable-displacement pump** is indicated by a **diagonal arrow** passing through the pump circle.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Fluid Power Symbols**
**Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme — Course PCMRT503**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gear Pump** is a positive-displacement rotary pump that uses the meshing action of two or more gears to move fluid by *displacement* (trapping a fixed volume between gear teeth and the casing, then forcing it into the discharge port). It belongs to the family of **fixed-displacement hydraulic pumps** and is the most widely used pump in low-to-medium pressure (up to **~210 bar**) industrial hydraulic systems.

In Module 2 (Hydraulic Pumps & Motors), "Gear" specifically refers to:
- **External Gear Pumps** (spur gear type) — most common
- **Internal Gear Pumps** (lunar/gerotor type) — compact, low-noise
- **Gear Motors** — same geometry operated in reverse to convert fluid power to mechanical rotary power

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Gear pump is studied as a rotary positive-displacement pump. Students must master (a) construction & working, (b) displacement & flow-rate equations, (c) volumetric & mechanical efficiency losses, and (d) performance curves (flow vs. pressure).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Paddle Wheel Ferry"

Imagine two interlocking wooden paddles (gears) sitting inside a sealed metal boat (housing). The boat has an **inlet door** on one side and an **outlet door** on the other, with a tight wall (the gear-mesh line) between them.

- As the paddles rotate, each *tooth gap* scoops oil from the inlet door, carries it around the outer edge of the paddle wheel, and dumps it out through the outlet door.
- The oil has nowhere to escape *backwards* because the meshing teeth form a perfect seal in the middle.
- Result: a continuous, pulse-free stream of oil is pushed out, **displacement-wise** — meaning each revolution moves a *fixed* volume.

That fixed volume is what we call the **Geometric Displacement (V_D)**.

### 1.3 Real-World Engineering Use

- Mobile hydraulics: tractor lifts, dump trucks, forklifts
- Machine tools: lubrication circuits, coolant supply
- Injection moulding machines: low-pressure clamping units
- Agricultural equipment & earth-movers
- Power-steering units (internal gear / gerotor pumps)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standards Used:**
> - Standard module (m): **2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6 mm**
> - Standard pressure angle: **20°** (ISO 6332)
> - Typical operating pressure: **up to 175–210 bar (2500–3000 psi)**
> - Typical speed: **1200–3000 rpm**
> - Volumetric efficiency at rated pressure: **≥ 90–95 %**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Details — External Gear Pump

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Drive Gear (Pinion)** | Connected to the prime mover (electric motor/IC engine); drives the idler |
| **Idler (Driven) Gear** | Rotates freely on a bearing pin; transfers fluid |
| **Housing / Body** | Precision-bored cavity that contains the gears with very small clearances (~5–15 µm) |
| **Side Plates / Bushings** | Axial support for gear shafts; pressure-loaded bushings ride in **hydrostatic balance grooves** to reduce wear |
| **Inlet Port** | Low-pressure suction side, typically at the gear-mesh center |
| **Outlet Port** | High-pressure discharge side, at the opposite side of the housing |
| **Seals** | Lip seals / O-rings at shaft entry points |

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Suction Stroke:** As gears unmesh at the inlet port, the volume between teeth *increases*, creating a **partial vacuum** (per Bernoulli's principle). Atmospheric pressure pushes oil from the reservoir into the void.
2. **Conveying Phase:** Fluid is locked in the inter-tooth spaces and carried around the periphery between the gear teeth and the casing wall.
3. **Discharge Stroke:** As gears re-mesh at the outlet port, the volume between teeth *decreases*, **forcing oil out** under pressure into the system.
4. **Sealing Mechanism:** The line of tooth contact between the two gears acts as a seal, preventing fluid from returning to the suction side.
5. **Why Continuous Flow?** Because there are typically **8–12 teeth in mesh simultaneously**, the discharge is practically **pulse-free** (unlike piston pumps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
