> **The "Why" Behind Meshing = Sealing:** At the pitch point, one tooth of the drive gear fully enters the space of the driven gear. Theoretically there is *zero clearance* at this point — it acts as a moving boundary wall separating high-pressure and low-pressure regions.

### 2.3 Key Design Equations — The KTU Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use **SI units** unless specified. For volumetric formulas in gear pumps, watch the **cm³/rev** vs **m³/rev** conversion (**1 cm³ = 10⁻⁶ m³**).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Theoretical Displacement (V_D)** | $V_D = 2\pi \, b \, m \, z \, h_k$ | cm³/rev | b = face width, m = module, z = no. of teeth, h_k = working depth |
| 2 | **Displacement (simplified)** | $V_D = \pi \, b \left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right)$ | cm³/rev | D_o = outer dia, D_i = root dia |
| 3 | **Empirical form** | $V_D = 2\pi \, b \, m^2 \, z \, k$ | cm³/rev | k = geometry coefficient ≈ 2 |
| 4 | **Theoretical Flow Rate** | $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$ | cm³/min | N = rpm |
| 5 | **Actual Flow Rate** | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} \cdot \eta_v = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$ | cm³/min | $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency |
| 6 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{L}}{V_D N}$ | — | Q_L = leakage flow |
| 7 | **Theoretical Torque** | $T_{th} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi}$ | N·m | ΔP in Pa, V_D in m³ |
| 8 | **Actual Torque** | $T_{act} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi \cdot \eta_m}$ | N·m | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency |
| 9 | **Hydraulic Power Output** | $P_{out} = \dfrac{Q_{act} \cdot \Delta P}{60}$ | kW | Q in lpm, ΔP in bar |
| 10 | **Shaft Power Input** | $P_{in} = \dfrac{2\pi \, N \, T_{act}}{60\,000}$ | kW | N in rpm, T in N·m |
| 11 | **Overall Efficiency** | $\eta_{o} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | — | — |
| 12 | **Pump Bulk Modulus (Stiffness)** | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta V / V_D}$ | bar | For calculating pressure rise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute-value safety:** When writing $\vert \Delta P \vert$ or $\vert V_D \vert$ in your answer script, **never** use the pipe symbol inside a markdown table. Use LaTeX `\vert ... \vert` or `\mid ... \mid` to keep tables rendering correctly.

### 2.4 Loss Mechanisms — *Why Efficiency < 100 %*

- **Internal Leakage (Volumetric loss):** High-pressure oil leaks back through:
  - Tooth-tip clearances (radial)
  - Side-plate clearances (axial)
  - Bushings / bearing clearances
- **Mechanical Friction (Mechanical loss):**
  - Gear-tooth meshing friction
  - Bearing & bushing friction
  - Fluid churning (viscous drag)
- **Cavitation risk:** At suction side if inlet pressure drops below fluid vapor pressure.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gear pumps are the *workhorses* of mobile hydraulics because they are:
- **Compact** for their displacement (high power density ≈ 5–10 kW/kg)
- **Cheap to manufacture** (no precision-machined pistons or valves)
- **Bidirectional** — flow reverses when rotation reverses
- **Self-priming** at moderate speeds
- **Tolerant of dirty oil** (when compared to vane/piston pumps)

Their drawbacks: **fixed displacement** (no easy flow control), **higher noise** (gear whine), and **limited high-pressure capability** compared to piston pumps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Geometric Displacement (V_D)

We derive V_D for an **external gear pump** with two identical spur gears (z teeth, module m, face width b).

**Step 1 — Volume trapped between adjacent teeth of ONE gear**

For a standard 20° full-depth gear (ISO):
- Addendum (a) = m
- Dedendum (b_d) = 1.25 m
- Working depth (h_k) = 2 m
- Total depth (h_t) = 2.25 m

The *effective* volume of fluid carried per tooth space (excluding the area occupied by the meshing gear's tooth that penetrates the space) is:

$$
V_{tooth} \approx \pi \, m \, b \, h_k
$$

**Step 2 — Total volume per gear per revolution**

Since each gear has z teeth and z tooth spaces, and *all* spaces carry fluid in series around the periphery:

$$
V_{one \, gear} = z \cdot \pi \, m \, b \, h_k
$$

**Step 3 — Volume transferred per pump revolution**

Only ONE gear is the drive gear (driven by the shaft). However, because both gears rotate and each gear's tooth spaces carry fluid from inlet to outlet, the **net volume discharged per revolution = volume transferred by the driven gear** (the drive gear's spaces get partially refilled from the idler).

For a balanced pair, the displacement per revolution is:

$$
V_D = z \cdot \pi \, m \, b \, h_k = \pi \, b \, m \, z \, h_k
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute h_k = 2m (working depth for standard gear)**

$$
V_D = \pi \, b \, m \, z \, (2m) = 2\pi \, b \, m^2 \, z
$$

This matches Eq. (3) of the formula sheet with coefficient **k = 2**.

**Step 5 — Alternate form using Diameters**

For an external gear: $D_o = m(z+2)$ and $D_i = m(z-2.5)$. Then:

$$
D_o^2 - D_i^2 = m^2 \left[(z+2)^2 - (z-2.5)^2\right] = m^2 \cdot 9z - \text{constants}
$$

So at first-order:

$$
V_D \approx \pi \, b \left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right) \quad \text{(Eq. 2)}
$$

This matches the textbook Esposito form.

### 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical Flow Rate

By definition of a positive-displacement pump, every revolution displaces exactly V_D of fluid (ignoring leakage). So:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{V_D \cdot N}{1000} \;\; (\text{lpm, if } V_D \text{ in cm}^3 \text{ and } N \text{ in rpm})
$$

Derivation: Flow rate = displacement × revolutions per minute.

### 3.3 Derivation of Theoretical Torque

The hydraulic energy transferred per revolution = Pressure × Volume:

$$
W_{per \, rev} = \Delta P \cdot V_D
$$

The mechanical work done per revolution by the shaft torque T at angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$:

$$
W_{per \, rev} = T \cdot 2\pi
$$

Equating both energies (assuming 100 % mechanical efficiency):

$$
T_{th} \cdot 2\pi = \Delta P \cdot V_D \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T_{th} = \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi}
$$

This is Eq. (7).

### 3.4 Worked Example — Step-by-Step Numerical Solution

**Problem:** An external gear pump has **z = 12 teeth**, **module m = 4 mm**, and **face width b = 25 mm**. It runs at **N = 1450 rpm** against a pressure of **ΔP = 100 bar**. Calculate:
(a) Geometric displacement V_D
(b) Theoretical flow rate Q_th
(c) Theoretical driving torque T_th
(d) Theoretical hydraulic power P_out

**Given:**
- z = 12, m = 4 mm = 0.4 cm, b = 25 mm = 2.5 cm
- N = 1450 rpm, ΔP = 100 bar = 10⁷ Pa

**Solution (a) — Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_D &= 2\pi \, b \, m^2 \, z \\
&= 2\pi \times 2.5 \times (0.4)^2 \times 12 \\
&= 2\pi \times 2.5 \times 0.16 \times 12 \\
&= 2\pi \times 4.8 \\
&= 30.159 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b) — Theoretical Flow Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= \frac{V_D \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{30.159 \times 1450}{1000} \\
&= 43.731 \; \text{lpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (c) — Theoretical Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_D &= 30.159 \; \text{cm}^3 = 30.159 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^3 \\
T_{th} &= \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi} \\
&= \frac{30.159 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^{6}}{2\pi} \\
&= \frac{301.59}{6.2832} \\
&= 48.00 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (d) — Theoretical Hydraulic Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \frac{Q_{th} \cdot \Delta P}{60} = \frac{43.731 \times 100}{60} \\
&= 72.885 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Sanity Check:** For a gear pump of 30 cm³/rev at 1450 rpm and 100 bar, the *order of magnitude* of power should be **~70 kW** — exactly what we got. This validates the calculation.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | External Gear Pump | Internal Gear Pump | Vane Pump | Piston Pump |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure rating** | Up to 210 bar | Up to 175 bar | Up to 175 bar | Up to 700 bar |
| **Volumetric efficiency** | 90–95 % | 92–96 % | 85–95 % | 95–98 % |
| **Noise level** | High (gear whine) | Medium | Medium-Low | Lowest |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest |
| **Variable displacement?** | No | No | Yes (some) | Yes (axial piston) |
| **Typical application** | Tractors, mobile | Lubrication, low-noise | Machine tools | Presses, injection moulding |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Working Architecture of an External Gear Pump (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drive Shaft coupled to Motor] --> B[Drive Gear Pinion]
    B --> C[Mesh Zone Sealing Line]
    C --> D[Idler Driven Gear]
    D --> E[High Pressure Discharge Port]
    A1[Suction Reservoir Tank] -->|Atm Pressure| F[Low Pressure Inlet Port]
    F --> G[Unmeshing Zone Void]
    G --> H[Tooth Gap Conveying Region]
    H --> C
    C --> E
    E --> I[Hydraulic System Actuator]
    subgraph Housing_Chamber
    B
    C
    D
    G
    H
    end
    style Housing_Chamber fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block interpretation:** Fluid flows from the **reservoir** → **inlet port** → into the **unmeshing void** (suction) → carried around the periphery in the **conveying region** → through the **mesh zone** (the dynamic seal) → out the **discharge port** to the **hydraulic actuator**.

### 4.2 Internal Functional Flow of a Gear Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Suction Unmesh] --> S2[Step 2 Volume Increase Vacuum]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Oil Entry from Reservoir]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Conveying Around Periphery]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Mesh Sealing at Pitch Point]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Volume Decrease at Outlet]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Forced Discharge to System]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Continuous Pulse-Free Delivery]
    style S5 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px
    style S8 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Loss-Mechanism Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Input Shaft Power] --> M1[Gear Tooth Friction]
    P --> M2[Bearing and Bushing Friction]
    P --> M3[Fluid Churning Loss]
    M1 --> Mout[Mechanical Output Useful]
    M2 --> Mout
    M3 --> Mout
    MLoss[Mechanical Loss Sum] --> P
    Qth[Theoretical Flow] --> QL1[Radial Tip Clearance Leak]
    Qth --> QL2[Axial Side Plate Leak]
    Qth --> QL3[Bushing Clearance Leak]
    QL1 --> Qact[Actual Useful Flow]
    QL2 --> Qact
    QL3 --> Qact
    style MLoss fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000
    style Qact fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** When asked *"Why does volumetric efficiency drop with increasing pressure?"*, the answer is hidden in this diagram — **higher ΔP drives more leakage** through clearances QL1, QL2, QL3.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"With a neat sketch, explain the working of an external gear pump. State any two advantages."**

**Model Answer:**
An external gear pump consists of **two identical spur gears (drive and driven)** housed in a precision-bore casing with very small clearances. The drive gear is connected to a prime mover; the idler rotates freely. The line of tooth contact at the mesh point acts as a seal between the high-pressure (discharge) and low-pressure (suction) ports.

**Working Cycle (in 3 steps):**
1. **Suction:** As the gears unmesh at the inlet, volume between teeth increases, creating a vacuum that draws oil from the reservoir.
2. **Conveying:** Fluid is trapped between gear teeth and the casing wall, and is carried around the outer periphery to the outlet side.
3. **Discharge:** As the gears re-mesh at the outlet, the trapped fluid is squeezed out into the system under pressure.

**Advantages (any two):**
- Compact, simple, and cheap to manufacture
- Self-priming and bidirectional
- High volumetric efficiency at moderate pressures
- Tolerates contaminated fluid better than vane/piston pumps

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Compare external gear pump and internal gear pump on the basis of (i) construction, (ii) noise, (iii) pressure capability."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | External Gear Pump | Internal Gear Pump |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Two identical spur gears mesh externally; simple housing | An outer ring gear + inner rotor + crescent partition |
| **Noise** | Higher (gear-tooth whine at mesh) | Lower (fewer teeth mesh simultaneously) |
| **Pressure capability** | Up to ~210 bar | Up to ~175 bar |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — (Option 1, 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an external gear pump with a neat sketch. **7 marks**
**(b)** A gear pump has 12 teeth, module 4 mm, and face width 25 mm. It runs at 1450 rpm against a pressure of 100 bar. If volumetric efficiency is 92 % and mechanical efficiency is 90 %, calculate (i) theoretical and actual flow rate, (ii) actual driving torque, (iii) overall efficiency and input power. **7 marks**

**Model Solution (a):**
*(Provide construction & working as in Q1 of Part A. Include labelled diagram showing inlet port, outlet port, drive gear, idler, mesh zone, and casing. Discuss the role of the tooth-mesh line as the dynamic seal.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Geometric Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_D &= 2\pi \, b \, m^2 \, z \\
&= 2\pi \times 0.025 \times (0.004)^2 \times 12 \\
&= 2\pi \times 0.025 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 12 \\
&= 3.016 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^3/\text{rev} \\
&= 30.159 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Flow Rate:**

$$
Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N = 30.159 \times 1450 = 43\,731 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{min} = 43.731 \; \text{lpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual Flow Rate:**

$$
Q_{act} = Q_{th} \cdot \eta_v = 43.731 \times 0.92 = 40.23 \; \text{lpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual Driving Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{act} &= \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi \cdot \eta_m} \\
&= \frac{30.159 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{5}}{2\pi \times 0.90} \\
&= \frac{301.59}{5.6549} \\
&= 53.34 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \eta_v \times \eta_m = 0.92 \times 0.90 = 0.828 = 82.8 \,\%
$$

**Step 6 — Input Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= \frac{2\pi \, N \, T_{act}}{60\,000} = \frac{2\pi \times 1450 \times 53.34}{60\,000} \\
&= \frac{485\,834}{60\,000} = 8.097 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B — (Option 2, 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With a sketch, explain the construction and working of an **internal gear pump (gerotor type)**. Mention its advantages over the external gear pump. **7 marks**
**(b)** Derive the expression for the theoretical displacement of an external gear pump in terms of face width *b*, module *m*, number of teeth *z*, and tooth working depth *h_k*. A double-external gear pump delivers 50 lpm at 1500 rpm. Find its displacement per revolution. **7 marks**

**Model Solution (a):**
An internal gear pump (also called a **gerotor** or **lunar pump**) has:
- An **outer ring gear** with internal teeth
- An **inner rotor (gear)** with external teeth, offset by one tooth
- A **crescent-shaped partition** that seals the inlet from the outlet
- The inner rotor has *one fewer tooth* than the outer ring

**Working:**
1. As the inner rotor rotates, each of its teeth engages with a corresponding tooth on the outer ring.
2. Fluid is drawn into the *expanding* spaces between the inner and outer teeth at the inlet side.
3. Fluid is conveyed around the periphery.
4. At the outlet side, the meshing teeth *squeeze* the fluid out through the discharge port.

**Advantages over external gear pump:**
- Lower noise (less tooth-impact)
- More compact for same displacement
- Smoother flow (less pulsation)
- Can handle higher viscosity fluids efficiently

**Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation of V_D:**

Step 1: Volume trapped between adjacent teeth of one gear:

$$
V_{tooth} = \pi \, m \, b \, h_k
$$

Step 2: With z teeth on each gear, only the spaces that *carry* fluid count. Per revolution of the drive gear, the volume delivered equals the tooth spaces on the idler that are filled:

$$
V_D = z \cdot \pi \, m \, b \, h_k
$$

Step 3: Substituting $h_k = 2m$ for standard full-depth gear:

$$
\boxed{V_D = 2\pi \, b \, m^2 \, z}
$$

**Numerical part:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= 50 \; \text{lpm} = 50\,000 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{min} \\
N &= 1500 \; \text{rpm} \\
V_D &= \frac{Q_{th}}{N} = \frac{50\,000}{1500} \\
V_D &= 33.33 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where B.Tech Students Lose Marks in "Gear Pump" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch (most common, –2 marks):** Writing Q in cm³/min when the answer should be lpm, or using ΔP in bar without converting to Pa in torque equations. Always state your unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing V_D formulas:** Many students write $V_D = 2\pi b m^2 z$ for *internal* gear pump — wrong. The formula is derived for *external* spur gears. For internal/gerotor pumps, the formula is different.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Some derivations only account for one gear's tooth spaces; remember BOTH gears carry fluid — but the *net output* is from one gear's spaces that complete the journey.
> 4. **Drawing without labels:** A neat sketch with **unlabelled** parts loses 2–3 marks. Always label: drive gear, idler, inlet port, outlet port, mesh line, casing.
> 5. **Missing the "Sealing Mesh" point:** Examiners specifically look for the explanation that **gear-tooth contact at the pitch point acts as a moving seal**. Skipping this loses a key concept mark.
> 6. **Volumetric vs. Mechanical efficiency confusion:** $\eta_v$ accounts for *leakage*, $\eta_m$ for *friction*. Mixing them up in the torque/power formula yields wrong answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — GEAR PUMPS**
>
> ✅ Gear pump = **rotary positive-displacement** pump; fixed displacement; bidirectional.
>
> ✅ Two main types: **External (spur gear)** and **Internal (gerotor / crescent)**.
>
> ✅ **Three working stages**: Suction (unmesh) → Conveying (periphery) → Discharge (re-mesh).
>
> ✅ **Mesh point = dynamic seal** between HP and LP chambers.
>
> ✅ Key formula: $V_D = 2\pi \, b \, m^2 \, z$ (for standard 20° full-depth external gear).
>
> ✅ Flow rate: $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$ (lpm, with V_D in cm³/rev).
>
> ✅ Torque: $T_{act} = V_D \cdot \Delta P \, / \, (2\pi \cdot \eta_m)$.
>
> ✅ Power: $P_{out} = Q_{act} \cdot \Delta P \, / \, 60$ (kW, with Q in lpm, ΔP in bar).
>
> ✅ Overall efficiency: $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$.
>
> ✅ **Losses:** Volumetric (tip-clearance, side-plate, bushing leakage); Mechanical (tooth-friction, bearing, churning).
>
> ✅ **Pressure rating:** ≤ 210 bar (external); ≤ 175 bar (internal).
>
> ✅ **Advantages:** Simple, compact, cheap, self-priming, bidirectional.
>
> ✅ **Disadvantages:** No easy variable displacement, noisy at high pressure, limited high-pressure capability.
>
> ✅ Typical speed range: **1200–3000 rpm**.
>
> ✅ Standard module sizes (ISO): **2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6 mm**; pressure angle **20°**.
>
> ✅ **Bus-design rule:** Pressure-loaded bushings ride in **hydrostatic balance grooves** to reduce wear — a high-yield viva question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vane and Piston Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vane and Piston Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Positive Displacement Pumps

In hydraulic systems, energy is transmitted using an incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil of viscosity grade **ISO VG 32 to VG 68**). The heart of any hydraulic power pack is the **pump**, which converts the mechanical energy of an electric motor or engine into hydraulic (pressure) energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (PCMRT503, Module 2):**
> A **Positive Displacement Pump (PDP)** is a hydraulic machine that delivers a fixed volume of fluid per cycle, irrespective of the system back-pressure, by trapping a fixed quantity of fluid and forcibly displacing it into the discharge line. Vane and Piston pumps are the two most important categories of PDPs used in industry.

Hydraulic pumps are classified into three main families:

| Pump Family | Displacement Type | Typical Pressure Range |
|---|---|---|
| Gear Pumps | Fixed | Up to **210 bar** |
| **Vane Pumps** | Fixed & Variable | Up to **175 bar** |
| **Piston Pumps** | Fixed & Variable | Up to **420–700 bar** |

---

## 1.2 The Vane Pump — Concept & Intuition

### 1.2.1 Definition
A **vane pump** is a rotary positive-displacement pump that uses a set of sliding rectangular **vanes** (blades) mounted on a slotted rotor, which rotates eccentrically inside a circular stator ring. The space between consecutive vanes changes from minimum (at the inlet) to maximum (at the outlet), creating suction and discharge of fluid.

### 1.2.2 Real-World Analogy — *The Revolving Ice-Cream Scoop*

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **rotating cylindrical ice-cream scoop** placed slightly off-center inside a circular cookie-cutter (ring). As the scoop rotates:
> 1. On the side where the scoop moves *away* from the cutter wall, a **vacuum** is created (just like inhaling through a straw) — this is the **SUCTION / INLET** side.
> 2. On the opposite side, where the scoop presses *against* the wall, a thick chunk of fluid is **pushed out** — this is the **DISCHARGE / OUTLET** side.
> 3. The vanes (scoops) slide outward under centrifugal force, sealing the fluid packets between themselves.
>
> *That is exactly how an unbalanced vane pump works — except it is doing it thousands of times per minute!*

### 1.2.3 Types of Vane Pumps
- **Fixed Displacement Vane Pump** (Unbalanced type) — simplest, most common
- **Variable Displacement Vane Pump** — uses a movable stator ring (pressure-compensated, volume-controlled, or load-sensing)
- **Balanced Vane Pump** — dual cartridge design for high pressure

---

## 1.3 The Piston Pump — Concept & Intuition

### 1.3.1 Definition
A **piston pump** (also called a *reciprocating rotary pump*) uses a number of pistons (typically **7, 9, or 11**) reciprocating within precisely-bored cylinder bores, to deliver fluid at very high pressures. The pistons are driven either by a **swash plate** (inclined plate) — giving **axial piston pumps** — or by a rotating eccentric cam — giving **radial piston pumps**.

### 1.3.2 Real-World Analogy — *The Syringe Manifold*

> [!NOTE]
> Picture a hospital **IV drip system** with 9 syringes arranged in a circle, all connected to a single rotating manifold. As a motor turns a tilted plate underneath:
> 1. Half the syringes are being **pulled back (suction)** because the plate is moving away from them — drawing oil IN.
> 2. The other half are being **pushed in (discharge)** because the plate is moving toward them — squeezing oil OUT.
> 3. The transition happens at the **top dead-centre (TDC)** and **bottom dead-centre (BDC)** of each piston.
>
> *This is precisely how an axial piston pump (swash-plate type) operates in machines like excavators, injection moulding machines, and hydraulic presses.*

### 1.3.4 Types of Piston Pumps
- **Axial Piston Pump** — Swash-plate type (fixed & variable displacement)
- **Axial Piston Pump** — Bent-axis type (used in mobile hydraulics)
- **Radial Piston Pump** — Cam-driven (used in very high-pressure applications)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are piston pumps preferred over vane pumps in high-pressure industrial systems?**
> Because the pistons are in **pure axial/radial sliding contact** with the cylinder bore, they can be sealed effectively even at **350–700 bar**, whereas vane pumps suffer vane-tip wear and leakage at pressures above **175 bar**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vane Pump — Construction & Working

### 2.1.1 Main Components
1. **Stator (Ring / Cam Ring):** A precision circular ring forming the outer boundary. Its inner surface is a true circle.
2. **Rotor:** A solid cylindrical hub mounted on the drive shaft, having a centre that is **eccentric** (offset) with respect to the stator centre by a distance `e`.
3. **Vanes (Blades):** Rectangular hardened-steel blades sliding in radial slots on the rotor. They are pushed outward by:
   - **Centrifugal force** (dominant at high speed)
   - **Pressure-loaded side-pockets** behind the vanes (used at low speeds to maintain contact)
4. **Side Plates / Port Plates:** Two end covers with kidney-shaped **inlet** and **outlet** ports.
5. **Inlet Port:** Located on the side of *minimum* vane extension (low volume).
6. **Outlet Port:** Located on the side of *maximum* vane extension (high volume).

### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)
- The rotor is driven by an electric motor at speeds typically **900–1800 rpm**.
- Eccentricity `e` causes the rotor-to-ring gap to vary cyclically.
- At the **inlet zone**, the vanes retract → volume between two vanes *increases* → **low pressure** → oil is sucked in from the reservoir.
- As the rotor turns, the vanes are forced outward by centrifugal force, maintaining contact with the ring.
- At the **outlet zone**, the vanes are pushed outward → trapped volume *decreases* → oil is **forcibly squeezed** into the delivery line.
- The transition from suction to delivery happens at the **port plate seal point**, where the two kidney ports are separated.

---

## 2.2 Variable Displacement Vane Pump (Pressure-Compensated)

In this design, the stator ring is **not fixed**; it is mounted on a spring-loaded piston and can pivot/translate.

- When system pressure is **below the compensator setting**, the spring pushes the ring to its **maximum eccentricity** → maximum displacement → full flow delivery.
- When system pressure **rises above the setting**, the pressure acts on the compensator piston, opposing the spring, and the ring is pushed toward the rotor → **eccentricity reduces** → displacement decreases.
- At **cut-off pressure**, eccentricity = 0 → **theoretical zero flow** → pump delivers just enough to maintain pressure and compensate leakage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application:** Used in machine tools, press circuits, and energy-saving circuits (common in CNC machines manufactured in Kerala's industrial belt — Cochin, Calicut, and Trivandrum clusters).

---

## 2.3 Piston Pump — Construction & Working (Axial Swash-Plate Type)

### 2.3.1 Main Components
1. **Cylinder Block / Barrel:** A cylindrical block containing **Z** precisely-bored cylinder bores (Z = 7, 9, or 11).
2. **Pistons (Z nos.):** Hardened steel pistons with hemispherical heads that ride on the swash plate via a slipper / shoe.
3. **Swash Plate:** A precision-machined plate inclined at angle **α** to the axis of rotation. This angle determines the **stroke length** of the pistons.
4. **Valve Plate (Port Plate):** Two kidney-shaped ports (suction and delivery) at the end face of the cylinder block.
5. **Drive Shaft:** Connected to the cylinder block, rotating the entire assembly.

### 2.3.2 Working Principle
- The drive shaft rotates the cylinder block at high speed (typically **1500–3000 rpm**).
- As the pistons are constrained to follow the swash plate, each piston performs a **reciprocating motion**:
  - **Suction stroke:** piston moves *outward* (away from port plate) → volume increases → oil enters through the inlet port.
  - **Delivery stroke:** piston moves *inward* (toward port plate) → volume decreases → oil is discharged through the outlet port.
- The angle **α** of the swash plate controls the stroke length:
  - `α = 0°` → stroke = 0 → zero displacement (pump idling).
  - `α = 20°–30°` → maximum displacement.

### 2.3.3 Bent-Axis Type
In bent-axis pumps, the drive shaft and cylinder block are inclined at a fixed angle (e.g., **23°**). This eliminates the side-load on the pistons that exists in swash-plate designs, allowing higher pressures (up to **450 bar**) and longer life.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises **all critical formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme examination problems on Vane and Piston Pumps. The deliverable, theoretical, and percentage quantities are conventionally defined as:
> $Q_D$ = Actual (Delivered) Flow, $Q_T$ = Theoretical Flow, $\eta_v$ = Volumetric Efficiency, $\eta_m$ = Mechanical Efficiency, $\eta_o$ = Overall Efficiency.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Vane Pump Displacement | $V_D = 2\pi \left(R^2 - r^2\right) B$ | Ideal case, ignoring vane thickness; $V_D$ in m³/rev |
| 2 | Actual Vane Pump Displacement | $V_D = 2\left[\pi(R^2-r^2) - \dfrac{(R-r)\,t\,Z}{\cos\theta}\right] B$ | $t$ = vane thickness, $Z$ = no. of vanes, $\theta$ = vane tip angle |
| 3 | Theoretical Flow Rate (Vane / Piston Pump) | $Q_T = V_D \cdot N$ | $N$ in rev/s, $Q_T$ in m³/s; for $N$ in rpm, divide by 60 |
| 4 | Delivered Flow Rate | $Q_D = \eta_v \cdot Q_T = \eta_v \cdot V_D \cdot N$ | $0.90 \le \eta_v \le 0.98$ for piston pumps |
| 5 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_D}{Q_T} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_T}$ | $Q_L$ = leakage flow rate |
| 6 | Theoretical Power | $P_T = p \cdot Q_T$ | $p$ in Pa, $Q_T$ in m³/s → $P_T$ in W |
| 7 | Delivered Hydraulic Power | $P_D = p \cdot Q_D$ | W |
| 8 | Shaft (Input) Power | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{2\pi\,N\,T}{60}$ | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m → $P_{sh}$ in W |
| 9 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_D}{P_{sh}} = \dfrac{p\,Q_D}{2\pi N T / 60}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Stroke of Axial Piston | $s = D \tan\alpha$ | $D$ = piston pitch circle diameter, $\alpha$ = swash-plate angle |
| 12 | Theoretical Displacement — Axial Piston | $V_D = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot s \cdot Z = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot D \cdot \tan\alpha \cdot Z$ | $d$ = piston diameter, $Z$ = no. of pistons |
| 13 | Displacement — Radial Piston | $V_D = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{2} \cdot e \cdot Z$ | $e$ = eccentricity |
| 14 | Power in kW (using bar & LPM) | $P \,[kW] = \dfrac{p\,[bar] \times Q\,[LPM]}{600}$ | Standard industrial formula |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

| Pump Type | Industrial Sector | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Vane Pump (Fixed) | Machine Tools, Injection Moulding | Low–medium pressure hydraulics, lubrication |
| Vane Pump (Variable) | CNC Machines, Presses | Energy-efficient circuits, holding pressure |
| Axial Piston (Swash) | Mobile Hydraulics | Excavators, cranes, agricultural machinery |
| Axial Piston (Bent-Axis) | Marine, Heavy Industry | Ship hydraulics, steel mills, mining |
| Radial Piston | High-Pressure Test Rigs | Hydraulic presses, die-casting machines |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section presents **exhaustive derivations** of the two most-tested formulas in the KTU Module 2 syllabus: (A) Theoretical displacement of an unbalanced vane pump, and (B) Theoretical displacement of an axial piston pump. Every algebraic transition is justified by an explanatory step.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Displacement of an Unbalanced Vane Pump

### 3.1.1 Given / Assumed Geometry
- $R$ = Inner radius of the stator (cam ring)
- $r$ = Radius of the rotor
- $e$ = Eccentricity = $R - r$
- $B$ = Width of the vane (axial length of the rotor)
- $Z$ = Number of vanes
- $t$ = Thickness of each vane
- $\theta$ = Inclination angle of the vane tip from radial (zero in ideal case)

### 3.1.2 Geometric Setup
The rotor centre is displaced from the stator centre by a distance $e$. The trapping volume between two consecutive vanes, at any angular position $\phi$ measured from the line of centres, can be calculated.

For a **pair of vanes separated by an angle** $\frac{2\pi}{Z}$, the trapped area is the area of the segment between the rotor circle and the stator ring circle, **minus** the area occupied by the vane itself.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Volume between two consecutive vanes (at maximum throw position):**

The volume trapped between two vanes and bounded by the rotor and stator is:

$$V_{one} = \left[\pi \left(R^2 - r^2\right) - \frac{Z \cdot t \cdot (R - r)}{2\cos\theta}\right] \cdot \frac{1}{Z} \cdot B \cdot 2$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by number of vanes Z to get total swept volume per revolution:**

The total volume of fluid displaced per revolution (the **theoretical displacement**) is the sum of the volume delivered at the outlet port as the rotor makes one full turn:

$$V_D = 2\left[\pi(R^2 - r^2) - \frac{(R-r)\,t\,Z}{\cos\theta}\right] B$$

### 3.1.4 Special Case — Ideal Vane Pump (vanes infinitely thin, $t \to 0$, $\theta = 0$):

$$V_D = 2\pi(R^2 - r^2)B$$

This is the formula most commonly used in KTU problems unless stated otherwise.

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example
A vane pump has $R = 50$ mm, $r = 45$ mm, $B = 30$ mm, runs at $N = 1450$ rpm, and has $\eta_v = 0.95$. Calculate the theoretical flow rate and the delivered flow rate in LPM.

**Step A — Displacement per revolution:**

$$V_D = 2\pi(R^2 - r^2)B = 2\pi[(0.050)^2 - (0.045)^2](0.030)$$

$$V_D = 2\pi[0.0025 - 0.002025](0.030) = 2\pi(0.000475)(0.030)$$

$$V_D = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.000475 \times 0.030 = 8.952 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3\text{/rev}$$

**Step B — Theoretical Flow in m³/s (Note: N in rev/s = 1450/60 = 24.166):**

$$Q_T = V_D \cdot N = 8.952 \times 10^{-5} \times 24.166 = 2.1635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step C — Convert to LPM (× 60,000):**

$$Q_T = 2.1635 \times 10^{-3} \times 60\,000 = 129.8 \text{ LPM}$$

**Step D — Delivered Flow:**

$$Q_D = \eta_v \cdot Q_T = 0.95 \times 129.8 = 123.3 \text{ LPM}$$

> ✅ **Result:** $Q_T \approx 129.8$ LPM, $Q_D \approx 123.3$ LPM

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Displacement of an Axial Piston Pump (Swash-Plate)

### 3.2.1 Given / Assumed Geometry
- $d$ = Diameter of each piston
- $Z$ = Total number of pistons
- $D$ = Pitch circle diameter (centre-to-centre of pistons on the cylinder block)
- $\alpha$ = Angle of the swash plate with respect to the axis of rotation

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Stroke length of one piston:**

As the cylinder block rotates, each piston shoe follows the swash plate. The piston's *axial* displacement (the stroke) is the projection of the radial motion onto the cylinder axis:

$$s = D \tan\alpha$$

*Justification:* If the swash plate is flat and inclined at $\alpha$, then for a piston located at radius $D/2$ from the axis, the vertical (axial) lift between the highest and lowest point of contact is exactly $D \tan\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Volume displaced by ONE piston per revolution (one suction + one delivery stroke):**

Each piston fills and empties its cylinder once per revolution. Volume per piston per rev = piston area × stroke:

$$V_{one} = A \cdot s = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot s = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot D \tan\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Total theoretical displacement per revolution (Z pistons):**

$$V_D = Z \cdot V_{one} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot D \cdot \tan\alpha \cdot Z$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical flow rate at N rpm:**

$$Q_T = V_D \cdot \frac{N}{60} = \frac{\pi d^2 \cdot D \cdot \tan\alpha \cdot Z \cdot N}{4 \times 60}$$

**Step 5 — Delivered flow rate:**

$$Q_D = \eta_v \cdot Q_T$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example
An axial piston pump has $d = 25$ mm, $Z = 9$ pistons, $D = 80$ mm, $\alpha = 22.5°$, $N = 1500$ rpm, $\eta_v = 0.96$. Find $Q_T$, $Q_D$, and the delivered hydraulic power at $p = 200$ bar.

**Step A — Stroke:**

$$s = D \tan\alpha = 0.080 \times \tan(22.5°) = 0.080 \times 0.4142 = 0.03314 \text{ m} = 33.14 \text{ mm}$$

**Step B — Displacement per revolution:**

$$V_D = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot s \cdot Z = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} \times 0.03314 \times 9$$

$$V_D = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.03314 \times 9 = 1.464 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3\text{/rev}$$

**Step C — Theoretical flow rate (N in rev/s = 25):**

$$Q_T = 1.464 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 = 3.660 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} = 219.6 \text{ LPM}$$

**Step D — Delivered flow rate:**

$$Q_D = 0.96 \times 219.6 = 210.8 \text{ LPM}$$

**Step E — Delivered hydraulic power at 200 bar:**

$$P_D = \frac{p \cdot Q_D}{600} = \frac{200 \times 210.8}{600} = 70.27 \text{ kW}$$

> ✅ **Result:** $Q_T \approx 219.6$ LPM, $Q_D \approx 210.8$ LPM, $P_D \approx 70.3$ kW

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Table

| Property | Vane Pump | Axial Piston Pump | Radial Piston Pump |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max. Pressure (bar) | 175 | 420 | 700 |
| Volumetric Efficiency | 0.85 – 0.95 | 0.95 – 0.98 | 0.92 – 0.97 |
| Mechanical Efficiency | 0.85 – 0.92 | 0.90 – 0.95 | 0.90 – 0.95 |
| Noise Level | Medium | High | Medium |
| Variable Displacement? | Yes (movable ring) | Yes (swash angle) | No (fixed cam) |
| Cost | Low | High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are drawn using **block-level functional architecture** methodology, as physical cross-sectional drawings of pumps are too complex for pure text rendering. These diagrams are designed to map every working component and signal flow, which is precisely the level required for KTU 14-mark descriptive answers.

---

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Unbalanced Fixed Vane Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drive Motor AC Induction 1450 rpm] -->|Mechanical Shaft| B[Rotor with Z radial slots]
    B <-->|Sliding Contact| C[Sliding Vanes x Z blades]
    C -->|Outward Centrifugal Force| D[Stator Ring inner radius R]
    D --- E[Eccentric Offset e = R minus r]
    E --- B
    B -->|Suction Side| F[Inlet Port Kidney Shaped]
    B -->|Discharge Side| G[Outlet Port Kidney Shaped]
    F --> H[Reservoir 100 Litre]
    G --> I[Directional Control Valve]
    I --> J[Actuator Cylinder or Motor]
    J -->|Return Line| H
```

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Axial Piston Pump (Swash-Plate Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prime Mover Electric Motor] -->|Coupling| B[Drive Shaft]
    B --> C[Cylinder Block rotating at N rpm]
    C --> D[Pistons x Z arranged on pitch circle D]
    D -->|Slipper Shoe Contact| E[Swash Plate inclined at angle alpha]
    E --- F[Springs for variable displacement]
    F -->|Pressure Signal Feedback| G[Servo Piston]
    G -->|Controls| E
    C -->|TDC BDC transition| H[Valve Plate with two kidney ports]
    H -->|Suction Port| I[Inlet from Reservoir]
    H -->|Discharge Port| J[High Pressure Line to System]
    J --> K[Relief Valve setting]
    K -->|Excess Flow| I
```

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Pressure-Compensated Vane Pump Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LowPressure
    LowPressure: Below Compensator Setting
    LowPressure --> FullFlow: Spring pushes ring to max eccentricity
    FullFlow: Displacement V max e max
    FullFlow --> ReducedFlow: System pressure rises above setting
    ReducedFlow: Stator ring moves toward rotor
    ReducedFlow --> CutOff: Eccentricity approaches zero
    CutOff: Pump delivers only leakage flow to maintain pressure
    CutOff --> LowPressure: System demand resumes
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — Pump Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Required Operating Pressure bar] --> B{Pressure Greater than 175 bar}
    B -- NO --> C{Need Variable Displacement}
    B -- YES --> D{Axial or Radial Piston}
    C -- NO --> E[Fixed Vane Pump Low Cost]
    C -- YES --> F[Variable Vane Pump Pressure Compensated]
    D --> G{Compactness Priority}
    G -- YES --> H[Axial Piston Swash Plate]
    G -- NO --> I[Radial Piston High Pressure]
    E --> J[Final Selection]
    F --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the working of a fixed displacement unbalanced vane pump with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A fixed-displacement vane pump consists of a **cylindrical rotor** mounted eccentrically inside a **circular stator ring**. The rotor carries **Z rectangular vanes** in radial slots. As the rotor is driven by a motor, the vanes are thrown outward by **centrifugal force** and slide on the inner surface of the stator.

Because of the eccentricity, the gap between rotor and stator is **minimum on one side** and **maximum on the opposite side**. Fluid is **sucked in** through the inlet port (on the small-gap side) and **discharged** through the outlet port (on the large-gap side). The change in volume between successive vanes produces the pumping action. The volume of fluid delivered per revolution is given by $V_D = 2\pi(R^2 - r^2)B$ where $R$ is stator radius, $r$ is rotor radius, and $B$ is the vane width.

*(1 mark for construction, 1 mark for working, 1 mark for displacement formula.)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three advantages of piston pumps over vane pumps.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Higher operating pressure:** Piston pumps can operate up to **420–700 bar**, whereas vane pumps are typically limited to **175 bar**.
2. **Higher volumetric efficiency (95–98%)** due to tight piston-to-bore clearances, resulting in less internal leakage.
3. **Better variable-displacement capability** using the swash-plate mechanism, allowing smooth control of flow without throttling losses — leading to energy-efficient circuits.
4. *(Bonus 4th point)* Longer service life under continuous high-pressure operation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]*

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the construction and working of an **axial piston pump (swash-plate type)**. Derive the expression for its theoretical displacement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A swash-plate type axial piston pump has **9 pistons**, each of diameter **22 mm**, arranged on a pitch circle of **75 mm** diameter. The swash-plate angle is **20°** and the pump runs at **1440 rpm** with a volumetric efficiency of **95%**. The pump delivers oil at a pressure of **180 bar**. Calculate:
   (i) Theoretical displacement per revolution  
   (ii) Theoretical and actual flow rates in LPM  
   (iii) Theoretical and delivered hydraulic power in kW  
   (iv) Shaft power input if mechanical efficiency is **90%**   **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The main components of a swash-plate type axial piston pump are:
1. **Drive shaft** — connected to the prime mover (motor).
2. **Cylinder block / barrel** — contains **Z** precisely-bored axial cylinder bores (Z = 7, 9, 11 typical). Rotates with the drive shaft.
3. **Pistons** — Z hardened steel pistons fitted in the bores, free to reciprocate. Each has a hemispherical head that rides on the swash plate via a slipper.
4. **Swash plate** — an inclined plate set at angle $\alpha$ to the drive shaft axis. This angle is the **displacement control** parameter.
5. **Valve plate (port plate)** — a stationary plate at one face of the cylinder block, having two kidney-shaped ports (suction and delivery).
6. **Servo mechanism** — for variable displacement, a servo piston tilts the swash plate to change $\alpha$.

**Working (2 marks):**
As the cylinder block rotates, the slipper-shoe end of each piston is constrained to follow the swash plate. This forces the pistons to reciprocate inside their bores. On one side of the port plate, pistons move *outward* (away from port plate) — creating **suction** that draws oil from the reservoir. After crossing the port plate seal point, pistons move *inward* — **discharging** oil at high pressure. The transition happens at the top dead-centre (TDC) and bottom dead-centre (BDC).

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Stroke length: $s = D \tan\alpha$  
Displacement per piston per revolution: $V_{one} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot s = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot D \tan\alpha$  
Total theoretical displacement: $V_D = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot D \tan\alpha \cdot Z$

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:** $Z = 9$, $d = 22$ mm $= 0.022$ m, $D = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m, $\alpha = 20°$, $N = 1440$ rpm, $\eta_v = 0.95$, $p = 180$ bar, $\eta_m = 0.90$.

**(i) Theoretical displacement per revolution (1 mark):**

First, compute the stroke:
$$s = D \tan\alpha = 0.075 \times \tan(20°) = 0.075 \times 0.36397 = 0.02730 \text{ m}$$

Piston area:
$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.022)^2}{4} = 3.8013 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Theoretical displacement:
$$V_D = A \cdot s \cdot Z = 3.8013 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.02730 \times 9 = 9.34 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3\text{/rev}$$

**(ii) Theoretical and Actual flow rates (2 marks):**

$N$ in rev/s $= 1440 / 60 = 24$ rev/s

Theoretical flow:
$$Q_T = V_D \cdot N = 9.34 \times 10^{-5} \times 24 = 2.2416 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} = 134.5 \text{ LPM}$$

Actual delivered flow:
$$Q_D = \eta_v \cdot Q_T = 0.95 \times 134.5 = 127.8 \text{ LPM}$$

**(iii) Theoretical and Delivered hydraulic power (2 marks):**

Theoretical power:
$$P_T = \frac{p \cdot Q_T}{600} = \frac{180 \times 134.5}{600} = 40.35 \text{ kW}$$

Delivered power:
$$P_D = \frac{p \cdot Q_D}{600} = \frac{180 \times 127.8}{600} = 38.34 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Shaft input power (2 marks):**

$$P_{sh} = \frac{P_D}{\eta_m} = \frac{38.34}{0.90} = 42.6 \text{ kW}$$

> ✅ **Final Answers (A-b):** $V_D = 9.34 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/rev, $Q_T = 134.5$ LPM, $Q_D = 127.8$ LPM, $P_T = 40.35$ kW, $P_D = 38.34$ kW, $P_{sh} = 42.6$ kW.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **pressure-compensated variable displacement vane pump** with a sketch. How does it achieve flow reduction at high pressure? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fixed-displacement vane pump has the following specifications:  
Stator inner radius $R = 60$ mm, Rotor radius $r = 54$ mm, Vane width $B = 40$ mm, Number of vanes $Z = 12$, Vane thickness $t = 3$ mm, Vane tip angle $\theta = 5°$, Speed $N = 960$ rpm, Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.92$, System pressure $p = 100$ bar.  
Determine: (i) The theoretical displacement per revolution, (ii) The actual (delivered) flow rate in LPM, (iii) The delivered hydraulic power in kW. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A pressure-compensated vane pump is similar to the fixed-displacement type, **except the stator ring is not fixed rigidly**. Instead, it is mounted on a **compensator piston** and held against a **pre-compressed spring** on one side. A **pressure signal line** taps the system (discharge) pressure and routes it to the compensator piston chamber on the side opposite the spring.

**Working — Two-Stage Behaviour (3 marks):**

*Stage 1 — Below Compensator Setting:*  
The spring force is greater than the pressure force on the compensator piston. Hence, the stator ring is pushed to its **maximum eccentricity** position, and the pump delivers its **full rated flow**.

*Stage 2 — Above Compensator Setting:*  
When system pressure rises above the compensator setting, the pressure force on the compensator piston overcomes the spring force. The stator ring **moves toward the rotor**, reducing the eccentricity. The displacement reduces, and so does the flow.

*Stage 3 — At Cut-off:*  
At maximum set pressure, the stator ring becomes **concentric** with the rotor, eccentricity $e = 0$, and the pump delivers **theoretically zero flow** (only enough to maintain pressure and overcome leakage).

**Diagram Description (1 mark):**
Stator ring is movable in a slot; spring on left, hydraulic pressure acting on compensator piston on right. As pressure rises, ring slides right, reducing $e$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:** $R = 0.060$ m, $r = 0.054$ m, $B = 0.040$ m, $Z = 12$, $t = 0.003$ m, $\theta = 5°$, $N = 960$ rpm, $\eta_v = 0.92$, $p = 100$ bar.

**(i) Theoretical displacement per revolution (3 marks):**

Using the **actual vane pump displacement formula** (with vane thickness correction):

$$V_D = 2\left[\pi(R^2-r^2) - \frac{(R-r)\,t\,Z}{\cos\theta}\right] B$$

Compute each term:
$$R^2 - r^2 = (0.060)^2 - (0.054)^2 = 0.0036 - 0.002916 = 6.84 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\pi(R^2-r^2) = \pi \times 6.84 \times 10^{-4} = 2.1489 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$(R - r) = 0.006 \text{ m}$$

$$(R-r)\,t\,Z = 0.006 \times 0.003 \times 12 = 2.16 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\cos 5° = 0.9962$$

$$\frac{(R-r)\,t\,Z}{\cos\theta} = \frac{2.16 \times 10^{-4}}{0.9962} = 2.168 \times 10^{-4}$$

Therefore:
$$V_D = 2 \left[2.1489 \times 10^{-3} - 2.168 \times 10^{-4}\right] \times 0.040$$

$$V_D = 2 \times 1.9321 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.040 = 1.5457 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3\text{/rev}$$

**(ii) Actual flow rate in LPM (2 marks):**

$N$ in rev/s $= 960 / 60 = 16$ rev/s

$$Q_T = V_D \cdot N = 1.5457 \times 10^{-4} \times 16 = 2.473 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

Convert to LPM:
$$Q_T = 2.473 \times 10^{-3} \times 60\,000 = 148.4 \text{ LPM}$$

$$Q_D = \eta_v \cdot Q_T = 0.92 \times 148.4 = 136.5 \text{ LPM}$$

**(iii) Delivered hydraulic power in kW (2 marks):**

$$P_D = \frac{p \cdot Q_D}{600} = \frac{100 \times 136.5}{600} = 22.75 \text{ kW}$$

> ✅ **Final Answers (B-b):** $V_D = 1.546 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/rev, $Q_D = 136.5$ LPM, $P_D = 22.75$ kW.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in this Module — Costing 1 to 3 Marks Each:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the eccentricity:** $e = R - r$ in vane pump geometry. Some students write $e = R + r$ — this is **wrong** and leads to imaginary flow values.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert N from rpm to rev/s:** The formula $Q = V_D \cdot N$ requires $N$ in **rev/s**. If $N$ is given in rpm, divide by **60**. The bar/LPM formula $P = pQ/600$ already incorporates this conversion, but the SI form does not.
>
> 3. **Including vane thickness in the displacement formula when the problem says "ideal" or "neglecting vane thickness":** The two formulas $V_D = 2\pi(R^2 - r^2)B$ and $V_D = 2[\pi(R^2-r^2) - (R-r)tZ/\cos\theta]B$ are **not interchangeable** — read the question carefully.
>
> 4. **Using the wrong eccentricity in radial piston pumps:** For radial pumps, $e$ is the **cam eccentricity**, NOT the stator-rotor offset.
>
> 5. **Unit mixing in power calculation:** The formula $P = pQ/600$ is valid only when $p$ is in **bar** and $Q$ is in **LPM**. If you use SI units (Pa and m³/s), use $P = p \cdot Q$ directly to get Watts.
>
> 6. **Missing the pitch circle diameter in axial piston derivation:** Students sometimes use the piston diameter $d$ where $D$ (pitch circle diameter) should be used. Remember: $D$ is the diameter of the circle on which the pistons are *arranged*, NOT the piston bore diameter.
>
> 7. **Confusing $Q_T$ with $Q_D$:** In all KTU 14-mark problems, the question often asks for **delivered flow** $Q_D = \eta_v Q_T$. Students frequently deliver $Q_T$ instead and lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MUST-KNOW POINTS for KTU PCMRT503 Module 2 — Vane and Piston Pumps**

- ✅ A vane pump delivers fluid using **sliding vanes** on an **eccentrically-mounted rotor** inside a **circular stator ring**.
- ✅ The displacement of an ideal vane pump (vanes infinitely thin) is $\mathbf{V_D = 2\pi(R^2 - r^2)B}$.
- ✅ For real vane pumps with finite vane thickness: $\mathbf{V_D = 2[\pi(R^2-r^2) - (R-r)tZ/\cos\theta]B}$.
- ✅ A **pressure-compensated vane pump** uses a movable stator ring whose position is set by the balance of a **spring** and **system pressure**.
- ✅ At **cut-off pressure**, the stator becomes concentric with the rotor, and theoretical flow is zero.
- ✅ An **axial piston pump** uses a **swash plate** inclined at angle $\alpha$ to drive the pistons in reciprocating motion.
- ✅ Stroke of an axial piston: $\mathbf{s = D \tan\alpha}$.
- ✅ Theoretical displacement of an axial piston pump: $\mathbf{V_D = (\pi d^2/4) \cdot D \tan\alpha \cdot Z}$.
- ✅ The theoretical flow rate is always $Q_T = V_D \cdot N$ (with $N$ in rev/s).
- ✅ Delivered flow: $Q_D = \eta_v \cdot Q_T$, where $\eta_v$ is typically 0.90–0.98 for piston pumps and 0.85–0.95 for vane pumps.
- ✅ Hydraulic power (kW) = $\mathbf{p \text{ (bar)} \times Q \text{ (LPM)} / 600}$.
- ✅ Shaft input power = Delivered power / $\eta_m$.
- ✅ Overall efficiency $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$.
- ✅ Piston pumps operate at **higher pressures (up to 700 bar)** and offer **higher efficiency** than vane pumps, but are **more expensive** and **noisier**.
- ✅ Variable displacement is achieved in axial piston pumps by **tilting the swash plate** and in vane pumps by **moving the stator ring**.
- ✅ Radial piston pumps use a **fixed cam (eccentric ring)** and are inherently fixed-displacement, but offer the **highest pressures** (up to 700 bar).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pump Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pump Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pump Performance** refers to the quantitative characterization of a hydraulic pump's operational behavior under varying conditions of flow rate, pressure head, power input, and efficiency. It is conventionally expressed through a family of interrelated performance curves plotted between the dependent operating parameters (head, power, efficiency) and the independent variable (discharge or flow rate $Q$) at a constant impeller rotational speed $N$.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, pump performance encompasses four fundamental characteristics evaluated at the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**, which is the design operating condition where the pump operates at maximum hydraulic efficiency. The performance envelope is described by:

- **Characteristic Head ($H$)** — Energy added per unit weight of fluid, measured in meters of fluid column.
- **Volume Flow Rate ($Q$)** — Volumetric delivery per unit time, measured in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.
- **Shaft Power ($P_{sh}$)** — Mechanical power input at the pump coupling, measured in **kW**.
- **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$)** — Ratio of water power output to shaft power input (dimensionless, expressed as a percentage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The BEP, system resistance curve intersection, and Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) constitute the three high-weightage concepts under this topic. Any numerical problem on pump selection will invariably test one of these.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pedaling a bicycle (the pump) to push water up a hill through a pipe (the system). The harder and faster you pedal (higher $N$), the more water you can push. However, if you make the pipe narrower (higher system resistance), the water backs up and your legs get tired (lower efficiency). 

Now imagine trying to push water from a bucket placed on a table to a higher bucket. If the suction pipe is too long or the bucket is too shallow, air bubbles form inside the pump (this is **cavitation**), and the bicycle starts making a rattling sound instead of pumping water. The minimum water level needed to avoid this rattling is the **NPSH** requirement.

In essence, **pump performance tells us "how good" a pump is at converting mechanical energy into hydraulic energy, and under what conditions it operates best without breaking down.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard at sea level)
> - **Density of water at 4°C:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
> - **Standard reference temperature for hydraulic oil:** $40\text{°C}$ (ISO VG 46)
> - **1 HP = 745.7 W = 0.7457 kW**
> - **1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 10.197 m of water column**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Performance Parameters

### 2.1.1 Discharge ($Q$) — The Primary Independent Variable
The discharge is the volume of fluid delivered per unit time. For a centrifugal pump, $Q$ is related to the **meridional velocity** of the impeller ($V_{m2}$) and the **flow area** at the impeller outlet.

$$Q = \pi D_2 B_2 V_{m2}$$

where $D_2$ is the outer impeller diameter and $B_2$ is the outlet width. In KTU problems, $Q$ is the **x-axis** against which all performance curves are plotted.

### 2.1.2 Head Developed ($H$) — Energy per Unit Weight
The pump imparts mechanical energy to the fluid, which is expressed as a "head" in meters. The Euler equation for head is:

$$H_{Euler} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

For a radial flow pump (no pre-whirl), $V_{w1} \approx 0$, simplifying to:

$$H = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

The actual head developed is always less than the Euler head due to **hydraulic losses** (shock, friction, recirculation).

### 2.1.3 Shaft Power ($P_{sh}$)
The mechanical power supplied to the pump shaft by the prime mover (motor/engine):

$$P_{sh} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \text{ (in Watts, with } N \text{ in rpm and } T \text{ in N·m)}$$

Or equivalently:

$$P_{sh} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{1000 \eta_o} \text{ (in kW)}$$

### 2.1.4 Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$)
The product of three component efficiencies:

$$\eta_o = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{hyd}$$

- **Volumetric efficiency** ($\eta_{vol}$): Ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge (accounts for internal leakage past the impeller-volute clearance).
- **Mechanical efficiency** ($\eta_{mech}$): Ratio of power imparted to the fluid to shaft power (accounts for bearing and disc friction losses).
- **Hydraulic efficiency** ($\eta_{hyd}$): Ratio of actual head to Euler head (accounts for friction and shock losses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The component efficiency question is a guaranteed 3-mark direct question. The 14-mark problems always involve computing $\eta_o$ and then locating the BEP graphically or mathematically.

## 2.2 Pump Characteristic Curves (The Performance Family)

A complete pump performance characterization is a set of three curves plotted against $Q$ at a constant speed $N$:

| Curve | Trend with increasing $Q$ | Physical Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Head vs $Q$ ($H$-$Q$)** | Monotonically decreases (centrifugal); nearly constant (positive displacement) | Increased flow $\Rightarrow$ greater velocity $\Rightarrow$ reduced pressure conversion |
| **Power vs $Q$ ($P$-$Q$)** | Monotonically increases | Power is proportional to $QH$, and although $H$ drops, $Q$ rises faster |
| **Efficiency vs $Q$ ($\eta$-$Q$)** | Parabolic (peaks at BEP) | Compounded effect of $\eta_{vol}$, $\eta_{mech}$, $\eta_{hyd}$ each peaking at slightly different $Q$ |

### 2.2.1 The Best Efficiency Point (BEP)

The BEP is the operating point on the $H$-$Q$ curve where the overall efficiency $\eta_o$ attains its **maximum value**. KTU problems often ask: *"A pump operates at its BEP delivering 100 L/s at 30 m head with 80% efficiency. Find the shaft power."* — which uses:

$$P_{sh} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_o} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.100 \times 30}{0.80} = 36.79 \text{ kW}$$

## 2.3 Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)

NPSH is the **most critical performance parameter** on the suction side of a pump. It determines whether the pump will cavitate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (NPSH):** The total head available at the pump suction nozzle, measured in meters of fluid, **above the vapor pressure head** of the liquid at the pumping temperature.

### 2.3.1 Two Variants of NPSH

- **NPSH Available ($\text{NPSH}_A$):** Determined by the **system** (suction tank level, suction pipe length, friction, velocity, vapor pressure). It is what the installation provides.

$$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_{atm} - p_{v}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f$$

where $h_s$ is the static suction head (positive if suction lift, negative if flooded suction) and $h_f$ is the friction loss in the suction line.

- **NPSH Required ($\text{NPSH}_R$):** Determined by the **pump manufacturer** through testing. It is the minimum head needed at the suction nozzle to prevent cavitation. It increases with flow rate $Q$.

$$\text{NPSH}_R = f(Q, N)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Cavitation Rule:** The pump will cavitate if $\text{NPSH}_A < \text{NPSH}_R$. Always design with a safety margin of at least **1 m** (or 10–15%) above the required NPSH.

## 2.4 Affinity Laws (Pump Scaling Laws)

When the speed of a centrifugal pump is changed, the performance parameters transform according to the **Affinity Laws**. These are extremely high-yield for KTU numericals.

| Quantity | Affinity Law | Exponent |
|---|---|---|
| Flow rate | $Q \propto N$ | 1 |
| Head | $H \propto N^2$ | 2 |
| Power | $P \propto N^3$ | 3 |

Mathematically, for two speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \times V$ | Discharge from pipe cross-section | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $H = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | Euler head (radial pump) | m |
| 3 | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic (water) power | W |
| 4 | $P_{sh} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_o}$ | Shaft power | W |
| 5 | $P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Shaft power from torque | W |
| 6 | $\eta_o = \eta_{vol} \eta_{mech} \eta_{hyd}$ | Overall efficiency product | — |
| 7 | $\eta_{hyd} = \frac{H_{actual}}{H_{Euler}}$ | Hydraulic efficiency | — |
| 8 | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | — |
| 9 | $\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{sh}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | — |
| 10 | $\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_{atm} - p_v}{\rho g} - h_s - h_f$ | Available NPSH (suction lift) | m |
| 11 | $\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_{atm} - p_v}{\rho g} + h_s - h_f$ | Available NPSH (flooded suction) | m |
| 12 | $\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Affinity Law — Flow | — |
| 13 | $\frac{H_2}{H_1} = (N_2/N_1)^2$ | Affinity Law — Head | — |
| 14 | $\frac{P_2}{P_1} = (N_2/N_1)^3$ | Affinity Law — Power | — |
| 15 | $H_{system} = H_{static} + K Q^2$ | System resistance curve | m |
| 16 | $N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed (metric, $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $H$ in m) | — |
| 17 | $P_{sh} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_o} \times 10^{-3}$ | Shaft power in kW | kW |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pump performance data is the cornerstone of **pump selection, system design, and energy auditing** in industries. A chemical engineer selects a centrifugal pump to deliver 50 L/s of sulfuric acid at 40 m head — they consult the manufacturer's $H$-$Q$ curve and overlay it on the system curve to find the **operating point**. If the operating point falls far from the BEP, the pump is the wrong size.

In the **oil and gas sector**, drilling mud pumps are selected using affinity laws — changing the engine speed adjusts flow and pressure simultaneously, allowing real-time control of wellbore hydraulics. In **HVAC**, chilled water pumps are trimmed (impeller diameter reduced) to match building load, saving up to 30% energy annually.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Turbomachine Equation for Pump Head

The fundamental head developed by a pump originates from the angular momentum balance on the fluid as it passes through the impeller. We derive it step by step below.

### Step 1 — Velocity Triangle Setup
Consider the impeller with outer diameter $D_2$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s. The blade tip speed is:

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$

At the impeller outlet, the absolute velocity $\vec{V_2}$ has two components: the **whirl component** $V_{w2}$ (tangential) and the **meridional component** $V_{m2}$ (radial). The relative velocity $V_{r2}$ is along the blade direction.

### Step 2 — Apply Angular Momentum Theorem
Euler's equation of turbomachinery states that the energy transferred per unit weight of fluid is the change in moment of momentum:

$$H_{Euler} = \frac{1}{g}(V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$

For a **radial-flow pump with no pre-whirl** (no inlet guide vanes, axial inlet), the inlet whirl component is essentially zero:

$$V_{w1} \approx 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_{Euler} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Blade Tip Speed

$$H = \frac{V_{w2} \cdot \pi D_2 N}{60 \cdot g}$$

### Step 4 — Account for Hydraulic Losses
The Euler head is the **ideal** head. The actual head is reduced by the hydraulic efficiency $\eta_{hyd}$:

$$H_{actual} = \eta_{hyd} \times H_{Euler} = \frac{\eta_{hyd} \cdot V_{w2} \cdot \pi D_2 N}{60 g}$$

This is the theoretical foundation that explains why the $H$-$Q$ curve of a centrifugal pump **slopes downward** as $Q$ increases — at higher flow, the meridional velocity increases, the relative velocity angle $\beta_2$ changes, and $V_{w2}$ decreases.

## 3.2 Derivation: Shaft Power from Torque and Speed

The torque $T$ transmitted by the shaft does work as the shaft rotates. In one revolution, the work done is $W = 2\pi T$. The number of revolutions per second is $N/60$. Therefore, the rate of work (power) is:

$$P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi T \cdot N}{60} \text{ (in W)}$$

For $N$ in rpm and $T$ in N·m, this directly yields power in Watts. To convert to kW:

$$P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi T N}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi T N}{30000} \text{ (in kW)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Affinity Laws from Dimensional Analysis

The affinity laws can be derived rigorously by considering two geometrically similar pumps operating at different speeds. For geometric similarity, all linear dimensions scale by a factor $k$, and velocity at any point scales with the tip speed $u \propto D N$.

### Step 1 — Flow Rate Scaling
Discharge $Q = \pi D B V_m$. For geometrically similar pumps, $D_2/D_1 = B_2/B_1 = k$, and $V_{m2}/V_{m1} = u_2/u_1 = (D_2 N_2)/(D_1 N_1)$. Hence:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = k^2 \times \frac{V_{m2}}{V_{m1}} = k^2 \times k \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = k^3 \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

For the **same pump** (constant $k=1$):

$$\boxed{\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}}$$

### Step 2 — Head Scaling
Head has units of $\text{velocity}^2 / g$. Since velocity scales as $DN$:

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \frac{V_2^2}{V_1^2} = \left(\frac{D_2 N_2}{D_1 N_1}\right)^2 = k^2 \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$$

For same pump ($k=1$):

$$\boxed{\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2}$$

### Step 3 — Power Scaling
Power $P = \rho g Q H$. Substituting the scaling:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \times \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right) \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

For same pump ($k=1$):

$$\boxed{\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3}$$

## 3.4 Worked-Out KTU Numerical Example

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump delivers $Q_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ of water at a head of $H_1 = 25$ m when running at $N_1 = 1450$ rpm. The pump is now to be operated at $N_2 = 1750$ rpm. Calculate (a) the new discharge, (b) the new head, and (c) the new power if the original shaft power was $P_1 = 18$ kW.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Given:** $Q_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $H_1 = 25$ m, $N_1 = 1450$ rpm, $N_2 = 1750$ rpm, $P_1 = 18$ kW.

**Step (a): New Discharge**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1750}{1450} = 1.2069$$

$$Q_2 = 1.2069 \times 0.05 = 0.0603 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_2 \approx 60.3 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step (b): New Head**

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = (1.2069)^2 = 1.4566$$

$$H_2 = 1.4566 \times 25 = 36.42 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H_2 \approx 36.42 \text{ m}}$$

**Step (c): New Shaft Power**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = (1.2069)^3 = 1.7576$$

$$P_2 = 1.7576 \times 18 = 31.64 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 \approx 31.64 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice that a 20.7% increase in speed causes a 20.7% increase in flow, 45.7% increase in head, and 75.8% increase in power. This cubic relationship is why **speed control is highly energy-efficient only over a narrow range** — small speed boosts consume disproportionate power.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Cavitation Diagnosis

| Observation | $\text{NPSH}_A$ vs $\text{NPSH}_R$ | Diagnosis | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pump runs smoothly, rated flow delivered | $\text{NPSH}_A > \text{NPSH}_R + 1$ m | Healthy operation | Continue |
| Reduced discharge, rattling noise, vibration | $\text{NPSH}_A \approx \text{NPSH}_R$ | Incipient cavitation | Reduce $N$ or lower suction lift |
| Severe vibration, metal pitting on impeller | $\text{NPSH}_A < \text{NPSH}_R$ | Full cavitation | Shut down, redesign suction |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Pump Performance Evaluation Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the systematic process used to evaluate and characterize pump performance, from initial system definition to final operating point determination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Pumping Requirement] --> B[Specify Q requirement L per s]
    B --> C[Specify H requirement m of head]
    C --> D[Determine Fluid Properties rho mu]
    D --> E[Compute System Resistance Curve Hsys = Hstatic + K Q squared]
    E --> F[Select Candidate Pump from Catalog]
    F --> G[Obtain Manufacturer H vs Q Curve at Rated N]
    G --> H[Plot H vs Q and Hsys vs Q on Same Axes]
    H --> I{Find Intersection Point}
    I --> J[Identify Operating Point Qop Hop]
    J --> K[Compute Water Power Pwater = rho g Qop Hop]
    K --> L[Read Shaft Power Psh from P vs Q Curve]
    L --> M[Calculate Overall Efficiency eta = Pwater divided by Psh]
    M --> N{eta Greater than 60 percent at Operating Point}
    N -->|Yes| O[Acceptable: Pump is well sized]
    N -->|No| P[Reject: Reselect Pump or Trim Impeller]
    O --> Q[Check NPSHA Against NPSHR]
    P --> F
    Q --> R{NPSHA Greater than NPSHR plus 1 m Margin}
    R -->|Yes| S[FINAL: Approved for Installation]
    R -->|No| T[Modify Suction Line: Reduce Length, Increase Diameter, or Lower Static Lift]
    T --> Q
    S --> U[END]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Pump Performance Curves Family

This schematic represents the three canonical performance curves and their interrelations, including the location of the Best Efficiency Point (BEP).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERFCURVES[Performance Curves at Constant Speed N]
        direction TB
        HQ[Head H versus Flow Q Curve: Monotonically Decreasing] 
        PQ[Shaft Power Psh versus Flow Q Curve: Monotonically Increasing] 
        EQ[Efficiency eta versus Flow Q Curve: Parabolic with Peak]
    end
    HQ -->|Decrease in H| PHYSICS1[Higher velocity, reduced pressure conversion]
    PQ -->|Increase in Psh| PHYSICS2[P proportional to Q times H, Q dominates]
    EQ -->|Parabolic Peak| BEP1[Best Efficiency Point BEP Located Here]
    BEP1 -.->|All Three Curves Converge| DESIGN[Design Operating Condition]
    DESIGN --> PERFOUTPUT[Maximum eta, Minimum NPSHR, Lowest Vibration]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: NPSH Analysis and Cavitation Prevention Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: System Suction Parameters] --> B[Compute Atmospheric Pressure head]
    B --> C[Subtract Vapor Pressure head at Operating Temperature]
    C --> D[Add or Subtract Static Suction Head]
    D --> E[Subtract Friction Loss in Suction Pipe]
    E --> F[OUTPUT: NPSH Available NPSHA in meters]
    F --> G[Look Up NPSHR from Pump Curve at Operating Q]
    G --> H{Compare NPSHA versus NPSHR}
    H -->|NPSHA > NPSHR + 1m| I[SAFE: No Cavitation Expected]
    H -->|NPSHA ~ NPSHR| J[MARGINAL: Incipient Cavitation Risk]
    H -->|NPSHA < NPSHR| K[UNSAFE: Cavitation Will Occur]
    I --> L[Approved Design]
    J --> M[Reduce NPSHR by: Lowering N, Reducing Q, or using Inducer]
    K --> N[Redesign Suction: Shorter Pipe, Larger Diameter, Flooded Suction, Lower Fluid Temperature]
    M --> G
    N --> A
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Affinity Law Application Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Operating Point: N1, Q1, H1, P1] --> B[New Rotational Speed N2 Specified]
    B --> C[Compute Speed Ratio lambda = N2 divided by N1]
    C --> D[New Flow Q2 = lambda times Q1]
    C --> E[New Head H2 = lambda squared times H1]
    C --> F[New Power P2 = lambda cubed times P1]
    D --> G[New Operating Point: N2, Q2, H2, P2]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Verify: New Point lies on Pump Family Curve]
    H --> I{Valid}
    I -->|Yes| J[Accept: Pump operates at N2]
    I -->|No| K[Reject: Off-design, cavitation or overload possible]
```
