# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define the term "Best Efficiency Point" (BEP) of a centrifugal pump. Why is it preferred for continuous operation?**

**Model Answer:**
The Best Efficiency Point (BEP) is the specific operating point on a pump's head-flow ($H$-$Q$) characteristic curve at which the overall efficiency $\eta_o$ attains its maximum value. At BEP, the product of volumetric, mechanical, and hydraulic efficiencies is optimized, hydraulic losses are minimized, and the radial and axial thrusts on the impeller are balanced.

BEP is preferred for continuous operation because:
1. Energy consumption is minimized (lowest operating cost).
2. Mechanical vibrations and noise are at their lowest.
3. Cavitation tendency is at its minimum (lowest $\text{NPSH}_R$).
4. Bearing life and seal life are maximized, reducing maintenance.
5. The risk of recirculation and surge is eliminated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition — 1 mark; BEP identification rationale — 1 mark; 3 reasons for preference — 1 mark.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**What is Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)? Differentiate between NPSH available and NPSH required.**

**Model Answer:**
NPSH is the total head available at the pump suction nozzle, measured in meters of fluid, above the vapor pressure head of the liquid at the pumping temperature. It represents the margin by which the pressure at the impeller eye exceeds the vapor pressure, thereby preventing the formation of vapor bubbles (cavitation).

| Parameter | NPSH Available ($\text{NPSH}_A$) | NPSH Required ($\text{NPSH}_R$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Determined by** | The system/installation (suction tank, piping) | The pump manufacturer (design and test) |
| **Depends on** | Atmospheric pressure, vapor pressure, static suction head, friction losses | Pump design, flow rate $Q$, speed $N$ |
| **Formula** | $\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_{atm} - p_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f$ | Read from manufacturer's $H$-$Q$ curve at operating $Q$ |
| **Remedial action if low** | Increase suction head, reduce friction, lower temperature | Lower pump speed, reduce $Q$, use inducer |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing $\text{NPSH}_A$ and $\text{NPSH}_R$. Remember: **Available is what you have, Required is what you need.** Cavitation occurs when **Available < Required**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Module 2, Model Question Paper]**

**(a) A centrifugal pump running at 1450 rpm delivers 0.08 m³/s of water against a head of 30 m. The pump requires a shaft power of 32 kW. Determine: (i) the overall efficiency, (ii) the specific speed, and (iii) the type of pump according to specific speed classification. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the affinity laws for a centrifugal pump. A pump operating at 1450 rpm delivers 150 L/s at 25 m head. If the speed is increased to 1750 rpm, calculate the new discharge, head, and shaft power. Assume the original shaft power is 28 kW. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** $N = 1450$ rpm, $Q = 0.08 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $H = 30$ m, $P_{sh} = 32$ kW.

**(i) Overall Efficiency:**

The water power (hydraulic power output) is:

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.08 \times 30 = 23{,}544 \text{ W} = 23.544 \text{ kW}$$

The overall efficiency is:

$$\eta_o = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{sh}} = \frac{23.544}{32} = 0.7357$$

$$\boxed{\eta_o = 73.57\%}$$

**(ii) Specific Speed:**

Using the metric formula with $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ and $H$ in m:

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}} = \frac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.08}}{(30)^{3/4}}$$

Computing the components:

$$\sqrt{0.08} = 0.2828$$

$$(30)^{3/4} = (30)^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \ln 30} = e^{0.75 \times 3.4012} = e^{2.5509} = 12.819$$

$$N_s = \frac{1450 \times 0.2828}{12.819} = \frac{410.10}{12.819}$$

$$\boxed{N_s \approx 32.0 \text{ (SI units)}}$$

**(iii) Type of Pump:**
With $N_s \approx 32$, the pump is a **radial flow (centrifugal) pump**. Classification ranges (metric):
- $N_s < 50$: Radial flow pump
- $50 \le N_s \le 150$: Mixed flow pump
- $N_s > 150$: Axial flow pump

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** $\eta_o$ computation — 2 marks; $N_s$ computation — 3 marks; classification with reasoning — 2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Affinity Laws Explanation:**

The affinity laws are scaling relationships that predict how the performance parameters of a centrifugal pump (flow $Q$, head $H$, and power $P$) change when the rotational speed $N$ is varied, assuming the pump operates on a homologous (similar flow) point on its characteristic curve.

The three laws are:
1. **Flow Law:** $Q \propto N$, i.e., $Q_2/Q_1 = N_2/N_1$
2. **Head Law:** $H \propto N^2$, i.e., $H_2/H_1 = (N_2/N_1)^2$
3. **Power Law:** $P \propto N^3$, i.e., $P_2/P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^3$

These laws are valid for the same pump (geometrically identical) and are derived from dimensional analysis and the Euler turbomachine equation.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $N_1 = 1450$ rpm, $Q_1 = 150$ L/s $= 0.150 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $H_1 = 25$ m, $P_1 = 28$ kW, $N_2 = 1750$ rpm.

**Speed Ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1750}{1450} = 1.2069$$

**New Discharge:**

$$Q_2 = \lambda \times Q_1 = 1.2069 \times 0.150 = 0.1810 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_2 = 181.0 \text{ L/s}}$$

**New Head:**

$$H_2 = \lambda^2 \times H_1 = (1.2069)^2 \times 25 = 1.4566 \times 25 = 36.42 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H_2 = 36.42 \text{ m}}$$

**New Shaft Power:**

$$P_2 = \lambda^3 \times P_1 = (1.2069)^3 \times 28 = 1.7576 \times 28 = 49.21 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 49.21 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often write $N_2/N_1$ in the wrong order or forget to convert $Q$ from L/s to $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Also, do not mix up the three exponents (1, 2, 3) — this costs 1–2 marks. **Always state the formula symbolically before substituting numbers.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam - Module 2, Alternate Model Question]**

**(a) A centrifugal pump has the following performance at its Best Efficiency Point (BEP): Discharge = 100 L/s, Head = 40 m, Shaft Power = 50 kW. Calculate: (i) the overall efficiency at BEP, (ii) the water power developed, and (iii) the specific speed. (7 marks)**

**(b) Define cavitation. A pump draws water from a sump 3 m below the pump centerline. The suction pipe is 5 cm in diameter, 8 m long with a friction factor of 0.025. The atmospheric pressure is 10.3 m of water and the vapor pressure is 0.25 m of water at the operating temperature. Calculate the NPSH available when the pump delivers 30 L/s. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** $Q = 100$ L/s $= 0.100 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $H = 40$ m, $P_{sh} = 50$ kW.

**(i) Water Power:**

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.100 \times 40 = 39{,}240 \text{ W} = 39.24 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{water} = 39.24 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta_o = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{sh}} = \frac{39.24}{50} = 0.7848$$

$$\boxed{\eta_o = 78.48\%}$$

**(iii) Specific Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

With $N$ not explicitly given, we assume standard $N = 1450$ rpm (typical for 50 Hz industrial motors). If a different $N$ is given in the original problem, substitute accordingly.

$$N_s = \frac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.100}}{(40)^{3/4}} = \frac{1450 \times 0.3162}{(40)^{0.75}}$$

Computing $(40)^{0.75}$:

$$(40)^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \ln 40} = e^{0.75 \times 3.6889} = e^{2.7667} = 15.905$$

$$N_s = \frac{1450 \times 0.3162}{15.905} = \frac{458.49}{15.905}$$

$$\boxed{N_s \approx 28.83 \text{ (radial flow pump)}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Cavitation Definition:**

Cavitation is the phenomenon of vapor bubble formation in a liquid when local static pressure falls below the vapor pressure of the liquid. In a pump, cavitation occurs at the impeller eye where pressure is lowest. When these vapor bubbles travel to regions of higher pressure, they collapse violently, causing noise, vibration, impeller erosion, and a sharp drop in performance.

**NPSH Available Calculation:**

**Given:** Suction lift $h_s = 3$ m (pump above sump), pipe diameter $D = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m, pipe length $L = 8$ m, friction factor $f = 0.025$, $p_{atm}/\rho g = 10.3$ m, $p_v/\rho g = 0.25$ m, $Q = 30$ L/s $= 0.030 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

**Step 1: Velocity in Suction Pipe**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.030}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2} = \frac{0.030}{0.001963} = 15.28 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Friction Head Loss in Suction Pipe**

Using Darcy-Weisbach equation:

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.025 \times 8 \times (15.28)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.05}$$

Computing numerator: $0.025 \times 8 \times 233.48 = 46.696$
Computing denominator: $2 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 0.981$

$$h_f = \frac{46.696}{0.981} = 47.60 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Apply NPSH Available Formula (Suction Lift Case)**

$$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{p_{atm} - p_v}{\rho g} - h_s - h_f$$

$$\text{NPSH}_A = 10.3 - 0.25 - 3 - 47.60$$

$$\boxed{\text{NPSH}_A = -40.55 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** The negative NPSH indicates severe cavitation — the pump cannot operate in this configuration. To fix this: (1) reduce friction by enlarging the suction pipe, (2) install the pump below the sump water level (flooded suction), or (3) reduce the flow rate. The KTU examiner awards 2 marks for interpreting the result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pump Performance**

- ✅ **Three Performance Curves:** $H$-$Q$ (decreasing), $P$-$Q$ (increasing), $\eta$-$Q$ (parabolic, peaks at BEP).
- ✅ **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_o = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{hyd}$. Each component peaks at a different $Q$ — the product peaks at BEP.
- ✅ **Power Formulas:** $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ (in W with SI base units); $P_{sh} = P_{water}/\eta_o$; $P_{sh} = 2\pi NT/60$.
- ✅ **Affinity Laws Exponents:** Flow $\propto N^1$, Head $\propto N^2$, Power $\propto N^3$. **Cubic relationship for power is the most-tested concept.**
- ✅ **NPSH Rule:** $\text{NPSH}_A \geq \text{NPSH}_R + 1$ m safety margin. If violated, cavitation occurs.
- ✅ **NPSH Formulas:** Suction lift case uses minus sign for $h_s$; flooded suction case uses plus sign.
- ✅ **Specific Speed Formula (SI):** $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ with $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $H$ in m, $N$ in rpm. Classification: $<50$ radial, $50$–$150$ mixed, $>150$ axial.
- ✅ **System Curve:** $H_{system} = H_{static} + KQ^2$ — parabolic, originates from $H_{static}$ at $Q=0$.
- ✅ **Operating Point:** Intersection of pump $H$-$Q$ curve and system $H$-$Q$ curve. Best practice: operating point should be within $\pm 10\%$ of BEP.
- ✅ **Conversion Constants:** 1 HP = 0.7457 kW; 1 bar $\approx$ 10.2 m of water; $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ at 4°C.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting to convert L/s to $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ — this introduces a 1000-fold error and loses 2–3 marks instantly.
- ✅ **Cavitation Prevention Hierarchy:** (1) Lower pump speed, (2) Enlarge suction pipe, (3) Reduce static suction lift, (4) Use low-NPSH pump (e.g., inducer), (5) Cool the fluid to lower vapor pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics and Selection - Sizing of Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Selection - Sizing of Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pump Characteristics** refer to the graphical and mathematical relationships between a pump's primary performance parameters — **Flow Rate (Q)**, **Head (H)**, **Power Input (P)**, and **Efficiency (η)** — evaluated at a fixed rotational speed (N). The term **"Sizing of Pumps"** denotes the systematic engineering procedure of determining the most appropriate pump type, capacity, and rated power that satisfies a given hydraulic system's demand at the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.

Mathematically, the four canonical characteristic curves of a centrifugal (or rotary) pump are expressed as:

$$
Q = f_1(H), \quad H = f_2(Q), \quad P = f_3(Q), \quad \eta = f_4(Q)
$$

where each function is determined experimentally at a constant impeller speed **N (rpm)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart of the Hydraulic System"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a pump as the **human heart** in a hydraulic circuit. Just as the heart pumps blood (fluid) through arteries (pipes) against resistance (system pressure), a hydraulic pump pressurizes fluid to overcome system load. **Sizing** is analogous to a cardiologist prescribing the right heart for a patient — too small, and the body (system) fails from inadequate supply; too large, and energy is wasted and the pump operates inefficiently (off its BEP).

### 1.3 Key Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow Rate (Discharge) | Q | **m³/s** or **LPM** | Volume of fluid delivered per unit time |
| Head | H | **meters (m)** of fluid column | Energy imparted per unit weight of fluid |
| Pressure | P | **Pa** or **bar** | Force per unit area (1 bar ≈ 10.2 m of water) |
| Rotational Speed | N | **rpm** | Shaft angular velocity |
| Power (Input) | P_in | **kW** or **HP** | Mechanical power supplied to pump shaft |
| Power (Hydraulic/Output) | P_out | **kW** | Useful fluid power delivered |
| Efficiency | η | **%** (dimensionless) | Ratio of output to input power |
| Net Positive Suction Head | NPSH | **m** | Available head at pump suction to avoid cavitation |
| Specific Speed | N_s | dimensionless | Classification index for pump geometry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The selection of a pump requires matching the **Pump Characteristic Curve (H–Q)** with the **System Resistance Curve (H_sys–Q)** to identify the **Operating Point**, which must ideally coincide with the pump's **BEP** for maximum service life and minimum energy cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Canonical Pump Characteristic Curves

At a **fixed impeller speed N**, a centrifugal pump exhibits four interlinked curves plotted against the discharge **Q**:

1. **H–Q Curve (Head vs. Discharge):** For a centrifugal pump, head typically *decreases* parabolically as Q increases. This is known as a *drooping* characteristic.
2. **P–Q Curve (Power vs. Discharge):** Power input *increases* with Q (linear-ish slope).
3. **η–Q Curve (Efficiency vs. Discharge):** Efficiency rises from zero, peaks at the **BEP**, and falls again.
4. **NPSHR–Q Curve (Required NPSH vs. Discharge):** Required NPSH increases with Q.

### 2.2 System Resistance Curve (System Curve)

The hydraulic system downstream of the pump imposes a resistance that varies with flow:

$$
H_{sys} = H_{static} + H_{friction} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^2
$$

- **H_static** = Static head (elevation difference + pressure head difference), independent of Q.
- **H_friction = K·Q²** = Friction losses in pipes, fittings, valves — proportional to the square of flow (turbulent regime, Darcy–Weisbach model).

The intersection of the **Pump Curve (H vs Q)** and the **System Curve (H_sys vs Q)** gives the **Operating Point**.

### 2.3 Affinity Laws (Pump Scaling Laws)

> [!NOTE]
> **Affinity Laws** allow us to predict how a pump's performance changes when the speed (N) or impeller diameter (D) is altered. They are foundational to pump selection and speed-based duty adjustment.

**Law 1 — Capacity (Flow):**

$$
\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}
$$

**Law 2 — Head:**

$$
\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2
$$

**Law 3 — Power:**

$$
\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3
$$

When impeller diameter is trimmed (cut) instead of speed changed, replace N with D in the same ratios.

### 2.4 Specific Speed (N_s) — Geometric Classification Index

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

where N is in **rpm**, Q in **m³/s**, H in **m**.

- Low N_s → Radial flow pump
- Medium N_s → Mixed flow pump
- High N_s → Axial flow pump

### 2.5 Net Positive Suction Head Available (NPSHA)

$$
NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + \frac{P_{tank}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - h_{f,suction} - z_{s}
$$

- The condition for cavitation-free operation: **NPSHA ≥ NPSHR + safety margin (typically 0.5 to 1.0 m).**

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic (output) power | W (ρ in kg/m³, g in m/s², Q in m³/s, H in m) |
| 2 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$ | Shaft (input) power | W |
| 3 | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}}$ | Brake motor power | W |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{P_{shaft}} \times 100\%$ | Pump efficiency | % |
| 5 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed | dimensionless |
| 6 | $H_{sys} = H_{st} + K Q^2$ | System resistance curve | m |
| 7 | $H_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | m |
| 8 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Affinity Law 1 (Flow) | — |
| 9 | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Affinity Law 2 (Head) | — |
| 10 | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Affinity Law 3 (Power) | — |
| 11 | $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_{vap}}{\rho g} - h_f - z_s$ | NPSH Available | m |
| 12 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow (continuity) | m³/s |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** Centrifugal pumps circulate crude oil in pipelines, sized using H_sys–Q matching.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler feed pumps deliver high-pressure water; precise sizing prevents cavitation.
- **HVAC Systems:** Chilled water pumps are selected based on cooling load (Q) and system friction (H).
- **Hydraulic Presses:** Intensifier pumps must be sized for peak pressure and flow demands.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Affinity Laws (from Euler's Turbomachinery Equation)

Euler's pump equation gives the theoretical head imparted to the fluid as:

$$
H_{th} = \frac{u_2 c_{\theta 2} - u_1 c_{\theta 1}}{g}
$$

where $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ is the blade tip speed. At geometrically similar operating points, the velocity triangles scale linearly with both **D** and **N**. Substituting $u \propto D N$ and $c \propto D N$:

$$
H \propto (D N)^2
$$

For the **same pump** (D = constant), varying only N:

$$
H \propto N^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2
$$

Similarly, the flow Q is related to the meridional velocity component:

$$
Q \propto D^2 N \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} \quad (\text{for constant } D)
$$

Power is the product of head (energy/weight) × flow (weight/time):

$$
P = \rho g Q H \propto (D N)(D^2 N)(D^2 N^2) = D^5 N^3
$$

For a constant-D pump:

$$
\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This explains the **"Cube Law"**: a mere 10% increase in pump speed causes a ~33% increase in power demand, which is critical in motor selection.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Pump Sizing by Operating Point

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump is required to deliver water at **Q = 0.05 m³/s** against a total system head of **H = 20 m**. The pump's H–Q characteristic at N = 1450 rpm is given by $H = 30 - 500 Q^2$ (Q in m³/s, H in m). Determine the actual operating point, the hydraulic power, and the required motor size assuming 70% pump efficiency and 90% motor efficiency.

**Step 1 — Equate Pump Head and System Head.**
Assume system curve is approximately $H_{sys} = 20 + 200 Q^2$ (includes 20 m static + quadratic friction).

Set Pump = System:

$$
30 - 500 Q^2 = 20 + 200 Q^2
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Q.**

$$
10 = 700 Q^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q^2 = \frac{10}{700} = 0.01428
$$

$$
Q = \sqrt{0.01428} = 0.1195 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Check against demand.** Required Q = 0.05 m³/s. Since **0.1195 > 0.05**, the pump *exceeds* the demand at the natural operating point, meaning we must **throttle** the discharge valve to reduce Q, or **trim the impeller** to shift the curve.

**Step 4 — Recompute operating point with throttling.** Throttling raises the system curve. We iterate or impose Q = 0.05 m³/s as the new operating flow:

$$
H_{pump}(Q=0.05) = 30 - 500(0.05)^2 = 30 - 1.25 = 28.75\;\text{m}
$$

$$
H_{sys}(Q=0.05) = 20 + 200(0.05)^2 = 20 + 0.5 = 20.5\;\text{m}
$$

Since pump head > system head, the actual Q must be lower. Equating:

$$
30 - 500 Q^2 = 20 + 200 Q^2 \Rightarrow Q = 0.1195 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \;\text{(as above)}
$$

> The valve must be throttled such that effective $H_{sys}$ rises to ~28.75 m at Q = 0.05 m³/s. The throttle adds a virtual head:

$$
H_{throttle} = 28.75 - 20.5 = 8.25 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute hydraulic (output) power.**

$$
P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 28.75
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = 14{,}102\;\text{W} \approx 14.1 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute shaft power (input to pump).**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{14.1}{0.70} = 20.14 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute motor power (input to motor).**

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{20.14}{0.90} = 22.38 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 8 — Apply safety factor (KTU standard: 1.15 to 1.25).**

$$
P_{motor,\,selected} = 22.38 \times 1.15 = 25.74 \;\text{kW} \approx 26 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{Q_{op} = 0.1195\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}, \quad H_{op} = 28.75\;\text{m}, \quad P_{motor} \approx 26\;\text{kW}}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Affinity Law Application

**Problem:** A pump delivers 200 LPM at 30 m head running at 2900 rpm. The speed is reduced to 2500 rpm. Find the new flow, head, and power. Original input power is 5 kW.

**Step 1 — Affinity Law 1 (Flow):**

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 200 \times \frac{2500}{2900} = 172.4\;\text{LPM}
$$

**Step 2 — Affinity Law 2 (Head):**

$$
H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 30 \times \left(\frac{2500}{2900}\right)^2 = 30 \times 0.7433 = 22.3\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Affinity Law 3 (Power):**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = 5 \times (0.8621)^3 = 5 \times 0.6405 = 3.20\;\text{kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Observe the **cubic power reduction** — a 13.8% drop in speed yields a 36% drop in power. This is the principle behind **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** for energy-efficient pump operation in industrial hydraulics.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pump Selection Decision Tree

| Step | Question to Answer | Required Data / Calculation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | What **Q** is required? | From process demand (m³/s or LPM) |
| 2 | What **H_total** must be overcome? | $H = H_{static} + H_{friction}$ |
| 3 | Plot **system curve** $H_{sys}$ vs Q | $H_{sys} = H_{st} + K Q^2$ |
| 4 | Determine **NPSH_A** at suction | $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} - z_s - h_f$ |
| 5 | Compute **N_s** to choose impeller type | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ |
| 6 | Select pump whose H–Q curve intersects system curve near **BEP** | Manufacturer catalog |
| 7 | Verify **NPSH_R ≤ NPSH_A** | Cavitation check |
| 8 | Compute **shaft & motor power** with safety factor | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump} \eta_{motor}} \times SF$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Pump Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Demand] --> B[Required Flow Q]
    A --> C[Required Head H]
    C --> D[Static Head Hst]
    C --> E[Friction Losses Hf]
    B --> F[System Curve Hsys = Hst + K Q2]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Select Pump Type via Ns]
    B --> G
    C --> G
    G --> H[Plot Manufacturer H-Q Curve]
    F --> I[Plot System Curve]
    H --> J[Operating Point Intersection]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Near BEP?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Verify NPSH Ava vs Req]
    K -- No --> M[Trim Impeller or Change Speed]
    M --> H
    L --> N{NPSH OK?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Compute Shaft Power]
    N -- No --> P[Reduce Suction Losses / Lower Pump]
    P --> L
    O --> Q[Compute Motor Power with SF]
    Q --> R[Select Motor and VFD]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Pump Performance vs System Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pump H-Q Curve] --> C[Operating Point]
    B[System Hsys-Q Curve] --> C
    C --> D{Operating Near BEP}
    D -- Yes --> E[Stable Efficient Operation]
    D -- No --> F[Throttle Valve or Trim Impeller]
    F --> G[Shift System Curve Upward]
    F --> H[Shift Pump Curve Downward]
    G --> C
    H --> C
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Affinity Law Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Original Speed N1] --> Q1[Flow Q1]
    N1 --> H1[Head H1]
    N1 --> P1[Power P1]
    N2[New Speed N2] --> Q2[Flow Q2 = Q1 N2/N1]
    N2 --> H2[Head H2 = H1 N2 squared over N1 squared]
    N2 --> P2[Power P2 = P1 N2 cubed over N1 cubed]
    Q1 --> Q2
    H1 --> H2
    P1 --> P2
```

### 4.4 Pump Performance Schematic (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Q axis horizontal] --> B[H axis vertical]
    B --> C[Hpump curve parabolic downward]
    B --> D[Power curve increasing linear]
    B --> E[Efficiency curve bell shape]
    C --> F[BEP Best Efficiency Point]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Operating Point Intersection with System]
    G --> H[Design Margin 10 to 20 percent]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
Define **specific speed of a pump**. State its significance in pump selection.

**Model Answer:**  
Specific speed $N_s$ is a dimensionless index that classifies the geometric shape and type of an impeller. It is defined as:

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

where N is in **rpm**, Q in **m³/s**, and H in **m**.  
**Significance:** A low $N_s$ (10–60) indicates a **radial-flow** pump suitable for high-head low-flow duties (e.g., boiler feed pumps). A medium $N_s$ (60–300) corresponds to **mixed-flow** pumps, while a high $N_s$ (300–1000) indicates **axial-flow** propellers for high-flow low-head applications. It enables the engineer to select the most efficient impeller geometry for a given duty point.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
What is the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**? Why must a pump ideally operate near the BEP?

**Model Answer:**  
The **BEP** is the duty point on the pump's H–Q curve where the pump attains its **maximum efficiency**. Operating at or near the BEP is critical because:

- Energy losses (hydraulic, volumetric, mechanical) are minimized.
- Radial thrust on the impeller is balanced, extending bearing and seal life.
- Cavitation risk is lower, and NPSH requirements are most stable.
- Vibration, noise, and wear are minimal, ensuring long service life.

A typical engineering specification is to operate within **±10% to ±15% of BEP flow** to balance efficiency and reliability.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]*

**(a)** A centrifugal pump running at 1450 rpm delivers 0.1 m³/s of water against a head of 25 m. The pump efficiency is 75%.  
**(i)** Calculate the **shaft power** input to the pump. **(4 marks)**  
**(ii)** If the motor driving the pump is 88% efficient, determine the **electric power consumed**. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the concept of the **operating point** of a pump. Discuss the effect of **valve throttling** and **impeller trimming** on the operating point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a-i):**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.1 \times 25}{0.75}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{24{,}525}{0.75} = 32{,}700 \;\text{W} = 32.7\;\text{kW}
$$

**Model Solution (a-ii):**

$$
P_{electric} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{32.7}{0.88} = 37.16\;\text{kW}
$$

**Model Solution (b):**  
The **operating point** is the unique intersection of the pump's **H–Q characteristic curve** (supplied by the manufacturer) and the system's **H_sys–Q resistance curve** (derived from static head + friction losses). At this point, the energy supplied by the pump exactly equals the energy demanded by the system.

- **Valve Throttling:** Closing a discharge valve introduces an additional localized resistance, increasing the system curve's slope. The new operating point shifts **leftward (lower Q, higher H)**. This is energy-inefficient because throttled energy is dissipated as heat in the fluid.

- **Impeller Trimming:** Reducing the impeller diameter lowers the entire pump curve proportionally (using the diameter affinity laws). The new operating point also shifts left, but **without the throttling loss**, offering a more energy-efficient solution for fixed-speed installations. The trade-off is a permanent loss in maximum achievable head.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Alternative]*

**(a)** State and derive the **Affinity Laws** for a centrifugal pump. A pump running at 1500 rpm delivers 0.08 m³/s at 20 m head. If the speed is increased to 1800 rpm, determine the new **flow rate**, **head**, and **power input** (assume original power = 4 kW). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **NPSH**? Differentiate between **NPSH Available (NPSHA)** and **NPSH Required (NPSHR)**. Explain the phenomenon of **cavitation** and state the criteria to avoid it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**  
Affinity laws are stated in §2.3 and derived in §3.1. For this numerical part:

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 0.08 \times \frac{1800}{1500} = 0.096 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
H_2 = H_1 \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 20 \times (1.2)^2 = 20 \times 1.44 = 28.8\;\text{m}
$$

$$
P_2 = P_1 \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = 4 \times (1.2)^3 = 4 \times 1.728 = 6.91\;\text{kW}
$$

**Model Solution (b):**  
**Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** is the total head available at the pump suction port above the vapor pressure of the liquid, expressed in meters of fluid.

- **NPSH Available (NPSHA):** Determined by the system — atmospheric pressure, tank pressure, suction static head, and suction-side friction losses.
- **NPSH Required (NPSHR):** Determined by the pump design — the minimum suction head needed to keep the liquid from vaporizing inside the impeller.

**Cavitation** occurs when the local static pressure at the impeller eye falls below the liquid's vapor pressure, causing vapor bubbles to form. These bubbles collapse violently in higher-pressure regions, causing pitting, noise, vibration, and efficiency loss.

**Cavitation Avoidance Criterion:**

$$
NPSH_A \geq NPSH_R + \text{Safety Margin (typically 0.5 to 1.0 m)}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Evaluation:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting between LPM, m³/s, m³/h without showing the conversion factor explicitly. Always write the unit conversion step.
> 2. **Forgetting the Safety Factor:** Many students compute $P_{motor}$ directly without multiplying by 1.15 to 1.25. **Up to 2 marks** can be lost.
> 3. **Confusing NPSH_A and NPSH_R:** The condition is *NPSHA ≥ NPSHR*, not the reverse. Reversing this is a common logical error.
> 4. **Affinity Law Errors:** Mistaking the exponent — flow is linear, head is squared, power is cubed. Memorize this *cube law* explicitly.
> 5. **Static vs. Total Head:** Forgetting to include velocity head ($v^2/2g$) at the discharge tank when computing total system head. Examiners often test this.
> 6. **Density Confusion:** Using $\rho = 1000\;\text{kg/m}^3$ for water but forgetting to state it; or using SG for oils without converting.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pump characteristic curves** are always plotted at a **constant speed (N)** and consist of four curves: H–Q, P–Q, η–Q, and NPSHR–Q.
- The **operating point** is the intersection of the **pump curve** and the **system curve** ($H_{sys} = H_{st} + KQ^2$).
- The **BEP** is the flow rate at maximum efficiency; always size pumps to operate **within ±10–15% of BEP**.
- **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ (for constant impeller diameter).
- **Specific speed** $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ classifies the impeller as radial, mixed, or axial flow.
- **NPSH Criterion:** $NPSH_A \geq NPSH_R + \text{margin}$; otherwise cavitation damages the pump.
- **Shaft Power:** $P_{shaft} = \rho g Q H / \eta_{pump}$. Multiply by a **safety factor of 1.15–1.25** to get motor rating.
- **Valve throttling** wastes energy as heat; **impeller trimming** or **VFD speed control** are more energy-efficient alternatives.
- Always use **consistent SI units**: ρ in kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², Q in m³/s, H in m → Power in W.
- For **KTU numericals**, show all conversion steps explicitly — partial credit depends on visible working.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Motors - Limited Rotation Hydraulic Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Motors - Limited Rotation Hydraulic Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Limited Rotation Hydraulic Motor (LRHM)** is a specialized hydraulic actuator (also termed a *Hydraulic Rotary Actuator* or *Hydraulic Oscillating Motor*) that converts hydraulic fluid pressure energy into **rotary mechanical motion** confined to a strictly defined angular displacement — typically **less than 360°** and most commonly in the range of **90° to 270°**.

Unlike continuous-rotation hydraulic motors (used in vehicle drives, winches, and machine tool spindles), LRHMs are designed to **swing, oscillate, or index** a load through a precise angle and then hold it at the end of the stroke. The shaft rotates back and forth between two mechanical end-stops, making them the hydraulic equivalent of a pneumatic rotary actuator but with significantly higher torque density and stiffness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In Module 2, the focus is on **construction, working principle, torque generation, and angular displacement calculation** of the four principal types: **Rack-and-Pinion, Scotch-Yoke, Vane-type, and Piston-type** limited rotation motors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wrist of a Robot

Imagine the **wrist joint of a robotic arm** in a car welding line. The robot does not need the wrist to spin continuously like a fan blade. It only needs to **rotate 180° back and forth** to position the welding torch on different sides of a car body panel. 

- A regular electric motor would require additional **brakes, encoders, and gear reducers** to stop and hold at the exact angle.
- A **limited rotation hydraulic motor** does this **natively**: pressurized oil enters a chamber, pushes an internal mechanism (rack, piston, or vane), the mechanism rotates the output shaft through its designed arc, and the shaft comes to rest against an **internal mechanical stop** when oil flow is cut off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications of LRHMs:**
> - 🎯 Spot welding robot wrists (automotive assembly)
> - 🛡️ Gun turret positioning on armored vehicles
> - 🚜 Steering cylinders on skid-steer loaders
> - ⚓ Valve actuation in process industries (ball/butterfly valves)
> - 🛰️ Satellite dish and radar antenna positioning
> - 🏗️ Crane slewing and boom rotation

### 1.3 Why Use a Limited Rotation Motor Instead of a Piston-Cylinder Pair?

A linear hydraulic cylinder can rotate a load using a **linkage or rack-and-pinion** conversion, but this introduces backlash, slop, and additional mechanical parts. An LRHM **integrates the linear-to-rotary conversion inside the housing**, giving:

- **Compactness** — single unit instead of cylinder + linkage.
- **High torque-to-weight ratio**.
- **Stiff holding capability** — no drift under static load because oil is incompressible.
- **Precision** — repeatable end-position accuracy to within fractions of a degree.
- **Bidirectional control** with simple 4/3 or 4/2 directional control valves.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Angular displacement | $\theta$ | **radians (rad)** or **degrees (°)** | Total arc of rotation available |
| Output torque | $T$ | **N·m** | Rotational force produced |
| Operating pressure | $P$ | **bar** or **Pa** | Hydraulic supply pressure |
| Rotational speed | $N$ | **rpm** | Angular velocity |
| Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m$ | dimensionless (<1) | Accounts for friction losses |
| Power output | $P_{out}$ | **W (watts)** or **kW** | Useful mechanical power delivered |

> [!TIP]
> Always carry **bar → Pa conversion** ($1\ bar = 10^5\ Pa$) and **rpm → rad/s conversion** ($\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$) in your mental toolbox for the KTU exam hall.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Limited rotation hydraulic motors are classified based on the **internal mechanism that converts fluid pressure into rotary motion**. The four canonical types — *all examinable under the KTU 2024 scheme* — are analyzed below.

---

### 2.1 Type 1 — Rack-and-Pinion Limited Rotation Motor

**Construction:** A hydraulic cylinder is mounted **tangentially** to a pinion gear. The piston is rigidly connected to a **linear rack** (toothed bar) that meshes with the pinion shaft.

**Working Principle:**
- Pressurized oil enters chamber A → pushes piston outward → rack moves linearly → pinion rotates.
- Reversing the flow retracts the rack → pinion rotates in the opposite direction.
- The stroke length of the cylinder is mechanically limited by **adjustable stops** on the cylinder body.

**Geometric Relationship:** If the piston stroke is $L$ and the pinion pitch radius is $R$:

$$\theta = \frac{L}{R} \quad \text{[radians]}$$

**Torque Equation (Theoretical):**
The linear force generated by the piston is $F = P \times A$, where $A$ is the piston area. This force acts on the rack at radius $R$ from the pinion center, producing a torque:

$$T_{th} = P \cdot A \cdot R$$

**Actual (Useful) Torque Output** — applying mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$:

$$\boxed{T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m}$$

**Why this formula matters:** It is a *direct* conversion — torque is **constant** throughout the entire stroke, irrespective of angular position. This is the primary advantage of the rack-and-pinion design for applications requiring uniform torque delivery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanical Efficiency Range:** Typically **$\eta_m = 0.85$ to $0.95$** for rack-and-pinion units, due to gear-tooth friction and seal drag.

---

### 2.2 Type 2 — Scotch-Yoke Limited Rotation Motor

**Construction:** A double-acting piston drives a **slot (yoke)** that is cut into a disc mounted on the output shaft. The slot is oriented **perpendicular to the disc face**. A sliding pin (or roller) attached to the piston engages the slot.

**Working Principle:**
- Piston moves → pin moves linearly → pin slides inside the slot → slot (and hence the disc/shaft) rotates.
- The radial distance of the pin from the shaft centerline **varies sinusoidally** with the rotation angle, which is the key differentiator from the rack-and-pinion type.

**Geometric Analysis:** If the yoke radius (perpendicular distance from shaft center to slot centerline) is $R$, and the pin position is $\phi$ relative to the slot, the effective moment arm is:

$$M_{arm} = R \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

**Torque Equation (Theoretical):**
The piston force is $F = P \cdot A$, and only the tangential component generates torque. Therefore:

$$T_{th} = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

**Actual Useful Torque:**

$$\boxed{T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \sin(\phi) \cdot \eta_m}$$

**Key Insight — Why the Sine Function?**
At the **mid-stroke** ($\phi = 90°$), $\sin(\phi) = 1$, giving **maximum torque**.
At the **end-strokes** ($\phi = 0°$ or $180°$), $\sin(\phi) = 0$, giving **zero torque**.

This is a **mechanical disadvantage** of the scotch-yoke design — torque is **non-uniform** and drops to zero at the limits of rotation. However, the scotch-yoke is **self-locking** at the end positions because any external torque cannot back-drive the piston (the slot is purely radial at the extremes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap (KTU):** A common mistake is to use $T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m$ for scotch-yoke motors as if they were rack-and-pinion units. Always check the geometry — **rack-and-pinion is constant torque; scotch-yoke is sinusoidal torque**.

---

### 2.3 Type 3 — Vane-Type Limited Rotation Motor

**Construction:** A cylindrical rotor (with multiple sliding vanes) is mounted **eccentrically** inside a circular stator bore. The rotor is keyed to the output shaft. Each vane is spring-loaded (and often pressure-loaded) against the stator bore to maintain sealing contact.

**Working Principle:**
- Two ports (A and B) admit oil to opposite sides of the rotor.
- Oil pressure acts on the **projected vane area**, creating a net tangential force.
- The eccentricity $e$ between rotor and stator centers produces the torque arm.

**Vane Area Projected on the Tangential Plane:**
For a vane of axial length $b$ and radial extent from rotor radius $r$ to stator radius $R_s$:

$$A_{vane} = b \cdot (R_s - r)$$

**Torque Equation (Theoretical, single vane, eccentricity-based):**
The pressure acts over a moment arm that varies linearly from $e$ (at one port) to zero (at the other port). The average moment arm is $e/2$, leading to the classical vane motor formula:

$$T_{th} = P \cdot b \cdot (R_s^2 - r^2) \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

For multi-vane rotors (n vanes), the torque summation yields:

$$\boxed{T = P \cdot b \cdot (R_s^2 - r^2) \cdot \frac{n}{2} \cdot \eta_m}$$

**Characteristic:** Vane-type limited rotation motors offer **smooth torque delivery** (no abrupt zero crossings) and are commonly used for **lower-pressure, high-speed oscillation** applications.

---

### 2.4 Type 4 — Piston-Type Limited Rotation Motor

**Construction:** Two or more pistons are arranged in **radial bores** in a rotor block. The pistons are connected to the output shaft via a common swashplate or via a crank mechanism. The rotor is mounted eccentrically to the shaft.

**Working Principle:**
- Oil admitted through the shaft centerline enters the piston chambers.
- Pistons are forced outward, contacting a stationary cam ring (or the inner bore of the stator).
- The reaction force rotates the rotor relative to the cam.

**Torque Equation:**
For a single piston of effective area $A$ acting at eccentricity $e$:

$$T_{th} = P \cdot A \cdot e$$

For $n$ pistons, summed at their average positions:

$$\boxed{T = P \cdot A \cdot e \cdot n \cdot \eta_m}$$

**Typical Use:** Heavy-duty applications requiring **high torque in compact packages** (e.g., excavator swing drives, crane slewing rings).

---

### 2.5 Universal Output Power Equation (All Types)

Once torque $T$ and rotational speed $N$ are known, the mechanical power output is computed as:

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60} \quad \text{[watts, when } T \text{ in N·m and } N \text{ in rpm]}$$

Or equivalently:

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega \quad \text{[watts, when } \omega \text{ in rad/s]}$$

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

| Motor Type | Torque Equation | Angular Displacement | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rack-and-Pinion** | $T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m$ | $\theta = L / R$ | **Constant** torque throughout stroke |
| **Scotch-Yoke** | $T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \sin(\phi) \cdot \eta_m$ | $\theta = L / R$ (max at mid-stroke) | **Sinusoidal** torque, self-locking at ends |
| **Vane-Type** | $T = P \cdot b \cdot (R_s^2 - r^2) \cdot \dfrac{n}{2} \cdot \eta_m$ | $\theta \le 360°$ (eccentric design) | Smooth torque, multi-vane summation |
| **Piston-Type** | $T = P \cdot A \cdot e \cdot n \cdot \eta_m$ | Depends on cam profile | High torque density, heavy duty |

**Universal Constants to Memorize:**

$$1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 10^5\ \text{N/m}^2$$

$$1\ \text{rpm} = \frac{2\pi}{60}\ \text{rad/s} = \frac{\pi}{30}\ \text{rad/s} \approx 0.1047\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\text{Power (W)} = \text{Torque (N·m)} \times \omega\ (\text{rad/s})$$

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern **Industry 4.0** factories, LRHMs are the **workhorses of robotic welding cells**. A typical automotive body shop contains 200–400 limited rotation motors, each precisely indexing weld guns through 90°–180° arcs at cycle times of 60–90 seconds. Their **stiffness** (resistance to deflection under load) and **repeatability** (typically ±0.05°) make them irreplaceable in high-throughput manufacturing.

In **defense engineering**, LRHMs drive the **stabilized gun turrets** on main battle tanks, where they must hold position under vehicle vibration and recoil shock while consuming minimal hydraulic power.

> [!TIP]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb (Engineer's Quick Reference):**
> - Need **constant torque**? → Choose **Rack-and-Pinion**.
> - Need **self-locking at extremes**? → Choose **Scotch-Yoke**.
> - Need **smooth low-pressure oscillation**? → Choose **Vane-Type**.
> - Need **maximum torque density**? → Choose **Piston-Type**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives the governing equations from **first principles** using force/pressure-area relationships, and then works through a **complete KTU-style numerical problem** for each major type.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torque of a Rack-and-Pinion Limited Rotation Motor

**Starting Point — Newton's Second Law for Fluid Pressure:**
The pressure $P$ acting on a piston of cross-sectional area $A$ generates a linear force:

$$F = P \cdot A$$

**Step 1 — Convert Linear Force to Rotary Torque:**
The rack is tangent to the pinion at the pitch radius $R$. Therefore, the linear force $F$ acting at the pitch circle produces a torque about the pinion axis:

$$T_{th} = F \cdot R = P \cdot A \cdot R$$

**Step 2 — Apply Mechanical Efficiency:**
The actual useful torque delivered to the output shaft is reduced by friction in the seals, bearings, and gear mesh:

$$T = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m$$

**Step 3 — Angular Displacement from Stroke Length:**
Since the rack moves a linear distance $L$ equal to the piston stroke, and the pinion circumference at pitch radius is $2\pi R$ for $2\pi$ radians:

$$\theta = \frac{\text{Arc length}}{\text{Radius}} = \frac{L}{R}\ \text{radians}$$

**Step 4 — Final Consolidated Result:**

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{L}{R} \quad \text{and} \quad T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque of a Scotch-Yoke Limited Rotation Motor

**Starting Point — Geometry of the Slot:**
The yoke slot is cut at a radial distance $R$ from the shaft axis. The driving pin (attached to the piston) is located at polar angle $\phi$ from a reference axis.

**Step 1 — Resolve the Linear Force into Tangential and Radial Components:**
The piston force $F = P \cdot A$ acts along the piston axis. Its component **perpendicular to the slot centerline** (i.e., tangential to the rotation circle of radius $R$) is:

$$F_t = F \cdot \sin(\phi) = P \cdot A \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

The component **along the slot centerline** (radial) only causes the pin to slide in the slot and produces **no torque**.

**Step 2 — Compute Torque from the Tangential Force at Radius R:**

$$T_{th} = F_t \cdot R = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\boxed{T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \sin(\phi) \cdot \eta_m}$$

**Step 4 — Identify Maximum and Minimum Torque Points:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m & \text{at}\ \phi &= 90°\ (\text{mid-stroke}) \\
T_{min} &= 0 & \text{at}\ \phi &= 0°\ \text{or}\ 180°\ (\text{end-stroke})
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torque of a Vane-Type Limited Rotation Motor

**Starting Point — Pressure Force on a Single Vane:**
Consider a vane of axial length $b$ and radial extent $R_s - r$, where $R_s$ is the stator radius and $r$ is the rotor radius. The pressure $P$ acts on the projected area $A = b \times (R_s - r)$.

**Step 1 — Net Force on the Vane in the Tangential Direction:**
The pressure acts uniformly, but only the component perpendicular to the rotor radius contributes to torque. Integrating over the vane's radial extent:

$$F_{tangential} = P \cdot b \cdot \int_r^{R_s} dx = P \cdot b \cdot (R_s - r)$$

**Step 2 — Moment Arm (Average Eccentric Effect):**
For an eccentric rotor design, the effective moment arm averages to the eccentricity $e = R_s - r$ (the full radial difference). The torque contribution per vane, integrated over the rotation, yields:

$$T_{th,vane} = P \cdot b \cdot \frac{(R_s^2 - r^2)}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Sum Over All Vanes:**
For $n$ vanes acting in parallel:

$$T_{th} = n \cdot P \cdot b \cdot \frac{(R_s^2 - r^2)}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\boxed{T = \frac{n \cdot P \cdot b \cdot (R_s^2 - r^2) \cdot \eta_m}{2}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Output from Torque and Speed

**Step 1 — Angular Velocity Conversion:**
For a shaft rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Power is the Product of Torque and Angular Velocity:**

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60}\ \text{watts}\ \text{(when } T \text{ in N·m, } N \text{ in rpm)}}$$

---

### 3.5 Numerical Problem 1 — Rack-and-Pinion LRHM [KTU-Style]

> **Problem:** A rack-and-pinion limited rotation hydraulic motor has a piston diameter of **60 mm**, a pinion pitch radius of **40 mm**, and operates at a supply pressure of **120 bar**. The piston stroke is **120 mm**. If the mechanical efficiency is **0.90**, calculate:
> (a) The maximum theoretical torque.
> (b) The actual useful torque.
> (c) The angular displacement of the output shaft.
> (d) The output power when running at **15 rpm**.

**Given Data:**
- $d = 60\ \text{mm} = 0.06\ \text{m}$
- $R = 40\ \text{mm} = 0.04\ \text{m}$
- $P = 120\ \text{bar} = 120 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$
- $L = 120\ \text{mm} = 0.12\ \text{m}$
- $\eta_m = 0.90$
- $N = 15\ \text{rpm}$

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theoretical Torque:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.06)^2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$T_{th} = P \cdot A \cdot R = (120 \times 10^5) \cdot (2.827 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.04)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{th} &= 1.2 \times 10^7 \cdot 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.04 \\
&= 1.2 \times 10^7 \cdot 1.1309 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 1357.1\ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{T_{th} \approx 1357\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Part (b) — Actual Useful Torque:**

$$T = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = 1357.1 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 1221.4\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Part (c) — Angular Displacement:**

$$\theta = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{0.12}{0.04} = 3.0\ \text{rad}$$

Converting to degrees: $\theta = 3.0 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 171.9°$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 3.0\ \text{rad} \approx 172°}$$

**Part (d) — Output Power:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot 15}{60} = \frac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 1221.4 \times 1.5708$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} \approx 1918.5\ \text{W} \approx 1.92\ \text{kW}}$$

---

### 3.6 Numerical Problem 2 — Scotch-Yoke LRHM [KTU-Style]

> **Problem:** A scotch-yoke limited rotation motor has a piston of diameter **50 mm**, yoke radius **30 mm**, and operates at **100 bar**. Determine the torque:
> (a) At the mid-stroke position.
> (b) At a pin angle of $\phi = 30°$ from the end-stroke.
> Assume $\eta_m = 0.88$.

**Given Data:**
- $d = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $R = 0.03\ \text{m}$, $P = 100 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$, $\eta_m = 0.88$

**Solution:**

**Piston Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Piston Force:**

$$F = P \cdot A = (100 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) = 19635\ \text{N}$$

**Part (a) — Torque at Mid-Stroke ($\phi = 90°$):**

$$T_a = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \sin(90°) \cdot \eta_m = 19635 \times 0.03 \times 1 \times 0.88$$

$$\boxed{T_a = 518.4\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Part (b) — Torque at $\phi = 30°$ from End:**

$$T_b = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \sin(30°) \cdot \eta_m = 19635 \times 0.03 \times 0.5 \times 0.88$$

$$\boxed{T_b = 259.2\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Analytical Observation:** Torque at 30° from end is **exactly half** of the mid-stroke torque — a direct consequence of the sine relationship, which is highly examinable.

---

### 3.7 Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Motor

| Selection Criterion | Rack-and-Pinion | Scotch-Yoke | Vane-Type | Piston-Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Torque profile** | Constant | Sinusoidal | Smooth | Pulsating (multi-piston) |
| **Self-locking at ends** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Max pressure rating** | Up to 350 bar | Up to 250 bar | Up to 175 bar | Up to 450 bar |
| **Typical angular range** | 90°–270° | 90°–180° | Up to 360° | 90°–270° |
| **Best for** | Welding guns | Valve actuators | Indexing tables | Excavator slewing |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low | Low | High |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Limited Rotation Hydraulic Motor System

The diagram below shows the complete **hydraulic circuit** of a typical limited rotation motor application, including supply, control, and feedback elements.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RES[RESERVOIR] --> PUMP[PUMP UNIT]
    PUMP --> FILT[FILTER ASSEMBLY]
    FILT --> RELIEF[RELIEF VALVE SET]
    RELIEF --> DCV[4 BY 3 DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE]
    DCV --> LRHM[LIMITED ROTATION HYDRAULIC MOTOR]
    LRHM --> LOAD[ROTARY MECHANICAL LOAD]
    DCV --> TANK1[RETURN TO TANK]
    RELIEF --> TANK2[RELIEF TO TANK]
    LRHM -.->|Angle Feedback| SENSOR[ROTARY POSITION SENSOR]
    SENSOR -.-> CTRL[CONTROLLER UNIT]
    CTRL -.->|PWM Signal| DCV
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **RES** (reservoir) supplies hydraulic fluid to the **PUMP**.
- The **FILT** (filter) removes contaminants to protect the LRHM.
- The **RELIEF** valve sets the maximum system pressure.
- The **DCV** (4/3 directional control valve) routes oil to either side of the LRHM, controlling rotation direction.
- The **LRHM** drives the **LOAD** (e.g., weld gun, valve disc).
- A **SENSOR** measures the rotation angle and feeds back to a **CTRL** (controller) for closed-loop position control.

---

### 4.2 Internal Architecture — Rack-and-Pinion LRHM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HOUSING[MOTOR HOUSING] --> PISTON[DOUBLE ACTING PISTON]
    PISTON --> RACK[TOOTHED RACK]
    RACK -.->|Mesh| PINION[PINION GEAR]
    PINION --> SHAFT[OUTPUT SHAFT]
    HOUSING --> STOP1[ADJUSTABLE STOP 1]
    HOUSING --> STOP2[ADJUSTABLE STOP 2]
    PORTA[PRESSURE PORT A] --> PISTON
    PORTB[PRESSURE PORT B] --> PISTON
```

---

### 4.3 Internal Architecture — Scotch-Yoke LRHM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HOUSING2[MOTOR HOUSING] --> PISTON2[PISTON WITH DRIVE PIN]
    PISTON2 --> SLOT[SLOT IN OUTPUT DISC]
    SLOT --> DISC[OUTPUT DISC]
    DISC --> SHAFT2[OUTPUT SHAFT]
    PORTA2[PORT A] --> PISTON2
    PORTB2[PORT B] --> PISTON2
    HOUSING2 --> END1[END STOP 1]
    HOUSING2 --> END2[END STOP 2]
```

**Working Read:**
- Oil entering **PORT A** pushes the piston → the pin slides in the slot → the disc rotates clockwise until it hits **END STOP 1**.
- Oil entering **PORT B** reverses the motion → disc rotates counter-clockwise to **END STOP 2**.

---

### 4.4 Comparative Torque Profiles Across the Stroke

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Torque vs. Angular Position for Different LRHM Types"
    x-axis "Angular Position (degrees)" [0, 45, 90, 135, 180]
    y-axis "Torque (N.m)" 0 --> 1200
    line "Rack-and-Pinion (Constant)" [1100, 1100, 1100, 1100, 1100]
    line "Scotch-Yoke (Sinusoidal)" [0, 778, 1100, 778, 0]
    line "Vane-Type (Smooth Curve)" [950, 1080, 1100, 1080, 950]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Scotch-Yoke** torque curve passes through **zero at both ends** of the stroke — this is why the mechanism is *self-locking* at the extremes (any external torque trying to back-drive the motor is resisted by the geometry of the slot). The **Rack-and-Pinion** delivers **uniform torque** throughout, making it the preferred choice for welding and pressing applications.
