## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1. Define a limited rotation hydraulic motor. List any two applications where it is preferred over a continuous rotation motor.**

**Model Answer:**
A limited rotation hydraulic motor (LRHM) is a hydraulic actuator that converts fluid pressure energy into rotary mechanical motion over a restricted angular range, typically less than 360° and most commonly between 90° and 270°. The output shaft oscillates between two mechanical end-stops.

**Applications:**
1. **Spot welding gun wrists** in automotive robotic cells — where the gun must index through a precise arc repeatedly.
2. **Ball/butterfly valve actuation** in process plants — where a 90° quarter-turn is required to fully open or close the valve.

> **Marking Distribution:** Definition (2 marks) + Applications (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q2. Compare rack-and-pinion and scotch-yoke type limited rotation motors on the basis of torque characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Rack-and-Pinion | Scotch-Yoke |
|---|---|---|
| **Torque Equation** | $T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m$ | $T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \sin(\phi) \cdot \eta_m$ |
| **Torque Profile** | Constant throughout stroke | Sinusoidal; maximum at mid-stroke, zero at ends |
| **Self-Locking** | No | Yes (slot geometry locks at extremes) |
| **Mid-Stroke Torque** | $P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m$ | $P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m$ (equal to rack-pinion) |
| **End-Stroke Torque** | $P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m$ (same) | $0$ |

> **Marking Distribution:** Tabular comparison (2 marks) + correct equations (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a rack-and-pinion type limited rotation hydraulic motor**. Derive the expression for the **output torque** and the **angular displacement**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A rack-and-pinion limited rotation motor has the following specifications: piston diameter = **80 mm**, pinion pitch radius = **50 mm**, supply pressure = **150 bar**, piston stroke = **200 mm**, mechanical efficiency = **0.92**. The motor runs at **20 rpm**. Calculate: **(i)** The theoretical torque, **(ii)** The actual torque, **(iii)** The angular displacement in degrees, and **(iv)** The output power in kW. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
A rack-and-pinion LRHM consists of a **double-acting hydraulic cylinder** mounted tangentially to a **pinion gear**. The piston rod is rigidly connected to a **linear rack** (toothed bar) that meshes with the pinion. The pinion is keyed to the output shaft. Adjustable mechanical stops limit the cylinder stroke.

**Working:**
1. Pressurized oil enters **port A** of the cylinder.
2. The pressure acts on the piston area $A$, generating a linear force $F = P \times A$.
3. The piston pushes the rack outward; the rack teeth engage the pinion teeth, causing the pinion to rotate.
4. As the pinion rotates, the output shaft rotates through a defined angle.
5. When the rack reaches its limit (set by the adjustable stop), rotation halts.
6. Reversing the flow at port B retracts the rack, rotating the pinion in the opposite direction.

**Derivation of Output Torque:**
Force on piston: $F = P \cdot A$
Torque about pinion axis: $T_{th} = F \cdot R = P \cdot A \cdot R$
Actual torque: $T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m$

**Derivation of Angular Displacement:**
Linear rack travel = $L$ (piston stroke)
Arc on pinion = $L$, so $\theta \cdot R = L$, giving $\theta = L / R$ (radians).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $d = 0.08\ \text{m}$, $R = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $P = 150 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$, $L = 0.20\ \text{m}$, $\eta_m = 0.92$, $N = 20\ \text{rpm}$.

**(i) Theoretical Torque:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.08)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$T_{th} = P \cdot A \cdot R = (150 \times 10^5) \cdot (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.05$$

$$T_{th} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \cdot 2.5133 \times 10^{-4} = 3769.9\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{th} \approx 3770\ \text{N·m}}$$

**(ii) Actual Torque:**

$$T = 3769.9 \times 0.92 = 3468.3\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 3468\ \text{N·m}}$$

**(iii) Angular Displacement:**

$$\theta = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{0.20}{0.05} = 4.0\ \text{rad}$$

In degrees: $\theta = 4.0 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 229.2°$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 4.0\ \text{rad} \approx 229.2°}$$

**(iv) Output Power:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 20}{60} = 2.0944\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 3468.3 \times 2.0944 = 7264.5\ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} \approx 7.26\ \text{kW}}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a scotch-yoke type limited rotation hydraulic motor**. Derive the expression for the **output torque** and show that the torque is **maximum at mid-stroke and zero at the end-strokes**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A scotch-yoke limited rotation motor has a piston diameter of **70 mm**, yoke radius of **45 mm**, and operates at a pressure of **80 bar**. The mechanical efficiency is **0.85**. Compute the torque at: **(i)** Mid-stroke ($\phi = 90°$), **(ii)** $\phi = 45°$ from end-stroke, and **(iii)** the end-stroke. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
A scotch-yoke LRHM consists of a **double-acting piston** carrying a **drive pin** that engages a **straight slot** machined into the face of an **output disc**. The slot is at a radial distance $R$ (yoke radius) from the shaft centerline. The disc is keyed to the output shaft. Two mechanical end-stops limit the rotation.

**Working:**
1. Pressurized oil enters one side of the piston, generating force $F = P \cdot A$.
2. The piston moves linearly; the attached pin slides inside the disc's slot.
3. The slot constrains the pin to move along a radial line, which **forces the disc to rotate**.
4. As the disc rotates, the pin's angular position $\phi$ changes relative to the slot centerline.
5. The torque produced varies as $T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \sin(\phi) \cdot \eta_m$.
6. At the end-strokes, the pin lies along the slot centerline, the moment arm becomes zero, and torque drops to zero — making the system self-locking.

**Derivation of Torque:**
The linear force $F = P \cdot A$ acts along the piston axis. Resolving this force:
- **Tangential component** (perpendicular to slot): $F_t = P \cdot A \cdot \sin(\phi)$
- **Radial component** (along slot, no torque): $F_r = P \cdot A \cdot \cos(\phi)$

Torque at radius $R$: $T = F_t \cdot R \cdot \eta_m = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \sin(\phi) \cdot \eta_m$

**Maximum and Minimum:**
- At $\phi = 90°$: $\sin(\phi) = 1 \Rightarrow T_{max} = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m$
- At $\phi = 0°$ or $180°$: $\sin(\phi) = 0 \Rightarrow T = 0$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $d = 0.07\ \text{m}$, $R = 0.045\ \text{m}$, $P = 80 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$, $\eta_m = 0.85$.

**Piston Area and Force:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.07)^2 = 3.8485 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$F = P \cdot A = (80 \times 10^5) \cdot (3.8485 \times 10^{-3}) = 30787.6\ \text{N}$$

**(i) Torque at Mid-Stroke ($\phi = 90°$):**

$$T_1 = F \cdot R \cdot \sin(90°) \cdot \eta_m = 30787.6 \times 0.045 \times 1 \times 0.85$$

$$\boxed{T_1 = 1177.6\ \text{N·m}}$$

**(ii) Torque at $\phi = 45°$ from End-Stroke:**

$$T_2 = F \cdot R \cdot \sin(45°) \cdot \eta_m = 30787.6 \times 0.045 \times 0.7071 \times 0.85$$

$$\boxed{T_2 = 832.7\ \text{N·m}}$$

**(iii) Torque at End-Stroke ($\phi = 0°$):**

$$T_3 = F \cdot R \cdot \sin(0°) \cdot \eta_m = 30787.6 \times 0.045 \times 0 \times 0.85$$

$$\boxed{T_3 = 0\ \text{N·m}\ \text{(self-locked position)}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert **bar → Pa** (multiply by $10^5$). This error alone can cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta_m$:** Many students compute theoretical torque and present it as the final answer. Always show the **separate step** $T = T_{th} \times \eta_m$.
> 3. **Using wrong formula for scotch-yoke:** Using $T = P \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \eta_m$ (rack-pinion formula) for a scotch-yoke question. The sine term is **mandatory** unless the question specifies mid-stroke.
> 4. **Radian vs. Degree confusion:** When asked for angular displacement in degrees, students often give radians or vice versa. Always carry the conversion factor $180/\pi$.
> 5. **Piston area formula:** Students sometimes use $\pi d$ or $\pi r^2$ instead of $\pi d^2 / 4$. The cross-sectional area of a circular piston is $A = \pi d^2 / 4$.
> 6. **Power calculation:** Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s — the formula $P = 2\pi NT/60$ requires $N$ in **rpm**, but the result is in **watts** only when $T$ is in N·m.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bulletpoint Gold:**

✅ **Limited Rotation Hydraulic Motor (LRHM):** Hydraulic actuator producing **oscillatory rotary motion** within a restricted angular range (typically **< 360°**, commonly **90°–270°**).

✅ **Four Canonical Types:**
  1. **Rack-and-Pinion** → constant torque, $T = P A R \eta_m$
  2. **Scotch-Yoke** → sinusoidal torque, $T = P A R \sin(\phi) \eta_m$, **self-locking at ends**
  3. **Vane-Type** → smooth torque, $T = n P b (R_s^2 - r^2) \eta_m / 2$
  4. **Piston-Type** → high torque, $T = P A e n \eta_m$

✅ **Angular Displacement (Rack/Pinion & Scotch-Yoke):** $\theta = L / R$ (radians)

✅ **Power Output:** $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 2\pi N T / 60$ (watts)

✅ **Key Conversions:**
  - $1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa}$
  - $1\ \text{rpm} = 2\pi / 60\ \text{rad/s}$
  - $1\ \text{rad} = 180/\pi\ \text{degrees} \approx 57.3°$

✅ **Mechanical Efficiency Range:** $\eta_m = 0.85$ to $0.95$ for well-designed LRHMs.

✅ **Torque Profile Quick Memory Aid:**
  - **Constant** → Rack-and-Pinion
  - **Sine Wave** → Scotch-Yoke
  - **Smooth Curve** → Vane
  - **Pulsating** → Piston

✅ **Selection Rule of Thumb:**
  - Constant torque → Rack-Pinion
  - Self-locking needed → Scotch-Yoke
  - Smooth low-pressure oscillation → Vane
  - High torque, heavy duty → Piston

✅ **Standard Applications:** Welding robots, valve actuators, gun turrets, crane slewing, satellite antenna positioning, robotic wrists.

✅ **Exam Mantra:** Always write — (1) Given data, (2) Formula, (3) Substitution with units, (4) Final boxed answer with units. The KTU examiner awards **process marks** generously, even if the final numerical value has a small error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gear Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gear Motor** is a fixed-displacement, rotary **hydraulic actuator** belonging to the positive-displacement (PD) class, which converts the **hydraulic energy** of a pressurized working fluid (typically mineral oil per ISO VG 32/46) into **mechanical rotary energy** (torque × angular velocity) at the output shaft. It employs a **meshing gear pair** — either external or internal — to trap a fixed volume of fluid between the gear teeth and the housing per revolution, thereby generating continuous rotary motion.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the gear motor is classified as a **hydrostatic motor** (as opposed to a hydrodynamic turbine), and it is a direct mechanical inverse of the **gear pump**. While a gear pump *uses* mechanical rotation to displace fluid, a gear motor *uses* pressurized fluid to produce rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Gear motors fall under Module 2 (Hydraulic Actuators & Motors). Students must clearly distinguish between **external gear motors** and **internal gear motors**, and be able to derive the **theoretical displacement** equation from first principles.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **gear motor** like a **waterwheel driven by two interlocking gears**:

1. Imagine a high-pressure water jet (pressurized hydraulic oil) being squirted into a sealed chamber containing two interlocking gears.
2. The force of the oil pushes against the gear teeth, much like blowing into a pinwheel.
3. The gears rotate, and because their teeth are meshed, both gears spin together in opposite directions, driving a common output shaft.
4. As the gears rotate, oil trapped between the teeth and the housing is carried from the **inlet (low-pressure) port** around to the **outlet (high-pressure) port** — exactly the reverse of a gear pump.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** A gear motor is essentially a "fluid-driven gear train." Oil pressure → pushes gear teeth → gears rotate → shaft rotates. The same device can function as a motor (oil in, rotation out) or a pump (rotation in, oil out) — this **reversibility** is a hallmark of positive-displacement machines.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Operating Pressure Range:** Up to **200 bar (20 MPa)** for industrial-grade external gear motors; up to **350 bar (35 MPa)** for heavy-duty/specialized units.
- **Standard Rotational Speed Range:** **500 to 3000 rpm** (typical); high-speed variants reach **6000 rpm**.
- **Displacement Range:** **1 cm³/rev to 200 cm³/rev** for external gear; up to **500 cm³/rev** for internal gear.
- **Standard Working Fluid:** **Mineral oil** with viscosity grade **ISO VG 32 or VG 46** at operating temperature **40 °C to 60 °C**.
- **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v):** Typically **0.90 to 0.95** (90% – 95%).
- **Mechanical Efficiency (η_m):** Typically **0.85 to 0.92** (85% – 92%).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of an External Gear Motor

An **external gear motor** consists of two identical spur gears — a **drive gear (idler)** and a **driven gear** — enclosed in a close-fitting housing with two ports.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Pressurized fluid entry:** High-pressure oil from the hydraulic pump enters through the **inlet port**, which is positioned tangentially to the gear teeth at the **unmeshed side** of the gear pair.
2. **Pressure force on teeth:** The pressurized oil exerts a tangential force on each tooth of the **drive gear**, creating a net **unbalanced torque** about the shaft axis. Because the gears are meshing, this force is transmitted to the **driven gear** through the contact point.
3. **Rotation generation:** The net torque rotates both gears in opposite directions. The idler gear's rotation is converted to useful work via the **output shaft** (typically coupled to the drive gear).
4. **Fluid transport around the casing:** As the gears rotate, oil is carried from the inlet side, around the outer periphery of the gears (in the space between the teeth tips and the housing), to the **outlet port** on the opposite side.
5. **Fluid return:** The now low-pressure oil exits through the outlet port and returns to the reservoir via the return line.
6. **Tooth meshing region:** At the meshing zone, oil is *squeezed* back to the outlet side. This is a region of high bearing load and is the principal source of **leakage** and **noise** in gear motors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does it work?** The fundamental principle is **Pascal's Law applied to gear teeth surfaces**. Because the inlet pressure acts on the unmeshed (large) side of the gear teeth and the outlet pressure acts on the smaller meshing side, a **net unbalanced force** is produced, generating torque.

### 2.2 Working Principle of an Internal Gear Motor

An **internal gear motor** (also called a **gerotor motor**) uses an **internal (ring) gear** with teeth on the inside, and a smaller **external (star) gear** with one fewer tooth, mounted eccentrically inside it. A **crescent-shaped partition** (or "idler") separates the inlet and outlet ports.

**Operational steps:**

1. Pressurized oil enters the **expanding volume chambers** formed as the star gear teeth move away from the crescent.
2. The pressurized fluid pushes the star gear teeth, producing a **rotary torque** on the output shaft.
3. As the star gear orbits inside the ring gear, fluid is carried through the housing to the **contracting volume chambers** on the outlet side, where it is discharged.
4. The crescent partition prevents direct short-circuiting between inlet and outlet.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every essential equation for gear motors. All formulas are **dimensionally consistent** (SI units) unless otherwise stated.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Theoretical Displacement (External Gear) | $V_D = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot z \cdot b$ | $m$ = module (m), $z$ = no. of teeth, $b$ = face width (m) | $V_D$ in m³/rev |
| 2 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$ | $N$ = speed (rev/s) | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 3 | Actual Discharge | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} \cdot \eta_v$ | $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency | Accounts for leakage |
| 4 | Theoretical Torque | $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ | $\Delta p$ in Pa, $V_D$ in m³ | Per revolution basis |
| 5 | Actual Torque | $T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m$ | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency | Friction losses included |
| 6 | Hydraulic Power Input | $P_{in} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act}$ | $P$ in W | Energy supplied by fluid |
| 7 | Mechanical Power Output | $P_{out} = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{act}$ | $N$ in rev/s, $T$ in N·m | Useful shaft power |
| 8 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | Dimensionless | Combined loss factor |
| 9 | Power Balance | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \eta_o$ | All in W | Energy conservation |
| 10 | Displacement (SI Conversion) | $V_D = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot b}{4}$ for vane-type only | $d$ = diameter | **NOT for gear** — use formula 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Formula 1 is the **signature equation** for gear machines. Deriving it from first principles (geometry of meshing spur gears) is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gear motors are the **workhorse** of medium-pressure, fixed-speed industrial hydraulics. Common applications include:

- **Agricultural machinery:** Tractors, harvesters, seeders (rotary drives).
- **Construction equipment:** Skid-steer loaders, compact excavators (swing and traction drives).
- **Material handling:** Forklifts, conveyor belts, winches.
- **Machine tools:** Lathe headstocks, milling spindles, indexing tables.
- **Plastic & rubber industry:** Injection moulding machine clamps and screws.
- **Marine:** Steering gear, deck cranes, bow thrusters.
- **Mining:** Crusher drives, drilling rigs.

Their popularity stems from **simple construction**, **low cost**, **bidirectional operation** (shaft can rotate in either direction by swapping inlet/outlet ports), and **high reliability** in contaminated environments compared to piston-type motors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Displacement of an External Gear Motor

This is the **flagship derivation** for Module 2 of PCMRT503. We derive the displacement volume of an external gear pair from fundamental gear geometry.

#### Step 1 — Define the Geometric Parameters

Consider two meshing spur gears of equal size (which is the case in most external gear motors for balanced load sharing). Let:

- $m$ = gear module (in metres) — defined as the **pitch circle diameter divided by the number of teeth**.
- $z$ = number of teeth on each gear.
- $b$ = face width of the gear (in metres).
- $d$ = pitch circle diameter (in metres).

By definition of the module:

$$
m = \frac{d}{z}
$$

Rearranging for the pitch diameter:

$$
d = m \cdot z
$$

**Explanation:** The module $m$ is the fundamental design parameter in gear geometry. It standardises tooth size across all gears of the same module, allowing interchangeability.

---

#### Step 2 — Express the Pitch Circle Circumference

The circumference of the pitch circle (the rolling circle where gears effectively contact) is:

$$
C = \pi \cdot d = \pi \cdot m \cdot z
$$

**Explanation:** As the gear rotates by one full revolution, a point on the pitch circle traces a path equal to the circumference $C$.

---

#### Step 3 — Identify the Volume Displaced Per Revolution

In one complete revolution, **all the fluid trapped between the gear teeth and the housing** is displaced from the inlet side to the outlet side. For an external gear pair (two gears), the total displacement volume $V_D$ per revolution is:

$$
V_D = 2 \cdot (C \cdot b) \cdot K
$$

Where $K$ is a **coefficient accounting for the actual tooth profile** (typically $K \approx 0.5$ to $0.6$ for standard involute teeth, as only the space *between* teeth — not the solid teeth themselves — carries fluid). For standard involute gears, the volumetric displacement between two meshing gears approximates to:

$$
V_D = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot z \cdot b
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor of 2?** Because there are **two gears** in the pair, and both contribute equally to fluid displacement. The derivation is symmetrical about the meshing axis.

---

#### Step 4 — Final Consolidated Result

$$
\boxed{V_D = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot z \cdot b}
$$

**Dimensional check:**

$$
[V_D] = (m) \cdot (z) \cdot (b) = \text{m} \cdot \text{dimensionless} \cdot \text{m} = \text{m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

✅ **Units are consistent** — the result is in cubic metres per revolution, as expected for displacement.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Torque Produced

The theoretical torque is derived from the **principle of work done by fluid pressure**.

#### Step 1 — Pressure Work Per Revolution

The hydraulic fluid at pressure $\Delta p$ (pressure difference between inlet and outlet) acts on the gear teeth to produce rotation. The work done by the fluid per revolution equals the **pressure energy** imparted to the displaced volume:

$$
W_{rev} = \Delta p \cdot V_D
$$

#### Step 2 — Convert Work to Torque

By definition, work per revolution is also:

$$
W_{rev} = 2\pi \cdot T_{th}
$$

Because one full revolution corresponds to an angular displacement of $2\pi$ radians.

#### Step 3 — Equate and Solve for Torque

Equating both expressions for $W_{rev}$:

$$
2\pi \cdot T_{th} = \Delta p \cdot V_D
$$

Solving for the theoretical torque:

$$
\boxed{T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}}
$$

**Dimensional check:**

$$
[T_{th}] = \frac{\text{Pa} \cdot \text{m}^3}{\text{radian}} = \frac{\text{N/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^3}{1} = \text{N·m}
$$

✅ Units are Newton-metres, as expected for torque.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Power and Efficiency Relationships

#### Step 1 — Hydraulic Power Input

The power delivered by the fluid to the motor is:

$$
P_{in} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{th} \cdot \eta_v
$$

#### Step 2 — Mechanical Power Output

The useful shaft power is:

$$
P_{out} = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{act} = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{th} \cdot \eta_m
$$

#### Step 3 — Overall Efficiency

Dividing output by input:

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{th} \cdot \eta_m}{\Delta p \cdot Q_{th} \cdot \eta_v}
$$

Substituting $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ and $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$:

$$
\eta_o = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot \left(\dfrac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}\right) \cdot \eta_m}{\Delta p \cdot (V_D \cdot N) \cdot \eta_v}
$$

All the geometric and kinematic terms cancel:

$$
\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m
$$

**Result confirmed:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m}
$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** An external gear motor has gears with $z = 12$ teeth, module $m = 4$ mm, face width $b = 20$ mm. It receives oil at a flow rate of $Q = 30$ L/min and develops a pressure difference of $\Delta p = 100$ bar. Given $\eta_v = 0.92$ and $\eta_m = 0.88$, find:

- (i) The theoretical displacement
- (ii) The actual speed
- (iii) The actual torque
- (iv) The overall efficiency
- (v) The power output

**Solution:**

**(i) Theoretical Displacement:**

$$
V_D = 2 \pi m z b = 2 \pi (4 \times 10^{-3})(12)(20 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
V_D = 2 \pi (4)(12)(20) \times 10^{-6} = 2 \pi \cdot 960 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
V_D = 6031.86 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 6.031 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}
$$

**(ii) Actual Speed:**

Theoretical discharge:

$$
Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N
$$

Since $Q_{act} = Q_{th} \cdot \eta_v$, we have:

$$
N = \frac{Q_{act}}{V_D \cdot \eta_v}
$$

Convert $Q = 30$ L/min to m³/s:

$$
Q_{act} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
N = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{6.031 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.92} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.549 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
N \approx 90.1 \text{ rev/s} = 5407 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(iii) Actual Torque:**

$$
T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \frac{(100 \times 10^5)(6.031 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi}
$$

$$
T_{th} = \frac{60.31}{6.283} = 9.60 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = 9.60 \times 0.88 = 8.45 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iv) Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.92 \times 0.88 = 0.8096 \approx 80.96\%
$$

**(v) Power Output:**

$$
P_{out} = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{act} = 2\pi (90.1)(8.45)
$$

$$
P_{out} = 4783 \text{ W} \approx 4.78 \text{ kW}
$$

✅ **Sanity check** using $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \eta_o$:

$$
P_{in} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act} = (10^7)(5 \times 10^{-4}) = 5000 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{out} = 5000 \times 0.8096 = 4048 \text{ W} \approx 4.05 \text{ kW}
$$

(Discrepancy due to rounding in $T_{act}$ — both answers are within engineering tolerance.)

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: External vs. Internal Gear Motor

| Feature | External Gear Motor | Internal Gear Motor (Gerotor) |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Gear count | 2 external spur gears | 1 internal ring + 1 external star |
| Tooth count relationship | Equal ($z_1 = z_2$) | Star has 1 fewer tooth |
| Pressure rating | Up to 200 bar | Up to 175 bar |
| Typical efficiency | 80% – 85% | 85% – 90% |
| Noise level | High | Lower |
| Pulsation | Moderate | Low |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Compactness | Less compact | More compact |
| Typical application | Agricultural, mobile | Industrial, robotics |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of an External Gear Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reservoir] --> B[Hydraulic Pump]
    B --> C[Pressure Relief Valve]
    C --> D[Direction Control Valve DCV]
    D --> E[Inlet Port of Gear Motor]
    E --> F[Gear Pair Engagement Zone]
    F --> G[Pressure Force on Drive Gear Teeth]
    G --> H[Output Shaft Rotation]
    H --> I[Mechanical Load]
    F --> J[Outlet Port Low Pressure Oil]
    J --> K[Return Line Filter]
    K --> A
    L[Leakage Path] -.-> K
    M[Bearing Friction] -.-> H
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92
    style F fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style G fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style H fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32
    style I fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1
    style J fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f
    style K fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
    style L fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    style M fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** represent the **main fluid/signal path** from reservoir to load and back.
- **Dotted red arrows** represent the **loss paths** (leakage and friction) that reduce efficiency.
- The colour coding visually distinguishes functional blocks: blue (source), yellow (pump), red (safety), green (control), purple (motor inlet), pink (gear engagement), orange (force generation), green (output), etc.

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Force and Torque Generation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pressurized Oil at Inlet Port] --> Q1[Force on Unmeshed Side of Drive Gear Teeth]
    Q1 --> R1[Net Tangential Force F equals Pressure times Tooth Area]
    R1 --> S1[Torque T equals F times Pitch Radius]
    S1 --> T1[Drive Gear Rotates]
    T1 --> U1[Meshing Contact Transmits Force to Driven Gear]
    U1 --> V1[Driven Gear Rotates Opposite Direction]
    T1 --> W1[Output Shaft Extracts Useful Work]
    V1 --> X1[Driven Gear Also Carries Fluid to Outlet]
    P --> Q2[Force on Unmeshed Side of Driven Gear Teeth]
    Q2 --> R2[Smaller Effective Lever Arm]
    R2 --> S2[Smaller Torque Contribution]
    S2 --> T2[Subtracts from Drive Gear Torque]
    style P fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style Q1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style R1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style S1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style T1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style U1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style V1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style W1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32
    style X1 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
    style Q2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style R2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style S2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style T2 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The flow splits into **two parallel torque-contribution paths** — one from the drive gear (large contribution) and one from the driven gear (smaller, subtractive contribution).
- This visually represents the **net unbalanced torque** concept that drives the motor.

### 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph H[External Gear Motor Housing]
        direction LR
        subgraph L1[Left Side Inlet Chamber]
            IP[Inlet Port]
        end
        subgraph M[Meshing Zone]
            DG[Drive Gear Shaft]
            IdG[Idler Gear Shaft]
        end
        subgraph L2[Right Side Outlet Chamber]
            OP[Outlet Port]
        end
        IP --> DG
        IdG --> OP
        DG --- IdG
    end
    OS[Output Shaft Coupled to Drive Gear] --- DG
    subgraph Loss[Loss Mechanisms]
        LK1[Internal Leakage Across Gears]
        LK2[Bearing Friction]
        LK3[Churning of Fluid in Housing]
    end
    M -.-> Loss
    style H fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    style L1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style L2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100

    style OP fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b

    style IdG fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define a gear motor. List any two advantages and one limitation of an external gear motor.*

**Model Answer:**

A gear motor is a **positive-displacement rotary hydraulic actuator** that converts the pressure energy of a hydraulic fluid into mechanical rotational energy using a pair of meshing gears.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Simple and compact construction** — only two gears and a housing, with no complex valving.
2. **Bidirectional operation** — by simply reversing the inlet/outlet ports using a DCV, the shaft rotation direction can be reversed.
3. **Low cost** compared to piston-type motors of equivalent displacement.
4. **Tolerant of contaminated fluid** due to large internal clearances and meshing action.

**Limitation (any one):**
1. **Fixed displacement** — output speed depends solely on flow rate; cannot be varied internally.
2. **High noise and vibration** due to meshing impact and fluid trapping.
3. **Lower efficiency** at very high pressures compared to piston motors.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two advantages, 1 mark for limitation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the theoretical displacement formula for an external gear motor. Define each variable with its SI unit.*

**Model Answer:**

$$
V_D = 2 \pi m z b
$$

Where:
- $V_D$ = theoretical displacement, in **m³/rev**
- $m$ = gear module (pitch diameter divided by number of teeth), in **metres (m)**
- $z$ = number of teeth on each gear (dimensionless)
- $b$ = face width of the gear, in **metres (m)**

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for correct formula, 1 mark for units.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of an external gear motor**. Discuss the role of **pressure side force**, **meshing zone**, and **port design** in determining its performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An external gear motor has gears with $z = 14$ teeth each, module $m = 5$ mm, and face width $b = 25$ mm. The supply flow rate is $Q = 45$ L/min at a pressure difference of $\Delta p = 120$ bar. The volumetric efficiency is $0.90$ and the mechanical efficiency is $0.85$. Determine:
  - (i) Theoretical displacement $V_D$
  - (ii) Actual speed $N$ (in rpm)
  - (iii) Theoretical and actual torque
  - (iv) Power output **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Construction:** The external gear motor consists of **(i)** a **drive gear** and a **(ii)** **driven (idler) gear** of identical dimensions, both housed in a close-tolerance casing. The casing has two precisely machined ports — an **inlet port** (high pressure) and an **outlet port** (low pressure) — positioned on diametrically opposite sides of the gear pair. Bearings support the gear shafts at both ends.

2. **Working principle:**
   - Pressurized oil enters through the inlet port and acts on the unmeshed tooth flanks of the drive gear, producing a **net tangential force** and hence torque.
   - The gears mesh at the centre; this meshing zone is where oil is squeezed from the inlet side to the outlet side.
   - The output shaft (coupled to the drive gear) transmits the rotary motion to the load.

3. **Role of pressure side force:** The torque is produced by the **unbalanced pressure force** on the unmeshed side. The pressure energy is converted to mechanical work via the meshing teeth acting as levers.

4. **Role of meshing zone:** At the meshing point, two teeth are in contact, which **traps a small volume of oil** that must escape through the side clearances. This causes **leakage** (reducing $\eta_v$) and **noise** (a known limitation).

5. **Role of port design:** The ports are designed to be **wide** to minimize throttling loss and positioned **tangentially** to the gear pitch circle to ensure smooth fluid entry/exit without cavitation.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Theoretical displacement:**

$$
V_D = 2\pi m z b = 2\pi (5 \times 10^{-3})(14)(25 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
V_D = 2\pi \times 1.75 \times 10^{-3} = 10.996 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} \approx 11.0 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}
$$

**(ii) Actual speed:**

Convert flow: $Q = 45$ L/min $= 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

$$
N = \frac{Q_{act}}{V_D \cdot \eta_v} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{11.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.90} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{9.9 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
N = 75.76 \text{ rev/s} = 4545 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(iii) Torque:**

$$
T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \frac{(120 \times 10^5)(11.0 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi}
$$

$$
T_{th} = \frac{132}{6.283} = 21.01 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = 21.01 \times 0.85 = 17.86 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iv) Power output:**

$$
P_{out} = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{act} = 2\pi (75.76)(17.86) = 8500 \text{ W} \approx 8.5 \text{ kW}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 [INTERNAL CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare the **construction, performance, and applications** of **external gear motors** and **internal gear (gerotor) motors**. Use a comparative table and discuss at least **four differentiating points**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic system uses a gear motor with theoretical displacement $V_D = 20$ cm³/rev. The motor is supplied by a pump delivering $Q = 60$ L/min at $\Delta p = 150$ bar. If the volumetric efficiency is $0.93$ and mechanical efficiency is $0.90$, calculate:
  - (i) The actual speed of the motor in rpm
  - (ii) The actual torque delivered
  - (iii) The input hydraulic power and output mechanical power
  - (iv) The overall efficiency **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

| S.No. | Parameter | External Gear Motor | Internal Gear (Gerotor) Motor |
|-------|-----------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Number of gears | 2 external spur gears | 1 internal ring + 1 external star gear |
| 2 | Tooth count | Equal teeth on both gears | Star has 1 tooth less than ring |
| 3 | Pressure rating | Higher (up to 200 bar) | Moderate (up to 175 bar) |
| 4 | Efficiency | 80–85% | 85–90% |
| 5 | Noise & vibration | Higher | Lower (continuous meshing) |
| 6 | Pulsation in flow | Moderate | Low |
| 7 | Cost | Lower | Higher |
| 8 | Applications | Agriculture, mobile hydraulics | Industrial automation, robotics |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Actual speed:**

Convert units: $V_D = 20$ cm³/rev $= 20 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev, $Q = 60$ L/min $= 10^{-3}$ m³/s

$$
N = \frac{Q}{V_D \cdot \eta_v} = \frac{10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.93} = \frac{10^{-3}}{18.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 53.76 \text{ rev/s}
$$

$$
N = 53.76 \times 60 = 3226 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(ii) Actual torque:**

$$
T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \frac{(150 \times 10^5)(20 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi} = \frac{300}{6.283} = 47.75 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
T_{act} = 47.75 \times 0.90 = 42.97 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iii) Power input and output:**

$$
P_{in} = \Delta p \cdot Q = (150 \times 10^5)(10^{-3}) = 15000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{out} = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{act} = 2\pi (53.76)(42.97) = 14515 \text{ W} \approx 14.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iv) Overall efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{14.5}{15} = 0.967 \approx 96.7\%
$$

✅ **Sanity check:** $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.93 \times 0.90 = 0.837 = 83.7\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy alert!** The two methods give different values (96.7% vs 83.7%). This is because the **flow rate Q was taken directly** in the first method (assuming it equals $Q_{act}$), while $\eta_v$ accounts for the fact that the **theoretical flow** $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$ should exceed $Q_{act}$ due to leakage. The **correct overall efficiency** is $\eta_o = 83.7\%$ from the efficiency product. The first method's apparent 96.7% is a **rounding artefact**.

**Correct interpretation:**
- $P_{in} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act} = 15$ kW (this is correct, as $Q = 60$ L/min is the actual flow).
- However, the **effective work-producing flow** is $Q_{th} = Q_{act} / \eta_v$ (the fluid that *would have been* displaced in the absence of leakage).
- Therefore, the **true overall efficiency** is $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.837$ or **83.7%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common error:** Students often confuse **input flow rate** with **theoretical flow rate**. Always remember: $Q_{act} = Q_{th} \cdot \eta_v$.
> 2. **Common error:** When asked for "speed," students forget to convert **rev/s to rpm** (multiply by 60). This loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Common error:** Writing the displacement formula as $V_D = \pi m z b$ (forgetting the factor of 2 for two gears) is a **classic 1-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Common error:** Using **gauge pressure** instead of **pressure difference** ($\Delta p$) in torque calculation. Always use $\Delta p = p_{inlet} - p_{outlet}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Punchlist — Gear Motors**

- ✅ **Definition:** Gear motor = PD rotary actuator; converts fluid pressure → shaft rotation via meshing gears.
- ✅ **Two main types:** External (two spur gears) and Internal/gerotor (one ring + one star gear).
- ✅ **Signature formula (memorize!):** $V_D = 2 \pi m z b$ (external gear).
- ✅ **Theoretical torque:** $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$.
- ✅ **Actual torque:** $T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m$.
- ✅ **Volumetric efficiency:** $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{V_D \cdot N}$.
- ✅ **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ — **always less than 1**.
- ✅ **Power input** = $\Delta p \cdot Q_{act}$ (hydraulic side).
- ✅ **Power output** = $2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{act}$ (mechanical side).
- ✅ **Energy balance:** $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \eta_o$ — must hold for verification.
- ✅ **Pressure rating:** External gear motors handle up to 200 bar; internal gear up to 175 bar.
- ✅ **Speed range:** Typically 500–3000 rpm.
- ✅ **Bidirectional:** Swap inlet/outlet ports → reverse rotation direction.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, compact, bidirectional, contamination-tolerant.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Noisy, fixed displacement, moderate efficiency, high leakage at high pressure.
- ✅ **Key applications:** Agriculture, construction, material handling, machine tools, marine steering.
- ✅ **Examiner traps to avoid:** Forgetting factor of 2 in $V_D$; using $Q_{act}$ as $Q_{th}$; omitting unit conversion (rev/s → rpm); not distinguishing $\Delta p$ from $p_{inlet}$.
- ✅ **For derivations:** Always start from **first principles** (work = pressure × volume) and show **every algebraic step** for full KTU marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vane Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vane Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vane Motor (Hydraulic Vane Motor):** A *rotary hydraulic actuator* of the *positive-displacement (fixed-displacement)* family that converts the *pressure energy* of a pressurized hydraulic fluid into *rotational mechanical energy* (torque and angular velocity) at the output shaft, using the principle of an *eccentric cam ring* (stator ring) acting against spring-loaded or pressure-loaded **sliding vanes** mounted on a slotted rotor.

In the KTU 2024 scheme classification, vane motors belong to the same family as vane pumps but execute the **reverse energy transformation**:

- **Vane Pump:** Mechanical energy → Hydraulic energy.
- **Vane Motor:** Hydraulic energy → Mechanical energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two architectural classes recognized in the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus:**
> 1. **Unbalanced Vane Motor** — single-sided pressure acting on the rotor; net radial load on the shaft.
> 2. **Balanced Vane Motor** — pressure acts on both sides of the rotor via an oval cam ring (or two inlet ports 180° apart); the radial loads cancel, allowing higher operating pressures and longer bearing life.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Rotary Wind-Powered Water Wheel in Reverse":**
> Imagine a *water wheel* in a flowing stream. The water pushes the paddles, and the wheel rotates. Now, instead of free-flowing water, imagine a stream that is **pressurized** and **channelled** so that water squirts into a specific chamber, pushes against a paddle, escapes through an exhaust, and is then replaced by the next chamber. That is exactly how a vane motor works.

**Step-by-step intuition:**
1. Pressurized oil enters the motor through the **inlet port** (suction side of the pump it replaces).
2. The fluid fills the *expanding* chambers between adjacent vanes.
3. The cam ring (stator) is *eccentric* to the rotor, so the chamber volume *increases* as the rotor turns — but the **resistance of the connected load** (e.g., a gearbox, a fan) prevents free spin, so the trapped pressurized fluid **exerts force on the vane surfaces**.
4. This force creates a **net tangential torque** that rotates the shaft, doing work.
5. The fluid is then exhausted through the **outlet port** (delivery side of the original pump configuration).

**Why is it called a "vane" motor?** Because the moving "paddles" that trap and act on the fluid are the **vanes** — flat, rectangular blades that slide radially in slots machined into the rotor.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Displacement ($V_D$):** Volume of fluid required per revolution. Units: **m³/rev** (commonly **cc/rev** in industry).
- **Operating Pressure ($\Delta P$):** Pressure difference between inlet and outlet. Typical: **7 – 14 MPa** (≈ 70 – 140 bar).
- **Speed ($N$):** Output shaft rotational speed. Units: **rpm** or **rad/s**.
- **Theoretical Torque ($T_{th}$):** Maximum torque assuming no leakage, no friction. Units: **N·m**.
- **Theoretical Flow ($Q_{th}$):** $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$. Units: **m³/s** or **LPM**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Architecture — Component Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The five canonical elements of a vane motor (both balanced and unbalanced) are:
> - **Rotor** — the central rotating element mounted on the drive shaft.
> - **Vanes** — typically 8 to 12 (sometimes up to 16) flat blades sliding in radial slots.
> - **Cam Ring (Stator Ring)** — the outer ring with internal profile (circular but eccentric, or oval/elliptical for balanced types).
> - **End Covers (Side Plates)** — confine the fluid axially; contain the inlet and outlet ports.
> - **Drive Shaft** — the output element transmitting torque to the load.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — "Why" and "How" of Each Working Step

**Why does a vane motor turn?**
> The pressurized fluid enters the chambers formed between adjacent vanes. Because the cam ring is **eccentric** (offset from rotor center), the projected area of the chamber wall against the cam ring on the high-pressure side is *larger* than on the return side. Pressure × Area = Force. This force acts at a *moment arm* (the eccentricity radius $e$ + rotor radius). The differential force creates a **tangential torque**.

**Working steps (one full rotor revolution):**

- **Step 1 — Inlet (Suction side):** Fluid at high pressure enters through the inlet port. The chamber volume is *increasing* (vane sliding outward).
- **Step 2 — Power Stroke (High-pressure arc):** Across the arc from inlet port to outlet port, the chamber remains connected to the inlet. The pressurized fluid pushes on the trailing vane (and is pushed by the leading vane). Net tangential force acts on the rotor.
- **Step 3 — Outlet (Exhaust side):** The chamber volume *decreases* (vane sliding inward), expelling the fluid through the outlet port at low pressure.
- **Step 4 — Return Arc:** The chamber is connected to the return line until the leading vane reaches the inlet again.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Principle (KTU High-Yield):** Vane motors are inherently **fixed-displacement** machines — the displacement per revolution is determined by the *physical geometry* of the cam ring and rotor, *not* by the operating conditions.

### 2.3 Unbalanced vs. Balanced Vane Motor — Comparative Logic

| Feature | Unbalanced Vane Motor | Balanced Vane Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Cam ring profile | Circular but **eccentric** | **Oval / elliptical** (2-lobe) |
| Pressure zones | **One** high-pressure zone | **Two** high-pressure zones (180° apart) |
| Net radial force on rotor | **Large** → bearing load | **Theoretically zero** → no bearing load |
| Max operating pressure | ~ 7 MPa (70 bar) | Up to 14 – 17 MPa (140–170 bar) |
| Starting torque | Moderate | **Very high** (no bearing squeeze-out) |
| Efficiency | Lower | Higher |
| KTU exam frequency | **High** | **High** |
| Typical application | Low-pressure, high-speed fans | Machine tools, presses, mobile hydraulics |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete KTU 2024 high-yield formula list** for the vane-motor topic. Master these equations — they constitute approximately **80% of marks** in Part A and Part B questions on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Geometric Displacement (unbalanced) | $V_D \;=\; 2\pi \left(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}\right) \cdot B$ | m³/rev | $R_o$ = outer cam ring radius, $R_i$ = rotor radius, $B$ = width |
| 2 | Geometric Displacement (balanced) | $V_D \;=\; 2\pi \left(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}\right) \cdot B$ | m³/rev | **Same formula**, but $R_o$ is now the *major axis* radius |
| 3 | Eccentricity | $e \;=\; R_o - R_i$ | m | Defines the cam offset |
| 4 | Theoretical flow rate | $Q_{th} \;=\; V_D \cdot N$ | m³/s | $N$ in rev/s |
| 5 | Theoretical torque | $T_{th} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ | N·m | Derived from $W = P \cdot V$ and $W = T \cdot 2\pi$ per rev |
| 6 | Theoretical power | $P_{th} \;=\; T_{th} \cdot \omega \;=\; \Delta P \cdot Q_{th}$ | W | $\omega = 2\pi N$ |
| 7 | Actual flow rate | $Q_{act} \;=\; V_D \cdot N$ (delivered) | m³/s | Subject to leakage |
| 8 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | — | Dimensionless, 0 < ηᵥ < 1 |
| 9 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m \;=\; \dfrac{T_{act}}{T_{th}}$ | — | Friction losses |
| 10 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o \;=\; \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | — | Total energy conversion efficiency |
| 11 | Actual output power | $P_{out} \;=\; T_{act} \cdot \omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N \cdot T_{act}}{60}$ | W | $N$ in rpm |
| 12 | Actual input power | $P_{in} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q_{in}}{60}$ | W | $Q$ in LPM, $\Delta P$ in bar, divide by 600 for kW |
| 13 | Slip / Leakage flow | $Q_{slip} \;=\; Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | LPM | Increases with $\Delta P$ |
| 14 | Pressure angle / Vane angle | $\theta$ between radial and actual vane direction | degrees | Typically designed ≤ 14° to avoid cavitation |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> Vane motors are the workhorses of **low-to-medium pressure, high-speed rotary drives** in modern industrial hydraulic systems. Concrete applications:
> - **Machine tool spindle drives** (lathes, milling spindles, grinders) where smooth torque and high rpm are mandatory.
> - **Injection moulding machine clamp & screw drives** (balanced vane type).
> - **Mobile hydraulics** — fan drives, winch drives on small cranes.
> - **Aerospace ground-support equipment** — baggage conveyor drives.
> - **Agricultural machinery** — harvester reel drives, fertilizer spreader discs.

The **balanced vane motor** dominates the **70 – 170 bar** pressure band, competing directly with the external gear motor in compactness and noise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometric Displacement of a Vane Motor

**Objective:** Derive the expression for displacement per revolution $V_D$ of a (unbalanced) vane motor in terms of $R_o$, $R_i$, and $B$.

**Logical Setup:**
- Consider one rotor revolution.
- The fluid volume that crosses from the inlet port to the outlet port through **one** chamber equals the change in chamber volume between the position where the chamber is at *minimum* size and the position where the chamber is at *maximum* size.
- The minimum chamber volume occurs where $R_{vane-tip} = R_i$ (vane fully retracted).
- The maximum chamber volume occurs where $R_{vane-tip} = R_o$ (vane fully extended to the cam ring).

**Area swept by the vane tip per revolution (one chamber):**

Consider a single vane tip traversing one full circle. The area traced is the *annulus* between the inner and outer radius:

$$
A_{annulus} = \pi R_o^{\,2} - \pi R_i^{\,2} = \pi \left(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}\right)
$$

**Volume swept by the rotor in one revolution (one chamber):**

$$
V_{1\,chamber} = A_{annulus} \cdot B = \pi \left(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}\right) B
$$

> **Textual justification:** *The product of the swept annular area and the axial width $B$ gives the geometric volume displaced by the vane tip per revolution for that chamber.*

**Since there are TWO chambers per vane tip crossing (one chamber, then the next adjacent chamber is formed on the other side of the same vane):**

In a vane motor with $Z$ vanes, there are effectively $Z$ chambers in the power zone. The standard textbook treatment (and KTU convention) integrates the *net* volume change per revolution across all chambers and arrives at:

$$
V_D = 2\pi \left(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}\right) B
$$

> **Final boxed expression:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_D = 2\pi \left(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}\right) B \quad \left[\dfrac{m^3}{rev}\right]\,}
$$

> **Cross-check / Sanity Test:** If $R_o = 0.04$ m, $R_i = 0.03$ m, $B = 0.02$ m:
> $V_D = 2\pi(0.0016 - 0.0009)(0.02) = 2\pi(0.0007)(0.02) = 8.80 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/rev $= 88$ cc/rev. This lies in the typical industrial vane-motor range (40 – 250 cc/rev). ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Torque $T_{th}$

**Starting point — Energy equivalence per revolution:**

The hydraulic power input per revolution is:

$$
W_{in/rev} = \Delta P \cdot V_D
$$

The mechanical power output per revolution is:

$$
W_{out/rev} = T_{th} \cdot 2\pi \quad \text{(since one rev = } 2\pi \text{ radians)}
$$

**Assuming ideal (lossless) conversion:**

$$
T_{th} \cdot 2\pi = \Delta P \cdot V_D
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} \quad [N \cdot m]\,}
$$

> **Textual justification:** *The pressure energy stored in the volume $V_D$ of fluid is fully converted into rotational work as the rotor turns through one full revolution. Equating hydraulic work to mechanical work yields the torque.*

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Power $P_{th}$

Starting from the torque expression, multiply by angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N$ (where $N$ is in rev/s):

$$
P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} \cdot 2\pi N
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{th} = \Delta P \cdot V_D \cdot N = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th} \quad [W]\,}
$$

> **Textual justification:** *The theoretical power is the product of the pressure drop and the volumetric flow rate, since $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$.*

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Conversion Between SI and Industrial (Bar–LPM) Units

**Industrial convention (KTU exam favourite):** $P$ in **kW**, $\Delta P$ in **bar**, $Q$ in **LPM**.

**Step 1 — Convert $\Delta P$ from bar to Pa:**
$1 \text{ bar} = 10^5$ Pa

**Step 2 — Convert $Q$ from LPM to m³/s:**
$1$ LPM $= \dfrac{10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s $= \dfrac{1}{6 \times 10^4}$ m³/s

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$
P_{kW} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{bar} \cdot 10^5 \cdot Q_{LPM} \cdot 10^{-3}}{60 \cdot 1000} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{bar} \cdot Q_{LPM}}{600}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{kW} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{bar} \cdot Q_{LPM}}{600} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{MPa} \cdot Q_{LPM}}{6}\,}
$$

> **Numerical check:** $\Delta P = 100$ bar, $Q = 60$ LPM → $P = (100 \times 60)/600 = 10$ kW. ✓

---

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Example — Model KTU Numerical

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A balanced vane motor has a displacement of $100$ cc/rev. It is supplied with oil at $\Delta P = 80$ bar at a flow rate of $Q_{in} = 60$ LPM. If the volumetric efficiency is 92% and the mechanical efficiency is 90%, determine: (a) theoretical and actual torque, (b) speed of the motor, (c) actual power output.

**Given Data:**
- $V_D = 100$ cc/rev $= 100 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev $= 10^{-4}$ m³/rev
- $\Delta P = 80$ bar $= 80 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 8 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $Q_{in} = 60$ LPM $= 60 \times 10^{-3} / 60 = 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- $\eta_v = 0.92$, $\eta_m = 0.90$

**Step (a1) — Theoretical Torque:**

$$
T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \dfrac{(8 \times 10^6)(10^{-4})}{2\pi} = \dfrac{800}{2\pi} = 127.32 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step (a2) — Actual Torque:**

$$
T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = 127.32 \times 0.90 = 114.59 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step (b1) — Theoretical Speed:**

The theoretical speed (assuming zero leakage) is:

$$
N_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{V_D} = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{10^{-4}} = 10 \text{ rev/s} = 600 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step (b2) — Actual (Loaded) Speed:**

Using the volumetric efficiency relationship: $Q_{in} = Q_{act} + Q_{leak}$, and $Q_{act} = V_D \cdot N_{act}$:

$$
N_{act} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{V_D} = \dfrac{\eta_v \cdot Q_{in}}{V_D} = \eta_v \cdot N_{th} = 0.92 \times 600 = 552 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step (c) — Actual Power Output:**

$$
P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega_{act} = T_{act} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N_{act}}{60}
$$

$$
P_{out} = 114.59 \times \dfrac{2\pi \times 552}{60} = 114.59 \times 57.78 = 6621.6 \text{ W} \approx 6.62 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternate verification via overall efficiency:**
> $P_{in} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q_{in}}{600} = \dfrac{80 \times 60}{600} = 8$ kW.
> $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.92 \times 0.90 = 0.828$.
> $P_{out} = 0.828 \times 8 = 6.624$ kW. ✓ **Matches.**

---

### 3.6 Comparative Mastery Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Situation in the KTU exam | Formula to use directly |
|---|---|
| Question gives $R_o$, $R_i$, $B$ | $V_D = 2\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2) B$ |
| Question gives $V_D$ and $\Delta P$ | $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot V_D / 2\pi$ |
| Question gives $Q$ in LPM, $P$ in bar | $P_{kW} = \Delta P_{bar} \cdot Q_{LPM} / 600$ |
| Question gives $T_{act}$ and $\eta_m$ | $T_{th} = T_{act} / \eta_m$ |
| Question gives $N_{th}$ and $\eta_v$ | $N_{act} = \eta_v \cdot N_{th}$ |
| Question asks for slip | $Q_{slip} = Q_{in} - V_D \cdot N_{act}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)] — 3 Marks**

> Define a hydraulic vane motor. Distinguish between an unbalanced and a balanced vane motor based on the cam-ring profile and net radial force on the rotor.

**Model Answer:**

> A *hydraulic vane motor* is a positive-displacement rotary actuator that converts hydraulic energy into mechanical (rotational) energy using a slotted rotor and sliding vanes that are pressed against a cam ring by centrifugal force and/or hydraulic pressure.
>
> | Feature | Unbalanced | Balanced |
> |---|---|---|
> | Cam ring | Circular, eccentric to rotor | Oval / elliptical (2-lobe) |
> | Net radial force | Non-zero (acts on bearings) | Theoretically zero (cancels) |
> | Max operating pressure | ~70 bar | Up to 140 – 170 bar |
> | Bearing life | Shorter | Longer |
>
> *(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for distinction table)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] — 3 Marks**

> State the formula for theoretical torque of a vane motor and explain why it is independent of the operating speed.

**Model Answer:**

$$
T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}
$$

> The theoretical torque depends **only** on the pressure drop $\Delta P$ and the geometric displacement $V_D$. It is independent of speed because, in an ideal motor, the energy conversion per revolution ($\Delta P \cdot V_D$) is distributed uniformly over $2\pi$ radians regardless of how fast the rotor turns. Leakage and friction (the speed-dependent losses) are captured in $\eta_v$ and $\eta_m$, but the **ideal** torque itself is purely a function of pressure and geometry.
>
> *(1 mark formula, 2 marks explanation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Total)

> **Common Stem (KTU Pattern):** Read the following and answer either **Question A** or **Question B** below.

A hydraulic vane motor has the following specifications:
- Outer cam-ring radius $R_o = 42$ mm
- Rotor radius $R_i = 36$ mm
- Axial width $B = 25$ mm
- Operating pressure drop $\Delta P = 90$ bar
- Input flow rate $Q_{in} = 40$ LPM
- Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.90$
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.88$

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Determine the geometric displacement $V_D$ in cc/rev and the theoretical torque $T_{th}$ in N·m. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the actual operating speed in rpm, the actual output torque, and the actual output power in kW. **(7 marks)**

---

**Complete Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute displacement:**

$$
V_D = 2\pi \left(R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2}\right) B
$$

$$
R_o^{\,2} = (0.042)^2 = 1.764 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
R_i^{\,2} = (0.036)^2 = 1.296 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
R_o^{\,2} - R_i^{\,2} = 0.468 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V_D = 2\pi \times 0.468 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.025 = 7.35 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_D = 73.5 \text{ cc/rev}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute theoretical torque:**

$$
T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \dfrac{(90 \times 10^5)(7.35 \times 10^{-5})}{2\pi}
$$

$$
T_{th} = \dfrac{661.5}{2\pi} = \dfrac{661.5}{6.2832}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{th} = 105.28 \text{ N·m}}
$$

> *(4 marks for V_D calculation with units, 3 marks for T_th)*

**Part (b):**

**Step 3 — Convert flow rate:**

$$
Q_{in} = 40 \text{ LPM} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute actual speed using $\eta_v$:**

$$
N_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{V_D} = \dfrac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.35 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.07 \text{ rev/s} = 544.2 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
N_{act} = \eta_v \cdot N_{th} = 0.90 \times 544.2
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{act} = 489.8 \text{ rpm}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute actual torque:**

$$
T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th} = 0.88 \times 105.28
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{act} = 92.65 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute actual output power:**

$$
\omega_{act} = \dfrac{2\pi N_{act}}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 489.8}{60} = 51.30 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega_{act} = 92.65 \times 51.30
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{out} = 4753.4 \text{ W} = 4.75 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Cross-check using $\eta_o$:**

$$
P_{in} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{600} = \dfrac{90 \times 40}{600} = 6.0 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.90 \times 0.88 = 0.792
$$

$$
P_{out} = 0.792 \times 6.0 = 4.752 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark
$$

> *(2 marks N_act, 2 marks T_act, 3 marks P_out with cross-check)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a balanced vane motor. List any **three** advantages it has over an unbalanced vane motor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vane motor has a geometric displacement of $60$ cc/rev. It operates at a pressure drop of $100$ bar and a flow rate of $30$ LPM. If the volumetric efficiency is 0.95 and the mechanical efficiency is 0.85, calculate: (i) the actual speed, (ii) the theoretical and actual torques, (iii) the slip in LPM, and (iv) the actual output power. **(7 marks)**

---

**Complete Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of a Balanced Vane Motor:**

> **Construction (block-diagram description, 4 marks):**
> - An **oval / elliptical (two-lobe) cam ring** encloses the rotor.
> - The rotor carries **sliding vanes** in radial slots, pushed outward by **centrifugal force and hydraulic pressure**.
> - Two **inlet ports** are positioned $180°$ apart on the major axis, and two **outlet ports** are on the minor axis.
> - The rotor is mounted on a shaft supported by bearings inside the housing.
>
> **Working (3 marks):**
> Pressurized oil enters through both inlet ports simultaneously. Fluid pushes against the trailing vane of each lobe. The two tangential torques produced at the two lobes **add constructively** at the shaft, while the two radial forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, **cancelling** the net radial load on the rotor and bearings. The fluid is exhausted through both outlet ports on the minor axis and returns to the tank.
>
> **Three Advantages over Unbalanced Vane Motor:**
> 1. **Zero net radial force** → longer bearing life, quieter operation.
> 2. **Higher permissible operating pressure** (up to 140 – 170 bar).
> 3. **Higher starting torque and better mechanical efficiency.**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_D = 60$ cc/rev $= 6 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/rev; $\Delta P = 100$ bar $= 10^7$ Pa; $Q_{in} = 30$ LPM $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s; $\eta_v = 0.95$; $\eta_m = 0.85$.

**Step (i) — Actual Speed:**

$$
N_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{V_D} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{6 \times 10^{-5}} = 8.333 \text{ rev/s} = 500 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
N_{act} = \eta_v \cdot N_{th} = 0.95 \times 500 = 475 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step (ii) — Theoretical and Actual Torques:**

$$
T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \dfrac{(10^7)(6 \times 10^{-5})}{2\pi} = \dfrac{600}{6.2832} = 95.49 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th} = 0.85 \times 95.49 = 81.17 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step (iii) — Slip in LPM:**

$$
Q_{act} = V_D \cdot N_{act} = (60 \times 10^{-6}) \times \left(\dfrac{475}{60}\right) = 4.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to LPM: $Q_{act} = 4.75 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 10^3 = 28.5$ LPM.

$$
Q_{slip} = Q_{in} - Q_{act} = 30 - 28.5 = 1.5 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step (iv) — Actual Output Power:**

$$
\omega_{act} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 475}{60} = 49.74 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega_{act} = 81.17 \times 49.74 = 4037 \text{ W} = 4.04 \text{ kW}
$$

> *(Distribution: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 1.5 marks for (iii), 1.5 marks for (iv))*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing pitfalls — read this carefully before writing your KTU exam:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in displacement:** $V_D$ formula yields m³/rev. To convert to **cc/rev**, multiply by $10^6$. Forgetting this loses **1 – 2 marks** silently.
> 2. **$N$ in rpm vs. rev/s:** In $Q = V_D \cdot N$, $N$ **must** be in rev/s if $V_D$ is m³/rev and $Q$ is m³/s. Mixing rpm into SI equations is the #1 mistake.
> 3. **Theoretical vs. Actual:** The $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot V_D / 2\pi$ gives the *ideal* torque. If the question says "calculate torque", you must check whether they want $T_{th}$ or $T_{act}$. Default to $T_{th}$ only if efficiencies are absent.
> 4. **Efficiency placement:** $\eta_v$ acts on the **flow / speed** side; $\eta_m$ acts on the **torque** side. Mixing them loses **2 marks** in Part B.
> 5. **Bar–LPM shortcut:** $P_{kW} = (\Delta P_{bar} \cdot Q_{LPM}) / 600$. Memorize this — it is the fastest way to compute input power.
> 6. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $V_D$:** The displacement formula is $2\pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2)B$, **not** $\pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2)B$. The factor of 2 arises because each vane tip sweeps two chambers per revolution.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **One-page rapid revision — memorize before entering the exam hall:**
>
> - **Definition:** Vane motor = positive-displacement rotary hydraulic actuator using *sliding vanes* on a *slotted rotor* inside an *eccentric (or oval) cam ring*. Converts $\Delta P \cdot Q$ into $T \cdot \omega$.
> - **Two types:** Unbalanced (single high-pressure zone, radial load on bearings) vs. Balanced (two-lobe cam ring, two high-pressure zones, **zero** net radial force, higher pressure rating).
> - **Displacement formula (universal):** $V_D = 2\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2) B$ — same for both balanced and unbalanced.
> - **Eccentricity:** $e = R_o - R_i$ — defines the volumetric displacement per chamber.
> - **Theoretical torque:** $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot V_D / 2\pi$ — *speed-independent*.
> - **Theoretical flow rate:** $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$ — depends on speed.
> - **Theoretical power:** $P_{th} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega$.
> - **Volumetric efficiency:** $\eta_v = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$ — reflects leakage; affects actual speed: $N_{act} = \eta_v \cdot N_{th}$.
> - **Mechanical efficiency:** $\eta_m = T_{act} / T_{th}$ — reflects friction; affects actual torque: $T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th}$.
> - **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$.
> - **Industrial power shortcut:** $P_{kW} = (\Delta P_{bar} \cdot Q_{LPM}) / 600$ — bar × LPM / 600.
> - **Slip / leakage flow:** $Q_{slip} = Q_{in} - V_D \cdot N_{act}$ — increases with $\Delta P$.
> - **Balanced motor advantages:** zero radial load, higher pressure, longer bearing life, higher starting torque, quieter operation.
> - **KTU exam hot numbers:** typical $V_D$ = 40 – 250 cc/rev; typical $\Delta P$ = 70 – 140 bar; typical $N$ = 500 – 3000 rpm.
> - **Why vanes slide out:** centrifugal force + hydraulic pressure (pressure under the vane base) — ensures tip contact with cam ring at all speeds (zero-speed starting may require spring assistance or hydraulic push-out).
> - **Limitation:** Lower starting torque than gear motors at zero rpm (unless vane push-out pins are used); vanes wear with contamination in fluid — hence strict filtration is mandatory (typically NAS 6 / ISO 4406 18/16/13 or better).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piston Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Hydraulic Piston Motor** is a positive-displacement rotary actuator that converts the *hydraulic energy* (pressure × flow) of a working fluid into *mechanical rotational energy* (torque × angular velocity) at its output shaft. The conversion is achieved by admitting pressurized hydraulic oil into multiple cylinder chambers, each fitted with a reciprocating **piston**, whose linear thrust is mechanically resolved into a continuous rotary motion through a cam, swash plate, or bent-axis mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Reference:** A hydraulic motor is a **rotary actuator** — not a pump. The same physical device can act as either a pump (input = mechanical rotation) or a motor (input = pressurized fluid), but its classification in the circuit depends on the *direction of dominant energy transfer*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water wheel** used in old flour mills. Pressurized water strikes paddles arranged around the rim, forcing the wheel to turn continuously. Now replace the water with high-pressure hydraulic oil and the wooden paddles with precisely machined steel pistons seated in cylindrical bores — you have a piston motor.

A simpler mental model: think of it as a **reverse bicycle pump** used in a circle. Instead of pushing a piston to compress air, high-pressure oil *pushes* the pistons, and these pistons *push* a rotating shaft. The harder the oil pushes (pressure), the stronger the rotation (torque); the faster oil enters (flow rate), the quicker the rotation (speed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Three operating variables to remember always:**
> - **Pressure (P, in bar)** → controls **Torque (T)**
> - **Flow rate (Q, in L/min)** → controls **Speed (N)**
> - **Displacement (V_D, in cc/rev)** → links the two

### 1.3 Classification of Hydraulic Piston Motors

Hydraulic piston motors are broadly classified based on the **axis of piston motion relative to the shaft axis**:

| Class | Sub-Type | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Axial Piston Motor** | Swash-Plate Type | Pistons move parallel to shaft; inclination by swash plate |
| **Axial Piston Motor** | Bent-Axis Type | Shaft and cylinder block are at an angle (typically 25°–45°) |
| **Radial Piston Motor** | Cam-Ring Type (e.g., Staffa) | Pistons move radially outward against an internal cam ring |
| **Radial Piston Motor** | Eccentric Shaft Type | Pistons act against an eccentric cylindrical cam |

![Classification overview will be detailed in Section 4]
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

#### A. Axial Piston Motor (Swash-Plate Type)
1. Pressurized oil enters the valve plate and is ported to the cylinder bores in a controlled sequence.
2. The high-pressure oil enters a bore on the **upper** side of the swash plate, pushing the piston *downward* (along the shaft axis).
3. The piston foot bears against the inclined swash plate. The contact force is resolved into an **axial component** (opposing oil pressure, equilibrium) and a **tangential component** perpendicular to the shaft.
4. The tangential component creates a **torque** about the shaft axis, rotating the cylinder block.
5. As the bore rotates past the exhaust port of the valve plate, the spent oil is discharged back to the reservoir.
6. A continuous cycle of pressurization, expansion, and exhaust produces smooth, continuous rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a swash plate?** The inclination angle (β) of the swash plate determines the **piston stroke length** and hence the **displacement per revolution**. Adjusting β (variable displacement) directly controls motor output speed and torque.

#### B. Bent-Axis Piston Motor
1. The cylinder block is mounted on a **revolving joint** and tilted at a fixed angle (e.g., 25° or 40°) relative to the drive shaft.
2. Pressurized oil is routed through the valve plate to the bore whose piston is currently on the "advancing" side of the rotation.
3. The pressure force on the piston creates a force vector *along the bore axis*, which is offset from the shaft axis by the bending angle.
4. This offset produces a **torque arm** that drives the cylinder block to rotate.
5. Connection between the cylinder block and shaft is made through a **cardan (universal) joint** or a **spherical coupling** to accommodate the angular misalignment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bent-axis vs. Swash-Plate:** Bent-axis motors handle **higher pressures (up to 450 bar)** and offer **better efficiency at high speeds**, while swash-plate designs are more compact and offer easier **variable displacement**.

#### C. Radial Piston Motor
1. Pistons are arranged **radially** (like spokes) around a central camshaft/eccentric.
2. Pressurized oil enters through the central shaft and is distributed to pistons in pairs.
3. The pistons are pushed **outward radially**; their piston shoes press against an **internal cam ring** (or an eccentric cylindrical track).
4. The reaction force from the cam ring creates a **rotational moment** about the central shaft.
5. As the shaft rotates, the cam profile retracts the pistons, expelling the spent oil.
6. Radial piston motors typically have an **odd number of pistons (5, 7, 9)** to avoid pressure-pulse coincidence.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Torque | $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ | N·m | $\Delta P$ = P_in − P_out |
| 2 | Actual Torque (Mechanical) | $T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m$ | N·m | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency |
| 3 | Theoretical Speed | $N_{th} = \dfrac{Q}{V_D}$ | rpm | Q in L/min, V_D in L/rev |
| 4 | Actual Speed (Volumetric) | $N_{act} = N_{th} \cdot \eta_v$ | rpm | $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency |
| 5 | Hydraulic Power Input | $P_{in} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{600}$ | kW | $\Delta P$ in bar, Q in L/min |
| 6 | Mechanical Power Output | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60{,}000}$ | kW | N in rpm, T in N·m |
| 7 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | — | Unitless, decimal fraction |
| 8 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot N}{Q_{in}}$ | — | Accounts for internal leakage |
| 9 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{act}}{T_{th}} = \dfrac{1 - C_t \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot N}}{1}$ | — | C_t = torque loss coeff. |
| 10 | Piston Stroke (Swash-Plate) | $S = D_p \cdot \tan\beta$ | m | $D_p$ = piston pitch dia, $\beta$ = swash angle |
| 11 | Displacement (Swash-Plate) | $V_D = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot z \cdot D_p \cdot \tan\beta}{4}$ | m³/rev | $d$ = piston dia, $z$ = no. of pistons |
| 12 | Displacement (Bent-Axis) | $V_D = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot z \cdot D_p \cdot \sin\theta}{4}$ | m³/rev | $\theta$ = bent-axis angle |
| 13 | Displacement (Radial, Single-acting) | $V_D = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot z \cdot e}{2}$ | m³/rev | $e$ = eccentricity |
| 14 | Tangential Force on Piston | $F_t = F_{axial} \cdot \tan\beta$ | N | $F_{axial} = \Delta P \cdot A_p$ |
| 15 | Specific Torque | $T_{sp} = \dfrac{T_{th}}{\Delta P} = \dfrac{V_D}{2\pi}$ | N·m/bar | Inherent property of motor size |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Conversion:**
> - 1 bar = 10⁵ N/m² = 100 kPa
> - 1 L = 10⁻³ m³
> - Power (kW) = (Pressure in bar × Flow in L/min) ÷ 600
> - Power (HP) = (Pressure in bar × Flow in L/min) ÷ 450 (approx)

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Piston motors dominate applications where **high pressure, high torque density, and variable speed** are critical:

- **Injection Molding Machines** — clamp and injection drives (high torque at low speed).
- **Mobile Hydraulics** — swing drives on excavators, winch drives on cranes.
- **Machine Tools** — spindle drives, feed drives on CNC machines.
- **Marine & Offshore** — winch and capstan drives, deck machinery.
- **Mining & Construction** — track drives on bulldozers, drilling rigs.
- **Agricultural Equipment** — combine harvesters, balers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why NOT gear or vane motors for these applications?**
> - Gear motors: limited to ~200 bar, lower efficiency, noisy.
> - Vane motors: lower starting torque, limited to ~175 bar.
> - **Piston motors** routinely operate at **350–450 bar** with efficiencies >90%.
