## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Torque for an Axial Piston Motor (Swash-Plate Type)

#### Step 1 — Free-Body Definition of a Single Piston

Consider one piston in a swash-plate motor with:
- Piston cross-sectional area: $A_p$ (m²)
- Piston pitch diameter (where piston foot sits): $D_p$ (m)
- Swash-plate inclination angle: $\beta$ (degrees)
- Pressure difference across the piston: $\Delta P$ (Pa)
- Number of pistons: $z$
- Piston bore diameter: $d$ (m)

The **axial force** generated by the pressurized oil on the piston is:

$$F_{axial} = \Delta P \cdot A_p = \Delta P \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4}$$

This force acts *along the bore axis*, pushing the piston toward the swash plate.

#### Step 2 — Resolution of Force at the Swash Plate Contact

At the piston foot, the swash plate surface is inclined at angle $\beta$ to the shaft axis. The reaction force $R$ from the swash plate acts *normal* to the plate surface. This reaction has two components:
- Axial component: $R \cos\beta$ (balances $F_{axial}$)
- Tangential component: $R \sin\beta$ (perpendicular to shaft, produces torque)

For equilibrium along the bore axis:

$$\Delta P \cdot A_p = R \cos\beta \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{\Delta P \cdot A_p}{\cos\beta}$$

The **tangential (rotational) force** at the piston foot, acting at radius $D_p/2$ from the shaft axis, is:

$$F_t = R \sin\beta = \frac{\Delta P \cdot A_p \cdot \sin\beta}{\cos\beta} = \Delta P \cdot A_p \cdot \tan\beta$$

#### Step 3 — Instantaneous Torque from a Single Piston

The torque produced by *one* piston about the shaft axis:

$$T_1 = F_t \cdot \frac{D_p}{2} = \Delta P \cdot A_p \cdot \tan\beta \cdot \frac{D_p}{2}$$

#### Step 4 — Displacement of the Motor (Per Revolution)

In one revolution, each piston completes one full stroke of length $S = D_p \cdot \tan\beta$. The volume swept by one piston per revolution is $A_p \cdot S$. For $z$ pistons:

$$V_D = z \cdot A_p \cdot S = z \cdot A_p \cdot D_p \cdot \tan\beta$$

#### Step 5 — Torque from All Pistons (Integrated Over a Revolution)

For a *multi-piston* motor with even number of pistons $z$, the pistons are symmetrically arranged, and at any instant approximately $z/2$ pistons are on the high-pressure side. The total torque is the *average* of torques from all pistons. Through integration over a full revolution (or recognizing the energy equivalence $W = \Delta P \cdot V_D$ for one revolution), we obtain:

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$$

Substituting the expanded form of $V_D$:

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot z \cdot A_p \cdot D_p \cdot \tan\beta}{2\pi} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot z \cdot \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot D_p \cdot \tan\beta}{8\pi}$$

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot z \cdot d^2 \cdot D_p \cdot \tan\beta}{8}$$

This confirms the torque is directly proportional to pressure, piston area, piston pitch diameter, swash angle, and number of pistons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Halving the swash angle $\beta$ (e.g., from 20° to 10°) cuts the displacement in half, halving the torque and doubling the achievable speed at the same flow — this is the *foundation of variable-speed control* in hydraulic circuits.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Design Parameter Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increase → Effect on Torque | Increase → Effect on Speed | Increase → Effect on Efficiency | Trade-off Concern |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piston diameter $d$ | $\uparrow$ (proportional to $d^2$) | $\downarrow$ (displacement rises) | Slightly $\downarrow$ (more leakage) | Higher mass, inertia |
| Piston count $z$ | $\uparrow$ linearly | $\downarrow$ (displacement rises) | $\uparrow$ (smoother flow) | Cost, complexity |
| Swash angle $\beta$ | $\uparrow$ (via $\tan\beta$) | $\downarrow$ (displacement rises) | $\uparrow$ up to ~20°, then $\downarrow$ | Mechanical stress, side-load wear |
| Pitch diameter $D_p$ | $\uparrow$ linearly | $\downarrow$ | Marginally $\downarrow$ | Larger package size |
| Operating pressure $\Delta P$ | $\uparrow$ linearly | (No direct effect) | $\downarrow$ (more leakage) | Seal life, component fatigue |
| Bent-axis angle $\theta$ | $\uparrow$ (via $\sin\theta$) | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Side loads on pistons |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format)

**Problem:** A swash-plate axial piston hydraulic motor has the following specifications:
- Number of pistons, $z = 9$
- Piston diameter, $d = 25$ mm
- Piston pitch circle diameter, $D_p = 80$ mm
- Swash-plate angle, $\beta = 20°$
- Actual flow supplied, $Q_{in} = 60$ L/min
- Pressure drop across the motor, $\Delta P = 200$ bar
- Volumetric efficiency, $\eta_v = 0.96$
- Mechanical efficiency, $\eta_m = 0.92$

**Find:**
1. Geometric displacement $V_D$ (cc/rev)
2. Theoretical and actual speed (rpm)
3. Theoretical and actual torque (N·m)
4. Hydraulic power input, mechanical power output, and overall efficiency

#### Solution:

**Part (1) — Geometric Displacement:**

$$V_D = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot z \cdot D_p \cdot \tan\beta}{4}$$

Substituting numerical values (converting mm → m for SI consistency, or using cc for the LHS):

$$V_D = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.025)^2 \cdot 9 \cdot (0.080) \cdot \tan 20°}{4}$$

$$\tan 20° = 0.36397$$

$$V_D = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 9 \cdot 0.080 \cdot 0.36397}{4}$$

$$V_D = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.2620579}{4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$$

$$V_D = \frac{5.1457 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 1.2864 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$$

Converting to cc/rev (1 m³ = 10⁶ cc):

$$V_D = 128.64 \text{ cc/rev}$$

**Part (2) — Speed:**

Theoretical speed:

$$N_{th} = \frac{Q_{in}}{V_D} = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ cc/min}}{128.64 \text{ cc/rev}} = 466.3 \text{ rpm}$$

Actual speed (with volumetric losses):

$$N_{act} = N_{th} \cdot \eta_v = 466.3 \cdot 0.96 = 447.6 \text{ rpm}$$

**Part (3) — Torque:**

Theoretical torque:

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \frac{200 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \cdot 1.2864 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}}{2\pi}$$

$$T_{th} = \frac{2572.8}{6.2832} = 409.5 \text{ N·m}$$

Actual torque (mechanical losses reduce delivered torque):

$$T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = 409.5 \cdot 0.92 = 376.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part (4) — Power & Efficiency:**

Hydraulic power input:

$$P_{in} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{600} = \frac{200 \cdot 60}{600} = 20 \text{ kW}$$

Mechanical power output:

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N_{act} \cdot T_{act}}{60{,}000} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 447.6 \cdot 376.7}{60{,}000}$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{1{,}058{,}950}{60{,}000} = 17.65 \text{ kW}$$

Overall efficiency:

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{17.65}{20.0} = 0.8825 = 88.25\%$$

**Verification using $\eta_v \cdot \eta_m$:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.96 \cdot 0.92 = 0.8832 = 88.32\%$$

The small discrepancy is due to rounding in intermediate steps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Summary (Box-Style):**
> - $V_D = 128.64$ cc/rev
> - $N_{th} = 466.3$ rpm, $N_{act} = 447.6$ rpm
> - $T_{th} = 409.5$ N·m, $T_{act} = 376.7$ N·m
> - $P_{in} = 20$ kW, $P_{out} = 17.65$ kW
> - $\eta_{overall} = 88.25\%$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a hydraulic piston motor. How does it differ from a hydraulic pump?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hydraulic piston motor is a positive-displacement rotary actuator that converts hydraulic (pressure–flow) energy into mechanical (torque–rotation) energy using reciprocating pistons arranged around a rotating element.

Difference from a pump:
- A **pump** converts *mechanical* input energy into *hydraulic* output energy.
- A **motor** converts *hydraulic* input energy into *mechanical* output energy.
- Constructionally similar, but the **direction of energy flow** and the **prime mover** differ.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the functions of (i) valve plate and (ii) swash plate in an axial piston motor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Valve Plate:** It acts as a flow-distribution device. It routes high-pressure oil to the expanding cylinder bores and connects the retracting bores to the low-pressure return line. It is stationary while the cylinder block rotates.

(ii) **Swash Plate:** It is a stationary (or angularly adjustable) inclined plate against which the piston feet slide. Its inclination angle (β) controls the piston stroke length, hence the motor displacement. Adjustment of β provides **variable displacement** and thus variable torque/speed characteristics.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an **axial piston hydraulic motor** of the swash-plate type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A swash-plate type axial piston hydraulic motor has **9 pistons**, each of **diameter 22 mm**, arranged on a **pitch circle of diameter 75 mm**. The **swash-plate angle is 18°**. The motor is supplied with a **flow of 50 L/min at a pressure of 180 bar**. If the **volumetric efficiency is 95%** and **mechanical efficiency is 90%**, determine:
- (i) Displacement per revolution
- (ii) Theoretical and actual speed
- (iii) Theoretical and actual torque
- (iv) Hydraulic power input, mechanical power output, and overall efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks):**

[Refer to the *Section 2.1.A* step-by-step working explanation. The student should reproduce the six logical steps:

1. Pressurized oil enters through the inlet port of the **valve plate**.
2. The valve plate channels oil into cylinder bores that are on the **expansion side** of the swash plate.
3. Each piston receives an **axial force** $F = \Delta P \cdot A_p$.
4. At the swash plate contact, the force is resolved into an **axial component** (balanced by the plate) and a **tangential component** (perpendicular to the shaft axis).
5. The tangential component acts at the pitch radius, producing a **rotational torque** on the cylinder block.
6. As the block rotates, the bore aligns with the exhaust port of the valve plate, and spent oil drains to the reservoir, completing the cycle.

A labeled sketch is mandatory for full 7 marks. — 3 marks for sketch, 4 marks for stepwise description.]

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given:
- $z = 9$, $d = 22$ mm $= 0.022$ m, $D_p = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m
- $\beta = 18° \Rightarrow \tan 18° = 0.32492$
- $Q = 50$ L/min $= 50{,}000$ cc/min, $\Delta P = 180$ bar $= 180 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $\eta_v = 0.95$, $\eta_m = 0.90$

**(i) Displacement:**

$$V_D = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot z \cdot D_p \cdot \tan\beta}{4}$$

$$V_D = \frac{3.1416 \cdot (0.022)^2 \cdot 9 \cdot 0.075 \cdot 0.32492}{4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$$

$$V_D = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 4.84 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 9 \cdot 0.075 \cdot 0.32492}{4}$$

$$V_D = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 4.84 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.21932}{4} = \frac{3.3319 \times 10^{-4}}{4}$$

$$V_D = 8.33 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 83.30 \text{ cc/rev}$$

**(ii) Speed:**

Theoretical:

$$N_{th} = \frac{Q}{V_D} = \frac{50{,}000}{83.30} = 600.2 \text{ rpm}$$

Actual:

$$N_{act} = N_{th} \cdot \eta_v = 600.2 \cdot 0.95 = 570.2 \text{ rpm}$$

**(iii) Torque:**

Theoretical:

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \frac{180 \times 10^5 \cdot 8.33 \times 10^{-5}}{2\pi} = \frac{1499.4}{6.2832} = 238.6 \text{ N·m}$$

Actual:

$$T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = 238.6 \cdot 0.90 = 214.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iv) Power & Overall Efficiency:**

Hydraulic power input:

$$P_{in} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{600} = \frac{180 \cdot 50}{600} = 15 \text{ kW}$$

Mechanical power output:

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N_{act} \cdot T_{act}}{60{,}000} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 570.2 \cdot 214.7}{60{,}000} = \frac{769{,}350}{60{,}000} = 12.82 \text{ kW}$$

Overall efficiency:

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{12.82}{15} = 0.855 = 85.5\%$$

**Verification:** $\eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.95 \cdot 0.90 = 0.855 = 85.5\%$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Piston-Motor Numericals:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency (–2 marks typical):** Students mix mm and m in the displacement formula. **Always convert to a single system** (prefer SI: convert mm → m), or use the cc-based variant of the formula.
> 2. **Missing $2\pi$ in torque denominator (–1 mark):** $T = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$, **not** $\Delta P \cdot V_D$.
> 3. **Confusion between $N_{th}$ and $N_{act}$:** $N_{th} = Q/V_D$ uses **ideal** flow; for actual motor behavior, multiply by $\eta_v$.
> 4. **Swash-plate vs. Bent-axis formula mix-up:** Swash-plate uses $\tan\beta$; bent-axis uses $\sin\theta$. The two are NOT interchangeable.
> 5. **Pressure in bar vs. Pa:** $T = \frac{P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ requires $P$ in **Pa** if $V_D$ is in m³. Quick check: if $P$ is in bar, $V_D$ must be in m³, and the answer is in N·m; never mix.
> 6. **Skipping the labeled diagram:** A 7-mark working-principle sub-question demands both a *clear sketch* (3 marks) and *textual description* (4 marks).

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set C]**

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the construction and working of a **radial piston hydraulic motor**. Compare it with an axial piston motor in terms of torque, speed, starting ability, and pressure rating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A radial piston hydraulic motor has **5 pistons**, each of **diameter 30 mm** and **stroke 25 mm** (single-acting). The motor is supplied with **flow at 80 L/min at a pressure of 250 bar**. The **overall efficiency is 88%**. Compute:
- (i) Displacement per revolution
- (ii) Actual operating speed
- (iii) Power output
- (iv) Torque developed **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Radial Piston Motor: Construction & Working (7 marks):**

Construction (brief): A **central drive shaft** is fitted with an **eccentric cam** or a **stationary cam ring**. Pistons (typically 5, 7, or 9) are arranged **radially outward** in cylinder bores mounted in a rotor body. Each piston shoe rides against the cam profile.

Working steps:
1. Pressurized oil enters through a port in the **central shaft** and is distributed to pairs of pistons in the high-pressure zone.
2. The pistons are forced **radially outward**, pressing their shoes against the **inner cam ring**.
3. The reaction force from the cam profile acts on each piston with a tangential component, generating **rotational torque** on the central shaft.
4. As the shaft rotates, pistons on the opposite side retract, expelling spent oil through a separate port.

Comparison table (full 7 marks requires explicit comparison):

| Parameter | Radial Piston Motor | Axial Piston Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Torque capacity** | Higher (multi-stroke pistons) | Moderate |
| **Operating speed** | Low to medium (≤1000 rpm) | High (up to 5000 rpm) |
| **Starting torque** | Very high (multi-piston compression) | Moderate to high |
| **Pressure rating** | Up to 500 bar (rugged design) | 350–450 bar (typical) |
| **Volumetric efficiency** | Slightly lower (more leakage paths) | Higher |
| **Mechanical efficiency** | Lower (more friction surfaces) | Higher |
| **Application** | Winches, conveyors, mixers | Spindles, feed drives, mobile |

**Part (b) — Radial Motor Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $z = 5$, $d = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m, $S = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m (single-acting), $Q = 80$ L/min, $\Delta P = 250$ bar, $\eta_{overall} = 0.88$.

**(i) Displacement per revolution:**

For single-acting radial piston motor:

$$V_D = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot z \cdot S}{4} = \frac{3.1416 \cdot (0.030)^2 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.025}{4}$$

$$V_D = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 5 \cdot 0.025}{4} = \frac{3.5343 \times 10^{-4}}{4}$$

$$V_D = 8.836 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 88.36 \text{ cc/rev}$$

**(ii) Actual operating speed:**

For a radial motor, $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$. To find actual speed, we use $\eta_v$ (not given separately). However, the *delivered* power and the *delivered* torque can be found using $\eta_{overall}$ directly.

For finding $N_{act}$, the standard practice in KTU is to assume $\eta_v$ is implicit in the given $Q$ at *actual* conditions, or use:

$$N_{act} = \frac{Q_{in}}{V_D} = \frac{80{,}000 \text{ cc/min}}{88.36 \text{ cc/rev}} = 905.4 \text{ rpm}$$

(Where the flow $Q$ supplied to the motor is the *actual* flow, so the resulting speed is the *actual* speed under load.)

**(iii) Power output:**

Hydraulic power input:

$$P_{in} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{600} = \frac{250 \cdot 80}{600} = 33.33 \text{ kW}$$

Mechanical power output:

$$P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \eta_{overall} = 33.33 \cdot 0.88 = 29.33 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Torque developed:**

Using the relationship $P = T \cdot \omega$:

$$T_{act} = \frac{P_{out} \cdot 60{,}000}{2\pi \cdot N_{act}} = \frac{29.33 \cdot 60{,}000}{2\pi \cdot 905.4}$$

$$T_{act} = \frac{1{,}759{,}800}{5688.0} = 309.4 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall for Radial Motor Problems:**
> 1. **Stroke ≠ Pitch circle diameter in formula:** $V_D$ for radial uses $S$ (stroke) NOT $D_p \cdot \tan\beta$ (swash plate).
> 2. **Single-acting vs. double-acting confusion:** For double-acting, $V_D$ is **doubled** (piston pressurizes on both strokes).
> 3. **Odd piston count required:** Specifying $z = 5$ is deliberate — even piston counts cause pressure-pulse and force-imbalance problems.
> 4. **Distinguishing $\eta_v$, $\eta_m$, and $\eta_{overall}$ in computation:** When only $\eta_{overall}$ is given, you can compute *output power* and *output torque* directly but you CANNOT separately determine $N_{act}$ from $\eta_v$ alone — use the supplied $Q$ as the actual flow.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist for Piston Motors (KTU 2024):**

- [x] **Energy Direction:** Pump converts *mechanical → hydraulic*; Motor converts *hydraulic → mechanical*.
- [x] **Core Equation:** $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ — memorize in this exact form.
- [x] **Speed Equation:** $N_{th} = \dfrac{Q}{V_D}$ — flow-driven, NOT pressure-driven.
- [x] **Power Identity:** $P_{in} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{600}$ kW (bar·L/min).
- [x] **Efficiency Cascade:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$.
- [x] **Swash-plate formula:** $V_D = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot z \cdot D_p \cdot \tan\beta}{4}$ — note the **tan β**.
- [x] **Bent-axis formula:** $V_D = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot z \cdot D_p \cdot \sin\theta}{4}$ — note the **sin θ**.
- [x] **Radial single-acting formula:** $V_D = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot z \cdot S}{4}$ — uses **stroke** not $D_p \cdot \tan\beta$.
- [x] **Torque Resolution:** Axial force on piston × tan β = tangential force producing rotation.
- [x] **Specific Torque:** $T_{sp} = \dfrac{V_D}{2\pi}$ — constant for a given motor, independent of operating pressure.
- [x] **Variable Displacement:** Achieved by adjusting swash-plate angle β; reduces both displacement and torque, increases speed.
- [x] **Pressure Range:** Piston motors operate at 350–450 bar; gear motors ≤200 bar; vane motors ≤175 bar.
- [x] **Bent-Axis vs. Swash-Plate:** Bent-axis = higher pressure, higher efficiency, fixed displacement. Swash-plate = lower pressure, variable displacement, more compact.
- [x] **Radial Motors:** Odd piston count, high starting torque, suited for low-speed high-torque applications.
- [x] **Valve Plate Function:** Stationary flow-distributor; routes HP to active bores and LP to retracting bores.
- [x] **Unit Discipline:** Always check Pa vs. bar; cc vs. m³; rpm vs. rad/s before substitution.
- [x] **Common Error Watch:** $T \neq \Delta P \cdot V_D$ — the $2\pi$ is mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Memory Aid (for the night before the exam):**
> *"Pressure makes Torque; Flow makes Speed; Displacement ties them together; Efficiency scales the result."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Motor Theoretical Torque, Power, and Flow Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Motor Theoretical Torque, Power, and Flow Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Hydraulic Motor** is a rotary mechanical actuator that converts hydraulic pressure energy (pressure × flow) into mechanical rotational energy (torque × angular speed). The **theoretical** performance parameters are the *ideal* values obtained when assuming **100% volumetric** and **mechanical efficiency** — i.e., zero internal leakage and zero friction.

The three fundamental theoretical parameters of a hydraulic motor are:

- **Theoretical Torque (T_th):** The ideal torque developed by the motor, derived purely from the input pressure and motor geometric displacement, with no losses.
- **Theoretical Flow Rate (Q_th):** The ideal volume of hydraulic fluid required by the motor per unit time to produce a given shaft speed, with no internal leakage.
- **Theoretical Power (P_th):** The ideal power output of the motor, calculated as the product of theoretical torque and angular velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Key Definition:** *Theoretical values are obtained by ignoring all losses (leakage, friction, heat). Real-world (actual) values are always LESS than theoretical values for torque and power, and ALWAYS GREATER for flow rate (due to internal leakage compensation).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Waterwheel of Pressurized Oil

Imagine a **water wheel** turned by a stream of water falling from height. The *height* of the fall represents **pressure**, the *flow rate* of the stream represents **flow (Q)**, and the *rotational force* turning the wheel represents **torque**. If you had a *perfect* water wheel (no friction in bearings, no water slipping back), the rotational torque would depend only on (pressure) × (bucket size) and the wheel's RPM would depend only on (flow) ÷ (bucket size).

A hydraulic motor behaves identically:
- The "bucket size" is the **Motor Displacement (V_m)** — the volume of oil swallowed per revolution.
- The "fall height" is the **Pressure Drop (ΔP)** across the motor.
- A "perfect" wheel gives the **Theoretical Torque**; a real wheel with friction and slippage gives the **Actual Torque** (less).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 0.1 \text{ MPa}$
> - $1 \text{ kW} = 1000 \text{ W} = 1000 \text{ N·m/s}$
> - $\omega \text{ (rad/s)} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ where $N$ is shaft speed in **rpm**
> - $1 \text{ Litre} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 10^3 \text{ cm}^3$ (cc)

---

### 1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydraulic motors power winches, excavator swing drives, conveyor rollers, injection molding machine screws, ship deck cranes, and combine harvester threshers. Engineers must know the *theoretical* performance to:

1. **Select** the correct motor size for a load.
2. **Predict** the maximum continuous power output before overheating.
3. **Calculate** overall system efficiency by comparing theoretical vs. actual values.
4. **Diagnose** wear — if actual torque falls well below theoretical, the motor is mechanically worn.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step Logic

1. Pressurized hydraulic oil from the pump enters the motor's inlet port.
2. The fluid pushes against the motor's internal working elements (gears, pistons, vanes).
3. The **pressure force** acts over the **displacement volume** to produce a **torque** on the output shaft.
4. As the shaft rotates, it **swallows** a fixed volume of fluid per revolution — this defines the motor's **displacement (V_m)**.
5. The rate at which fluid is swallowed is the **theoretical flow rate (Q_th)**, governed by the shaft speed.
6. The motor's output **power** is the product of the torque it delivers and the angular velocity at which it rotates.

> [!TIP]
> **The Three Golden Ratios of a Hydraulic Motor:**
> 1. $\text{Torque} \propto \text{Pressure} \times \text{Displacement}$
> 2. $\text{Speed} \propto \dfrac{\text{Flow}}{\text{Displacement}}$
> 3. $\text{Power} = \text{Torque} \times \text{Speed} = \text{Pressure} \times \text{Flow}$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table as your one-stop reference for any motor numerical in the KTU ESE.**

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula (SI Units) | Practical Engineering Units | Boundary / Assumptions |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Motor Displacement (volumetric) | $V_m$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{rev}$ | $\text{cc/rev}$ or $\text{cm}^3/\text{rev}$ | Fixed by motor geometry |
| 2 | Theoretical Torque | $T_{th}$ | $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_m}{2\pi}$ | $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_m}{2\pi \cdot 10^6}$ (when $\Delta P$ in bar, $V_m$ in cc/rev) → Nm | No friction, no leakage |
| 3 | Theoretical Flow Rate | $Q_{th}$ | $Q_{th} = V_m \cdot N$ | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N}{1000}$ (when $V_m$ in cc/rev, $N$ in rpm, $Q$ in LPM) | No internal leakage |
| 4 | Angular Velocity | $\omega$ | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s | $N$ in rpm | — |
| 5 | Theoretical Power (from T & N) | $P_{th}$ | $P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega$ | $P_{th} = \dfrac{2\pi N \cdot T_{th}}{60{,}000}$ kW (when $N$ in rpm, $T_{th}$ in Nm) | No losses |
| 6 | Theoretical Power (from P & Q) | $P_{th}$ | $P_{th} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th}$ | $P_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q_{th}}{600}$ kW (when $\Delta P$ in bar, $Q_{th}$ in LPM) | No losses |
| 7 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v$ | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{th}}{Q_{act}}$ | Always $\leq 1$ | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} + Q_{leak}$ |
| 8 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m$ | $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{act}}{T_{th}}$ | Always $\leq 1$ | $T_{act} = T_{th} - T_{loss}$ |
| 9 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o$ | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | $= \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{T_{act} \cdot \omega}{\Delta P \cdot Q_{act}}$ | Combined effect |
| 10 | Specific Torque (Sanity Check) | $k_T$ | $k_T = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}$ Nm/bar | $k_T = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi \cdot 100}$ Nm/bar per cc | Used in motor selection charts |

### 2.3 Cross-Verification of Power Equations (A KTU Favourite Trick)

Both expressions for $P_{th}$ (using torque/speed and pressure/flow) **must** give the same answer. This is the engineer's most powerful self-check in any numerical. If they don't match, you have either:
- A unit conversion error, OR
- A wrong assumption about which pressure/flow to use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Conservation Principle:**
> $$P_{th(T,\omega)} \equiv P_{th(P,Q)} \implies \left(T_{th} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\right) \equiv \left(\Delta P \cdot Q_{th}\right)$$
> This identity is a guaranteed KTU 2-mark short answer if asked to "verify" or "show that".

### 2.4 Why Theoretical Values Matter in Engineering Practice

The theoretical values act as an **upper performance envelope**. Real motors are always below this envelope, and the ratio of actual to theoretical tells the engineer the *quality* of the motor.

- In **new** motors: $\eta_v > 0.95$, $\eta_m > 0.90$.
- In **worn** motors: $\eta_v$ may drop below 0.80 — a tell-tale sign of internal leakage requiring service.
- In **high-pressure piston motors**: $\eta_o$ can exceed **0.92** (most efficient class).
- In **low-pressure gear motors**: $\eta_o$ typically **0.70 – 0.85**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Theoretical Torque ($T_{th}$)

**Starting Premise:** A hydraulic motor of displacement $V_m$ m³/rev receives pressurized oil at pressure $\Delta P$ Pa (gauge pressure at inlet minus back pressure at outlet).

**Step 1 — Energy Balance per Revolution:**
During one complete revolution, the motor swallows exactly $V_m$ cubic metres of oil. The hydraulic energy delivered to the motor per revolution is the product of pressure (force per unit area) and the displaced volume:

$$W_{rev} = \Delta P \cdot V_m \quad \text{[Joules per revolution]}$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical Work Done per Revolution:**
On the output shaft, this same energy appears as a torque $T_{th}$ acting through an angular displacement of $2\pi$ radians (one full turn):

$$W_{rev} = T_{th} \cdot 2\pi \quad \text{[Joules per revolution]}$$

**Step 3 — Equating the Two Energies (Conservation of Energy):**
By the law of conservation of energy, in the ideal (loss-less) case, all hydraulic input energy becomes mechanical output energy:

$$\Delta P \cdot V_m = T_{th} \cdot 2\pi$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $T_{th}$:**

$$\boxed{T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_m}{2\pi} \quad \text{[N·m]}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Units Adaptation:**
Substitute $\Delta P$ in **bar** ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$) and $V_m$ in **cc/rev** ($1 \text{ cc} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$):

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P_{bar} \cdot 10^5 \cdot V_{cc} \cdot 10^{-6}}{2\pi} = \frac{\Delta P_{bar} \cdot V_{cc}}{2\pi \cdot 10}$$

$$\boxed{T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_m}{20\pi} \quad \text{Nm, when } \Delta P \text{ in bar, } V_m \text{ in cc/rev}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $20\pi$ and not $2\pi \cdot 10$?** Both are the same — examiners will accept either form, but $20\pi$ is the "standard" KTU textbook form.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Theoretical Flow Rate ($Q_{th}$)

**Starting Premise:** The motor rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm). Each revolution consumes exactly $V_m$ cubic centimetres of oil (geometric displacement).

**Step 1 — Volume per Revolution:**
By definition of displacement:
$$V_{per\ rev} = V_m \quad \text{[cc/rev]}$$

**Step 2 — Total Volume per Minute:**
For $N$ revolutions per minute, the volume swallowed per minute is:

$$Q_{th} = V_m \cdot N \quad \text{[cc/min]}$$

**Step 3 — Convert cc/min to LPM:**
Since $1 \text{ Litre} = 1000 \text{ cc}$:

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{V_m \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{[LPM]}}$$

**Step 4 — SI Form (m³/s):**
If $N$ is in revolutions per second ($n = N/60$):

$$Q_{th} = V_m \cdot \frac{N}{60} \cdot 10^{-6} = \frac{V_m \cdot N}{6 \times 10^7} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/s]}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Theoretical Power ($P_{th}$)

**Method A — From Torque and Speed:**

**Step 1 — Angular Velocity:**
For a shaft rotating at $N$ rpm, the angular velocity in rad/s is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 2 — Power as Torque × Angular Velocity:**

$$P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = T_{th} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $T_{th}$ from Derivation I:**

$$P_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_m}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_m \cdot N}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Units (kW, with $\Delta P$ in bar, $V_m$ in cc/rev, $Q$ in LPM):**

$$P_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q_{th}}{600} \quad \text{[kW]}$$

**Step 5 — Alternative Form (using T in Nm, N in rpm):**

$$P_{th} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{th}}{60{,}000} \quad \text{[kW]}$$

$$\boxed{P_{th} = \frac{2\pi N T_{th}}{60{,}000} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q_{th}}{600} \quad \text{[kW]}}$$

**Method B — From Pressure and Flow (Hydraulic Power Identity):**

**Step 1 — Hydraulic Power Definition:**
Power delivered by the fluid is pressure drop × volumetric flow:

$$P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kW (ΔP in Pa, Q in m³/s):**

$$P_{hyd} \text{ [W]} = \Delta P \text{ [Pa]} \cdot Q_{th} \text{ [m}^3\text{/s]}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Unit Form (ΔP in bar, Q in LPM):**

$$P_{hyd} \text{ [kW]} = \frac{\Delta P \text{ [bar]} \cdot Q \text{ [LPM]}}{600}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by 600?** Because $1 \text{ bar} \times 1 \text{ LPM} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} \times \frac{10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} = \frac{100}{60} \text{ W} = \frac{1}{6} \text{ W} = \frac{1}{6000} \text{ kW}$. Hence the magic number **600**.

---

### 3.4 Master Continuity Check (Analytical Matrix)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Sanity Check — Apply this matrix to every motor problem:**

| Given Quantity | Symbol | Path 1 (T-ω route) | Path 2 (P-Q route) | Must Match? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure | $\Delta P$ | Used in $T_{th}$ | Used in $P_{th}$ directly | $\checkmark$ |
| Flow | $Q_{th}$ | Used in $N$ | Used in $P_{th}$ directly | $\checkmark$ |
| Torque | $T_{th}$ | Output of $T_{th}$ eq. | $P_{th}/\omega$ | $\checkmark$ |
| Power | $P_{th}$ | $T_{th} \cdot \omega$ | $\Delta P \cdot Q_{th}$ | $\checkmark$ |

If the two paths disagree by more than $\pm 2\%$, suspect a **unit conversion error** — this is the most common mistake KTU students make.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Energy Conversion Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow of energy** through a hydraulic motor, mapping the exact transformation chain from pressurized fluid input to mechanical shaft output. This is the KTU-recommended schematic style for module 2 questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pump Source"] --> B["Pressurized Oil Inlet P_in bar"]
    B --> C["Hydraulic Motor Block V_m cc per rev"]
    C --> D["Pressure Drop Delta P across Motor"]
    C --> E["Theoretical Flow Q_th LPM"]
    C --> F["Shaft Torque T_th N.m"]
    C --> G["Shaft Speed N rpm"]
    F --> H["Theoretical Power P_th kW"]
    G --> H
    D --> F
    E --> G
    D --> I["Theoretical Power via P Q Route"]
    E --> I
    I --> H
    H --> J["Mechanical Output Shaft"]
    C -.-> K["Internal Leakage Q_leak"]
    C -.-> L["Friction Loss T_loss"]
    K -.-> M["Actual Values Q_act T_act"]
    L -.-> M
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **solid arrows** represent the *theoretical (loss-less)* energy path.
- The **dotted arrows** represent the *real (loss-affected)* path that produces actual values.
- The diagram visually anchors the three derived equations:
  1. $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot V_m / 2\pi$ (paths: B → D → F)
  2. $Q_{th} = V_m \cdot N / 1000$ (paths: C → E)
  3. $P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega$ (paths: F, G → H)

### 4.2 System-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["Input Domain"]
        P1["Pressure Delta P bar"]
        Q1["Volumetric Flow Q LPM"]
    end
    subgraph Motor["Hydraulic Motor"]
        VM["Displacement V_m cc per rev"]
        T1["Torque Block T_th eq"]
        N1["Speed Block N eq"]
    end
    subgraph Output["Output Domain"]
        T2["Shaft Torque T_th N.m"]
        N2["Shaft Speed N rpm"]
        P2["Mechanical Power P_th kW"]
    end
    P1 --> T1
    VM --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    Q1 --> N1
    VM --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    T2 --> P2
    N2 --> P2
```

**Interpretation Notes:**

- The **input domain** is what the hydraulic power unit delivers.
- The **motor block** contains the displacement $V_m$ as the **geometric "translator"** that converts pressure to torque and flow to speed.
- The **output domain** is the mechanical work delivered to the load (winch, conveyor, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
Define **theoretical torque** of a hydraulic motor. State the formula relating it to motor displacement and pressure drop.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** *Definition:* The theoretical torque of a hydraulic motor is the ideal torque developed assuming 100% mechanical efficiency — i.e., no friction, wear, or churning losses — calculated purely from the input pressure drop and the motor's geometric displacement.
- **(2 Marks)** *Formula:* 
$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_m}{2\pi} \quad \text{(SI: N·m)}$$
where $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop across the motor in Pa, and $V_m$ is the motor displacement in m³/rev. In engineering units ($\Delta P$ in bar, $V_m$ in cc/rev):
$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_m}{20\pi} \quad \text{N·m}$$

---

**Q.A2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
A hydraulic motor with displacement $V_m = 50$ cc/rev operates at a pressure of $120$ bar. Calculate the **theoretical flow rate** required to drive the motor at $1000$ rpm. *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Stating the formula:
$$Q_{th} = \frac{V_m \cdot N}{1000} \text{ LPM}$$
- **(1 Mark)** Substituting values:
$$Q_{th} = \frac{50 \times 1000}{1000} = 50 \text{ LPM}$$
- **(1 Mark)** Final answer with unit: **$Q_{th} = 50$ LPM**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **theoretical torque** and **theoretical power** of a hydraulic motor. State clearly the assumptions made. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**
- **(2 Marks)** Stating the assumptions: 100% volumetric efficiency (no internal leakage) and 100% mechanical efficiency (no friction losses).
- **(2 Marks)** Derivation of $T_{th}$ as shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes, leading to $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot V_m / 2\pi$.
- **(2 Marks)** Derivation of $P_{th}$ as shown in **Section 3.3** of these notes, leading to $P_{th} = 2\pi N T_{th} / 60{,}000$ kW.
- **(1 Mark)** Final boxed answer with units.

**(b)** A hydraulic motor with a displacement of $80$ cc/rev is supplied with oil at a flow rate of $60$ LPM at a pressure of $140$ bar. Calculate:
  (i) The theoretical shaft speed.  
  (ii) The theoretical torque.  
  (iii) The theoretical power output. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Theoretical Shaft Speed (2 Marks):**

Using $Q_{th} = V_m \cdot N / 1000$, solving for $N$:

$$N = \frac{Q_{th} \times 1000}{V_m} = \frac{60 \times 1000}{80} = 750 \text{ rpm}$$

**(2 Marks)** → **$N = 750$ rpm**

**(ii) Theoretical Torque (2 Marks):**

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_m}{20\pi} = \frac{140 \times 80}{20\pi} = \frac{11200}{62.832} = 178.25 \text{ N·m}$$

**(2 Marks)** → **$T_{th} \approx 178.25$ N·m**

**(iii) Theoretical Power (3 Marks):**

**Path 1 — Torque-Speed Route (1.5 Marks):**

$$P_{th} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{th}}{60{,}000} = \frac{2\pi \times 750 \times 178.25}{60{,}000} = \frac{8{,}39{,}900}{60{,}000} \approx 14.00 \text{ kW}$$

**Path 2 — Pressure-Flow Route (1.5 Marks):**

$$P_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q_{th}}{600} = \frac{140 \times 60}{600} = 14.00 \text{ kW}$$

**(3 Marks)** → **$P_{th} = 14$ kW** (both routes agree — verified ✓)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **energy conversion process** in a hydraulic motor. Show how pressure, flow, torque, and speed are inter-related through the motor displacement. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**
- **(2 Marks)** Block diagram of energy conversion (refer **Section 4.1** Mermaid schematic).
- **(2 Marks)** Identification of the three key parameters: $\Delta P$ (input), $V_m$ (motor), $T_{th}, N, P_{th}$ (outputs).
- **(2 Marks)** Explanation of the three relations:
  - $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot V_m / 2\pi$ (pressure → torque)
  - $N = Q_{th} \cdot 1000 / V_m$ (flow → speed)
  - $P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega$ (torque × speed → power)
- **(1 Mark)** Concluding statement on conservation of energy (theoretical input power = theoretical output power).

**(b)** A hydraulic motor runs at $1440$ rpm, develops a theoretical torque of $240$ N·m, and has a displacement of $100$ cc/rev. Calculate the **theoretical flow rate** consumed and the **pressure drop** across the motor. Also find the theoretical power. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Theoretical Flow Rate (2 Marks):**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{V_m \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{100 \times 1440}{1000} = 144 \text{ LPM}$$

**(2 Marks)** → **$Q_{th} = 144$ LPM**

**(ii) Theoretical Power (2 Marks):**

$$P_{th} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{th}}{60{,}000} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440 \times 240}{60{,}000} = \frac{21{,}71{,}549}{60{,}000} \approx 36.19 \text{ kW}$$

**(2 Marks)** → **$P_{th} \approx 36.19$ kW**

**(iii) Pressure Drop (3 Marks):**

From $P_{th} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th} / 600$, solving for $\Delta P$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{P_{th} \times 600}{Q_{th}} = \frac{36.19 \times 600}{144} = 150.8 \text{ bar}$$

**Cross-check via $T_{th}$ equation (1 Mark embedded):**

$$\Delta P = \frac{T_{th} \times 20\pi}{V_m} = \frac{240 \times 20\pi}{100} = \frac{4800\pi}{100} \approx 150.8 \text{ bar} \checkmark$$

**(3 Marks)** → **$\Delta P \approx 150.8$ bar** (both routes match ✓)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Disaster (–3 to –4 marks):** Forgetting to divide by $1000$ when $V_m$ is in cc/rev and $Q$ is in LPM. *Always* write down units before substituting.
> 2. **The $2\pi$ Trap (–2 marks):** Using $2\pi$ for torque (correct) but accidentally using $\pi$ for power, or vice versa. Remember: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$, and the factor $2\pi$ cancels out in $P_{th}$.
> 3. **Using Actual Values as Theoretical (–1 to –2 marks):** If a problem gives an *overall efficiency* of 85%, do **not** plug it into the $T_{th}$ formula. $T_{th}$ is *always* loss-free by definition.
> 4. **Missing Cross-Verification (–1 mark):** KTU examiners reward students who verify their answer using the *second* power formula. Always end with: *"Verified by P-Q route: $P_{th} = \Delta P \cdot Q/600 = …$ kW ✓"*
> 5. **Skipping the Assumption Statement (–1 mark):** Always write *"Assuming 100% volumetric and mechanical efficiency (theoretical case)"* at the top of every derivation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This!):**
>
> ✅ **Three Core Theoretical Parameters of a Hydraulic Motor:**
> - $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot V_m / 2\pi$ → depends on **pressure**
> - $Q_{th} = V_m \cdot N / 1000$ → depends on **speed**
> - $P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th}$ → depends on **both**
>
> ✅ **The Magic Conversion Number for Power:**
> - $P_{th} \text{ [kW]} = \dfrac{\Delta P \text{ [bar]} \times Q \text{ [LPM]}}{600} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{th}}{60{,}000}$
>
> ✅ **Assumption Set (always state before solving):**
> - 100% volumetric efficiency (no leakage)
> - 100% mechanical efficiency (no friction)
> - Steady-state operation
> - Incompressible fluid
>
> ✅ **Theoretical vs. Actual — Three Quick Comparisons:**
> - $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$ (extra flow leaks back to tank) → $\eta_v = Q_{th} / Q_{act} < 1$
> - $T_{act} < T_{th}$ (friction losses) → $\eta_m = T_{act} / T_{th} < 1$
> - $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ — combined efficiency is **product**, not sum!
>
> ✅ **Displacement Triangle (KTU Favourite Diagram Type):**
> - Pressure + Displacement → **Torque**
> - Flow + Displacement → **Speed** (via $N$)
> - Torque + Speed → **Power**
>
> ✅ **Sanity Numbers for Quick Estimation:**
> - $T_{th} \approx \Delta P_{bar} \times V_{cc} / 62.83$ Nm (since $20\pi \approx 62.83$)
> - A 100 cc/rev motor at 200 bar gives $T_{th} \approx 318$ Nm
> - A 100 cc/rev motor at 1000 rpm needs $Q_{th} = 100$ LPM
> - 1 kW ≈ 1.341 HP
>
> ✅ **Examiner's Pet Phrases (use these in your answers):**
> - *"In the ideal (loss-less) case…"*
> - *"By conservation of energy…"*
> - *"Substituting the engineering units (bar, LPM, cc/rev)…"*
> - *"This is verified by the alternative pressure-flow route…"*
>
> ✅ **Most Important Equation to Memorize Cold:**
> $$\boxed{P_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q_{th}}{600} \text{ kW}}$$
> If you forget everything else, this single equation (with the right units) solves 80% of KTU motor numericals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Motor Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Motor Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **hydraulic motor** is a rotary fluid power actuator that converts hydraulic energy (pressure × flow) supplied by a pump into mechanical energy in the form of **rotational torque and angular speed** delivered at the output shaft. Performance of a hydraulic motor is quantified by its **displacement, torque, speed, power output, and efficiencies** under specified operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A hydraulic motor's *performance* refers to the quantitative relationship between its **input parameters** (pressure $\Delta P$, flow rate $Q$) and **output parameters** (torque $T$, rotational speed $N$, power $P_{out}$), characterized by three efficiencies — **Volumetric ($\eta_v$), Mechanical ($\eta_m$), and Overall ($\eta_o$)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel Reimagined

Imagine a **water wheel in an old mill**: pressurized water (hydraulic oil) is squirted into chambers (motor chambers) that push against angled vanes, causing the shaft to spin. The harder the water hits the vanes (high **pressure**), the more twisting force you get (**torque**). The more water you pour in per second (**flow rate**), the faster the wheel spins (**speed**). But due to leaks past clearances (water splashing out unused) and friction at the bearings (drag), you don't get back *all* the energy you put in — that's where **efficiencies** come in.

| Analogy Element | Hydraulic Motor Equivalent | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure of incoming water | Pressure drop across motor | $\Delta P$ |
| Volume of water per second | Flow rate | $Q$ |
| Twisting effort on shaft | Torque | $T$ |
| Rotation speed of wheel | Angular velocity | $\omega, N$ |
| Water splashing past gaps | Internal leakage | $Q_{leak}$ |
| Bearing friction | Mechanical drag | $T_{loss}$ |

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units Used in KTU Numericals**
> - **Displacement ($D_m$):** $\text{cm}^3/\text{rev}$ (or cc/rev)
> - **Pressure Drop ($\Delta P$):** $\text{bar}$ (1 bar = $10^5$ Pa)
> - **Flow Rate ($Q$):** $\text{LPM}$ (1 LPM = $\frac{10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s)
> - **Speed ($N$):** $\text{rpm}$
> - **Torque ($T$):** $\text{N·m}$
> - **Power ($P$):** $\text{kW}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step

1. **Pressurized oil enters** the motor through the inlet port at pressure $P_1$.
2. Oil **expands across the motor chambers** from inlet pressure $P_1$ to outlet pressure $P_2$, generating a **pressure drop** $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$.
3. This pressure acts on the **displacement chambers** of volume $D_m$ per revolution, creating a **hydraulic force** on the rotor/stator interface.
4. The unbalance of pressure forces produces a **theoretical torque** on the output shaft.
5. The shaft rotates at **angular velocity $\omega$**, doing mechanical work.
6. **Some oil leaks internally** (between high-pressure and low-pressure chambers), reducing the *effective* flow → this is the origin of **volumetric loss**.
7. **Friction** between rotating/sliding parts and churning of oil causes **mechanical loss**, reducing the *effective* torque.

### 2.2 Why Each Efficiency Exists

- **Volumetric Efficiency $\eta_v$:** Internal leakage means not all the supplied flow $Q_{act}$ contributes to rotation. Only $Q_{th} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{act}$ does useful work.
- **Mechanical Efficiency $\eta_m$:** Friction and drag reduce the actual torque below the theoretical value: $T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th}$.
- **Overall Efficiency $\eta_o$:** The combined effect of both losses: $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:----------|:--------|:-----:|:------|
| 1 | Theoretical Torque | $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}$ | N·m | $\Delta P$ in Pa, $D_m$ in m³/rev |
| 1a | Theoretical Torque (practical) | $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{20\pi}$ | N·m | $\Delta P$ in bar, $D_m$ in cc/rev |
| 2 | Actual Torque | $T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th}$ | N·m | Mechanical loss deducted |
| 3 | Theoretical Flow | $Q_{th} = D_m \cdot N$ | m³/s | $N$ in rev/s, $D_m$ in m³/rev |
| 3a | Theoretical Flow (practical) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{D_m \cdot N}{1000}$ | LPM | $D_m$ in cc/rev, $N$ in rpm |
| 4 | Actual Flow | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{Q_{th}}{\eta_v}$ | m³/s | Includes leakage |
| 5 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | $N$ in rpm |
| 6 | Theoretical Power | $P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th}$ | W | Hydraulic to mech. ideal |
| 6a | Theoretical Power (practical) | $P_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q_{th}}{600}$ | kW | $\Delta P$ in bar, $Q_{th}$ in LPM |
| 7 | Output Power | $P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega = \eta_m \cdot P_{th}$ | W | After mech. loss |
| 8 | Input Power | $P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{act} = \dfrac{P_{th}}{\eta_v}$ | W | Hydraulic power supplied |
| 8a | Input Power (practical) | $P_{in} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q_{act}}{600}$ | kW | $\Delta P$ in bar, $Q_{act}$ in LPM |
| 9 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | — | Always less than 1 |
| 10 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{th}}{Q_{act}} = \dfrac{D_m \cdot N}{Q_{act}}$ | — | Ratio of effective to actual flow |
| 11 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{act}}{T_{th}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{th}}$ | — | Ratio of actual to ideal torque |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** **"T-D-P / T-Q-P"**
> - **Torque** from **D**isplacement and **P**ressure
> - **Flow** from **D**isplacement and **N** (rpm) → hence **P**ower

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydraulic motors are critical in:
- **Heavy earth-moving equipment** (excavators, loaders) requiring high torque at low speeds.
- **Winches and cranes** for lifting — gear motors combine hydraulic motors with gear reducers.
- **Agricultural machinery** (harvester combines) requiring variable speed.
- **Injection molding machines** for precise rotation of molds and screws.
- **Marine steering systems** and **ship deck machinery** — motors drive capstans and rudders.
- **Conveyor drives** and **mixers** in process industries.

Performance metrics allow engineers to **select the right motor** for an application based on the **torque-speed-power envelope** and the **operating point efficiency** — crucial for **energy conservation** and **system cooling**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Torque

Consider a hydraulic motor with displacement $D_m$ m³/rev. In one revolution, oil at pressure $\Delta P$ does work:

$$
W = \Delta P \cdot D_m
$$

This work is converted entirely into rotational mechanical work:

$$
W = T_{th} \cdot (2\pi)
$$

where $2\pi$ is the angular displacement in one revolution. Equating both:

$$
T_{th} \cdot (2\pi) = \Delta P \cdot D_m
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical Flow Rate

Displacement $D_m$ is the volume of oil needed per revolution. If the motor rotates at $N$ rev/s, the volume of oil required per second is:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{th} = D_m \cdot N\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Power Relationships

**Theoretical Power** (mechanical ideal):

$$
P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega
$$

Substituting $T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}$ and $\omega = 2\pi N$:

$$
P_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2\pi N = \Delta P \cdot D_m \cdot N
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{th} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega\,}
$$

**Output Power** (after mechanical loss):

$$
P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega = (\eta_m \cdot T_{th}) \cdot \omega = \eta_m \cdot P_{th}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{out} = \eta_m \cdot P_{th}\,}
$$

**Input Power** (hydraulic supply):

$$
\boxed{\,P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{act}\,}
$$

**Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{\eta_m \cdot P_{th}}{\frac{P_{th}}{\eta_v}} = \eta_m \cdot \eta_v
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_o = \eta_m \cdot \eta_v\,}
$$

---

### 3.4 Comprehensive Worked Example — End-to-End

**Problem Statement [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]:**

A hydraulic motor has a displacement of **50 cc/rev** and operates at a pressure of **100 bar**. The motor runs at **1200 rpm**. The volumetric efficiency is **95%** and the mechanical efficiency is **90%**. Determine:
1. Theoretical and actual torque
2. Theoretical and actual flow rate
3. Theoretical power and output power
4. Input power and overall efficiency

**Given Data:**
- $D_m = 50$ cc/rev $= 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev
- $\Delta P = 100$ bar $= 100 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $= 10^7$ Pa
- $N = 1200$ rpm $= 20$ rev/s
- $\eta_v = 0.95$, $\eta_m = 0.90$

---

**Step 1 — Angular Velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{th} &= \frac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi} \\
&= \frac{(10^7) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi} \\
&= \frac{500}{2\pi} \\
&= 79.577 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual Torque:**

$$
T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th} = 0.90 \times 79.577 = 71.62 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical Flow Rate:**

$$
Q_{th} = D_m \cdot N = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 60 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 5 — Actual Flow Rate:**

$$
Q_{act} = \frac{Q_{th}}{\eta_v} = \frac{60}{0.95} = 63.16 \text{ LPM} = 1.0526 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical Power:**

$$
P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = 79.577 \times 125.66 = 10{,}000 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ kW}
$$

(Verification using $P_{th} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th}$: $10^7 \times 10^{-3} = 10{,}000$ W ✓)

**Step 7 — Output Power:**

$$
P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega = 71.62 \times 125.66 = 9{,}000 \text{ W} = 9 \text{ kW}
$$

(Equivalent to $\eta_m \cdot P_{th} = 0.9 \times 10 = 9$ kW ✓)

**Step 8 — Input Power:**

$$
P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{act} = 10^7 \times 1.0526 \times 10^{-3} = 10{,}526 \text{ W} = 10.526 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 9 — Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{9000}{10526} = 0.855 = 85.5\%
$$

(Cross-check: $\eta_o = \eta_m \cdot \eta_v = 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.855$ ✓)

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter

| Parameter | Effect on $T_{th}$ | Effect on $N$ | Effect on $P_{out}$ | Engineering Insight |
|:----------|:------------------:|:-------------:|:-------------------:|:--------------------|
| Increase $\Delta P$ | Linear $\uparrow$ | No direct effect | $\uparrow$ | Stronger torque for heavy loads |
| Increase $Q_{act}$ | No direct effect | Linear $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (via $\omega$) | Faster shaft rotation |
| Increase $D_m$ | Linear $\uparrow$ | Decreases (at fixed $Q$) | Net $\uparrow$ (limited) | High-displacement motors: high torque, low speed |
| Increase $\eta_v$ | No effect on $T_{th}$ | Increases actual speed | $\uparrow$ | Reduces internal leakage |
| Increase $\eta_m$ | Improves $T_{act}$ | No effect | $\uparrow$ | Reduces friction losses |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Flow Architecture — Hydraulic Motor Energy Conversion

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrates the energy transformations from hydraulic input to mechanical output, including all efficiency loss channels.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hydraulic Supply Unit]
    A -->|Delta P and Q_actual| B[Motor Inlet Manifold]
    B --> C[Pressure Energy Chamber]
    C -->|Delta P times Q_actual| D[Hydraulic Input Power P_in]
    
    D -->|Loss factor eta_v| E[Effective Hydraulic Power]
    D -.->|Internal Leakage Q_leak| F1[Volumetric Loss Sink]
    
    E -->|Conversion Dm and omega| G[Mechanical Conversion Stage]
    G -->|T_theoretical times omega| H[Theoretical Mechanical Power P_th]
    
    H -->|Loss factor eta_m| I[Output Shaft Power P_out]
    H -.->|Friction and Churning| F2[Mechanical Loss Sink]
    
    I --> J[Load Application Winch Excavator Crane]
    
    K[Pressure Drop Delta P] --> C
    L[Actual Flow Q_act] --> C
    M[Motor Displacement Dm] --> G
    N[Speed N rpm] --> G
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **Path A → D → E → H → I → J** represents the **useful energy chain**.
- **Path D → F1** shows **volumetric losses** (internal leakage bypassing chambers).
- **Path H → F2** shows **mechanical losses** (friction at bearings, rotor-stator contact, oil churning).
- **Efficiency multipliers $\eta_v$ and $\eta_m$** are applied at the respective loss nodes.

---

### 4.2 Analytical Relationship Map — Input–Output Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pressure Drop Delta P] -->|multiplied by Dm over 2 pi| TT[T_theoretical]
    P -->|multiplied by Q_act| PIN[P_input Hydraulic]
    
    Q[Flow Rate Q_actual] -->|divided by eta_v| QTH[Q_theoretical]
    Q --> PIN
    
    DM[Displacement Dm] -->|multiplied by N| QTH
    DM --> TT
    
    N[Shaft Speed N rpm] -->|multiplied by 2 pi over 60| W[Angular Velocity omega]
    QTH -->|Delta P times Q_th| PTH[P_theoretical]
    
    TT -->|multiplied by omega| PTH
    W -->|multiplied by T_theoretical| PTH
    
    PTH -->|multiplied by eta_m| POUT[P_output]
    PIN -->|divided by eta_v| PTH
    
    TT -->|multiplied by eta_m| TACT[T_actual]
    TACT -->|multiplied by omega| POUT
```

**Key Cross-Couplings Shown:**
- $\Delta P$ influences both **Torque** and **Input Power** paths.
- $D_m$ is the **pivot parameter** — it links both flow (with $N$) and torque (with $\Delta P$).
- $\omega$ is the **speed-coupling bridge** linking theoretical power to output power.

---

### 4.3 Performance Characteristic Curves (Conceptual Sketch in Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PerformanceCurves
    direction LR
    C1[Curve 1: Torque T vs Pressure Delta P - Linear - Slope Dm over 2 pi]
    C2[Curve 2: Speed N vs Flow Q - Linear - Slope 1 over Dm]
    C3[Curve 3: Power P vs Flow Q - Parabolic - P equals Delta P times Q]
    C4[Curve 4: Efficiency eta vs Operating Point - Dome shaped]
    end
    
    C1 --> Note1[Higher displacement motor means steeper torque line]
    C2 --> Note2[Higher Dm means lower speed for same flow]
    C3 --> Note3[Power peaks at maximum Q and Delta P]
    C4 --> Note4[Best efficiency at design operating point]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Curves (Typical KTU Question Type):**
> - $T$ vs $\Delta P$ — **straight line through origin**, slope $= D_m/2\pi$
> - $N$ vs $Q_{act}$ — **straight line through origin**, slope $= 1/D_m$
> - $\eta_o$ vs $\Delta P$ / $Q$ — **dome-shaped curve** peaking at the design point

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms related to hydraulic motor performance:**
(a) Volumetric efficiency, (b) Mechanical efficiency, (c) Overall efficiency.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$):** It is the ratio of the **theoretical flow rate** required to drive the motor at a given speed to the **actual flow rate** supplied.

$$
\eta_v = \frac{Q_{th}}{Q_{act}} = \frac{D_m \cdot N}{Q_{act}}
$$

It accounts for **internal leakage** from high-pressure to low-pressure chambers. Typical values range from **0.92 to 0.98** for industrial motors.

**(b) Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$):** It is the ratio of the **actual torque delivered** at the output shaft to the **theoretical torque** developed by the pressure acting on the displacement.

$$
\eta_m = \frac{T_{act}}{T_{th}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{th}}
$$

It represents losses due to **friction** between moving parts, **churning** of oil, and viscous drag. Typical values range from **0.85 to 0.95**.

**(c) Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$):** It is the ratio of the **useful mechanical output power** to the **hydraulic input power** supplied to the motor.

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m
$$

It is the **product** of volumetric and mechanical efficiencies and represents the **total effectiveness** of energy conversion.

---

**Q.A2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Derive the expression for the theoretical torque of a hydraulic motor in terms of pressure drop and displacement.**

**Model Answer:**

Consider a hydraulic motor with **displacement** $D_m$ m³/rev. In one complete revolution, oil at a pressure drop of $\Delta P$ does work equal to:

$$
W = \Delta P \times D_m
$$

This work is converted into **rotational mechanical work** as the shaft turns through $2\pi$ radians:

$$
W = T_{th} \times 2\pi
$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$
T_{th} \times 2\pi = \Delta P \times D_m
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}\,}
$$

This expression shows that the theoretical torque is **directly proportional** to both the pressure drop and the motor displacement, and is **independent of speed**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Convention:** Answer **any ONE** of the following two full questions. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

A hydraulic motor has a displacement of **25 cm³/rev**. It is supplied with oil at a flow rate of **30 LPM** at a pressure of **140 bar**. The motor runs at **1100 rpm** with an overall efficiency of **85%**. Determine:

**(a)** Theoretical and actual torque, theoretical power, and output power.

**(b)** Volumetric and mechanical efficiencies of the motor.

---

#### **Solution for Part B – Question A**

**Given:**
- $D_m = 25$ cc/rev $= 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev
- $Q_{act} = 30$ LPM $= 30 \times 10^{-3}/60 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $\Delta P = 140$ bar $= 140 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $= 1.4 \times 10^{7}$ Pa
- $N = 1100$ rpm $= 1100/60 = 18.333$ rev/s
- $\eta_o = 0.85$

**Angular Velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1100}{60} = 115.19 \text{ rad/s}
$$

---

**Part (a) — Torque and Power**

**Theoretical Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{th} &= \frac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi} \\
&= \frac{1.4 \times 10^{7} \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi} \\
&= \frac{350}{2\pi} \\
&= 55.70 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Theoretical Power:**

$$
P_{th} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th}
$$

We first compute $Q_{th} = D_m \cdot N = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 18.333 = 4.583 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s $= 27.5$ LPM

$$
P_{th} = 1.4 \times 10^{7} \times 4.583 \times 10^{-4} = 6416.7 \text{ W} \approx 6.417 \text{ kW}
$$

**Input Power:**

$$
P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{act} = 1.4 \times 10^{7} \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 7000 \text{ W} = 7 \text{ kW}
$$

**Output Power:**

$$
P_{out} = \eta_o \cdot P_{in} = 0.85 \times 7 = 5.95 \text{ kW} = 5950 \text{ W}
$$

**Actual Torque:**

$$
T_{act} = \frac{P_{out}}{\omega} = \frac{5950}{115.19} = 51.65 \text{ N·m}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Individual Efficiencies**

**Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_v = \frac{Q_{th}}{Q_{act}} = \frac{27.5}{30} = 0.9167 = 91.67\%
$$

**Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_m = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{th}} = \frac{5950}{6416.7} = 0.9273 = 92.73\%
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.9167 \times 0.9273 = 0.85 = 85\% = \eta_o \;\; ✓
$$

---

### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

A hydraulic motor operates at a pressure of **175 bar** and delivers a torque of **200 N·m** with mechanical efficiency of **92%**. The motor has volumetric efficiency of **95%** and rotates at **1440 rpm**. Determine:

**(a)** Motor displacement and theoretical flow rate.

**(b)** Input power and overall efficiency.

---

#### **Solution for Part B – Question B**

**Given:**
- $\Delta P = 175$ bar $= 175 \times 10^{5}$ Pa
- $T_{act} = 200$ N·m
- $\eta_m = 0.92$
- $\eta_v = 0.95$
- $N = 1440$ rpm $= 24$ rev/s

**Angular Velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1440}{60} = 150.80 \text{ rad/s}
$$

---

**Part (a) — Displacement and Theoretical Flow**

**Theoretical Torque:**

$$
T_{th} = \frac{T_{act}}{\eta_m} = \frac{200}{0.92} = 217.39 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Motor Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_m &= \frac{T_{th} \cdot 2\pi}{\Delta P} \\
&= \frac{217.39 \times 2\pi}{175 \times 10^{5}} \\
&= \frac{1365.97}{1.75 \times 10^{7}} \\
&= 7.805 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} \\
&= 78.05 \text{ cc/rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Theoretical Flow Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= D_m \cdot N \\
&= 7.805 \times 10^{-5} \times 24 \\
&= 1.8732 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
&= 112.39 \text{ LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Input Power and Overall Efficiency**

**Actual Flow Rate:**

$$
Q_{act} = \frac{Q_{th}}{\eta_v} = \frac{112.39}{0.95} = 118.31 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Input Power (Hydraulic):**

$$
P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{act} = 1.75 \times 10^{7} \times \frac{118.31 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 34.51 \text{ kW}
$$

**Output Power:**

$$
P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega = 200 \times 150.80 = 30.16 \text{ kW}
$$

**Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{30.16}{34.51} = 0.874 = 87.4\%
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.874 = 87.4\% = \eta_o \;\; ✓
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit conversion blunder:** Most students lose 1–2 marks by mixing units — e.g., using $D_m$ in cc/rev but $\Delta P$ in Pa without converting. **Always** write the unit conversion line explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $2\pi$:** $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot D_m / 2\pi$, NOT $\Delta P \cdot D_m$. Examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $Q_{th}$ and $Q_{act}$:** Remember $\eta_v = Q_{th}/Q_{act}$ (so $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$). Reverse ratio is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step:** The check $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ catches arithmetic errors and earns **partial marks** even if a final answer is wrong.
> 5. **Power formula blind use:** Some students write $P = \Delta P \cdot Q$ using **$Q_{act}$** when they want $P_{th}$ — always match flow to the power type.
> 6. **Angular velocity in rpm vs rad/s:** Substituting $N$ (rpm) directly into $P = T \cdot N$ gives wrong units — convert to rad/s.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

#### **Must-Know Formulas**
- **Theoretical Torque:** $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}$ — independent of speed.
- **Theoretical Flow:** $Q_{th} = D_m \cdot N$ — directly proportional to speed.
- **Theoretical Power:** $P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th}$
- **Actual Torque:** $T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th}$
- **Actual Flow:** $Q_{act} = Q_{th} / \eta_v$
- **Output Power:** $P_{out} = \eta_m \cdot P_{th}$
- **Input Power:** $P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{act} = P_{th}/\eta_v$
- **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$

#### **Key Practical Conversions**
- Power (kW) = $\dfrac{\Delta P \text{(bar)} \times Q \text{(LPM)}}{600}$
- Torque (N·m) = $\dfrac{\Delta P \text{(bar)} \times D_m \text{(cc/rev)}}{20\pi}$
- $\omega$ (rad/s) = $\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ when $N$ is in rpm

#### **Conceptual Anchors**
- **Higher $D_m$** → Higher torque, Lower speed (constant $Q$)
- **Higher $\Delta P$** → Higher torque, Same speed (constant $Q$)
- **Higher $Q_{act}$** → Higher speed, Same torque (constant $\Delta P$)
- $\eta_v$ depends on **operating pressure** (higher $\Delta P$ → more leakage → lower $\eta_v$)
- $\eta_m$ depends on **operating speed** (higher $N$ → more friction → lower $\eta_m$)
- $\eta_o$ is the **figure of merit** for motor selection.

#### **Common KTU Numerical Pattern**
- Given: $D_m$, $\Delta P$, $Q_{act}$ (or $N$), $\eta_v$, $\eta_m$
- Find: $T_{th}, T_{act}, Q_{th}, P_{th}, P_{out}, P_{in}, \eta_o$
- **Strategy:** Start from the *given* parameter and propagate through the formulas, validating with $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ at the end.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Way Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Three Way Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Three-Way Direction Control Valve (DCV)** is a fluid power component possessing **three working ports** and **two discrete switching positions** (or in some configurations, three positions). It is designated in standard ISO 1219 / ISO 5599 fluid power symbology as a **3/2 valve** (3 ports, 2 positions) or **3/3 valve** (3 ports, 3 positions). Its fundamental function is to either **connect the pressure supply (P) to the actuator port (A)** or **vent the actuator port to the exhaust/tank (T/R)**, but never both simultaneously in its basic binary form.

The three universally standardized ports are:
1. **Port P (Pressure / Supply):** Inlet for pressurized fluid (hydraulic oil or compressed air).
2. **Port T (Tank / Exhaust):** Return path to the reservoir (hydraulics) or atmosphere (pneumatics).
3. **Port A (Cylinder / Actuator):** Working port connected to the single-acting cylinder or load device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** The term "3-way" refers strictly to the **number of ports**, not the number of positions. A 3/2 valve is the most frequently tested configuration in KTU examinations for controlling **single-acting cylinders**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **household bathroom shower diverter valve** — the small knob you pull up to send water either to the showerhead or to the bathtub spout, but never to both at once. That diverter is essentially performing the job of a 3-way valve:

- Pulled **up** → water (pressure) flows to the showerhead (actuator).
- Pushed **down** → water is **cut off** from the showerhead, and any residual water drains out (exhaust to tank/atmosphere).

A pneumatic 3-way solenoid valve works identically: in one position it admits compressed air to push a piston forward; in the other position it seals the supply and vents the piston chamber, allowing a spring or gravity to return the piston.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Clarification:** Think of a 3-way valve as a **smart traffic policeman** at a T-junction — he either lets traffic flow from the main road (P) onto the side street (A), or he lets traffic from the side street (A) exit onto a one-way escape road (T). Never both routes open simultaneously.

## 1.3 Classification of Three-Way Valves

| Classification Parameter | Sub-Types |
|---|---|
| **By Normal Position** | Normally Closed (NC) / Normally Open (NO) |
| **By Actuation Method** | Solenoid, Pilot-operated, Manual, Mechanical (Cam/Plunger), Pneumatic Pilot, Hydraulic Pilot |
| **By Spool/Poppet Type** | Poppet type, Spool type, Rotary type |
| **By Center Condition (3/3 only)** | Closed Center, Tapped Center, Tandem Center |
| **By Return Mechanism** | Spring Return, Detent (no spring), Air Return, Hydraulic Return |

## 1.4 Standard Graphical Symbols (ISO 1219)

| Symbol Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Two adjacent squares** | Each square = one valve position |
| **Arrows inside squares** | Indicate flow path direction (P→A, A→T) |
| **Blocked squares (T-shape)** | Indicate blocked/closed ports |
| **Triangle (actuator symbol)** | Solenoid (continuous line) or pneumatic pilot (long-dash) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, students are expected to **draw the ISO symbol** of a 3/2 DCV and label all three ports (P, T, A). Memorize the standard square-block representation — this is a guaranteed 2-mark question topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Position-by-Position Analysis

### Position 1 (De-energized / Normal Position)
- For a **Normally Closed (NC) 3/2 valve**: The spool/poppet blocks the path from P to A. Port A is connected to T (exhaust). The cylinder, if connected, retracts via spring force.
- For a **Normally Open (NO) 3/2 valve**: Port P is connected to A (cylinder extends), and T is blocked.

### Position 2 (Energized / Actuated Position)
- For **NC type**: The solenoid or pilot signal shifts the spool, connecting **P → A**. The cylinder receives pressurized fluid and extends.
- Port **T is blocked** in this position.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "two positions, three ways"?** Because the valve has **two stable (or momentary) operating states**, and across both states, it can route flow among **three distinct ports**.

## 2.2 Detailed Constructional Features

### 2.2.1 Spool-Type 3/2 Valve
- A cylindrical **spool** slides axially within a precision-bored **sleeve/body**.
- Lands and grooves on the spool either cover or expose the port openings.
- Returned by a **helical compression spring** in the de-energized state.
- Sealing achieved via small clearance (lap fit) — for higher pressures, metal-to-metal contact or O-rings are used.

### 2.2.2 Poppet-Type 3/2 Valve
- Uses a **ball, cone, or disc poppet** that seats against a valve seat.
- Ideal for **low-pressure, leak-tight applications** (e.g., vacuum, instrument air).
- The poppet is lifted by the actuator force (solenoid plunger or pilot pressure).

## 2.3 Force Balance on the Spool (Analytical Foundation)

The actuation force required to shift a pilot-operated or directly-actuated 3/2 spool must overcome:
1. **Spring preload force** ($F_s$)
2. **Pressure-induced hydraulic forces** acting on the spool end-areas
3. **Friction forces** (Stiction + Coulomb)

For a directly actuated solenoid valve:

$$F_{sol} \geq F_s + P_1 \cdot A_1 - P_2 \cdot A_2 + F_f$$

Where:
- $F_{sol}$ = Solenoid electromagnetic force (**N**)
- $F_s$ = Spring preload (**N**)
- $P_1, P_2$ = Upstream and downstream pressures (**Pa**)
- $A_1, A_2$ = Spool end areas (**m²**)
- $F_f$ = Friction force (**N**)

> [!NOTE]
> For **pilot-operated valves**, the solenoid only needs to move a small pilot spool, which then routes pilot pressure to shift the main spool. This is why pilot-operated 3/2 valves can handle **high flow rates with small solenoids**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{SG}}$ | Liquid flow rate through valve | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $Q = C_v \cdot P_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}}$ (approx.) | Gas/air flow rate (subsonic) | $\text{Nm}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | Cylinder piston velocity | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A_p \cdot L}{Q}$ | Cylinder stroking time | $\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $F_{sol} \geq F_s + \Delta P \cdot A_{spool}$ | Solenoid force requirement | $\text{N}$ |
| 6 | $P_{pilot} \cdot A_{pilot} \geq F_s$ | Pilot pressure force balance | $\text{N}$ |
| 7 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2} \left(1 - \beta^4\right) \cdot K$ | Pressure drop across valve (orifice model) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 8 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_o \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Orifice flow equation (general) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $\eta_{vol} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{pump}}$ | Volumetric efficiency of system | dimensionless |
| 10 | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | Piston effective area | $\text{m}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the above formulas, $\Delta P$ denotes **pressure drop** (not absolute value). In markdown tables, write the absolute quantity using `\\vert` or `\\mid` only when rendered in LaTeX. Avoid literal pipe characters in tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Three-way valves are ubiquitous in:
- **Automotive:** Pneumatic braking systems, air suspension control.
- **Manufacturing:** Control of single-acting clamping cylinders, stamping press safety circuits, paint spray guns.
- **Process Industries:** Single-acting diaphragm actuator control for chemical dosing valves.
- **Robotics:** End-effector gripping using single-acting pneumatic fingers.
- **Medical:** Dental chairs, hospital bed positioning, oxygen delivery systems.
- **Packaging:** Bottle-filling line pneumatic actuators, label dispenser trigger valves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application Question:** "Explain how a 3/2 valve controls a single-acting cylinder." — This is a 7-mark classic that appears in almost every KTU ESE paper. Master the block diagram + working explanation format.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time Taken for a Single-Acting Cylinder to Extend via 3/2 Valve

**Given:**
- Cylinder bore diameter $D = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Cylinder stroke length $L = 0.20 \text{ m}$
- Supply pressure $P_s = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- Air consumption (free air delivery) $Q_{FAD} = 2 \text{ L/s} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$

**Step 1: Calculate the piston effective area.**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2$$

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0025 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

*Conversion logic:* The piston is a circle, so area = (π/4) × diameter². The diameter is converted to SI meters (0.05 m) before squaring.

**Step 2: Calculate the geometric volume of the cylinder chamber.**

$$V_{cyl} = A_p \times L = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20$$

$$V_{cyl} = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 392.7 \text{ cm}^3$$

*Conversion logic:* Volume swept by the piston equals effective area multiplied by stroke length. Unit is m³.

**Step 3: Convert the cylinder volume to free air volume at atmospheric pressure using Boyle's Law (isothermal assumption for slow pneumatic actuation).**

$$P_s \cdot V_{cyl} = P_{atm} \cdot V_{free}$$

$$V_{free} = \frac{P_s \cdot V_{cyl}}{P_{atm}} = \frac{6 \times 10^5 \times 3.927 \times 10^{-4}}{1.013 \times 10^5}$$

$$V_{free} = \frac{235.62}{101300} = 2.326 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

*Conversion logic:* Under isothermal expansion (valid for slow pneumatic processes, KTU standard assumption), the free air consumed equals the compressed volume multiplied by the pressure ratio.

**Step 4: Calculate the time to fill the cylinder (and hence the extension time).**

$$t = \frac{V_{free}}{Q_{FAD}} = \frac{2.326 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$t = 1.163 \text{ s}$$

*Conversion logic:* Time = Volume ÷ Flow rate. The cylinder will fully extend in approximately 1.16 seconds under ideal conditions (no leakage, no throttling losses).

## 3.2 Derivation: Spool Actuation Force for a Spring-Return 3/2 Solenoid Valve

**Given:**
- Supply pressure $P_s = 50 \text{ bar} = 50 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- Spool diameter at pressure-end $D_s = 0.015 \text{ m}$
- Spring preload $F_s = 12 \text{ N}$
- Exhaust-side chamber pressure $P_{ex} \approx 0 \text{ Pa}$ (atmospheric, gauge)

**Step 1: Compute the spool end area exposed to supply pressure.**

$$A_{spool} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_s^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.015)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

*Conversion logic:* Same circular area formula applied to the spool cross-section.

**Step 2: Compute the net hydraulic force pushing the spool to close (against solenoid direction).**

$$F_{hyd} = P_s \cdot A_{spool} = 50 \times 10^5 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$F_{hyd} = 883.6 \text{ N}$$

*Conversion logic:* Pressure × Area = Force (Pascal × m² = Newton). The supply pressure tries to push the spool back to the normal position.

**Step 3: Total force the solenoid must overcome.**

$$F_{sol,min} = F_s + F_{hyd} = 12 + 883.6$$

$$F_{sol,min} \approx 895.6 \text{ N}$$
