Also, in the bleed-off circuit, remember that the cylinder still receives the *throttled* flow; the excess goes to tank — do not confuse this with a meter-out circuit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Supplementary Exam 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of a **pressure-compensated flow control valve** with a neat diagram. Explain how it maintains a constant flow rate irrespective of load pressure variations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A non-pressure-compensated flow control valve is used to control the speed of a hydraulic motor. The motor has a displacement of 50 cc/rev. The pump delivers 40 L/min at 180 bar. The load torque on the motor is 60 Nm. The valve is set to deliver a flow of 25 L/min to the motor. Calculate:
1. The pressure drop across the FCV.
2. The motor speed in rpm.
3. The motor's output power.

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Pressure-Compensated FCV [7 marks]

**Construction (3 marks):**
A pressure-compensated FCV consists of:
1. A **variable metering orifice** (needle/poppet) — manually adjustable to set the desired flow.
2. A **pressure compensator spool** — a floating spool kept in equilibrium by a spring on one side and the downstream (load) pressure on the other.
3. A **drain port** — returns excess oil (bypassed by the compensator) to the tank.
4. **Three ports**: **P** (pressure inlet), **A** (outlet to actuator), **T** (tank drain).
5. An internal **pilot passage** connecting the downstream pressure to the compensator spool's spring-loaded end.

**Working (4 marks):**
- Oil from the pump enters at **P** and passes through the **variable orifice** to port **A**.
- A portion of the oil goes to the actuator; the rest is bled off through the compensator to **T**.
- The **load pressure** at **A** is fed back through the **pilot line** to the compensator spool end opposite the spring.
- If load pressure **increases**, downstream pressure pushes the compensator spool against the spring, **reducing** the drain area. This causes upstream pressure to rise, increasing the **effective pressure drop** $\Delta P$ across the metering orifice. Since $Q = C_d A_v \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$, the *increase* in $\Delta P$ compensates for the *load-induced* drop, keeping the flow constant.
- Conversely, if load pressure **decreases**, the spring pushes the spool, **increasing** the drain area, reducing $\Delta P$ across the orifice, again keeping flow constant.
- The result: **$Q$ is a function of $A_v$ (knob setting) only**, independent of $P_L$.

```
   P (Inlet) ─────┬─────[Variable Orifice]───── A (Outlet)
                  │           ↑
                  │       [Spring-loaded Compensator Spool]
                  │           ↓
                  └──────────[Drain]─── T (Tank)
                              ↑
                  [Pilot line from A → Compensator]
```

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Flow into motor and pressure drop (2 marks):**
Given: $Q = 25$ L/min, pump pressure $P_s = 180$ bar, motor displacement $V_D = 50$ cc/rev.

**Step 2 — Motor pressure $P_m$ (1 mark):**
Load torque $T_L = 60$ Nm. Motor pressure is determined by load:
$$P_m = \frac{2 \pi \cdot T_L}{V_D} = \frac{2 \pi \times 60}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.5398 \times 10^6 \ \text{Pa} \approx 75.4 \ \text{bar}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure drop across FCV (1 mark):**
$$\Delta P_{FCV} = P_s - P_m = 180 - 75.4 = 104.6 \ \text{bar} = 10.46 \ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Motor speed (2 marks):**
$$n = \frac{Q}{V_D} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}/60}{50 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$n = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.333 \ \text{rev/s} = 500 \ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 5 — Motor output power (1 mark):**
$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi n T_L}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 500 \times 60}{60} = \pi \times 1000 = 3141.6 \ \text{W} = 3.14 \ \text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In motor problems, students often confuse **cc/rev** with **L/min conversion**. The displacement in SI must be in **m³/rev**: $50 \ \text{cc/rev} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^3/\text{rev}$. Use $V_D$ in m³/rev and $Q$ in m³/s when applying $n = Q/V_D$ directly. Also, the motor pressure is *not* the pump pressure — it is set by the load torque.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **1. Unit Consistency:** Convert all pressures to **Pa** (N/m²), areas to **m²**, and flow rates to **m³/s** before substituting into formulas. A 14-mark numerical problem can be reduced to 6 marks with one unit slip.
> **2. Area Confusion:** In double-acting cylinders, distinguish between **cap-end (full bore) area** and **rod-end (annulus) area** for extension and retraction calculations.
> **3. Orifice vs Pressure-Compensated:** Do not apply the simple orifice equation to a pressure-compensated FCV in problems where load varies — the compensator maintains $\Delta P$ constant, not the flow through the orifice equation.
> **4. Symbol Notation:** ISO 1219 uses square boxes with two arrows. Pilot-operated valves have a **dashed line** for the pilot. Always include both a *symbol* and a *functional block* in your sketches.
> **5. Cavitation and Aeration:** In meter-in circuits, the return side of the cylinder may develop vacuum — this is **cavitation**, not aeration. Examiner deducts marks for confusing these.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Flow Control Valves & Its Types**

- ✅ **FCV controls flow rate, NOT pressure.** Pressure is governed by the load.
- ✅ The governing equation is $Q = C_d A_v \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- ✅ **Two broad types:** Non-Compensated (simple, cheap, load-dependent) and Pressure-Compensated (expensive, accurate, load-independent).
- ✅ **Pressure-Compensated FCV** has **3 ports** (P, A, T) — never forget the drain port.
- ✅ **Meter-In** = valve on inlet line; risk of cavitation; suitable for positive loads.
- ✅ **Meter-Out** = valve on outlet line; provides back-pressure; **preferred for over-running loads** (load + motion in same direction).
- ✅ **Bleed-Off** = valve shunts excess flow to tank; **most energy-efficient**.
- ✅ **Speed of cylinder** = $v = Q / A_p$; **Speed of motor** = $n = Q / V_D$.
- ✅ **Stroking time** = $t = V_{cyl} / Q = A_p L / Q$.
- ✅ **Throttling power loss** = $P_{loss} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ — dissipated as heat.
- ✅ Standard oil density $\rho \approx 860 \text{–} 880 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ for mineral oils.
- ✅ ISO 1219 symbols: **two arrows meeting at a point** = restriction; **perpendicular arrow with slash** = adjustable.
- ✅ Pressure-compensated FCV: when load rises, compensator **closes** drain → $\Delta P$ rises → flow constant.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark problems: always include a **labeled diagram**, **formula used**, **substitution with units**, and **final answer in sensible engineering units** (bar, L/min, m/s, rpm, kW).
- ✅ Common numericals: bore diameter, rod diameter, stroke, pump flow, load pressure, find time/speed/force.
- ✅ Common confusion: bore ≠ rod diameter; **cap area** = $\pi D^2/4$; **annulus area** = $\pi (D^2 - d^2)/4$.

> **END OF NOTES — Module 3, Topic: Flow Control Valves and Its Types**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proportional Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Proportional Valves # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Proportional Valves ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Proportional Valve (KTU Definition):** A proportional valve is an electro-hydraulic control valve whose output parameter (flow rate, pressure, or direction of fluid flow) varies **continuously and proportionally** to an input electrical reference signal (current $I$ in **mA** or voltage $V$ in **V DC**). It functions as the interface between an electronic controller and a hydraulic actuator, enabling stepless (analog) control rather than the discrete ON/OFF action of conventional solenoid-operated valves. The three principal functional families of proportional valves recognized in the KTU 2024 syllabus are: | Family | Controlled Output | Typical Symbol Tag | |---|---|---| | Proportional Pressure Valve (PPV) | System Pressure $p$ | $p = f(I)$ | | Proportional Flow Control Valve (PFCV) | Flow Rate $Q$ | $Q = f(I)$ | | Proportional Directional Control Valve (PDCV) | Flow Direction & Magnitude | $\theta, Q = f(I)$ | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Bathroom Faucet:** > Think of a normal tap in your house. It has only **two positions** — fully open or fully closed. Now imagine a *magic tap* that has a small dimmer switch (electrical knob). Turn the dimmer to **30 %** and exactly 30 % of water comes out smoothly. Turn it to **80 %** and the flow smoothly becomes 80 %. The dimmer is the **electrical reference signal**, the tap is the **proportional valve**, and the smoothly changing water is the **proportional hydraulic output**. > > In industrial terms, a machine operator sets a desired speed for a hydraulic motor by turning a knob in a control panel. The electronics convert this to a milliamp current, which moves the valve spool by a proportional distance, which in turn allows a proportional flow of oil — and the motor turns at the *exact* desired speed. ## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Feature: Servo vs. Proportional vs. Conventional > [!TIP] > **Where Proportional Valves Sit in the Control Hierarchy:** > > 1. **Conventional Solenoid Valve** — Two states only (ON/OFF). Operated by a binary solenoid coil. > 2. **Proportional Valve** — *Infinitely many* stepless states between fully open and fully closed. Uses a **proportional solenoid** producing a force proportional to current. Usually operates in **open-loop**, with a single spool position feedback (LVDT) internally. > 3. **Servo Valve** — Similar to proportional but with **closed-loop spool position feedback**, much higher dynamic response, lower hysteresis, and finer resolution. Used in high-precision applications like aerospace and machine tools. > > The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies servo valves as a *separate advanced topic*. The proportional valve is the "industrial workhorse" bridging conventional valves and high-end servo valves. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (KTU 2024 Emphasis) - **Standard Reference Signal Range:** $4 \text{ mA} \to 20 \text{ mA}$ (current loop) or $0 \text{ V} \to 10 \text{ V}$ (voltage loop). - **Proportional Solenoid Force:** Typically $0 \text{ N} \to 60 \text{ N}$ or $0 \text{ N} \to 100 \text{ N}$ depending on coil size. - **Hysteresis:** $\leq 5\%$ (industrial grade) or $\leq 1.5\%$ (high-performance grade). - **Response Time:** $20 \text{ ms} \to 50 \text{ ms}$ for step input. - **Operating Pressure:** Up to **$350 \text{ bar}$** standard; up to **$420 \text{ bar}$** in high-pressure variants. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The proportional valve operates on the **force-balance principle** between an electromagnetic force generated by a proportional solenoid and a hydraulic feedback force from the controlled pressure acting on the spool. ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Input Stage:** The electronic controller (PLC / microcontroller) sends a reference current $I_{ref}$ to the proportional solenoid coil. 2. **Electromagnetic Conversion:** The current generates a magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the solenoid. According to Ampere's Law applied to the magnetic circuit: $$F_{mag} = k_{m} \cdot I_{ref}$$ where $k_m$ is the **magnetic force constant** of the solenoid (in N/A). 3. **Spool Displacement:** The electromagnetic force overcomes the opposing spring force $F_{spring} = k_s \cdot x_{spool}$ and moves the valve spool by distance $x_{spool}$. 4. **Hydraulic Output:** The new spool position opens a metering orifice area $A_{eff}$ proportional to $x_{spool}$. By the orifice equation: $$Q = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}}$$ 5. **Force Balance Equilibrium:** At steady state, the electromagnetic force is balanced by the spring force plus the hydraulic flow force on the spool lands. 6. **Closed-Loop Internal Feedback:** A Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) senses the actual spool position $x_{actual}$ and feeds it back to the integrated electronics, which then correct the coil current to achieve the *targeted* spool position. ## 2.2 Mathematical Model of the Proportional Solenoid The governing equation linking electrical input to magnetic force is: $$F_{mag}(t) = \frac{N^2 \cdot \mu_0 \cdot A_{core}}{2 \cdot g^2(t)} \cdot I^2(t)$$ where: - $N$ = number of turns in the solenoid coil - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$ (**permeability of free space**) - $A_{core}$ = cross-sectional area of the armature (m²) - $g(t)$ = variable air-gap length (m) - $I(t)$ = instantaneous coil current (A) > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The force is **quadratic** in current, which is undesirable. To achieve a *linear* relationship $F_{mag} \propto I$, manufacturers either add a **constant bias current** $I_0$ and operate around it, or use a **flux feedback coil** within the proportional solenoid. The result is: > $$F_{mag} = k_1 \cdot I + k_2$$ (linearised form for small signal variations around the operating point). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains the **mandatory, high-yield formulas** for solving any KTU 2024 numerical or descriptive problem on Proportional Valves. | # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Units | When to Apply | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_{mag} = k_m \cdot I$ | $k_m$ = solenoid force constant | N, A | Force from current input | | 2 | $F_{spring} = k_s \cdot x$ | $k_s$ = spring rate, $x$ = spool displacement | N/m, m | Opposing spring force | | 3 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{2\Delta p / \rho}$ | Standard orifice flow equation | m³/s | Flow through proportional orifice | | 4 | $A_{eff} = \pi \cdot d_{spool} \cdot x$ | For cylindrical spool with land width $x$ | m² | Effective metering area | | 5 | $p_{out} = K_p \cdot I_{ref} + p_{min}$ | Proportional pressure output | bar, mA | PPV transfer function | | 6 | $Q_{out} = K_q \cdot I_{ref}$ | Proportional flow output | L/min, mA | PFCV transfer function | | 7 | $Hysteresis \; \% = \dfrac{\vert I_{inc} - I_{dec} \vert}{I_{max}} \times 100$ | $I_{inc}, I_{dec}$ = currents at same output during increase / decrease | %, mA | Quantifies valve non-linearity | | 8 | $Step \; Response \; Time = t_{90\%}$ | Time to reach 90% of final value | ms | Dynamic performance metric | | 9 | $Dither \; Signal = A_{dith} \sin(2\pi f_{dith} t)$ | $A_{dith}$ = amplitude, $f_{dith}$ = frequency (100-200 Hz) | V, Hz | Used to reduce solenoid friction/hysteresis | | 10 | $Bandwidth \; f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau}$ | $\tau$ = system time constant | Hz | Frequency response limit | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe Symbol Note:** In KTU answer scripts, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ notation in handwriting. In printed notes, $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ is preferred over `|` inside markdown tables to avoid rendering issues. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Proportional valves are the **backbone of modern industrial automation** because they enable: - **Energy-efficient** hydraulic systems (variable flow on demand, not constant). - **Soft-start / soft-stop** capability in injection moulding machines, reducing mechanical shock. - **Synchronization** of multiple hydraulic cylinders in large press lines. - **Force / position / velocity control** in mobile hydraulics (excavators, harvesters, cranes). - **Closed-loop pressure / flow regulation** in steel mills, paper machines, and CNC press brakes. > [!TIP] > **Field Example:** In an injection moulding machine, a proportional pressure valve controls the **clamping force** on the mould. The operator sets a percentage on the HMI, the PLC sends $4\text{--}20 \text{ mA}$, the PPV delivers a proportional hydraulic pressure, and the mould closes with the **exact clamping tonnage** — no over-pressure, no under-pressure, no energy waste. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Spool Force Balance Equation The spool of a proportional valve is acted upon by **four forces** simultaneously. Deriving the equilibrium equation is a **guaranteed 14-mark derivation question** in KTU ESE. Below is the complete step-by-step derivation with no shortcuts. ### Step 1 — Identify the Forces Acting on the Spool 1. **Electromagnetic force** from the proportional solenoid: $F_{mag}$ 2. **Spring restoring force** from the centering spring: $F_{spring}$ 3. **Hydraulic flow force** on the spool land: $F_{hyd}$ 4. **Friction force** at the spool bushing: $F_{fric}$ ### Step 2 — Write Newton's Second Law for the Spool For a spool of mass $m$ and displacement $x_{spool}$: $$m \cdot \frac{d^2 x_{spool}}{dt^2} = F_{mag} - F_{spring} - F_{hyd} - F_{fric}$$ ### Step 3 — Express Each Force in Terms of $x_{spool}$ **Spring Force** (Hooke's Law): $$F_{spring} = k_s \cdot x_{spool}$$ **Solenoid Force** (linearized with bias): $$F_{mag} = k_m \cdot (I_{ref} - I_0) + F_{bias}$$ where $I_0$ is the bias current and $F_{bias}$ is the corresponding magnetic force at the bias. **Hydraulic Flow Force** (from momentum change of fluid jet over the spool land): $$F_{hyd} = 2 \cdot C_d \cdot C_v \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta p \cdot \cos\theta$$ For a symmetric spool land geometry, this simplifies to: $$F_{hyd} = K_{hyd} \cdot x_{spool} \cdot \Delta p$$ where $K_{hyd}$ is the **hydraulic stiffness** coefficient. **Friction Force** (Coulomb friction model): $$F_{fric} = \mu \cdot F_{normal} \cdot \text{sgn}(\dot{x}_{spool})$$ For low-speed analysis, this is often linearized as a viscous damping: $$F_{fric} \approx B \cdot \frac{dx_{spool}}{dt}$$ where $B$ is the **viscous damping coefficient**. ### Step 4 — Substitute Back into the Force Balance Substituting all four terms: $$m \cdot \frac{d^2 x_{spool}}{dt^2} = k_m(I_{ref} - I_0) + F_{bias} - k_s \cdot x_{spool} - K_{hyd} \cdot x_{spool} \cdot \Delta p - B \cdot \frac{dx_{spool}}{dt}$$ ### Step 5 — Group Like Terms Rearranging with all $x_{spool}$ terms on one side and the input on the other: $$m \cdot \frac{d^2 x_{spool}}{dt^2} + B \cdot \frac{dx_{spool}}{dt} + (k_s + K_{hyd} \cdot \Delta p) \cdot x_{spool} = k_m \cdot (I_{ref} - I_0) + F_{bias}$$ ### Step 6 — Identify the Standard Second-Order ODE Form This is a **second-order linear ODE** in standard mass-spring-damper form: $$m \ddot{x} + B \dot{x} + K_{eq} \cdot x = K_{input} \cdot u(t) + F_{bias}$$ with: - Equivalent stiffness: $K_{eq} = k_s + K_{hyd} \cdot \Delta p$ - Input gain: $K_{input} = k_m$ - Control input: $u(t) = I_{ref} - I_0$ ### Step 7 — Define Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio By comparing with the canonical form $m\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n m \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 m \cdot x = F(t)$: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_s + K_{hyd} \cdot \Delta p}{m}}$$ $$\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{m(k_s + K_{hyd} \cdot \Delta p)}}$$ ### Step 8 — Steady-State Solution At steady state, $\ddot{x} = 0$ and $\dot{x} = 0$: $$x_{spool,ss} = \dfrac{k_m \cdot (I_{ref} - I_0) + F_{bias}}{k_s + K_{hyd} \cdot \Delta p}$$ This is the **static characteristic equation** of the proportional valve and is the most important equation for KTU Part A questions. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Types of Proportional Valves vs. Characteristics | Characteristic | PPV (Pressure) | PFCV (Flow) | PDCV (Directional) | |---|---|---|---| | **Controlled Variable** | Pressure $p$ | Flow $Q$ | Direction + Flow $Q$ | | **Transfer Function** | $p = K_p I + p_{min}$ | $Q = K_q I$ (at constant $\Delta p$) | $Q = K_{dcv} I$ | | **Spool Movement** | Small axial displacement | Larger displacement | Larger displacement | | **Typical Application** | Clamping force, accumulator charging | Cylinder speed, motor speed | Forward/reverse + speed control | | **Feedback Element** | Pressure sensor in outlet | Flow sensor (orifice + DP cell) | Spool LVDT + downstream sensor | | **Hysteresis (industrial)** | $\le 3\%$ | $\le 5\%$ | $\le 5\%$ | | **Cost Index (relative)** | 1.0× | 1.4× | 1.8× | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style) **Problem:** A proportional pressure valve has a linear transfer characteristic $p = 5I + 2$, where $p$ is in bar and $I$ is in mA. If the input current is increased from $6 \text{ mA}$ to $14 \text{ mA}$, determine: 1. The output pressure at each current level. 2. The change in output pressure. 3. The valve sensitivity in $\text{bar/mA}$. **Solution:** **Part (1):** At $I = 6 \text{ mA}$: $$p_1 = 5 \times 6 + 2 = 30 + 2 = 32 \text{ bar}$$ At $I = 14 \text{ mA}$: $$p_2 = 5 \times 14 + 2 = 70 + 2 = 72 \text{ bar}$$ **Part (2):** Change in pressure: $$\Delta p = p_2 - p_1 = 72 - 32 = 40 \text{ bar}$$ **Part (3):** Valve sensitivity: $$S_v = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\Delta I} = \dfrac{72 - 32}{14 - 6} = \dfrac{40}{8} = 5 \text{ bar/mA}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** The sensitivity equals the slope of the transfer function $p = 5I + 2$, which is $K_p = 5 \text{ bar/mA}$. ✓ This confirms the result. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Proportional Valve System The figure below shows the complete **block-level signal flow** of a closed-loop proportional valve system, from operator input to hydraulic actuator output, including all internal feedback paths. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Operator HMI Input
Setpoint Value] --> B[PLC / Microcontroller
PID Controller] B --> C[Digital to Analog
Converter DAC] C --> D[Current Amplifier
4 to 20 mA Output] D --> E[Proportional Solenoid
Electromagnetic Coil] E --> F[Valve Spool
Moves Proportionally] F --> G[Hydraulic
Orifice Opening] G --> H[Hydraulic
Actuator Output] H --> I[Process Variable
Pressure Flow Position] I --> J[Sensor
Transducer] J --> K[Signal
Conditioner] K --> B F --> L[LVDT
Spool Position Sensor] L --> M[Internal
Feedback Amplifier] M --> E classDef inputNode fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef ctrlNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef valveNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef actNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef fbNode fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 class A,B inputNode class C,D ctrlNode class E,F,G valveNode class H,I actNode class J,K,L,M fbNode ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - The **outer loop** (A → B → ... → I → J → K → B) is the *process control loop*. > - The **inner loop** (F → L → M → E) is the *internal spool position loop* built into the proportional valve electronics. > - This dual-loop architecture gives proportional valves a significant accuracy advantage over open-loop proportional valves. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Sectional Functional Schematic of a Proportional Pressure Relief Valve ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Inlet Port P
Pump Supply] --> CV[Check Valve] CV --> CH[Control Chamber] CH --> SP[Valve Spool] SP --> OP[Outlet Port T
To Tank] CH --> SOL[Proportional Solenoid
Force Fmag] SOL --> SP SP --> SPR[Centering Spring
Force Fspring] SPR --> SP SP --> LVDT2[LVDT Sensor
Reads Spool Position] classDef portNode fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef mechNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef elecNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 class P,OP,CV,CH portNode class SP,SPR mechNode class SOL,LVDT2 elecNode ``` ## 4.3 Symbol Table for Proportional Valve (KTU 2024 Drawing Convention) | Component | Schematic Symbol Description | ISO 1219 Reference | |---|---|---| | Proportional Solenoid | Rectangle on the control port with a diagonal arrow through it | ISO 1219-1 | | Spool Position Feedback | Dashed line returning from spool to electronics block | ISO 1219-2 | | Variable Orifice | Two arrows converging to a point, one straight, one slanted | Standard | | 4/3 Proportional DCV | Square with 3 positions, 4 ports, arrows in middle box | ISO 1219-1 | > [!TIP] > **KTU Drawing Tip:** In your ESE answer scripts, always draw the proportional valve symbol with the **diagonal arrow** crossing the solenoid rectangle. This single mark distinction fetches full marks in the "Draw the symbol" sub-question. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **"Define a proportional valve. How does it differ from a conventional solenoid-operated directional control valve?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** A proportional valve is an electro-hydraulic control valve whose output parameter (flow, pressure, or direction) varies **continuously and proportionally** with an input electrical reference signal (typically $4\text{--}20 \text{ mA}$). - **(1 mark)** A conventional solenoid DCV has only **two discrete states** (ON/OFF) governed by the binary energization of the solenoid. The spool moves to one of two fixed end positions. - **(1 mark)** A proportional valve uses a **proportional solenoid** whose force is proportional to the applied current, allowing the spool to take **any intermediate position** between fully open and fully closed, thus providing **stepless (analog) control**. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"State any three performance characteristics used to evaluate a proportional valve."** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Hysteresis** — Maximum percentage difference between the input signal required to achieve the same output during increasing vs. decreasing input sweeps. Lower is better ($\le 5\%$ industrial, $\le 1.5\%$ high-performance). 2. **Step Response Time ($t_{90\%}$)** — Time taken for the output to reach $90\%$ of its final value after a step input. Typical range $20\text{--}50 \text{ ms}$. 3. **Repeatability** — Ability of the valve to reproduce the same output for the same input over multiple cycles. Expressed as a percentage of full scale. *(Acceptable alternatives: Linearity, Threshold, Resolution, Bandwidth.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the **working principle of a proportional pressure valve**. Derive the steady-state spool force balance equation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A proportional flow control valve has a transfer function $Q = 0.8 I$ where $Q$ is in L/min and $I$ is in mA. The valve is connected to a hydraulic cylinder of area $A = 50 \text{ cm}^2$. The load requires a velocity of $0.4 \text{ m/s}$. Determine: 1. The required flow rate $Q$ in L/min. 2. The required input current $I$ in mA. 3. The hydraulic power supplied if the load pressure is $80 \text{ bar}$. --- ### Model Answer for Question A #### Part (a) — Working Principle + Derivation (7 marks) **Block Diagram (3 marks):** The complete block diagram is given in Section 4.1 of these notes. The student should reproduce a similar flowchart showing: - Input reference signal → Amplifier → Proportional solenoid → Spool displacement → Variable orifice → Pressure/Flow output → Sensor → Feedback to comparator. **Working Principle (2 marks):** 1. The electronic controller sends a current reference $I_{ref}$ (e.g., $4\text{--}20 \text{ mA}$) to the proportional solenoid. 2. The solenoid generates a magnetic force $F_{mag} = k_m \cdot I_{ref}$. 3. The spool moves against the spring force $F_{spring} = k_s \cdot x_{spool}$ and hydraulic flow force. 4. At equilibrium, the effective metering orifice area $A_{eff}$ is proportional to spool displacement, producing an output flow $Q$ given by the orifice equation. 5. An LVDT senses the actual spool position and closes an internal feedback loop, ensuring the spool reaches its target position accurately. **Steady-State Derivation (2 marks):** At steady state, accelerations and velocities are zero. From the force balance derived in Section 3.1: $$x_{spool,ss} = \dfrac{k_m \cdot (I_{ref} - I_0) + F_{bias}}{k_s + K_{hyd} \cdot \Delta p}$$ The corresponding flow output is: $$Q_{out} = C_d \cdot \pi \cdot d_{spool} \cdot x_{spool,ss} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}}$$ Substituting $x_{spool,ss}$ confirms the linear relationship $Q \propto I_{ref}$ in the design operating range. #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** - Transfer function: $Q = 0.8 I$ (Q in L/min, I in mA) - Cylinder area: $A = 50 \text{ cm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ - Required velocity: $v = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$ - Load pressure: $p_L = 80 \text{ bar} = 80 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$ **Part (1) — Required flow rate:** $$Q = A \cdot v = 50 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 = 200 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ Converting to L/min (1 m³/s = $60\,000$ L/min): $$Q = 200 \times 10^{-4} \times 60\,000 = 1200 \text{ L/min}$$ **Part (2) — Required input current:** From the transfer function $Q = 0.8 I$: $$I = \dfrac{Q}{0.8} = \dfrac{1200}{0.8} = 1500 \text{ mA}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is unrealistically high — in practice, a different transfer function gain $K_q$ would be selected. KTU expects students to compute mechanically and comment on the practical feasibility. **Part (3) — Hydraulic power supplied:** $$P = p_L \cdot Q = 80 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \times 200 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$P = 80 \times 10^{5} \times 0.02 = 160\,000 \text{ W} = 160 \text{ kW}$$ **Result Summary:** - $Q = 1200 \text{ L/min}$ - $I = 1500 \text{ mA}$ - $P = 160 \text{ kW}$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **proportional directional control valve (PDCV)**. Compare it with a servo valve in terms of (i) spool feedback, (ii) dynamic response, and (iii) typical applications. **(b) [7 Marks]** A proportional pressure valve is used to control the clamping force in a hydraulic press. The valve has a linear characteristic $p = 8I + 5$ (bar, mA). The cylinder bore diameter is $D = 100 \text{ mm}$. Calculate: 1. The clamping force when $I = 8 \text{ mA}$. 2. The current required to produce a clamping force of $50 \text{ kN}$. 3. The pressure sensitivity in $\text{bar/mA}$ and the force sensitivity in $\text{kN/mA}$. --- ### Model Answer for Question B #### Part (a) — PDCV vs. Servo (7 marks) **Construction & Working (4 marks):** A proportional directional control valve consists of: - A **4/3 spool valve** with machined metering notches on the spool lands. - **Two proportional solenoids** on either side of the spool (one for each flow direction). - **LVDT** for internal spool position feedback to the integrated electronics. - A **dither generator** producing a high-frequency small-amplitude oscillation ($100\text{--}200 \text{ Hz}$) to overcome spool friction and reduce hysteresis. When a current $I$ is applied to solenoid A, the spool moves to the right by an amount proportional to $(I - I_0)$, opening metering edges P→A and B→T. The flow to the cylinder is therefore proportional to $I$. Reversing current to solenoid B reverses the flow direction. **Comparison Table (3 marks — 1 mark per row):** | Feature | Proportional Valve | Servo Valve | |---|---|---| | **(i) Spool Feedback** | Single-stage LVDT; mechanical-electrical hybrid | Dual-stage with mechanical feedback (jet pipe / flapper) or electrical feedback (LVDT + electronic) | | **(ii) Dynamic Response** | Bandwidth $5\text{--}25 \text{ Hz}$, $t_{90\%} \approx 30 \text{ ms}$ | Bandwidth $50\text{--}200 \text{ Hz}$, $t_{90\%} \le 5 \text{ ms}$ | | **(iii) Applications** | Injection moulding, mobile hydraulics, presses, machine tools (low to mid precision) | Aerospace flight controls, CNC precision machines, active suspension, military | #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** - Transfer function: $p = 8I + 5$ (bar, mA) - Bore diameter: $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$ - Cylinder area: $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ **Part (1) — Clamping force at $I = 8 \text{ mA}$:** $$p = 8(8) + 5 = 64 + 5 = 69 \text{ bar} = 69 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$ $$F = p \cdot A = 69 \times 10^{5} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$F = 54.19 \times 10^{3} \text{ N} \approx 54.2 \text{ kN}$$ **Part (2) — Current for $F = 50 \text{ kN}$:** $$p = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.366 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 63.66 \text{ bar}$$ $$I = \dfrac{p - 5}{8} = \dfrac{63.66 - 5}{8} = \dfrac{58.66}{8} = 7.33 \text{ mA}$$ **Part (3) — Sensitivities:** Pressure sensitivity: $$S_p = \dfrac{dp}{dI} = 8 \text{ bar/mA}$$ Force sensitivity: $$S_F = S_p \cdot A = 8 \text{ bar/mA} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 8 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa/mA} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$S_F = 6283 \text{ N/mA} = 6.283 \text{ kN/mA}$$ **Result Summary:** - $F(I=8) = 54.2 \text{ kN}$ - $I(F=50\text{ kN}) = 7.33 \text{ mA}$ - $S_p = 8 \text{ bar/mA}$, $S_F = 6.28 \text{ kN/mA}$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Proportional Valve Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per wrong/missing unit**. Always write units explicitly: bar, mA, L/min, N, kW, m/s. > 2. **Confusing the three proportional valve families** — A common trap question asks "Explain proportional *flow* valve" and students answer about pressure. Know all three: PPV, PFCV, PDCV. > 3. **Skipping the block diagram in 7-mark "Explain" questions** — Examiners allocate **2–3 marks purely for the block diagram/schematic**. A clear, labelled diagram is non-negotiable. > 4. **Writing the orifice equation with wrong constant** — Use $C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2\Delta p / \rho}$ for incompressible flow. The constant $\sqrt{2}$ is inside the square root, **not** outside. > 5. **Forgetting the dither signal explanation** — When asked about hysteresis reduction, mention the **dither** (high-frequency AC superimposed on DC reference). This is a favourite 2-mark add-on. > 6. **Comparing proportional with servo incorrectly** — Servo valves are **closed-loop**; proportional valves are **open-loop with internal spool position feedback only**. Many students incorrectly claim proportional valves are fully closed-loop. > 7. **Sign convention for spool forces** — In the force balance, magnetic force is **positive**, spring and hydraulic forces are **negative** (opposing). Drawing force arrows opposite to the actual direction costs marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Proportional Valves (Module 3)** - **Definition:** Electro-hydraulic valve giving **stepless, proportional** output to an electrical input. - **Three Families:** PPV (pressure), PFCV (flow), PDCV (direction + flow). - **Standard Signal:** $4\text{--}20 \text{ mA}$ current loop or $0\text{--}10 \text{ V}$ DC voltage. - **Proportional Solenoid:** Produces force $F_{mag} = k_m \cdot I$ (linearized). Uses a **dither** signal to overcome friction. - **Key Components:** Proportional solenoid, centered spool, LVDT feedback, integrated electronics, dither generator. - **Force Balance:** $F_{mag} = F_{spring} + F_{hydraulic} + F_{friction}$ at equilibrium. - **Transfer Function Forms:** - PPV: $p = K_p \cdot I + p_{min}$ - PFCV: $Q = K_q \cdot I$ (at constant $\Delta p$) - **Performance Metrics:** Hysteresis ($\le 5\%$), step response ($20\text{--}50 \text{ ms}$), bandwidth, repeatability, linearity, threshold. - **Dither:** Low-amplitude, high-frequency ($\sim 100\text{--}200 \text{ Hz}$) AC signal superimposed on the DC command to **reduce static friction and hysteresis**. - **LVDT:** Linear Variable Differential Transformer — senses spool position and feeds back to electronics for accurate positioning. - **Proportional vs Servo:** - Proportional → open-loop, single-stage, lower cost, mid precision, industrial. - Servo → closed-loop, dual-stage, higher cost, high precision, aerospace / CNC. - **Applications:** Injection moulding, mobile hydraulics, press brakes, steel mills, die casting, machine tools, harvester automation. - **Energy Efficiency:** Proportional valves deliver **flow on demand**, reducing throttling losses by up to **60 %** compared to conventional metering systems. - **KTU Hot Topics:** Force balance derivation, transfer function graphs, hysteresis loop explanation, dither signal concept, block diagram of closed-loop system, numerical on sensitivity and transfer function. - **Safety Note:** Always install a **pressure-relief valve** upstream of a proportional pressure valve to protect against over-pressure transients. - **Standard Reference:** ISO 1219-1 (symbols), ISO 4401 (mounting interfaces), VDMA 24562 (performance testing). - **Key Math:** $F_{mag} = k_m I$, $F_{spring} = k_s x$, $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2\Delta p / \rho}$, $Hysteresis = \Delta I / I_{max} \times 100$. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy for Proportional Valves:** > 1. Always begin a 7-mark "Explain" question with a **labelled block diagram**. > 2. Always include the **transfer function equation** in a 14-mark question, even if not explicitly asked. > 3. For numericals, **re-check unit conversions** ($\text{cm}^2 \to \text{m}^2$, $\text{bar} \to \text{Pa}$, $\text{m}^3/\text{s} \to \text{L/min}$). > 4. Memorize the **force balance derivation** — it is the single most probable 7-mark analytical question in Module 3.

topics.title: Servo Valves: Mechanical Type and Electrohydraulic Servo Valves
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Servo Valves: Mechanical Type and Electrohydraulic Servo Valves

# 🎯 Module 3 — Servo Valves: Mechanical Type and Electrohydraulic Servo Valves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic belongs to **Module 3 – Control & Actuation Elements** of PCMRT503. Servo valves are *the* high-precision interface between an electronic control signal and a hydraulic actuator. Expect 14-mark ESE questions involving constructional sketches, stage-wise force/torque balance, and block-diagram-based transfer function analysis.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Servo Valve?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **servo valve** is a *closed-loop, multi-stage, high-bandwidth hydraulic control valve* that precisely regulates fluid flow (and hence actuator position/velocity/force) in proportion to a *low-power input signal* — either a mechanical deflection (mechanical type) or an electrical current (electrohydraulic type). Unlike a proportional valve, a servo valve uses **internal mechanical feedback** to achieve static accuracy better than **±0.5%** and frequency response of **100–500 Hz**.

**Key distinguishing features (Board-exam favourite):**

| Feature | Proportional Valve | Servo Valve |
|---|---|---|
| Internal Feedback | ❌ None (external LVDT only) | ✅ Built-in mechanical feedback |
| Stages | Usually 1 | Always 2 (or 3) |
| Bandwidth | 5–25 Hz | 50–500 Hz |
| Hysteresis | 3–6 % | < 0.5 % |
| Response | Moderate | Very fast |
| Cost | Low | High |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steering Wheel" Intuition 🚗

Imagine driving a car:
- **Ordinary proportional valve** = Turning the steering wheel by *feel*. You overshoot, correct, overshoot again (hunting).
- **Mechanical servo valve** = Turning the wheel with a *hydraulic power-steering boost*. Small hand movement → big wheel force, *and* the steering linkage feeds back the wheel's actual angle so the boost stops exactly where you want.
- **Electrohydraulic servo valve (EHSV)** = Same as above, but your "hand movement" is replaced by a **tiny electrical current** (mA) sent to an electromagnetic coil. The valve itself does the rest — it is a *power amplifier with position feedback*.

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule of Servo Valves:** *Signal in → Hydraulic power out — automatically corrected to match the signal.* The "automatically corrected" part is the feedback — without it, you only have an *open-loop* proportional valve, not a true servo valve.

### 1.3 Classification of Servo Valves (KTU Module 3 Scope)

```
Servo Valves
├── 1. Mechanical (Hydraulic) Servo Valves
│       └── Jet-Pipe Type (Single/Double Stage)
│
└── 2. Electrohydraulic Servo Valves (EHSV)
        ├── (a) Single-stage Nozzle-Flapper
        ├── (b) Two-stage Nozzle-Flapper  ← MOST COMMON
        │       └── With mechanical feedback (spool position)
        ├── (c) Two-stage Jet-Pipe driven by torque motor
        └── (d) Three-stage (EHSV + booster)
```

### 1.4 Why Servo Valves Matter (Engineering Utility)

- **Aerospace**: Flight control surfaces (actuator bandwidth > 100 Hz).
- **Machine tools**: CNC axis positioning to **±1 µm**.
- **Steel mills**: Roll-gap control under 1000-ton loads.
- **Injection moulding**: Clamp force profiling.
- **Ship steering / gun turrets**: Smooth, repeatable motion under varying load.

Standard reference metric used throughout industry: **Null Bias ≤ 3 %**, **Resolution ≤ 0.1 %**, **Threshold ≤ 0.5 %** of rated flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanical (Jet-Pipe) Servo Valve

> [!NOTE]
> A **mechanical servo valve** uses a *purely hydraulic* amplification principle — no electricity. The input is a **mechanical displacement** (lever pull, pedal push, cam profile) applied to a small deflection nozzle called a *jet pipe*.

#### 2.1.1 Construction (Two-Stage Jet-Pipe)

1. **Pilot Stage (1st stage)**:
   - **Jet pipe (receiver)** — a small movable nozzle that can swivel ±0.5 mm.
   - Two receiving ports (control ports) of a double-acting slave cylinder.
   - Filtered supply at constant pressure $P_s$ (typically **210 bar**).
2. **Power Stage (2nd stage)**:
   - Four-way sliding spool with land edges.
   - Spool lands cover/uncover ports to actuator (A, B) and tank (T).
3. **Feedback Linkage**:
   - A rigid lever/feedback spring connects the *spool displacement* back to the *jet-pipe nozzle*.
   - This is the heart of the closed loop.

#### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Null (equilibrium):** The jet pipe aims exactly at the *null receiver slot* between the two control ports. Pressures $P_1 = P_2$, so the spool stays centred (hydraulic null). No flow → no actuator motion.

1. **Input**: Operator pushes input lever by $x_i$ → jet pipe tip deflects by $x_j$.
2. **Pressure Differential**: The jet now impinges asymmetrically on the two receiver ports. Pressure in the *near* port rises; pressure in the *far* port drops.
3. **Pressure Error Signal**: $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 \neq 0$ drives the spool.
4. **Spool Motion**: Spool moves by $x_v$ → fluid flows to actuator → load moves.
5. **Mechanical Feedback**: Feedback spring (or lever) pulls the jet pipe *back* toward null.
6. **New Equilibrium**: Spool stops when feedback moment = input moment.

#### 2.1.3 Governing Equations (Jet-Pipe Stage)

The pressure difference generated by a deflected jet pipe is given by:

$$\Delta P_{jet} = \frac{\partial P}{\partial x_j} \cdot x_j$$

where the **jet-pipe sensitivity** $K_j = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x_j}$ depends on supply pressure, receiver geometry, and fluid density. Typical value: $K_j \approx 5 \text{ to } 20 \text{ bar/mm}$.

The power-stage spool equation (Newton's 2nd law for the spool):

$$m_s \ddot{x_v} + B_s \dot{x_v} + K_{sp} x_v = A_{sp} \, \Delta P_{jet} - F_{load,eq}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why jet-pipe valves are robust:** The jet pipe is just a *tube with a hole* — it can tolerate *very dirty fluid* (up to NAS 10) compared to nozzle-flapper valves (need NAS 6 or cleaner). This is why jet-pipe servos dominate steel mills and marine steering.

#### 2.1.4 Advantages & Limitations of Mechanical Servo Valves

| ✅ Advantages | ❌ Limitations |
|---|---|
| Insensitive to fluid contamination | Slow bandwidth (10–30 Hz) |
| High static stiffness | Limited to mechanical input only |
| Simple, rugged, field-repairable | Cannot interface directly with electronics |
| Handles high flow (up to 1500 lpm) | Larger size & weight |

---

### 2.2 Electrohydraulic Servo Valve (EHSV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** 90 % of exam questions on servo valves concern the **Two-Stage Nozzle-Flapper EHSV** with **mechanical feedback spring**. Memorise its block diagram — it comes up every year.

#### 2.2.1 Block-Level Architecture (Two-Stage EHSV)

```
 ┌────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐
 │  TORQUE MOTOR  │───▶│  NOZZLE-FLAPPER      │───▶│  SPOOL (2nd)     │──▶ Flow to Load
 │ (electrical →  │    │  (1st stage hydraulic│    │  (power stage +  │
 │  mechanical    │    │   amplifier)         │    │  feedback spring)│
 │  torque)       │    │                      │    │                  │
 └────────────────┘    └──────────────────────┘    └──────────────────┘
        ▲                                                │
        │                                                │
        └────── Mechanical Feedback (bending spring) ────┘
```

The valve is a **cascade of two amplifiers**:
- **Stage 1 (Torque motor + Nozzle-Flapper)**: $i$ (mA) → torque → flapper deflection → $\Delta P$ across spool ends.
- **Stage 2 (Spool)**: $\Delta P$ → spool motion $x_v$ → flow $Q_L$ to actuator.
- **Feedback**: A *bending spring* (cantilever) couples $x_v$ back to the flapper, closing the inner loop.

#### 2.2.2 Stage 1 — Torque Motor

- A pair of polarized permanent magnets + an armature + a coil.
- Current $i$ through coil produces a *differential flux* → torque $T_m$ rotates the armature by angle $\theta$.
- Armature is rigidly attached to the **flapper** (a flexible arm with a ball/sphere tip sealing two nozzles).
- **Torque equation:**

$$T_m = K_t \cdot i$$

$$J_m \ddot{\theta} + B_m \dot{\theta} + K_{sp} \theta = T_m - T_{fb}$$

where:
- $K_t$ = motor torque constant (Nm/A) — typically **0.05–0.2 Nm/A**
- $J_m$ = armature moment of inertia
- $B_m$ = viscous damping
- $K_{sp}$ = bending spring stiffness
- $T_{fb}$ = feedback torque from spool via spring

#### 2.2.3 Stage 1 — Nozzle-Flapper Hydraulic Amplifier

- Two fixed **nozzles** face the flapper.
- Orifice areas: $A_{N1}, A_{N2}$.
- Supply pressure $P_s$ → fixed orifice (orifice $\Omega$) → splits into two nozzle paths.
- Pressure under each nozzle: $P_1, P_2$.

If the flapper moves **toward** nozzle 1 by $\Delta x_f$:

$$P_1 \uparrow \quad \text{and} \quad P_2 \downarrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta P = P_1 - P_2 \neq 0$$

The **flow gain** of the flapper-nozzle stage is:

$$K_{nf} = \frac{\partial (\Delta P)}{\partial x_f} \quad [\text{bar/mm}]$$

Typical: **40–100 bar/mm** (very high gain — that's why we need a feedback spring to stabilise it).

#### 2.2.4 Stage 2 — Sliding Spool with Mechanical Feedback

The spool has **two control lands** at its ends exposed to $P_1$ and $P_2$:

$$F_{spool} = A_{sp}(P_1 - P_2) = A_{sp} \cdot \Delta P$$

Spool equation of motion:

$$m_v \ddot{x_v} + B_v \dot{x_v} = A_{sp} \cdot \Delta P - F_{load,eq} - F_{spring}$$

The feedback spring (a thin cantilever or hair spring) connects the **spool mid-stroke to the flapper tip**:

$$x_f = x_{f,signal} - x_{f,fb} = \theta \cdot r_{arm} - K_{fb} \cdot x_v$$

#### 2.2.5 Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Block Diagram)

For small-signal analysis, each stage is a first-order lag:

$$\frac{x_v(s)}{i(s)} = \frac{K_v}{\left(1 + \tau_e s\right)\left(1 + \tau_v s\right)}$$

where:
- $K_v$ = overall valve gain (m/A) — typically **0.5–5 mm/mA**
- $\tau_e$ = electrical time constant of torque motor (≈ **0.005–0.02 s**)
- $\tau_v$ = spool/second-stage time constant (≈ **0.003–0.01 s**)

The -3 dB **bandwidth** of the valve is:

$$f_{-3dB} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi(\tau_e + \tau_v)} \quad [\text{Hz}]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam shortcut:** A modern EHSV has $-3$ dB bandwidth of **50–500 Hz**. If your calculation gives < 20 Hz, you have made an error in the time-constant identification.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table — these are the equations the 14-mark questions expect.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_m = K_t \cdot i$ | Torque motor torque from current | Nm |
| 2 | $T_m = K_{sp}\theta + B_m \dot{\theta} + J_m \ddot{\theta}$ | Armature torque balance | Nm |
| 3 | $\Delta P = K_{nf} \cdot x_f$ | Nozzle-flapper pressure gain | bar |
| 4 | $A_{sp} \cdot \Delta P = m_v \ddot{x_v} + B_v \dot{x_v} + F_{fb}$ | Spool force balance | N |
| 5 | $x_f = r \cdot \theta - K_{fb} \cdot x_v$ | Flapper net deflection (signal − feedback) | mm |
| 6 | $Q_L = K_q \cdot x_v - K_c \cdot P_L$ | Spool flow equation (linearised) | lpm |
| 7 | $P_L = \dfrac{Q_L - Q_{load}}{...}$ | Load pressure dynamics | bar |
| 8 | $K_v = \dfrac{K_t \cdot K_{nf} \cdot K_{spool}}{K_{sp} \cdot K_{fb}}$ | Overall valve gain | mm/mA |
| 9 | $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi(\tau_e + \tau_v)}$ | Valve bandwidth | Hz |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{hyd,out}}{P_{hyd,in} \cdot \eta_{mech}}$ | Overall efficiency | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **No "|" symbols used** — the table above uses only **safe** characters; where absolute value is needed, write $\vert x \vert$ using `\vert`.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Derivations are not for rote — they are to test *whether you understand the feedback principle*. Show every algebraic step, define every symbol, and end with a clear physical interpretation.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Spool Displacement vs Input Current

**Goal:** Find the closed-form relationship $x_v(i)$ for a two-stage EHSV at steady state (all time derivatives = 0).

**Step 1 — Write the torque balance on the armature (steady state):**

At equilibrium, $\ddot{\theta} = 0$ and $\dot{\theta} = 0$. The torque equation becomes:

$$T_m = K_{sp} \cdot \theta + T_{fb}$$

The feedback spring transmits the spool force $F_{fb}$ back to the armature tip. Let the moment arm of the feedback spring on the armature be $r_{fb}$. Then:

$$T_{fb} = F_{fb} \cdot r_{fb} = (K_{fb} \cdot x_v) \cdot r_{fb}$$

**Step 2 — Relate flapper deflection to armature angle:**

The flapper is rigidly attached to the armature; the flapper tip is at radius $r$ from the pivot:

$$x_f = r \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3 — Write the spool force balance (steady state, no load):**

At steady state with zero load force, the spool has stopped. So $\ddot{x_v} = 0$ and $\dot{x_v} = 0$:

$$A_{sp} \cdot \Delta P = F_{fb} = K_{fb} \cdot x_v$$

**Step 4 — Nozzle-flapper gain:**

$$\Delta P = K_{nf} \cdot x_f = K_{nf} \cdot r \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 4 into Step 3:**

$$A_{sp} \cdot K_{nf} \cdot r \cdot \theta = K_{fb} \cdot x_v$$

Solve for $\theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{K_{fb} \cdot x_v}{A_{sp} \cdot K_{nf} \cdot r}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into Step 1:**

$$K_t \cdot i = K_{sp} \cdot \theta + K_{fb} \cdot x_v \cdot r_{fb}$$

$$K_t \cdot i = K_{sp} \left( \frac{K_{fb} \cdot x_v}{A_{sp} \cdot K_{nf} \cdot r} \right) + K_{fb} \cdot x_v \cdot r_{fb}$$

**Step 7 — Factor $x_v$:**

$$K_t \cdot i = x_v \left[ \frac{K_{sp} \cdot K_{fb}}{A_{sp} \cdot K_{nf} \cdot r} + K_{fb} \cdot r_{fb} \right]$$

**Step 8 — Final closed-form result:**

$$\boxed{\;x_v = \frac{K_t \cdot i}{\dfrac{K_{sp} \cdot K_{fb}}{A_{sp} \cdot K_{nf} \cdot r} \; + \; K_{fb} \cdot r_{fb}}\;}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- The **denominator** represents the total *mechanical stiffness* restoring the armature.
- $K_{fb}$ appears twice — once from spool feedback path, once from mechanical-spring restoring.
- $K_{nf}$ in the denominator means **higher nozzle-flapper gain → more stable but less sensitive**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall Valve Transfer Function

**Goal:** Derive $\dfrac{Q_L(s)}{I(s)}$ for the two-stage EHSV.

**Step 1 — Torque motor transfer function (2nd order):**

From $J_m \ddot{\theta} + B_m \dot{\theta} + K_{sp}\theta = K_t i - K_{fb} r_{fb} x_v$:

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{T_{net}(s)} = \frac{1}{J_m s^2 + B_m s + K_{sp}}$$

**Step 2 — Flapper deflection (algebraic):**

$$x_f(s) = r \cdot \theta(s) - K_{fb} \cdot x_v(s)$$

**Step 3 — Pressure difference:**

$$\Delta P(s) = K_{nf} \cdot x_f(s)$$

**Step 4 — Spool equation:**

$$x_v(s) = \frac{A_{sp}}{m_v s^2 + B_v s} \left[ \Delta P(s) - \frac{F_{load}}{A_{sp}} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 4:**

$$x_v(s) = \frac{A_{sp} \cdot K_{nf}}{m_v s^2 + B_v s} \left[ r \cdot \theta(s) - K_{fb} \cdot x_v(s) - \frac{F_{load}}{A_{sp} K_{nf}} \right]$$

**Step 6 — Collect $x_v$ on the left:**

$$x_v \left[ 1 + \frac{A_{sp} K_{nf} K_{fb}}{m_v s^2 + B_v s} \right] = \frac{A_{sp} K_{nf} r}{m_v s^2 + B_v s} \, \theta(s) - \frac{F_{load}}{m_v s^2 + B_v s}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $x_v / \theta$:**

$$\frac{x_v(s)}{\theta(s)} = \frac{A_{sp} K_{nf} r}{m_v s^2 + B_v s + A_{sp} K_{nf} K_{fb}}$$

**Step 8 — Multiply with $\theta / i$ (using Step 1, neglecting $x_v$ feedback in torque-motor eqn for simplicity):**

$$\frac{x_v(s)}{i(s)} = \frac{K_t \cdot A_{sp} K_{nf} r}{(J_m s^2 + B_m s + K_{sp})(m_v s^2 + B_v s + A_{sp} K_{nf} K_{fb})}$$

**Step 9 — Dominant-pole approximation:**

In a well-designed EHSV, the torque motor pole is much faster than the spool pole. We can simplify:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{x_v(s)}{i(s)} \approx \frac{K_v}{(\tau_e s + 1)(\tau_v s + 1)}\;}$$

where $K_v = \dfrac{K_t A_{sp} K_{nf} r}{K_{sp} K_{fb}}$, $\tau_e = \dfrac{B_m}{K_{sp}}$ and $\tau_v = \dfrac{B_v}{A_{sp} K_{nf} K_{fb}}$.

**Step 10 — Flow transfer function:**

Using the standard linearised spool equation $Q_L = K_q x_v - K_c P_L$ and load dynamics, the **flow-to-current** transfer function becomes:

$$\frac{Q_L(s)}{I(s)} = \frac{K_{qv}}{(\tau_e s + 1)(\tau_v s + 1)} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + s \tau_L}$$

where $K_{qv} = K_q K_v$ and $\tau_L$ = load time constant.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Jet-Pipe Servo Steady-State Gain

For the **mechanical (jet-pipe) servo**, the steady-state relation between input lever displacement $x_i$ and spool displacement $x_v$:

The jet-pipe deflection $x_j$ is set by the input lever (linkage ratio $\alpha$):

$$x_j = \alpha \cdot x_i - \beta \cdot x_v$$

Pressure difference generated by jet pipe (empirical):

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \rho Q_s^2}{\pi^2 d_r^4} \cdot \sin(\phi) \approx K_j \cdot x_j$$

For small deflections, $\sin(\phi) \approx \tan(\phi) = x_j / L_r$, so:

$$\Delta P = K_j \cdot x_j = K_j (\alpha x_i - \beta x_v)$$

Spool equation (steady state):

$$A_{sp} \Delta P = K_{sp2} \cdot x_v \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_v = \frac{A_{sp} \Delta P}{K_{sp2}}$$

Substituting:

$$x_v = \frac{A_{sp} K_j (\alpha x_i - \beta x_v)}{K_{sp2}}$$

$$x_v \left( 1 + \frac{A_{sp} K_j \beta}{K_{sp2}} \right) = \frac{A_{sp} K_j \alpha}{K_{sp2}} \, x_i$$

$$\boxed{\;x_v = \frac{A_{sp} K_j \alpha}{K_{sp2} + A_{sp} K_j \beta} \, x_i\;}$$

**Interpretation:** When feedback $\beta \rightarrow 0$, $x_v \to \infty$ (open-loop — unstable). When $\beta$ is large, $x_v \to 0$ (over-damped). The designer's job is to pick $\beta$ such that closed-loop bandwidth is maximised without oscillation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Comparison

| Parameter | Jet-Pipe Mechanical | Nozzle-Flapper EHSV (2-stage) | Jet-Pipe EHSV (2-stage) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Mechanical displacement | Electrical current (mA) | Electrical current (mA) |
| **1st Stage** | Jet-pipe deflection | Nozzle-flapper | Jet-pipe |
| **Sensitivity $K_{nf}$ or $K_j$** | 5–20 bar/mm | 40–100 bar/mm | 8–25 bar/mm |
| **Bandwidth** | 10–30 Hz | 50–250 Hz | 30–80 Hz |
| **Fluid Cleanliness** | NAS 10 (tolerant) | NAS 6 (demanding) | NAS 8 (moderate) |
| **Hysteresis** | < 1 % | < 0.5 % | < 0.7 % |
| **Power Consumption** | None (hydraulic only) | 50–200 mA | 50–200 mA |
| **Typical Use** | Marine, Steel mills | Machine tools, Aerospace | Mobile, Marine |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | High | Medium–High |

### 4.4 Component-Level Architecture of a Two-Stage EHSV

```mermaid
graph TB

        nodeCoil[Coil Armature] --> nodeMagnet[Permanent Magnet]
        nodeMagnet --> nodeFlapperTip[Flapper Tip]
    end
    subgraph NF[NOZZLE FLAPPER STAGE]
        nodeN1[Nozzle 1] --> nodeP1[Pressure P1]
        nodeN2[Nozzle 2] --> nodeP2[Pressure P2]

        nodeOrifice --> nodeN2
    end
    subgraph SP[SECOND STAGE SPOOL]

        nodeSpoolBody --> nodeLand2[End Land P2]

```

### 4.5 Working Sequence — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Null
    Null --> Deflected: Current i applied
    Deflected --> TorqueGenerated: Torque motor produces Tm
    TorqueGenerated --> FlapperMoves: Flapper deflects
    FlapperMoves --> PressureImbalance: P1 rises P2 falls
    PressureImbalance --> SpoolShifts: Spool moves xv
    SpoolShifts --> FlowToActuator: QL to load
    FlowToActuator --> FeedbackEngages: Feedback spring bends
    FeedbackEngages --> FlapperCounter: Flapper moves back
    FlapperCounter --> NewEquilibrium: New null at reduced flapper deflection
    NewEquilibrium --> Null: i removed and feedback restores
```
