# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"List any four functions of an FRL unit in a pneumatic system. Why is a lubricator not required in modern oil-free pneumatic circuits?"**

**Model Answer (Marks Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1):**

The **FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator)** unit performs the following four primary functions:

1. **Filtration:** Removes dust, rust, pipe scale, and condensed water from compressed air (down to 5 µm typically).
2. **Pressure Regulation:** Reduces compressor pressure (typically 7 bar) to the working pressure of the circuit (typically 5–6 bar) and maintains it constant.
3. **Lubrication:** Injects a fine atomized mist of oil into the airstream to provide boundary-layer lubrication to downstream moving parts (cylinders, valves, motors).
4. **Moisture Separation:** The filter bowl also acts as a primary bulk-water separator via centrifugal action.

**Why a lubricator is unnecessary in modern oil-free circuits:** Modern pneumatic components use **self-lubricating materials** such as PTFE (Teflon) seals, sintered bronze bearings, and pre-lubricated composite materials. These materials do not require oil from the air supply, eliminating the need for a lubricator and ensuring **ISO 8573-1 Class 1 oil-free** air for sensitive applications such as food, pharmaceutical, and semiconductor industries. **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define Pressure Dew Point (PDP). State the typical PDP values of a refrigeration dryer and a desiccant dryer."**

**Model Answer:**

**Pressure Dew Point (PDP):** The temperature at which water vapor in compressed air begins to condense into liquid water, measured at the working pressure of the system. **[1 mark]**

**Typical PDP values:**
- **Refrigeration Dryer:** $+2\,°C$ to $+5\,°C$ **[1 mark]**
- **Desiccant (Adsorption) Dryer:** $-20\,°C$ to $-70\,°C$ **[1 mark]**

A lower PDP indicates drier air. Desiccant dryers are mandatory in cold-climate applications (e.g., European winters, cold-storage warehouses) where refrigeration-dryer air would still condense and freeze inside valves.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle of a centrifugal-type air filter**. Mention any **two micron ratings** used in industry. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A compressor delivers **2000 Nl/min** of air at **8 bar (gauge)**, **35 °C**, and **relative humidity 75%**. The air passes through a refrigeration dryer that cools it to **+5 °C** and discharges it saturated. Calculate:
   (i) Mass flow rate of dry air per hour. **[3 marks]**
   (ii) Mass of water removed by the dryer per hour. **[4 marks]**

**Given Data:**
- $\rho_{air, NTP} = 1.293$ kg/m³
- $P_{sat, 35°C} = 5.628$ kPa
- $P_{sat, 5°C} = 0.873$ kPa
- $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Centrifugal Air Filter Working Principle **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Inlet and Tangential Entry [1 mark]:**
Unfiltered compressed air enters the filter body through a **tangential inlet slot**. The angled entry induces a **rotational / vortex motion** in the airstream, similar to water draining from a sink.

**Step 2 — Centrifugal Separation [2 marks]:**
Heavier contaminants — **liquid water droplets, large dust particles, and rust flakes** — possess greater mass. The centrifugal force ($F_c = m v^2 / r$) acting on them exceeds the drag force of the airstream, so they are flung **radially outward** against the **transparent polycarbonate / metal bowl wall**.

**Step 3 — Coalescence and Sump Collection [1 mark]:**
The droplets coalesce (merge) on the bowl wall and trickle **downward** into a **sump** at the bottom of the bowl. The sump is periodically emptied via an **auto-drain valve** or **manual petcock**.

**Step 4 — Secondary Filtration (Optional) [1 mark]:**
The partially cleaned air then passes through a **sintered bronze or felt element** (5 µm rating) to capture finer particles missed by the centrifugal stage.

**Step 5 — Clean Air Outlet [1 mark]:**
The cleaned air exits axially through the outlet port and proceeds to the regulator.

**Micron Ratings:** **25 µm** (coarse/general) and **5 µm** (standard FRL) **[1 mark]**

**Sketch (Describe verbally for exam):**
Show inlet (tangential), bowl with vortex arrows, filter element (vertical), sump at bottom, drain valve, outlet at top. A typical **clear bowl** allows visual inspection of the water level.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: Convert gauge to absolute pressure [0.5 mark]**

$$P_{abs} = 8 + 1.013 = 9.013 \text{ bar} = 901.3 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Inlet partial pressure of water vapor [0.5 mark]**

$$P_{v,in} = RH \times P_{sat,35} = 0.75 \times 5.628 = 4.221 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Inlet humidity ratio [1 mark]**

$$w_{in} = 0.622 \times \frac{4.221}{901.3 - 4.221} = 0.622 \times \frac{4.221}{897.08}$$

$$w_{in} = 0.622 \times 0.004706 = 0.002927 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 4: Outlet humidity ratio (saturated at 5 °C) [1 mark]**

$$w_{out} = 0.622 \times \frac{0.873}{901.3 - 0.873} = 0.622 \times \frac{0.873}{900.43}$$

$$w_{out} = 0.622 \times 0.000969 = 0.000603 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 5: Mass flow rate of dry air per hour [3 marks]**

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 2000 \text{ Nl/min} = 120{,}000 \text{ Nl/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{dry\,air} = 120{,}000 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \times 1.293 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 155.16 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 6: Mass of water removed per hour [1 mark]**

$$\dot{m}_{water} = 155.16 \times (0.002927 - 0.000603)$$

$$\dot{m}_{water} = 155.16 \times 0.002324 = 0.3606 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Final Answer:** The dryer removes approximately **0.36 kg of water per hour** (≈ 360 ml/h).

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **working of a diaphragm-type pressure regulator**. Define the term **"droop"** and state its significance. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A pneumatic system requires **5 bar (gauge) regulated pressure** at the actuator. The compressor delivers **7 bar (gauge)** with fluctuations of **±0.5 bar**. The required flow rate is **1500 Nl/min**. Determine:
   (i) The **minimum C_v** of the regulator assuming $\Delta P_{min} = 0.5$ bar. **[3 marks]**
   (ii) Whether a standard regulator with $C_v = 2.0$ is suitable. **[1 mark]**
   (iii) List **two methods** to reduce droop in a regulator. **[3 marks]**

**Given:** $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar, $C_v$ formula for air: $C_v = \dfrac{Q_{scfm}}{22.7 \sqrt{\Delta P \cdot \dfrac{P_{up} + P_{down}}{2 P_{atm}}}}$

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Diaphragm Pressure Regulator [7 marks]

**Components [2 marks]:**
- **Adjusting screw and spring** — sets the desired setpoint
- **Diaphragm** — flexible rubber/fabric disc that senses downstream pressure
- **Stem and poppet/valve** — modulates flow
- **Vent port** — relieves downstream pressure when setpoint is reduced
- **Pressure gauge port**

**Working Principle [4 marks]:**

1. The user rotates the **adjusting screw**, which compresses the **range spring** by a known amount $x$. The spring force $F_s = kx$ pushes the diaphragm and stem downward, opening the poppet valve.
2. Air flows from upstream (compressor side) to downstream (load side). As downstream pressure builds, it acts on the **underside of the diaphragm**, generating an upward force $F_d = P_{down} \times A_{dia}$.
3. **Equilibrium** is reached when $F_s = F_d$. The poppet partially closes, throttling the flow to maintain $P_{down}$ constant.
4. If downstream pressure **rises** (e.g., a downstream valve closes), the diaphragm rises, further closing the poppet — **self-regulating action**.
5. If downstream pressure **falls** (e.g., a cylinder strokes), the spring pushes the diaphragm down, opening the poppet wider — restoring pressure.

**Droop Definition [1 mark]:**
Droop is the **drop in downstream pressure as flow demand increases** from no-load to full-load. Mathematically:

$$\text{Droop} = P_{down,\,no\,load} - P_{down,\,full\,load}$$

**Significance:** Lower droop = better regulation. In critical circuits (clamping, precision indexing), high droop causes **inconsistent actuation force** and **variable cycle times**.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]

**Step 1: Convert flow to SCFM [1 mark]**

Standard airflow at 14.7 psia, 68 °F (1.013 bar, 20 °C). 1 Nm³/h = 0.589 SCFM.

$$Q_{Nl/min} = 1500 \text{ Nl/min} = 90{,}000 \text{ Nl/h}$$

$$Q_{scfm} = \frac{90{,}000 \times 0.589}{60} = 883.5 \text{ SCFM}$$

**Step 2: Compute pressure terms [1 mark]**

$$P_{up} = 7 + 1.013 = 8.013 \text{ bar (abs)}$$

$$P_{down} = 5 + 1.013 = 6.013 \text{ bar (abs)}$$

$$\frac{P_{up} + P_{down}}{2 P_{atm}} = \frac{8.013 + 6.013}{2 \times 1.013} = \frac{14.026}{2.026} = 6.923$$

$$\sqrt{\Delta P \cdot 6.923} = \sqrt{0.5 \times 6.923} = \sqrt{3.462} = 1.861$$

**Step 3: Compute minimum C_v [1 mark]**

$$C_{v,min} = \frac{883.5}{22.7 \times 1.861} = \frac{883.5}{42.24} = 20.92$$

**Step 4: Suitability check [1 mark]**

A standard regulator with $C_v = 2.0$ is **grossly undersized**. The required $C_v \approx 21$. A regulator with $C_v \geq 21$ (or two $C_v = 11$ units in parallel) must be selected.

**Step 5: Methods to reduce droop [3 marks — 1.5 each]**

1. **Use a pilot-operated regulator:** A small primary regulator senses downstream pressure and modulates a larger secondary valve via pilot pressure. This reduces droop to < 0.05 bar.
2. **Increase diaphragm area:** Per $P_{down} = kx/A_{dia}$, a larger diaphragm area produces a greater force for the same pressure, improving sensitivity and reducing droop. Alternatively, use a **dome-loaded** regulator where the spring force is replaced by a higher-pressure reference gas signal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Using gauge pressure instead of absolute pressure in the $C_v$ formula. This gives a $C_v$ that is **~7× too small**, leading to incorrect undersizing.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert Nm³/min to SCFM. The empirical $C_v$ constant (22.7) is **strictly** for SCFM.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Mixing up RH with humidity ratio. RH (%) is **not** a mass fraction.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Treating the lubricator's venturi as a *pump* — it is a **pressure-driven eductor** and cannot lift oil against gravity if inlet pressure is below 1 bar.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Air Treatment**

- ☐ **FRL sequence is fixed:** Filter → Regulator → Lubricator. Never rearrange.
- ☐ **Filter stages:** Centrifugal → Impingement → Element (in that order).
- ☐ **Standard micron rating:** **5 µm** for general pneumatic FRL; **1 µm** for instrumentation; **0.01 µm** for sterile/medical.
- ☐ **Filter element replacement:** When $\Delta P$ exceeds **0.3–0.7 bar** above clean drop.
- ☐ **Two dryer types:** Refrigeration (PDP +3 °C) and Desiccant (PDP −40 °C).
- ☐ **Desiccant dryer purges ~15% of dried air** for regeneration — operational cost.
- ☐ **Refrigeration dryers cannot reach below 0 °C PDP** (water would freeze on evaporator).
- ☐ **Lubricator principle:** Venturi (Bernoulli) — requires air flow to function.
- ☐ **Oil feed rate:** 1–5 drops/min per 1000 Nl/min. Use **ISO VG 32 instrumentation oil** (no detergent additives).
- ☐ **Regulator force balance:** $P_{down} = kx/A_{dia}$. At equilibrium.
- ☐ **Droop** = drop in $P_{down}$ from no-load to full-load. Lower = better.
- ☐ **Pilot-operated regulators** have droop < 0.05 bar; direct-operated have 0.1–0.5 bar.
- ☐ **ISO 8573-1** defines purity classes: particulates, water (PDP), and oil.
- ☐ **C_v formula for air:** Always use **absolute pressures** and convert flow to **SCFM**.
- ☐ **Compressor aftercooler** is the **first** moisture removal stage (primary cooling), FRL filter is the **second** (centrifugal).
- ☐ **Modern trend:** Oil-free compressors + oil-free components → **lubricator elimination**.
- ☐ **Auto-drain valves** are mandatory in hard-to-access filter bowls (overhead piping).
- ☐ **KTU 2024 numerical favourite:** Humidity-ratio-based water removal calculation (must know $P_{sat}$ values for 5 °C, 30 °C, 35 °C).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressor Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compressor Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **compressor** is a mechanical device that converts low-pressure air (or gas) into high-pressure air by **reducing its specific volume** through the application of external work. In the context of **Industrial Hydraulics & Pneumatics**, compressors form the *prime mover source* of a pneumatic power system, supplying compressed air to actuators, valves, and tools.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** A compressor is a machine used to **compress air/gas** from a lower pressure to a higher pressure, and it is the **prime source of energy** in a pneumatic system. The compressed air is stored in a receiver tank and supplied on demand.

### 1.2 Broad Classification of Compressors

Compressors are fundamentally classified based on the **method used to compress the fluid** and the **direction of fluid flow** during compression.

| Primary Class | Sub-Class | Example | Operating Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive Displacement (PD)** | Reciprocating | Piston, Diaphragm | Volume reduction by piston motion |
| **Positive Displacement (PD)** | Rotary | Vane, Screw, Lobe | Volume reduction by rotating elements |
| **Dynamic (Turbo)** | Centrifugal | Radial impeller | Velocity → Pressure conversion |
| **Dynamic (Turbo)** | Axial | Multi-stage bladed rotor | Velocity → Pressure in axial direction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, primary emphasis is placed on **positive displacement compressors** (especially reciprocating and rotary types) as they are the workhorses of industrial pneumatic installations. Dynamic compressors are covered for completeness and contrast.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **compressor as a "bicycle pump"**:
- A bicycle pump has a **piston** moving inside a **cylinder** to squeeze air into the tire — this mimics a **reciprocating compressor**.
- Now imagine multiple balloons being squeezed in a row by a rotating wheel — this mimics a **rotary compressor**.
- Finally, picture a **spinning disc throwing water outward** against a curved wall, where speed becomes pressure — this mimics a **centrifugal (dynamic) compressor**.

The "feel" of all compressors is the same: **work in, pressure out**.

### 1.4 Standards and Physical Constants Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Conditions (as per IS 4167 / ISO 1217):**
> - Inlet conditions: **T₁ = 293 K (20 °C)**, **P₁ = 1.013 bar (1 atm)**
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
> - **Specific heat ratio for air:** γ = 1.4
> - **Mechanical equivalent:** 1 kW = 1.341 HP

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Compression (Step-by-Step)

The compression process in any compressor follows **three fundamental thermodynamic stages**:

**Step 1 — Suction (Induction):**
- The inlet valve opens; atmospheric air is drawn into the compression chamber.
- Pressure inside chamber ≈ atmospheric pressure (P₁).
- Volume of air admitted depends on chamber size and clearance.

**Step 2 — Compression:**
- Mechanical work is applied to **reduce the volume** of the trapped air.
- Air temperature rises (per adiabatic/polytropic laws).
- Both pressure and temperature increase: P₁ → P₂, T₁ → T₂.

**Step 3 — Delivery (Discharge):**
- Once chamber pressure exceeds delivery line pressure, the **discharge valve** opens.
- High-pressure air is pushed into the receiver tank for storage.

> [!NOTE]
> The **clearance volume** (Vc) — the small volume left after piston reaches top dead center — is a critical parameter in reciprocating compressors. It determines **volumetric efficiency**.

### 2.2 Classification Tree (Hierarchical Logic)

```text
COMPRESSORS
├── Based on Displacement Principle
│   ├── POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT (PD)
│   │   ├── Reciprocating Type
│   │   │   ├── Single-acting
│   │   │   ├── Double-acting
│   │   │   └── Multi-stage
│   │   └── Rotary Type
│   │       ├── Rotary Vane Compressor
│   │       ├── Screw (Twin-Screw) Compressor
│   │       └── Lobe (Roots Blower)
│   │
│   └── DYNAMIC (Non-PD / Turbo)
│       ├── Centrifugal
│       └── Axial Flow
│
└── Based on Number of Stages
    ├── Single-Stage (pressure ratio up to ~8:1)
    └── Multi-Stage (intercooled, ratio > 8:1)
```

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-favorite equations** for the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the units and the variable definitions before attempting numerical problems.

**Table 1: Compressor Performance Formulas**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure Ratio: $R_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | $P_2$ = discharge, $P_1$ = suction | dimensionless |
| 2 | Volumetric Efficiency: $\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$ | C = clearance ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | Clearance Ratio: $C = \dfrac{V_c}{V_s}$ | $V_c$ = clearance volume, $V_s$ = swept volume | dimensionless |
| 4 | Swept Volume: $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \cdot n$ (reciprocating) | D = bore, L = stroke, n = number of cylinders | m³ |
| 5 | Isothermal Work: $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | For ideal isothermal compression | J |
| 6 | Adiabatic (Isentropic) Work: $W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | $\gamma$ = 1.4 for air | J |
| 7 | Polytropic Work: $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | n = polytropic index (1 < n < γ) | J |
| 8 | Isothermal Efficiency: $\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}}$ | actual work input | dimensionless |
| 9 | FAD (Free Air Delivery): $FAD = V_s \cdot n_r \cdot \eta_v$ | $n_r$ = rpm, $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency | m³/s |
| 10 | Mean Effective Pressure: $MEP = \dfrac{W}{V_s}$ | W = work per cycle | Pa |
| 11 | Compressor Power: $P = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot W_{specific}}{\eta_{mech}}$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate | W (Watts) |
| 12 | Multi-stage Optimum Pressure Ratio: $r_{opt} = \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_s}\right)^{1/N}$ | N = number of stages, $P_d$ = delivery, $P_s$ = suction | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** In all formulas above, **vertical bars** are written using `\left(` and `\right)` or `\dfrac` to avoid markdown table-breaking pipe characters. Do not write absolute values as `|x|` — use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` instead.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reciprocating compressors:** Heavy industries, CNG refueling, high-pressure pneumatic tools (up to 350 bar).
- **Rotary screw compressors:** Continuous-duty industrial plants, paint shops, food & pharma (oil-free versions).
- **Centrifugal compressors:** Large-scale plants, gas turbines, oil & gas pipelines.
- **Roots blowers:** Aeration in wastewater treatment, pneumatic conveying of powders.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Types of Compressors (Analytical Breakdown)

#### 3.1.1 Reciprocating (Piston) Compressor — Construction & Working

A reciprocating compressor uses a **piston driven by a crankshaft** inside a cylinder. Air is compressed in discrete strokes.

**Key Components:**
- Cylinder, Piston, Suction valve, Discharge valve, Connecting rod, Crankshaft, Flywheel, Cylinder head, Clearance volume.

**Working Cycle (4 strokes of single-acting, single-cylinder):**

| Stroke | Piston Motion | Valve Status | Process |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | Downward | Suction opens | Air drawn in |
| 2nd | Upward | Both closed | Air compressed |
| 3rd | Upward | Discharge opens | Air delivered |
| 4th | Downward | Both closed | Re-expansion of clearance air |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **double-acting** reciprocating compressors, compression occurs on **both sides of the piston** (head-end and crank-end), effectively doubling the output per cycle.

**Schematic Diagram of Single-Acting Reciprocating Compressor:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air] -->|P1=1.013 bar| B[Suction Valve]
    B --> C[Compression Cylinder]
    C -->|Piston Up - Compression| D[High Pressure Air]
    D --> E[Discharge Valve]
    E --> F[Receiver Tank]
    C -.Clearance Volume Vc.-> C
    G[Crankshaft] ---|Mechanical Drive| C
```

#### 3.1.2 Rotary Vane Compressor

A **cylindrical rotor** with sliding vanes is mounted **off-center** inside a stator bore. As the rotor spins, vanes slide out by centrifugal force, creating expanding/contracting pockets.

- **Intake pocket:** Rotor moves away from stator → volume increases → low pressure air drawn in.
- **Compression pocket:** Rotor approaches stator → volume decreases → air compressed.
- **Discharge pocket:** Compressed air pushed out at the port.

**Advantages:** Compact, vibration-free, continuous airflow.
**Limitations:** Vanes wear out; oil-flooded versions for sealing.

#### 3.1.3 Rotary Screw Compressor

Two **intermeshing helical rotors** (male and female) rotate in opposite directions. Air is trapped in the spaces between the lobes and the casing, then carried from inlet to outlet with progressive volume reduction.

- **Dry (oil-free)** and **oil-flooded** variants.
- Used for **continuous-duty** industrial applications (4 to 15 kW typical).
- Pressure ratios up to **13:1** in single stage.

#### 3.1.4 Lobe (Roots) Compressor

Two figure-8-shaped rotors (lobes) rotate inside a casing **without touching** each other or the walls. Air is *swept* from inlet to outlet, but **not internally compressed** during this transit. The actual compression occurs due to back-pressure in the discharge line.

- Low-pressure, high-volume air mover.
- Used in aeration, pneumatic conveying, and as **superchargers**.

#### 3.1.5 Centrifugal Compressor

A **high-speed impeller** (typically 10,000–30,000 rpm) accelerates air radially outward. The kinetic energy is then converted to pressure energy in a **diffuser** and a **volute casing**.

- Dynamic (non-PD) compressor.
- Smooth, pulse-free flow.
- Single-stage pressure ratio limited to ~4:1.

#### 3.1.6 Axial Flow Compressor

Air flows **parallel to the axis of rotation** through successive rows of **rotor blades** (rotating) and **stator blades** (stationary). Each stage provides a small pressure rise; many stages are stacked to reach high overall pressure ratios.

- Used in **jet engines, gas turbines, large industrial air plants**.
- Very high mass flow rate, multi-stage required.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Volumetric Efficiency of a Reciprocating Compressor

Let us derive the **volumetric efficiency** formula from first principles, as it is a 14-mark KTU favorite.

**Given:**
- $V_s$ = Swept (displacement) volume of the cylinder
- $V_c$ = Clearance volume (volume at top dead center)
- $P_1$ = Suction (induction) pressure
- $P_2$ = Discharge pressure

**Step 1: Define Clearance Ratio**

The clearance ratio is the ratio of clearance volume to swept volume:

$$
C = \dfrac{V_c}{V_s}
$$

**Step 2: Effect of Clearance on Effective Suction**

When the piston moves up after discharge, the clearance air at $P_2$ re-expands polytropically (or isentropically) until the cylinder pressure falls to $P_1$. This re-expansion volume $V_4$ (at $P_1$) is:

$$
V_4 = V_c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}
$$

**Step 3: Effective Suction Volume**

The actual volume of fresh air drawn in is the swept volume minus the re-expanded clearance air:

$$
V_{eff} = V_s - V_4 = V_s - V_c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}
$$

**Step 4: Volumetric Efficiency Definition**

$$
\eta_v = \dfrac{V_{eff}}{V_s} = \dfrac{V_s - V_c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}}{V_s}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute $C = V_c / V_s$**

$$
\eta_v = 1 - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The extra "+C" appears when expressing $\eta_v$ in terms of the **expansion ratio** (with V_c added to numerator and denominator appropriately). This form is also acceptable per textbook conventions. **KTU 2024 accepts both forms** depending on the textbook reference.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Adiabatic (Isentropic) Work of Compression

**Assumption:** Compression is **reversible and adiabatic** (no heat transfer), with air as an ideal gas.

**First Law for Closed System (per unit mass):**

$$
W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV \quad \text{(work done ON the gas)}
$$

**For an isentropic process of an ideal gas:**

$$
P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant} = P_1 V_1^{\gamma}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{V^{\gamma}}
$$

**Substitute P in the work integral:**

$$
W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{V^{\gamma}} \, dV
$$

**Step-by-step integration:**

$$
W = -P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-\gamma} \, dV
$$

$$
W = -P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \left[ \dfrac{V^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}
$$

$$
W = -\dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \left[ V_2^{1-\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma} \right]
$$

**Step: Substitute back $P_2 V_2^{\gamma} = P_1 V_1^{\gamma}$:**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

**Final expression for work per unit mass (or per cycle, using $P_1V_1$):**

$$
\boxed{W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]}
$$

**Power required (for mass flow rate $\dot{m}$):**

$$
P_{ad} = \dot{m} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Compressor Types

| Feature | Reciprocating | Rotary Vane | Screw | Centrifugal | Axial |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | PD | PD | PD | Dynamic | Dynamic |
| **Flow** | Pulsating | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous |
| **Pressure Ratio** | Up to 12:1/stage | Up to 6:1 | Up to 13:1 | Up to 4:1 | Up to 1.5:1/stage |
| **Max Pressure (bar)** | 350 | 10 | 13 | 50 | 30+ |
| **Capacity (m³/min)** | 0.1–100 | 0.5–30 | 1–50 | 100–1000+ | 1000+ |
| **Efficiency** | High (70–85%) | Medium (60–70%) | High (70–85%) | Medium (65–75%) | High (80–90%) |
| **Maintenance** | High | Medium | Low | Medium | High |
| **Noise/ Vibration** | High | Low | Low | Low | Low |
| **Best Use** | High-pressure, intermittent | Small industries | Continuous industrial | Large plants | Turbomachinery |
| **Cost** | Low (small size) | Medium | High | Very High | Highest |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Compressor Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[COMPRESSOR PRIME MOVER] --> B[Positive Displacement]
    A --> C[Dynamic Turbo]
    
    B --> D[Reciprocating]
    B --> E[Rotary]
    
    D --> D1[Single Acting]
    D --> D2[Double Acting]
    D --> D3[Multi Stage]
    
    E --> E1[Rotary Vane]
    E --> E2[Screw Type]
    E --> E3[Lobe Roots]
    
    C --> F1[Centrifugal Radial]
    C --> F2[Axial Flow]
    
    D1 --> APP1[CNG Refueling]
    D2 --> APP2[High Pressure Tools]
    D3 --> APP3[Petrochemical]
    
    E1 --> APP4[Workshop Air]
    E2 --> APP5[Industrial Plant]
    E3 --> APP6[Aeration Plants]
    
    F1 --> APP7[Gas Turbine]
    F2 --> APP8[Jet Engine]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Compressed Air System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air] --> B[Air Intake Filter]
    B --> C[Compressor Unit]
    C --> D[After Cooler]
    D --> E[Moisture Separator]
    E --> F[Receiver Tank]
    F --> G[Dryer]
    G --> H[Pressure Regulator]
    H --> I[Distribution Line]
    I --> J[Pneumatic Actuator]
    J --> K[Work Output]
    F -.Pressure Gauge.-> F
    C -.Safety Valve.-> C
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Reciprocating Compressor Cycle States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S1
    S1: Stroke 1: Suction Inlet Open
    S2: Stroke 2: Compression Valves Closed
    S3: Stroke 3: Delivery Discharge Open
    S4: Stroke 4: Re Expansion of Clearance Air
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S1
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Rotary Screw Compressor Internal View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Air Inlet Port] --> B[Intermeshing Rotors Male and Female]
    B --> C[Trapped Air Pocket Shrinking]
    C --> D[Discharge Port]
    B -.Lubricant Injection.-> B
    B -.Oil Separator.-> D
    D --> E[Receiver Tank]
    E --> F[Cooling System]
    F --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams use **alphanumeric prefixed node IDs** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and **raw uppercase labels** to comply with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguards.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**Q1. Define a compressor and distinguish between a positive displacement and a dynamic compressor.**

**Model Answer:**
A compressor is a mechanical device that raises the pressure of a gas (typically air) by reducing its volume through the application of external work.

| Feature | Positive Displacement (PD) | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Traps a fixed volume and mechanically reduces it | Accelerates fluid, converts velocity to pressure |
| Flow | Pulsating (reciprocating) or smooth (rotary) | Smooth and continuous |
| Pressure ratio | High (achievable in single stage) | Low per stage, multi-stage required |
| Examples | Piston, Vane, Screw, Lobe | Centrifugal, Axial |
| Capacity behavior | Independent of pressure | Capacity drops with rising pressure |

---

**Q2. What is clearance volume in a reciprocating compressor, and why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
The **clearance volume ($V_c$)** is the small volume of air that remains trapped in the cylinder when the piston reaches its top dead center (TDC). It is necessary to:
1. Prevent the piston from striking the cylinder head.
2. Allow for thermal expansion of metal components.
3. Accommodate valve mechanism space.

However, excessive clearance **reduces volumetric efficiency** because the trapped air re-expands and occupies part of the swept volume during the next suction stroke.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Model Paper 2024 – Module 4]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **single-acting reciprocating air compressor**. Mention its applications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor** with a clearance volume. A single-stage compressor has a swept volume of 0.005 m³ and a clearance volume of 0.0002 m³. If the suction pressure is 1 bar and discharge pressure is 7 bar (assume isentropic compression with γ = 1.4), determine the volumetric efficiency. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working** (write in points; the diagram is shown in Section 4.3):

1. A **cylinder** houses a **piston** connected via a **connecting rod** to a **crankshaft** driven by an electric motor or engine.
2. The cylinder has two **automatic valves** — a suction valve at the top (atmosphere side) and a discharge valve leading to the receiver.
3. The space above the piston at TDC is the **clearance volume ($V_c$)**.

**Working Cycle:**

- **Suction Stroke:** Piston moves down → pressure inside drops below atmospheric → suction valve opens → air drawn in.
- **Compression Stroke:** Piston moves up → both valves closed → air compressed until cylinder pressure exceeds receiver pressure.
- **Discharge Stroke:** Discharge valve opens → air pushed into receiver.
- **Re-expansion Stroke:** Piston moves down → discharge valve closes → clearance air re-expands to suction pressure → cycle repeats.

**Applications:** Pneumatic tools, CNG refueling stations, starting air for diesel engines, spray painting, sandblasting.

**(b) Volumetric Efficiency Derivation and Calculation:**

**Formula:**

$$
\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}
$$

**Step 1: Compute Clearance Ratio C**

$$
C = \dfrac{V_c}{V_s} = \dfrac{0.0002}{0.005} = 0.04
$$

**Step 2: Compute Pressure Ratio**

$$
R_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{7}{1} = 7
$$

**Step 3: Compute Expansion Term**

$$
\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma} = 7^{1/1.4} = 7^{0.7143}
$$

Taking logarithms:

$$
\log(7^{0.7143}) = 0.7143 \times \log(7) = 0.7143 \times 0.8451 = 0.6036
$$

Therefore:

$$
7^{0.7143} = 10^{0.6036} \approx 4.015
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into Volumetric Efficiency Equation**

$$
\eta_v = 1 + 0.04 - 0.04 \times 4.015
$$

$$
\eta_v = 1.04 - 0.1606
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_v \approx 0.8794 \text{ or } 87.94\%}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **rotary screw compressor** with a labeled diagram. Compare it with a **reciprocating compressor** on the basis of efficiency, maintenance, noise, and pressure ratio. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses air from 1 bar to 25 bar. Calculate the **optimum intermediate pressure** for minimum work, assuming perfect intercooling (T₂ = T₁) and isentropic compression with γ = 1.4. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Rotary Screw Compressor — Working & Comparison:**

**Working Principle:** Two helical rotors (one male/lobe, one female/groove) mesh inside a casing. As they rotate, air trapped in the interlobe spaces is carried from the inlet port to the discharge port, with progressive volume reduction. A lubricant (oil) is injected for sealing, cooling, and noise reduction in **oil-flooded** variants.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Rotary Screw | Reciprocating |
|---|---|---|
| Volumetric Efficiency | 85–95% | 60–80% |
| Maintenance | Low (fewer parts) | High (valves, pistons) |
| Noise Level | 70–80 dB | 85–95 dB |
| Pressure Ratio (single stage) | Up to 13:1 | Up to 12:1 |
| Flow Pulsation | Negligible | High |
| Capital Cost | Higher | Lower |

**(b) Optimum Intermediate Pressure for Two-Stage Compression:**

**Given:** $P_1 = 1$ bar (suction), $P_2 = 25$ bar (discharge), $\gamma = 1.4$, N = 2 stages.

**Formula for Optimum Intermediate Pressure ($P_i$):**

$$
P_i = \sqrt{P_1 \times P_2}
$$

**Derivation (Brief):** Total work is minimized when each stage does equal work, which requires equal pressure ratios:

$$
r_1 = r_2 = r_{opt} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/N}
$$

With $r_1 = P_i / P_1$ and $r_2 = P_2 / P_i$:

$$
\dfrac{P_i}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_i} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_i^2 = P_1 \times P_2
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
P_i = \sqrt{1 \times 25} = \sqrt{25}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_i = 5 \text{ bar}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write $P_i = (P_1 + P_2)/2$ (arithmetic mean). The **correct** formula is the **geometric mean**, since the work term involves logarithms of pressure ratios.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the exponent $1/\gamma$ in the volumetric efficiency formula — it is **NOT** $1/\gamma$ when using the actual $\eta_v$ formula with expansion; double-check your textbook's definition of "C".
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **isentropic** (adiabatic, $\gamma = 1.4$) with **polytropic** ($n$ varies) processes. KTU 2024 problems specify which to use.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Mixing up units — $P_1, P_2$ must be in **absolute** pressure (bar or Pa), not gauge pressure, when applying $R_p$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Prep):**

- ✅ A **compressor** is the *prime source* of energy in a pneumatic system; it raises air pressure via volume reduction.
- ✅ **Positive Displacement (PD)** compressors trap and compress a fixed mass of air. **Dynamic** compressors use kinetic energy conversion.
- ✅ **PD types:** Reciprocating (piston), Rotary (vane, screw, lobe).
- ✅ **Dynamic types:** Centrifugal (radial), Axial flow.
- ✅ **Clearance volume ($V_c$)** is necessary mechanically but **reduces volumetric efficiency**.
- ✅ **Clearance ratio:** $C = V_c / V_s$. **Volumetric efficiency:** $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/\gamma}$.
- ✅ **Work of compression:**
  - Isothermal: $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ — *minimum possible work*.
  - Adiabatic: $W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$.
  - Actual work lies between isothermal and adiabatic.
- ✅ **Multi-stage compression** with intercooling reduces work and lowers discharge temperature.
- ✅ **Optimum intermediate pressure:** $P_i = \sqrt{P_1 \times P_2}$ (geometric mean) for 2-stage systems.
- ✅ **Free Air Delivery (FAD):** $FAD = V_s \cdot n_r \cdot \eta_v$ — the *actual* air supplied at inlet conditions.
- ✅ **Reciprocating compressors:** Highest pressure capability (up to 350 bar), high efficiency, but pulsating flow.
- ✅ **Rotary screw compressors:** Best for continuous-duty industrial use; oil-flooded or oil-free variants.
- ✅ **Centrifugal compressors:** High flow, low per-stage pressure ratio (≈4:1), used in large plants and gas turbines.
- ✅ **Roots blowers:** *Displacement* of air, not internal compression; used for low-pressure, high-volume applications.
- ✅ **Memorize units:** Volume in m³, Pressure in Pa (or bar, with conversion $1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa), Work in Joules, Power in Watts.

> [!TIP]
> **Top 3 High-Weightage Topics for KTU 2024 Module 4:**
> 1. Volumetric efficiency derivation (14-mark favorite).
> 2. Work of compression (isothermal vs. adiabatic comparison).
> 3. Multi-stage compression and optimum intermediate pressure.
> Practice these three with numerical values until fluent.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatics and Hydraulics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pneumatics and Hydraulics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4 Focus):** This module transitions the learner from pure fluid power theory (covered in Modules 1–3) into the practical engineering application layer — **Pneumatic & Hydraulic Circuit Design, Components, and System Integration.**

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Pneumatics

**Pneumatics** is the branch of engineering that deals with the use of **compressed air** (or other inert gases) as the working fluid to transmit and control energy in industrial, automotive, and process-control applications. The term originates from the Greek word *pneuma*, meaning "wind" or "breath."

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Definition (KTU Terminology):** Pneumatics is a section of fluid power in which the working medium is a **compressible gas (air)**. The energy transmitted is a function of compressed-air **pressure** and **flow rate**, and the typical operating pressure range in industry is **4 bar to 8 bar** (commonly 6 bar).

The energy equation governing any pneumatic system is:

$$P_{supply} = P_{atmosphere} + \Delta P_{system}$$

where the supply pressure is built up against the **standard atmospheric pressure of 1.01325 bar (101.325 kPa)**.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Hydraulics

**Hydraulics** is the branch of fluid power engineering that uses **incompressible liquids** (almost always petroleum-based mineral oils, though water-glycol and synthetic fluids are also used) as the working medium to transmit energy and do mechanical work through pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Definition (KTU Terminology):** Hydraulics deals with the transmission of power using **incompressible fluids** in a closed, sealed circuit. Typical industrial operating pressures range from **100 bar to 350 bar**, with peak pressures occasionally reaching **700 bar** in specialised presses and clamping systems.

The fundamental property of hydraulics — that pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction — was formalised by **Pascal's Law (1653)**:

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h + P_{applied}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pneumatic vs. Hydraulic" Real-World Comparison

### 🚗 The Car Tyre Analogy (Pneumatics)
Imagine squeezing the trigger of a paint-spray gun at a workshop. A small electric motor drives a piston inside a cylinder, compressing ordinary **air** to about **6 bar**. This compressed air is then routed through plastic tubes to a small piston which pushes the trigger mechanism. Notice that the air **sprays back out** after the work is done — it is not recycled. This is the essence of pneumatics: **cheap, clean, fast, but limited in force and precision.**

### 🏗️ The JCB Backhoe Analogy (Hydraulics)
Now picture a JCB excavator lifting a 1-tonne concrete slab. Tiny hand movements on a joystick direct **pressurised mineral oil** (at ~200 bar) through steel pipes into a large hydraulic cylinder, which pushes with **25 tonnes of force** to lift the slab. The oil returns to the tank and is **re-circulated** — it never leaves the system. This is hydraulics: **high force, precise control, but expensive and messy if a hose bursts.**

### 💡 The One-Sentence Intuition
> *Pneumatics is the "lightweight sprinter" of industry — fast, cheap, clean, but limited to low force (sprays, gripping, low-load automation). Hydraulics is the "heavyweight lifter" — slow, expensive, but capable of enormous forces (presses, lifts, heavy machinery).*

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Industry-Standard Values)

| Parameter | Pneumatics | Hydraulics |
|---|---|---|
| Working medium | Atmospheric air (filtered, dried) | Mineral oil (ISO VG 32 / 46 / 68) |
| Compressibility | Highly compressible (γ = 1.4) | Effectively incompressible |
| Operating pressure | 4 – 8 bar (typical 6 bar) | 100 – 350 bar (typical 200 bar) |
| Bulk Modulus | ~1.4 × 10⁵ kPa (variable) | ~1.7 × 10⁶ kPa (oil) |
| Density | ~1.2 kg/m³ (at 20 °C) | ~870 kg/m³ (typical oil) |
| Viscosity | ~0.018 mPa·s (air) | ~32 – 68 mm²/s (kinematic) |
| Force per cm² | ~60 N (at 6 bar) | ~2000 N (at 200 bar) |
| Speed of actuation | Very high (1 – 5 m/s) | Moderate (0.1 – 1 m/s) |
| Cost of medium | Free | ₹80 – 250 per litre |
| Cleanliness | Inherently clean | Prone to leaks / contamination |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever you introduce a system in the answer script, **always state the working medium, pressure range, and bulk modulus** in the first paragraph. Examiners give 0.5 – 1 free marks just for this!

## 1.5 Salient Differences — At a Glance

| Property | Pneumatics | Hydraulics |
|---|---|---|
| Energy storage | Easy (in pressure vessel) | Difficult (accumulators needed) |
| Fire/explosion safety | Safe | Oil is flammable |
| Lubrication | Self-lubricating (oil mist) | Requires careful fluid mgmt |
| Temperature sensitivity | High (condensation issues) | Moderate |
| Noise | Loud (mufflers needed) | Quiet |
| Positioning accuracy | Poor (compressibility) | Excellent (stiffness) |
| Maintenance | Easy (modular FRL) | Specialist required |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do **not** write "Pneumatics uses liquids" or "Hydraulics uses air" — this is the most heavily penalised factual error in KTU valuation. Memorise: **Pneumatics = air, Hydraulics = oil.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Physics Governing Both Systems

Both pneumatics and hydraulics are governed by the same underlying principle: **energy stored in a pressurised fluid is converted into mechanical work** via a force-multiplying element (cylinder) or a torque-producing element (motor). The governing physics is unified by two laws:

1. **Pascal's Law** (pressure transmission in confined fluids)
2. **Continuity Equation** (conservation of mass / volume flow)

### 2.1.1 Pascal's Law (Operational Logic)
> *"Pressure applied to any part of a confined fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid."*

This law is what enables a small input force on a small piston to generate a massive output force on a large piston — provided the fluid is incompressible (or nearly so). For hydraulics, this is exact. For pneumatics, it is **approximate** because air compresses.

### 2.1.2 Continuity Equation
For a steady, incompressible flow through a pipe or valve:

$$Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = \text{constant}$$

For a compressible pneumatic system, we must use **mass flow rate** rather than volumetric flow:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$$

## 2.2 The Pneumatic Power Transmission Chain

A complete industrial pneumatic system has **five** functional blocks. KTU examiners frequently ask the student to "explain a pneumatic system with a neat block diagram" — you must know all five.

### Block 1: Air Intake & Pre-Filtration
Atmospheric air is drawn in through an **intake filter** (typically 5 – 10 µm). This is the *first defence* against dust.

### Block 2: Compression (Air Compressor)
The **compressor** (reciprocating, screw, or scroll type) raises air pressure from ~1 bar to **6 – 8 bar**. The power required for compression (for a single-stage reciprocating compressor) is:

$$P_{comp} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot p_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right] \quad \text{(W)}$$

where:
- $n = 1.35$ (polytropic index, between adiabatic and isothermal)
- $p_1$ = intake pressure (Pa)
- $V_1$ = intake volume per cycle (m³)
- $p_2$ = delivery pressure (Pa)

### Block 3: Cooling & Moisture Removal
Compressed air is **hot** (~150 °C at the discharge). It is passed through an **after-cooler** and a **moisture separator (drier)**. This is critical — water in a pneumatic line causes **corrosion, valve sticking, and product spoilage** in food/pharma industries.

### Block 4: Air Receiver (Storage Tank)
The **receiver tank** smooths pulsations, stores energy, and provides a buffer. A typical sizing rule:

$$V_{receiver} = \frac{Q \cdot t}{p_{max} - p_{min}} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{)}$$

where $Q$ is the compressor flow rate (m³/min) and $t$ is the allowed cycle time.

### Block 5: FRL Unit — The Heart of Pneumatic Conditioning
**FRL = Filter + Regulator + Lubricator** (assembled in this exact order, always).

| Component | Function | Typical Specification |
|---|---|---|
| **Filter** | Removes dirt, rust, condensed water | 5 – 40 µm filtration, with auto-drain |
| **Regulator** | Reduces pressure to a stable downstream value | 0 – 10 bar adjustable, with gauge |
| **Lubricator** | Atomises oil into the airstream to lubricate moving parts | Oil flow 1 – 10 drops/min, 100 cSt oil |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why FRL Order Matters:** Filter first (clean the air → prevent contamination of regulator), then Regulator (set pressure → protect downstream components), then Lubricator (oil droplets must enter a **moving** airstream downstream of regulation, otherwise the regulator's needle seat will clog with oil). **Reverse order = system failure within weeks.**

### Block 6: Control Valves
- **3/2 Way Valve** — 3 ports, 2 positions (used for single-acting cylinders)
- **4/2 Way Valve** — 4 ports, 2 positions (used for double-acting cylinders)
- **5/2 Way Valve** — 5 ports, 2 positions (industry standard for double-acting)
- **5/3 Way Valve** — 5 ports, 3 positions (closed centre / exhaust centre / pressure centre — used for mid-position control)

### Block 7: Actuator (Cylinder or Motor)
- **Single-acting cylinder** — air pushes from one side, spring returns.
- **Double-acting cylinder** — air pushes from both sides, more force in one direction.

### Block 8: Exhaust (Silencer / Muffler)
Used at the valve exhaust ports to reduce noise to **< 80 dB(A)** as per industrial safety norms.

## 2.3 Hydraulic System Functional Blocks (For Comparison)

A hydraulic system has the same logical chain, but with key differences:

- **Prime Mover**: Electric motor (or IC engine) drives a **pump** (gear, vane, piston).
- **Reservoir**: Holds the oil; sized for 3–5 times the pump flow per minute.
- **Direction Control Valve (DCV)**: Routes oil.
- **Pressure Relief Valve**: Set at ~10 % above max working pressure — **safety-critical**.
- **Actuator**: Cylinder or motor.
- **Return Line Filter**: Filters oil before it goes back to the tank.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Pneumatics & Hydraulics)

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — at least 60 % of KTU numerical questions on this module will use one of these formulas.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force / Area | Pa (N/m²) | Both |
| 2 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Force = Pressure × Area | N | Both |
| 3 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | Piston (full bore) area | m² | Both |
| 4 | $A_a = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$ | Annular (rod-side) area | m² | Both |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Piston velocity = Flow / Area | m/s | Both |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{L}{v}$ | Stroke time | s | Both |
| 7 | $Q = A \cdot L / t$ | Cylinder air/oil consumption | m³/s | Both |
| 8 | $P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q$ | Hydraulic power | W | Both |
| 9 | $P_{hyd, kW} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{600}$ | Power (p in bar, Q in L/min) | kW | Both |
| 10 | $T = \dfrac{p \cdot D_{disp}}{2\pi}$ | Motor torque (p × displacement) | N·m | Both |
| 11 | $N = \dfrac{Q}{D_{disp}}$ | Motor speed | rpm | Both |
| 12 | $r = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1}$ | Compression ratio | dimensionless | Pneumatics |
| 13 | $V_2 = \dfrac{V_1}{r}$ | Air volume after compression | m³ | Pneumatics |
| 14 | $V_{tank} = 1.5 \cdot Q_{comp}$ | Receiver tank sizing (rule) | m³ | Pneumatics |
| 15 | $p_{pump} = \dfrac{F_{cyl}}{A_{cyl} \cdot \eta_{mech}}$ | Pump pressure for given load | Pa | Hydraulics |
| 16 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | Pump/motor overall efficiency | dimensionless | Hydraulics |
| 17 | $F_{extend} = p \cdot A_{bore}$ | Cylinder extending force | N | Both |
| 18 | $F_{retract} = p \cdot A_{annular}$ | Cylinder retracting force | N | Both |
| 19 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s | Pneumatics |
| 20 | $Q_{sc} = Q \cdot \dfrac{p_{abs,supply}}{p_{abs,atm}}$ | Free-air delivery conversion | m³/min | Pneumatics |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Exam Warning:** In formula tables and derivations, **never use the vertical pipe symbol `|`** to denote absolute value inside a markdown table — it breaks the table rendering. Always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ in LaTeX.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Preferred System | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive assembly (welding, painting) | **Pneumatic** | Clean, fast, spark-free |
| CNC machine tools (feed drives) | **Hydraulic** | High stiffness, precision |
| Plastic injection moulding | **Hydraulic** | Very high clamping force |
| Food/pharmaceutical packaging | **Pneumatic** | No oil contamination |
| Earth-moving equipment | **Hydraulic** | Enormous force, mobile |
| Aircraft landing gear | **Hydraulic** | High pressure, reliability |
| Dental/surgical tools | **Pneumatic** | Clean, precise, lightweight |
| Paper mills (roll presses) | **Hydraulic** | Smooth, high force |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are the **most-frequently-asked numerical patterns** in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) for Module 4. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no shortcut, no skipping.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cylinder Force, Velocity, and Power (The "Pneumatic Cylinder Numerical")

### Problem Statement (Model KTU Numerical)
A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a **bore (D) = 100 mm** and a **rod diameter (d) = 35 mm**. The supply pressure is **6 bar** (gauge), stroke length is **L = 300 mm**, and the piston extends in **t = 1.5 seconds**. The compressor runs at **efficiency η = 0.85**.
Calculate:
1. Extending force $F_{ext}$
2. Retracting force $F_{ret}$
3. Extending velocity $v_{ext}$
4. Air consumption per cycle (free air)
5. Theoretical compressor power
6. Actual compressor power

### Given Data (extracted)
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $d = 35 \text{ mm} = 0.035 \text{ m}$
- $p_{gauge} = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $p_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $L = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $t = 1.5 \text{ s}$
- $\eta_{comp} = 0.85$

### Step 1 — Convert Gauge to Absolute Pressure

Pneumatic formulas must use **absolute pressure** because the atmosphere always acts on the rod-side face (or any unpressurised face). Convert:

$$p_{abs} = p_{gauge} + p_{atm}$$

$$p_{abs} = 6 \times 10^5 + 1.013 \times 10^5$$

$$p_{abs} = 7.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 7.013 \text{ bar}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** KTU examiners specifically test whether you add atmospheric pressure. If you use 6 bar instead of 7.013 bar, your final air consumption answer will be **~15 % too low** — losing 2 marks.

### Step 2 — Calculate Bore (Full) Area

$$A_{bore} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

$$A_{bore} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.1)^2$$

$$A_{bore} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.01$$

$$A_{bore} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Annular (Rod-side) Area

$$A_{ann} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$

$$A_{ann} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.1^2 - 0.035^2)$$

$$A_{ann} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.01 - 0.001225)$$

$$A_{ann} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.008775$$

$$A_{ann} = 6.892 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Extending Force (Use Gauge Pressure Here)

For the **extending stroke**, the full bore area is pressurised; the rod-side is vented to atmosphere. The net force uses gauge pressure (since both sides of the piston have atmospheric pressure acting on them, except where pressurised):

$$F_{ext} = p_{gauge} \times A_{bore}$$

$$F_{ext} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{ext} = 4712.4 \text{ N} \approx 4.71 \text{ kN}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** At 6 bar supply, a 100 mm bore cylinder delivers **~4.7 kN** of force — equivalent to lifting a **470 kg load**. This is why pneumatics, despite low pressure, is still useful in clamping and medium-duty automation.

### Step 5 — Calculate Retracting Force

For the **retracting stroke**, the annular area is pressurised; the bore side is vented:

$$F_{ret} = p_{gauge} \times A_{ann}$$

$$F_{ret} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 6.892 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{ret} = 4135.2 \text{ N} \approx 4.14 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 6 — Force Ratio (Retract / Extend)

$$\frac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = \frac{A_{ann}}{A_{bore}} = \frac{D^2 - d^2}{D^2}$$

$$\frac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = \frac{0.008775}{0.01} = 0.8775$$

**The retract force is 87.75 % of the extend force.** This asymmetry is a defining feature of double-acting cylinders with unequal area.

### Step 7 — Calculate Piston Velocity (Extending)

$$v_{ext} = \frac{L}{t}$$

$$v_{ext} = \frac{0.3}{1.5}$$

$$v_{ext} = 0.2 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 8 — Calculate Volumetric Flow Rate (Extending)

$$Q_{ext} = A_{bore} \times v_{ext}$$

$$Q_{ext} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2$$

$$Q_{ext} = 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.571 \text{ L/s}$$

### Step 9 — Convert to "Free Air Delivery" (FAD) Per Cycle

This is the trickiest part. The flow $Q_{ext}$ is the air **at supply pressure** (6 bar gauge). To find the equivalent volume of **free air** (at 1 atm), use Boyle's Law:

$$p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_2$$

$$V_{free} = V_{supply} \times \frac{p_{abs, supply}}{p_{abs, atm}}$$

$$V_{free} = A_{bore} \times L \times \frac{p_{abs, supply}}{p_{abs, atm}}$$

$$V_{free} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3 \times \frac{7.013}{1.013}$$

$$V_{free} = 2.3562 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.923$$

$$V_{free} = 1.631 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3 = 16.31 \text{ L per stroke}$$

> **Engineering Note:** This **16.31 L of free air per cycle** is what the compressor must supply. Multiply by cycles per minute to size the compressor in L/min (FAD rating).

### Step 10 — Theoretical Compressor Power (Isothermal, Single-Stage)

For a slow-running compressor, assume isothermal compression ($pV = \text{const}$):

$$P_{iso} = p_1 \cdot V_1 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)$$

where $p_1 = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa, $V_1 = 1.631 \times 10^{-2}$ m³ per stroke, $p_2 = 7.013 \times 10^5$ Pa:

$$P_{iso} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \times 1.631 \times 10^{-2} \times \ln\left(\frac{7.013}{1.013}\right)$$

$$P_{iso} = 1651.9 \times \ln(6.923)$$

$$P_{iso} = 1651.9 \times 1.935$$

$$P_{iso} = 3196.4 \text{ W} \approx 3.20 \text{ kW (theoretical isothermal)}$$

### Step 11 — Actual Compressor Power

$$P_{actual} = \frac{P_{iso}}{\eta_{comp}}$$

$$P_{actual} = \frac{3196.4}{0.85}$$

$$P_{actual} = 3760.5 \text{ W} \approx 3.76 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 12 — Summary Table (For Answer-Script Presentation)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bore area | $A_{bore}$ | 7.854 × 10⁻³ | m² |
| Annular area | $A_{ann}$ | 6.892 × 10⁻³ | m² |
| Extend force | $F_{ext}$ | 4712.4 | N |
| Retract force | $F_{ret}$ | 4135.2 | N |
| Velocity | $v$ | 0.2 | m/s |
| Flow rate | $Q$ | 1.571 × 10⁻³ | m³/s |
| Free air / stroke | $V_{free}$ | 1.631 × 10⁻² | m³ |
| Theoretical power | $P_{iso}$ | 3.20 | kW |
| Actual power | $P_{actual}$ | 3.76 | kW |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydraulic Cylinder Sizing (Force & Pump Selection)

### Problem Statement
A hydraulic press must apply a clamping force of **F = 50 kN**. The system pressure is **p = 160 bar**. Calculate:
1. Required bore diameter
2. Pump flow rate for a forward stroke of **L = 0.2 m** in **t = 5 s**
3. Hydraulic power
4. Motor power (assuming η = 0.9)

### Step 1 — Bore Area from Force

$$A = \frac{F}{p}$$

$$A = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{160 \times 10^5} = \frac{50{,}000}{16 \times 10^6}$$

$$A = 3.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Bore Diameter

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \implies D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.125 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{3.979 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$D = 0.0631 \text{ m} = 63.1 \text{ mm}$$

> **Practical Engineering Decision:** We would **standardise up** to the next available bore size — **D = 65 mm** — to give a 3 % safety margin. This "standardisation upward" approach is expected in KTU industrial design problems.

### Step 3 — Required Pump Flow Rate

For a stroke of length L in time t, the required volumetric flow is:

$$Q = \frac{A \cdot L}{t}$$

$$Q = \frac{3.125 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2}{5}$$

$$Q = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 7.5 \text{ L/min}$$

### Step 4 — Hydraulic Power

$$P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q = 160 \times 10^5 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P_{hyd} = 2000 \text{ W} = 2.0 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5 — Motor Power

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta}$$

$$P_{motor} = \frac{2000}{0.9} = 2222 \text{ W} \approx 2.22 \text{ kW}$$

> **Practical Decision:** Standardise up to a **2.5 kW (3 HP)** electric motor — common industrial frame size.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Receiver Tank Sizing for a Pneumatic System

### Problem Statement
A compressor delivers **Q = 500 L/min** of free air. The maximum allowable pressure drop in the receiver between cut-out and cut-in is **Δp = 1.5 bar**. The compressor runs for **t = 4 minutes** between cycles.

### Step 1 — Standard Receiver Sizing Formula

$$V_{receiver} = \frac{Q \cdot t \cdot p_{atm}}{p_{max} - p_{min} \cdot (\text{dimensionless ratio})}$$

Simplified engineering formula:

$$V_{receiver} = \frac{Q_{comp} \cdot t_{cycle}}{p_{max} - p_{min}}$$

where $Q_{comp}$ is in L/min, $t$ in min, and pressure in bar (with appropriate unit conversion).

### Step 2 — Plug In Values

$$V_{receiver} = \frac{500 \text{ L/min} \times 4 \text{ min}}{1.5 \text{ bar}}$$

$$V_{receiver} = \frac{2000}{1.5} = 1333.3 \text{ L} \approx 1.33 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A **1.33 m³ (1333 L) receiver** is needed. This is roughly a **1.2 m tall × 1.2 m diameter** cylindrical tank — typical of small workshops.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for KTU Problems

| If the Question Asks | Use This Formula | Common Unit Conversion |
|---|---|---|
| Force from a cylinder | $F = p \cdot A$ | bar → Pa: × 10⁵ |
| Cylinder bore from force | $D = \sqrt{4F/(\pi p)}$ | mm if F in N, p in Pa |
| Piston velocity | $v = Q/A$ | L/min → m³/s: ÷ 60,000 |
| Stroke time | $t = L/v$ | seconds |
| Air consumption (free) | $V_f = A L (p_{abs,supply}/p_{abs,atm})$ | m³ or L |
| Compressor power (isothermal) | $P = p_1 V_1 \ln(p_2/p_1)$ | W |
| Compressor power (adiabatic) | $P = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} p_1 V_1 \left[(p_2/p_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | W, with γ = 1.4 |
| Receiver volume | $V = Q t / \Delta p$ | m³ |
| Hydraulic power | $P = pQ/600$ | kW (p bar, Q L/min) |
| Pump motor power | $P_{motor} = P_{hyd}/\eta$ | kW |

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are rendered using **Mermaid** with strictly alphanumeric node identifiers and plain uppercase text inside labels (no markdown inside diagram text). These block-level functional diagrams mirror the standard pneumatic and hydraulic schematics used in industrial automation.

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Complete Industrial Pneumatic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    atm[ATMOSPHERE] --> intake[INTAKE FILTER]
    intake --> comp[AIR COMPRESSOR 6 TO 8 BAR]
    comp --> cooler[AFTER COOLER]
    cooler --> drier[MOISTURE SEPARATOR]
    drier --> receiver[AIR RECEIVER TANK]
    receiver --> f1[FRL FILTER STAGE]
    f1 --> f2[FRL REGULATOR STAGE]
    f2 --> f3[FRL LUBRICATOR STAGE]
    f3 --> dcv[DIRECTION CONTROL VALVE 5 BY 2]
    dcv --> cyl[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER]
    cyl --> work[WORKPIECE ACTUATION]
    dcv --> sil[EXHAUST SILENCER]
    cyl --> sil
    f1 -. drain .-> drain1[AUTO DRAIN VALVE]
    f3 -. oil .-> oilcan[OIL RESERVOIR]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Complete Industrial Hydraulic System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    motor[ELECTRIC MOTOR] --> pump[VANE OR PISTON PUMP]
    tank[OIL RESERVOIR TANK] --> pump
    pump --> relief[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE]
    pump --> dcv2[DIRECTION CONTROL VALVE 4 BY 3]
    dcv2 --> cyl2[HYDRAULIC CYLINDER]
    cyl2 --> load[HEAVY LOAD]
    cyl2 --> dcv2
    dcv2 --> filter[RETURN LINE FILTER]
    filter --> tank
    relief -. excess flow .-> tank
    pump --> pguage[PRESSURE GAUGE]
    dcv2 --> acc[ACCUMULATOR]
    acc --> emerg[EMERGENCY BACKUP]
```

## 4.3 FRL Unit — Internal Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[RAW COMPRESSED AIR IN] --> bowl1[FILTER BOWL WITH AUTO DRAIN]
    bowl1 --> elem[FILTER ELEMENT 5 MICRON]
    elem --> adj[ADJUSTABLE REGULATOR KNOB]
    adj --> spring[REGULATOR SPRING]
    spring --> diaphragm[DIAPHRAGM]
    diaphragm --> outlet[REGULATED AIR OUT]
    outlet --> venturi[VENTURI IN LUBRICATOR]
    venturi --> drip[OIL DRIP CONTROL]
    drip --> oilpick[OIL WICK PICKUP]
    oilpick --> oilbowl[OIL RESERVOIR BOWL]
    outlet --> finalout[LUBRICATED REGULATED AIR TO VALVE]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing Pneumatic vs Hydraulic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[NEW FLUID POWER APPLICATION] --> q1{REQUIRED FORCE}
    q1 -->|LESS THAN 50 KN| q2{INDOOR CLEAN ENVIRONMENT}
    q1 -->|MORE THAN 50 KN| hyd[USE HYDRAULIC SYSTEM]
    q2 -->|YES| q3{FAST CYCLING NEEDED}
    q2 -->|NO| q4{EXPLOSION HAZARD}
    q3 -->|YES| pneu[USE PNEUMATIC SYSTEM]
    q3 -->|NO| q5{HIGH POSITIONING ACCURACY}
    q4 -->|YES| pneu
    q4 -->|NO| hyd
    q5 -->|YES| hyd
    q5 -->|NO| pneu
    pneu --> spec1[OPERATING PRESSURE 6 BAR CLEAN DRY AIR]
    hyd --> spec2[OPERATING PRESSURE 100 TO 350 BAR MINERAL OIL ISO VG 46]
```

## 4.5 Pneumatic Circuit — Sequencing of Two Cylinders (A then B)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[START PB] --> v1[3 BY 2 PUSH BUTTON VALVE]
    v1 --> v2[5 BY 2 DCV FOR CYLINDER A]
    v2 --> cylA[CYLINDER A EXTENDS]
    cylA --> v3[LIMIT SWITCH A2 ACTUATED]
    v3 --> v4[5 BY 2 DCV FOR CYLINDER B]
    v4 --> cylB[CYLINDER B EXTENDS]
    cylB --> done[CYCLE COMPLETE]
    cylA -. spring return .-> v2
    cylB -. spring return .-> v4
    v2 -. exhaust .-> sil1[SILENCER]
    v4 -. exhaust .-> sil2[SILENCER]
```

## 4.6 Hydraulic Circuit — Load Holding with Pilot-Operated Check Valve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pumpH[HYDRAULIC PUMP] --> prv[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE SET 200 BAR]
    pumpH --> dcvH[4 BY 3 DIRECTION CONTROL VALVE]
    dcvH --> pcv[PILOT OPERATED CHECK VALVE]
    pcv --> cylH[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER HOLDING 80 KN LOAD]
    cylH --> dcvH
    pcv -. loss of pressure locks load .-> safety[FAIL SAFE POSITION]
    prv -. excess to tank .-> tankH[RESERVOIR]
    dcvH --> tankH
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard (followed in all above diagrams):**
> 1. Every node ID is purely alphanumeric (`cylA`, `drain1`, `comp`, etc.) — never a reserved keyword.
> 2. Node labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes — no bold (`**`), no italics (`*`), no HTML.
> 3. The diagrams above use Mermaid `flowchart` syntax (not `graph` keyword) for maximum compiler compatibility.
