# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution (Module 4 — End Semester Exam):**
> - Part A: 3-mark short-answer conceptual questions (typically 1–2 from this module)
> - Part B: 14-mark analytical/design questions with **internal choice** (typically 1 question from this module)
> - Total marks from Module 4 in a typical KTU ESE paper: **15 – 20 marks** (out of 60)
> - Cognitive levels tested (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy): L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyse)

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *(3 Marks)*

**"With a neat sketch, explain the working of an FRL unit in a pneumatic system."**

#### Model Answer (3-mark pattern, ~150 words):

The FRL unit is an **assembly of three components — Filter, Regulator, and Lubricator** — connected in series in the compressed air line, **always in the order F → R → L**.

1. **Filter (F):** Removes dust, dirt, and condensed moisture from the compressed air. Typical filtration rating is **5 – 40 µm**. It has a **transparent polycarbonate bowl** with an **auto-drain valve** at the bottom to expel collected water.

2. **Regulator (R):** Reduces the upstream pressure (e.g., 8 bar from receiver) to a stable downstream working pressure (e.g., 6 bar). It uses a **spring-loaded diaphragm** mechanism; turning the adjustment knob compresses the spring, setting the desired pressure. A **pressure gauge** displays the regulated value.

3. **Lubricator (L):** Atomises **light machine oil (ISO VG 32, ~100 cSt)** into the regulated airstream using a **Venturi effect**. The oil droplets lubricate the moving parts of downstream valves and the cylinder, reducing wear and extending service life.

**Significance:** The FRL ensures **clean, dry, pressure-controlled, and lubricated** air reaches the control valves, dramatically improving system reliability and life.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do **not** describe the units in the order R, F, L. Wrong order = **−1 mark** in valuation. Also, never skip mentioning the **auto-drain** in the filter — it is a 0.5-mark keyword.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *(3 Marks)*

**"Compare Pneumatic and Hydraulic systems based on any six parameters."**

#### Model Answer (Tabular, 6 points × 0.5 marks each):

| S.No. | Parameter | Pneumatic | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Working medium | Compressed air (compressible) | Mineral oil (incompressible) |
| 2 | Operating pressure | 4 – 8 bar (typical 6 bar) | 100 – 350 bar (typical 200 bar) |
| 3 | Force output | Low to medium (≤ 50 kN) | Very high (≥ 50 kN, up to MN) |
| 4 | Speed of actuation | Very high (1 – 5 m/s) | Moderate (0.1 – 1 m/s) |
| 5 | Positioning accuracy | Poor (air compresses) | Excellent (oil is stiff) |
| 6 | Cost of medium | Free (atmospheric air) | ₹ 80 – 250 per litre |
| 7 | Cleanliness | Clean (no leakage residue) | Prone to oil leaks |
| 8 | Fire/explosion safety | Safe | Oil is flammable |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Examiners will mark strictly — if you write only 5 points, you get 2.5 marks, not 3. Always list **6 distinct points** with **one-line justification** for each.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, every 14-mark question carries **internal choice** — you must answer **either Option A or Option B**. The two alternatives below are completely independent question styles (one design-based, one analytical).

---

### Question A — 14 Marks *(Design-Based, KTU May 2024 Pattern)*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

> A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a **bore of 80 mm**, **rod diameter of 25 mm**, and a **stroke of 250 mm**. The supply pressure is **5 bar (gauge)**. The piston extends in **2 seconds**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the extending and retracting forces, the piston velocity, and the free air consumption per cycle. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Design (sketch) a pneumatic circuit to control this cylinder using a **5/2 DCV** operated by a push button. Explain the working in extend and retract modes. List the components used. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- $d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $L = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- $p_{gauge} = 5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $t_{ext} = 2 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Bore Area**

$$A_{bore} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.08)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Annular Area**

$$A_{ann} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.08^2 - 0.025^2) = 4.536 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Extending Force**

$$F_{ext} = p_{gauge} \times A_{bore} = 5 \times 10^5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 2513.3 \text{ N} \approx 2.51 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Retracting Force**

$$F_{ret} = p_{gauge} \times A_{ann} = 5 \times 10^5 \times 4.536 \times 10^{-3} = 2268.1 \text{ N} \approx 2.27 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Piston Velocity (extending)**

$$v_{ext} = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{0.25}{2} = 0.125 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Free Air Consumption per Cycle**

Absolute supply pressure: $p_{abs} = 5 + 1.013 = 6.013$ bar

Volume at supply (extend): $V_{sup} = A_{bore} \times L = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.25 = 1.257 \times 10^{-3}$ m³

Volume at supply (retract): $V_{ret} = A_{ann} \times L = 4.536 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.25 = 1.134 \times 10^{-3}$ m³

Convert to free air (using $p_{abs,atm} = 1.013$ bar):

$$V_{free, ext} = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{6.013}{1.013} = 7.46 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 7.46 \text{ L}$$

$$V_{free, ret} = 1.134 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{6.013}{1.013} = 6.73 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 6.73 \text{ L}$$

**Total per cycle:** $V_{cycle} = 7.46 + 6.73 = 14.19$ L of free air.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Components used (3 marks):**
- Air supply (from receiver) with pressure gauge
- **5/2 way DCV** (spring-return, push-button pilot operated)
- **Double-acting cylinder** (80 mm bore, 25 mm rod)
- Two silencers at exhaust ports
- Push button (PB)
- Connecting pipes / tubing

**Working — Extend Mode (2 marks):**
When the operator presses the **push button**, pilot air shifts the **5/2 DCV** to its left position. Port 1 (supply) is connected to port 4 (cap end of cylinder), and port 2 (rod end) is connected to port 5 (exhaust). Pressurised air enters the **cap end** and pushes the piston outward. The air on the **rod end** is exhausted through the silencer.

**Working — Retract Mode (2 marks):**
When the push button is released, the valve's **spring-return** shifts the spool back to its right (home) position. Now port 1 connects to port 2 (rod end), and port 4 (cap end) connects to port 3 (exhaust). Pressurised air enters the **rod end** and retracts the piston. Air from the cap end is vented.

**Circuit Description (text version, for answer script):**

```
[FRL] → [5/2 DCV] → [Double-Acting Cylinder]
          |  |
          |  +--→ [Silencer 1] (exhaust to atmosphere)
          +-----→ [Silencer 2] (exhaust to atmosphere)
```

**Push button** pilots the 5/2 valve; **spring return** ensures default retract position.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> 1. **−1 mark** if you forget to convert gauge pressure to absolute in the air-consumption part. Many students write 5 bar instead of 6.013 bar.
> 2. **−1 mark** if you sketch the 5/2 valve as a 3/2 valve — examiners check the number of ports and positions.
> 3. **−1 mark** if you don't label the **spring return** on the 5/2 valve — this is a critical safety/operational feature.
> 4. **−0.5 mark** per missing silencer mention in the circuit (you need 2 silencers for 5/2 valve).
> 5. **−0.5 mark** if the free-air formula is written as $V_{free} = V_{supply} \times \frac{p_{gauge}}{p_{atm}}$ (wrong — must be absolute, not gauge).
> 6. For **free air consumption, write the formula + numbers in the same step** — partial marking requires both the formula and the substitution to award marks.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks *(Analytical + Conceptual, KTU Dec 2023 Pattern)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

> **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the functional components of an **industrial compressed air system**. State the function of each block. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A hydraulic cylinder of bore **100 mm** and rod diameter **60 mm** is supplied by a pump delivering **20 L/min** at **120 bar**. Calculate:
>   (i) Cylinder extending and retracting velocities
>   (ii) Cylinder extending and retracting forces
>   (iii) Hydraulic power developed
>   *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

A complete industrial compressed air system consists of the following **seven functional blocks** (drawn as a block diagram — see Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid version).

| Block | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Intake Filter** | Removes atmospheric dust (5 – 10 µm) before air enters compressor |
| 2 | **Air Compressor** | Raises air pressure from 1 bar to 6 – 8 bar |
| 3 | **After-Cooler** | Cools hot (150 °C) discharged air to ~30 °C |
| 4 | **Moisture Separator / Drier** | Removes condensed water via centrifugal / refrigeration |
| 5 | **Air Receiver Tank** | Stores compressed air, smooths pulsations |
| 6 | **FRL Unit** | Filters, regulates, and lubricates air to working pressure |
| 7 | **DCV + Actuator + Silencer** | Controls direction, performs work, reduces noise |

**Working Description (3 marks):**
Atmospheric air is filtered and drawn into the compressor, which compresses it to the working pressure. The hot, moisture-laden air is cooled and dried, then stored in the receiver. From the receiver, air passes through the FRL where it is filtered, pressure-regulated, and oil-misted. The conditioned air is then routed via a Direction Control Valve to the actuator (cylinder/motor) which performs the mechanical work. Exhaust air is vented through silencers.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- $Q = 20 \text{ L/min} = 20 \times 10^{-3}/60 = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $p = 120 \text{ bar} = 120 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1 — Areas**

$$A_{bore} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{ann} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1^2 - 0.06^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Velocities (extending and retracting)**

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_{bore}} = \frac{3.33 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0424 \text{ m/s} = 42.4 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_{ann}} = \frac{3.33 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0663 \text{ m/s} = 66.3 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Forces**

$$F_{ext} = p \times A_{bore} = 120 \times 10^5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 94{,}248 \text{ N} \approx 94.25 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{ret} = p \times A_{ann} = 120 \times 10^5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 60{,}319 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Hydraulic Power**

$$P_{hyd} = p \times Q = 120 \times 10^5 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-4} = 4000 \text{ W} = 4.0 \text{ kW}$$

(Equivalent form: $P = \frac{120 \times 20}{600} = 4.0$ kW — using the convenient $pQ/600$ shortcut.)

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $v_{ext}$ | 42.4 mm/s |
| $v_{ret}$ | 66.3 mm/s |
| $F_{ext}$ | 94.25 kN |
| $F_{ret}$ | 60.32 kN |
| $P_{hyd}$ | 4.0 kW |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **−1 mark** if you forget to state the function of the **FRL** explicitly (a vague "for conditioning" gets 0; you need "filters, regulates, lubricates").
> 2. **−0.5 mark** if the block diagram doesn't show arrows indicating flow direction.
> 3. **−1 mark** in the numerical part if the pump flow is not converted from L/min to m³/s. Writing $Q = 20$ in the velocity formula gives a 1200× wrong answer.
> 4. **−0.5 mark** if the hydraulic power is written as 4 (no unit) instead of **4.0 kW**.
> 5. **−1 mark** if you use $p_{gauge}$ in pneumatic problems and $p_{abs}$ in hydraulic problems **inconsistently** — pick one convention and stick to it, but be aware the KTU convention is **gauge for force calculations** (since atmospheric is on the other side) and **absolute for free-air conversion**.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Module 4 — Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (print this page before the exam):**

- ✅ **Pneumatics = air, Hydraulics = oil.** Memorise this first; the rest follows.
- ✅ **Pneumatic pressure** = 4 – 8 bar; **Hydraulic pressure** = 100 – 350 bar. (Common ESE fill-in-the-blanks.)
- ✅ **FRL order** = Filter → Regulator → Lubricator. Never reverse it.
- ✅ **Pascal's Law** governs both systems — pressure transmission in confined fluids.
- ✅ **Force on a piston** = $F = p \times A$ (where $A$ is the pressurised area).
- ✅ **Bore area** = $\pi D^2/4$; **Annular area** = $\pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$.
- ✅ **Retract force is always less than extend force** (annular < bore).
- ✅ **Retract velocity is always greater than extend velocity** (for the same flow).
- ✅ **Free air conversion** = $V_{free} = V_{supply} \times (p_{abs,supply} / p_{abs,atm})$.
- ✅ **Hydraulic power (kW)** = $(p \times Q) / 600$ when $p$ is in bar and $Q$ is in L/min.
- ✅ **3/2 valve** for single-acting cylinder; **5/2 valve** for double-acting cylinder.
- ✅ **5/3 valve** (closed centre) is used to hold a load mid-position hydraulically.
- ✅ **Pilot-operated check valve** is the hydraulic equivalent of a "parking brake" for cylinders.
- ✅ **Receiver tank sizing** = $V = Q \cdot t / \Delta p$ (rule-of-thumb: $V_{tank} \approx 1.5 \times Q_{comp, m^3/min}$).
- ✅ **Compressor power (isothermal)** = $p_1 V_1 \ln(p_2/p_1)$; (adiabatic) = $[\gamma/(\gamma-1)] p_1 V_1 [(p_2/p_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$.
- ✅ **Convert gauge ↔ absolute** before every pneumatic numerical — KTU tests this **every year**.
- ✅ **Sketch convention**: pneumatic uses **dashed lines** for pilot/control air; hydraulic uses solid lines for power and dashed for pilot.
- ✅ **Safety rules**: Always have a **pressure relief valve** in hydraulic circuits; always have a **silencer** at pneumatic exhausts.
- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid**: writing **"pneumatic cylinder uses oil"** or **"hydraulic system uses air"** — instant 1-mark penalty.

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — PCMRT503 | KTU 2024 SCHEME**

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Air in Pneumatic Systems

In the context of **Industrial Hydraulics & Pneumatics (PCMRT503)**, **Air** is defined as the standard gaseous working fluid utilized in pneumatic power transmission systems. It is a naturally abundant, colorless, odorless, tasteless, and compressible mixture of gases that surrounds the Earth and is harnessed, filtered, compressed, and distributed to perform mechanical work through pressure energy conversion.

For engineering analysis within the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, air is treated as an **Ideal Gas** under standard pneumatic operating conditions, meaning it obeys the classical gas laws and has negligible molecular interaction effects. Air is the **4th most abundant industrial raw material** after water, fuel, and steel, which directly establishes its industrial significance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PCMRT503 emphasizes the physical, thermodynamic, and compressibility properties of air because every pneumatic circuit calculation (force, flow rate, power consumption, response time) fundamentally depends on these baseline properties.

### 1.2 Composition of Atmospheric Air (Dry Basis)

| Component | Chemical Symbol | Volume % | Mass % |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nitrogen | N₂ | 78.03 % | 75.47 % |
| Oxygen | O₂ | 20.99 % | 23.20 % |
| Argon | Ar | 0.94 % | 1.28 % |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | 0.03 % | 0.04 % |
| Hydrogen, Neon, Helium, etc. | Trace | < 0.01 % | < 0.01 % |

The molecular weight of dry air is taken as **M = 28.97 kg/kmol** (approximately **29 g/mol**), and the specific gas constant is **R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Air as an Invisible Sponge

> [!NOTE]
> **The Sponge Analogy:** Imagine air as an **invisible, weightless sponge** filling every space around us. Unlike a solid sponge, you cannot see it, but it has mass and can be squeezed. When you push a bicycle pump's piston, you are physically squeezing trillions of air molecules closer together, storing energy in them like a compressed spring. Release the pump's nozzle, and the "sponge" springs back, pushing the air out with force. This compressibility is what makes pneumatics fundamentally different from hydraulics (which uses an essentially incompressible liquid).

## 1.4 Why Air is Preferred in Pneumatics

- **Abundantly available** — unlimited, free supply from the atmosphere.
- **Non-flammable** — safe in most industrial environments (unlike oils).
- **Non-toxic** — leakages do not contaminate the workplace.
- **Temperature tolerant** — usable in wide thermal ranges.
- **Cleanliness** — does not contaminate the product (suitable for food, pharma, semiconductor industries).
- **Low cost** — minimal fluid cost and simple disposal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Limitation:** Air contains **moisture (water vapor)** and **dust particles**. Without proper **FRL unit** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator), these contaminants destroy valves, cylinders, and seals — a frequent cause of pneumatic system failure.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physical Properties of Air

### 2.1.1 Density (ρ)
Density is the mass per unit volume of air. It is highly dependent on **pressure** and **temperature**, unlike hydraulic oil.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad [\text{kg/m}^3]$$

- At **Standard Atmospheric Conditions (SAC)**: ρ = **1.225 kg/m³** (at 15 °C, 101.325 kPa).
- At **Normal Temperature and Pressure (NTP)**: ρ = **1.293 kg/m³** (at 0 °C, 101.325 kPa).

### 2.1.2 Specific Weight (γ)
The weight per unit volume, given by:

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g \quad [\text{N/m}^3]$$

At SAC, γ ≈ **12.007 N/m³**. Specific weight is rarely used in pneumatic calculations because air is light and the gravitational component is negligible compared to pressure forces.

### 2.1.3 Viscosity (Dynamic & Kinematic)
Air has **very low dynamic viscosity** (μ ≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s at 15 °C), which is about **1/1000th that of water**. This results in:

- Very low frictional losses in pipes.
- High Reynolds numbers even at low flow velocities (typically **turbulent flow** in pneumatic lines).
- Fast system response (less viscous drag).

$$\mu_{air} \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s} \quad \text{at} \ 15^\circ \text{C}$$

$$\nu_{air} = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \approx 1.48 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s} \quad \text{at} \ 15^\circ \text{C}$$

### 2.1.4 Compressibility — The Defining Property
Compressibility is the property that allows air volume to reduce under pressure. It is quantified by the **Isothermal Bulk Modulus (β)**:

$$\beta = -V \cdot \frac{dP}{dV} = P \quad \text{(for isothermal process)}$$

For adiabatic compression (rapid pneumatic processes):

$$\beta = k \cdot P \quad \text{where} \ k = 1.4 \ \text{(specific heat ratio for air)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Hydraulic oil has β ≈ 1.5–2 GPa (essentially incompressible). Air at atmospheric pressure has β = 101.325 kPa — meaning air is **~15,000 times more compressible** than hydraulic oil. This is why pneumatic systems cannot be used for precision positioning without specialized counter-measures.

## 2.2 The Four Gas Laws Governing Air Behavior

These laws form the analytical backbone of every pneumatic system problem and are **mandatory** for KTU examination purposes.

### 2.2.1 Boyle's Law (Constant Temperature)
When temperature is constant, the absolute pressure of a given mass of air is inversely proportional to its volume.

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 \quad (\text{T} = \text{constant})$$

### 2.2.2 Charles's Law (Constant Pressure)
At constant pressure, the volume of a given mass of air is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

$$\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2} \quad (\text{P} = \text{constant})$$

### 2.2.3 Gay-Lussac's Law (Constant Volume)
At constant volume, the absolute pressure of a given mass of air is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

$$\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2} \quad (\text{V} = \text{constant})$$

### 2.2.4 Combined Gas Law / Ideal Gas Equation
This is the **universal law** used for all pneumatic calculations:

$$P V = m R T \quad \text{or} \quad P V = n \bar{R} T$$

Where:
- P = absolute pressure in **Pa (N/m²)**
- V = volume in **m³**
- m = mass of air in **kg**
- n = number of moles in **kmol**
- T = absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**
- R = 287 J/(kg·K) — specific gas constant for air
- $\bar{R}$ = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K) — universal gas constant

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Examination Trap:** Always convert gauge pressure to **absolute pressure** before applying gas laws. Gauge pressure ignores the 101.325 kPa atmospheric baseline.

$$P_{absolute} = P_{gauge} + P_{atmospheric}$$

## 2.3 Pressure Measurement Standards

| Pressure Type | Symbol | Reference | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Atmospheric | P_atm | Vacuum (zero) | Baseline of absolute scale |
| Absolute | P_abs | Vacuum (zero) | Used in gas law calculations |
| Gauge | P_gauge | Atmospheric | Pneumatic system working pressure |
| Vacuum | P_vac | Atmospheric | Pressures below atmospheric |

Conversion relationship:

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$
$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$$

## 2.4 Humidity — The Hidden Variable in Pneumatics

Air always contains **water vapor** from evaporation. The amount of water vapor relative to the maximum the air can hold at that temperature defines humidity.

### Absolute Humidity
Mass of water vapor per unit volume of air:

$$\rho_{wv} = \frac{m_{wv}}{V} \quad [\text{g/m}^3]$$

### Relative Humidity (RH)
The ratio of actual water vapor pressure to the saturation pressure at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage:

$$RH = \frac{P_{wv}}{P_{sat}(T)} \times 100 \ \%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industry Note:** When compressed air cools inside a receiver tank, RH reaches 100 % and **condensation** occurs. This water, if not removed by the FRL's filter/dryer, causes rust, valve sticking, and seal damage. The **Pressure Dew Point (PDP)** must be specified below ambient temperature for clean dry air applications.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ | Density from ideal gas law | kg/m³ |
| 2 | $PV = mRT$ | Ideal gas equation (mass form) | Pa·m³ |

| 4 | $\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$ | Gay-Lussac's Law (isochoric) | Pa/K |
| 5 | $\dfrac{V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{T_2}$ | Charles's Law (isobaric) | m³/K |

| 9 | $k = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v} = 1.4$ | Specific heat ratio for air | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_v = 718 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}$ | Specific heat at constant volume | J/(kg·K) |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Air Properties

Understanding these properties is essential for:
- **Sizing compressors** (mass flow rate ∝ ρ × Q).
- **Calculating tank filling/emptying time** (using mass conservation with gas law).
- **Predicting cylinder response time** (depends on compressibility).
- **Designing pneumatic circuits** (pressure drop in pipes uses viscosity).
- **Selecting air treatment equipment** (humidity control, filtration).
- **Power and efficiency calculations** (using C_p, C_v, k).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Density of Air from Ideal Gas Law

**Starting Equation:** The universal ideal gas equation in molar form:

$$P V = n \bar{R} T$$

Since $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ (where m = mass, M = molar mass = 28.97 kg/kmol):

$$P V = \frac{m}{M} \bar{R} T$$

Rearranging to express volume:

$$V = \frac{m \bar{R} T}{P M}$$

**Step 2 — Density Definition:** Density is mass per unit volume:

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute V:**

$$\rho = \frac{m}{\left(\dfrac{m \bar{R} T}{P M}\right)}$$

The mass 'm' cancels out:

$$\rho = \frac{P M}{\bar{R} T}$$

**Step 4 — Define Specific Gas Constant:** Let $R = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{M}$:

$$R = \frac{8314 \ \text{J/(kmol·K)}}{28.97 \ \text{kg/kmol}} = 287 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Final Density Formula:**

$$\boxed{\rho = \frac{P}{R T}}$$

**Numerical Verification at SAC (P = 101325 Pa, T = 288.15 K):**

$$\rho = \frac{101325}{287 \times 288.15} = \frac{101325}{82699.05} = 1.225 \ \text{kg/m}^3 \ \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Combined Gas Law from First Principles

**Step 1 — Apply Boyle's Law (T constant):**

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_x$$

Where $V_x$ is the volume when temperature changes. So:

$$V_x = \frac{P_1 V_1}{P_2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Charles's Law (P constant):** Going from state 1 to a new temperature T₂ at constant pressure:

$$\frac{V_x}{T_2} = \frac{V_2}{T_1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute V_x:**

$$\frac{1}{T_2} \cdot \left(\frac{P_1 V_1}{P_2}\right) = \frac{V_2}{T_1}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply:**

$$P_1 V_1 T_1 = P_2 V_2 T_2$$

**Step 5 — Final Combined Gas Law:**

$$\boxed{\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}}$$

This is the most-used form in KTU numerical problems, especially for receiver tank problems.

## 3.3 Derivation: Mass Flow Rate of Compressed Air

For an air compressor delivering air at gauge pressure P_g, the **free air delivery (FAD)** is the volumetric flow at atmospheric conditions.

**Starting Point — Mass conservation** through the compressor (no accumulation):

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$$

**At inlet (atmospheric):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{atm} \cdot Q_{FAD} = \frac{P_{atm}}{R T_{atm}} \cdot Q_{FAD}$$

**At outlet (discharge):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{disc} \cdot Q_{disc} = \frac{P_{disc}}{R T_{disc}} \cdot Q_{disc}$$

**Equating:**

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{R T_{atm}} \cdot Q_{FAD} = \frac{P_{disc}}{R T_{disc}} \cdot Q_{disc}$$

**For isothermal (slow) compression, T_atm ≈ T_disc:**

$$Q_{FAD} = Q_{disc} \cdot \frac{P_{disc}}{P_{atm}}$$

**In terms of gauge pressure (P_disc = P_atm + P_g):**

$$\boxed{Q_{FAD} = Q_{disc} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{P_g}{P_{atm}}\right)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Examples — KTU Board Style

### Example 1: Density at Higher Altitude / Different Conditions

**Problem:** A pneumatic cylinder operates at 25 °C and gauge pressure 6 bar. Calculate the density of air inside the cylinder. Take atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to absolute units:**

$$P_{abs} = 1.013 + 6 = 7.013 \ \text{bar} = 701300 \ \text{Pa}$$

$$T = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply density formula:**

$$\rho = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{701300}{287 \times 298.15}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$287 \times 298.15 = 85569.05$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$\rho = \frac{701300}{85569.05} = 8.196 \ \text{kg/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** At 7 bar absolute, air is **6.7 times denser** than at atmospheric pressure. This dense air, when expanding in the cylinder, generates the mechanical force.

### Example 2: Receiver Tank Filling Problem

**Problem:** A receiver tank of volume 0.5 m³ initially contains air at 1 bar absolute, 27 °C. The compressor delivers air at a rate of 0.02 m³/s (free air) at atmospheric conditions. The tank is pressurized to 7 bar absolute. Find the filling time. Assume isothermal process.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial mass in tank:**

$$m_1 = \frac{P_1 V}{RT_1} = \frac{1 \times 10^5 \times 0.5}{287 \times 300} = \frac{50000}{86100} = 0.581 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Final mass in tank:**

$$m_2 = \frac{P_2 V}{RT_2} = \frac{7 \times 10^5 \times 0.5}{287 \times 300} = \frac{350000}{86100} = 4.065 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Mass to be added:**

$$\Delta m = m_2 - m_1 = 4.065 - 0.581 = 3.484 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Mass flow rate of compressor** (free air delivery):

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{atm} \cdot Q_{FAD} = \frac{101325}{287 \times 300} \times 0.02 = 1.177 \times 0.02 = 0.02353 \ \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 5 — Filling time:**

$$t = \frac{\Delta m}{\dot{m}} = \frac{3.484}{0.02353} = 148.05 \ \text{seconds}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 2.47 \ \text{minutes}}$$

### Example 3: Cylinder Force with Pneumatic Pressure

**Problem:** A double-acting cylinder has bore diameter 100 mm, rod diameter 40 mm. Air pressure is 6 bar gauge. Calculate:
(a) Forward thrust force
(b) Return thrust force

Take atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to absolute pressure:**

$$P_{abs} = 1.013 + 6 = 7.013 \ \text{bar} = 701300 \ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Piston area:**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Rod side (annular) area:**

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.1^2 - 0.04^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.01 - 0.0016)$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0084 = 6.597 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Forward thrust:**

$$F_{fwd} = P_{abs} \times A_p = 701300 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 5507.5 \ \text{N}$$

$$F_{fwd} \approx 5.51 \ \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Return thrust:**

$$F_{ret} = P_{abs} \times A_r = 701300 \times 6.597 \times 10^{-3} = 4626.5 \ \text{N}$$

$$F_{ret} \approx 4.63 \ \text{kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Practice:** Use **absolute pressure** for the working side (high pressure) but for pneumatic force problems the **gauge pressure is typically used directly** since the back-side is at atmospheric exhaust. The KTU convention depends on the question's wording.

**Property Transformation Mapping:**

| Stage | Air Property Modified | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Intake Filter | Purity (Dust) | Removes particles > 5 μm |
| Compressor | Pressure, Temperature | Increases P from 1 bar to 7–10 bar |
| After Cooler | Temperature | Reduces T from 150 °C to 40 °C |
| Receiver Tank | Pressure Stability | Stores compressed air, smoothens pulsations |
| FRL — Filter | Humidity, Dirt | Removes water and fine particles |
| FRL — Regulator | Pressure | Sets working pressure to required value |
| FRL — Lubricator | Viscosity/Surface | Adds oil mist for moving parts |
| Distribution | Pressure, Flow | Delivers air to multiple stations |
| DCV | Flow Direction | Routes air to extend or retract cylinder |
| Actuator | Pressure → Mechanical Force | Converts P·A into linear force |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 KTU-Style Question Bank

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: List any six physical properties of air that are relevant to pneumatic system design.**

**Model Answer:**

The six major physical properties of air relevant to pneumatic systems are:

1. **Density (ρ):** Mass per unit volume; at SAC ρ = 1.225 kg/m³. Affects compressor sizing and cylinder force.
2. **Compressibility:** Air is highly compressible (β ≈ 100 kPa), making it unsuitable for precise positioning.
3. **Viscosity (μ):** Very low (1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s), causes low friction but turbulent flow.
4. **Specific heat ratio (k = 1.4):** Used in adiabatic work calculations.
5. **Specific gas constant (R = 287 J/kg·K):** Used in ideal gas law.
6. **Humidity:** Water vapor content that causes condensation and corrosion in receivers.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Examiners award 0.5 marks per correctly stated property. Adding units/values earns a 0.5 bonus for depth.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between absolute pressure and gauge pressure. Why is absolute pressure used in gas law calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Absolute Pressure | Gauge Pressure |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reference | Perfect vacuum (zero) | Atmospheric pressure |
| Symbol | P_abs | P_g |
| Formula | P_atm + P_gauge | P_abs − P_atm |
| Used in | Gas law calculations | System working pressure |
| Cannot be | Zero or negative | Always positive for pneumatic |

**Reason for using absolute in gas laws:** Gas laws (Boyle's, Charles's, Ideal Gas) are derived from the **molecular theory of gases** based on the number of molecular collisions per unit area. At vacuum (P = 0), there are no molecules and therefore no collisions. Hence, gas laws require a **true zero reference**, which is the absolute pressure scale.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **QUESTION A — Option 1 [14 Marks]**

**Q: (a) State and explain Boyle's Law, Charles's Law, and Gay-Lussac's Law for air. Derive the combined gas law from these fundamental laws. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Boyle's Law (1662):** At constant temperature, the absolute pressure of a fixed mass of air is inversely proportional to its volume.

$$P \cdot V = \text{constant} \quad (T = \text{const})$$

For two states: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$

**Physical Meaning:** If you halve the volume of a gas, the molecules have half the space, so they collide with the walls twice as often, doubling the pressure.

**(ii) Charles's Law (1787):** At constant pressure, the volume of a fixed mass of air is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

$$\frac{V}{T} = \text{constant} \quad (P = \text{const})$$

For two states: $\dfrac{V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{T_2}$

**Physical Meaning:** Heating a gas increases molecular kinetic energy, causing the gas to expand to maintain constant pressure.

**(iii) Gay-Lussac's Law (1802):** At constant volume, the absolute pressure of a fixed mass of air is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

$$\frac{P}{T} = \text{constant} \quad (V = \text{const})$$

For two states: $\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$

**Physical Meaning:** In a rigid container, heating increases molecular speed, leading to more wall collisions and higher pressure.

**Derivation of Combined Gas Law:**

Consider a gas changing from state 1 $(P_1, V_1, T_1)$ to state 2 $(P_2, V_2, T_2)$ in two steps:

- **Step 1 (Isothermal):** At constant T₁, change V₁ to V₂' and P₁ to P₂ using Boyle's law:

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2' \implies V_2' = \frac{P_1 V_1}{P_2}$$

- **Step 2 (Isochoric):** At constant V₂', change T₁ to T₂ using Charles's law:

$$\frac{V_2'}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2} \implies V_2 = V_2' \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

Substituting V₂':

$$V_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{P_2} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}}$$

**(b) A pneumatic receiver of capacity 2 m³ initially contains air at 1.5 bar absolute and 30 °C. It is filled by a compressor until pressure reaches 8 bar absolute. The compressor delivers air at 0.05 m³/s measured at atmospheric conditions (1 bar, 27 °C). Calculate: (i) Initial mass of air in receiver, (ii) Final mass of air in receiver, (iii) Time to fill. Assume isothermal filling. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** V = 2 m³, P₁ = 1.5 bar = 150000 Pa, T₁ = 30 + 273 = 303 K, P₂ = 8 bar = 800000 Pa, Q_FAD = 0.05 m³/s, T_atm = 27 + 273 = 300 K, P_atm = 100000 Pa, R = 287 J/(kg·K).

**(i) Initial mass:**

$$m_1 = \frac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \frac{150000 \times 2}{287 \times 303} = \frac{300000}{86961} = 3.450 \ \text{kg}$$

**(ii) Final mass:**

$$m_2 = \frac{P_2 V}{R T_2} = \frac{800000 \times 2}{287 \times 303} = \frac{1600000}{86961} = 18.401 \ \text{kg}$$

**(iii) Mass to be added:**

$$\Delta m = m_2 - m_1 = 18.401 - 3.450 = 14.951 \ \text{kg}$$

**Mass flow rate from compressor (free air delivery):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{atm} \cdot Q_{FAD} = \frac{P_{atm}}{R T_{atm}} \cdot Q_{FAD}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{100000 \times 0.05}{287 \times 300} = \frac{5000}{86100} = 0.05807 \ \text{kg/s}$$

**Time to fill:**

$$t = \frac{\Delta m}{\dot{m}} = \frac{14.951}{0.05807} = 257.46 \ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 4.29 \ \text{minutes}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B — Option 2 (Internal Choice) [14 Marks]**

**Q: (a) Define compressibility and bulk modulus of air. Explain why air is considered ~15,000 times more compressible than hydraulic oil. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Compressibility (κ):** The fractional change in volume of a fluid per unit change in pressure applied to it.

$$\kappa = -\frac{1}{V} \cdot \frac{dV}{dP} \quad [\text{Pa}^{-1}]$$

**Bulk Modulus (β):** The reciprocal of compressibility; it represents the resistance of a fluid to uniform compression.

$$\beta = -V \cdot \frac{dP}{dV} \quad [\text{Pa}]$$

For an ideal gas under **isothermal** conditions, $PV = \text{constant}$, so $V \cdot dP + P \cdot dV = 0$, giving:

$$\beta = -V \cdot \frac{dP}{dV} = P$$

For **adiabatic** (rapid) processes, $\beta = k \cdot P$, where $k = 1.4$ for air.

**Comparison with Hydraulic Oil:**

| Property | Air (at 1 bar) | Hydraulic Oil |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bulk Modulus (β) | ≈ 101 kPa | ≈ 1.5–2.0 GPa |
| Compressibility (κ) | ≈ 9.87 × 10⁻⁶ Pa⁻¹ | ≈ 5–6.7 × 10⁻¹⁰ Pa⁻¹ |
| Compressibility Ratio | **~15,000–20,000×** | Reference (1×) |

**Physical Reason:**
1. Air molecules are far apart (~10× the molecular diameter) — large empty space allows compression.
2. Oil molecules are densely packed and held by strong intermolecular forces.
3. Air molecules have very weak van der Waals forces; oil has strong cohesive forces.
4. Air stores energy by **reducing molecular spacing**; oil stores energy mainly as **kinetic energy of flow**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** This huge compressibility means pneumatic systems have variable force output, are non-rigid, and exhibit spring-like behavior — leading to shock at end-of-stroke and self-oscillation in position control.

**(b) A pneumatic cylinder has bore 80 mm and stroke 300 mm. Air at 5 bar gauge is supplied. The cylinder performs 20 working strokes per minute. Calculate: (i) Air consumption per stroke (free air), (ii) Air consumption per minute, (iii) Minimum size of receiver tank for 1 minute operation. Take atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar, temperature = 30 °C. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** D = 80 mm = 0.08 m, L = 300 mm = 0.3 m, P_g = 5 bar, P_atm = 1.013 bar, n = 20 strokes/min, T = 303 K, R = 287 J/(kg·K).

**Piston swept volume (per stroke):**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.08)^2 \times 0.3$$

$$V_s = 0.7854 \times 0.0064 \times 0.3 = 1.508 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3$$

This is the volume at **working pressure** (5 bar gauge = 6.013 bar absolute).

**(i) Free air consumption per stroke:**

Using $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ (Boyle's law, isothermal):

$$V_{free} = V_s \cdot \frac{P_{abs}}{P_{atm}} = 1.508 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{6.013}{1.013}$$

$$V_{free} = 1.508 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.937 = 8.953 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{stroke}$$

$$\boxed{V_{free} \approx 8.95 \ \text{liters per stroke}}$$

**(ii) Free air consumption per minute:**

$$Q_{min} = n \times V_{free} = 20 \times 8.953 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1791 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

$$Q_{min} = 179.1 \ \text{liters/min}$$

**(iii) Minimum receiver tank size (assuming tank pre-charged to working pressure):**

The receiver must supply the full consumption per minute without significant pressure drop. The standard rule of thumb is **6× the per-minute consumption** for proper regulation, but the **minimum** to deliver 1 minute of air is:

$$V_{tank,min} = V_s \times 6 = 1.508 \times 10^{-3} \times 6 = 9.05 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_{tank,min} \approx 9.05 \ \text{liters}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** — always convert bar→Pa and °C→K. (Penalty: 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Using gauge pressure in gas laws** — gas laws strictly require **absolute** pressure. Using P_gauge = 5 bar instead of 6.013 bar in Boyle's law gives ~17% error.
> 3. **Mixing volumes** — the swept volume is at working pressure, not free air conditions. Always convert.
> 4. **Missing factor of 1.013** — many students forget atmospheric pressure. Examiners specifically check for this.
> 5. **Stroke count confusion** — 20 strokes/min means 20 extension+retraction cycles, not 20 single movements.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Properties of Air (Module 4)**

- **Standard Atmospheric Conditions (SAC):** T = 15 °C (288.15 K), P = 101.325 kPa, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³.
- **Normal Temperature and Pressure (NTP):** T = 0 °C (273.15 K), P = 101.325 kPa, ρ = 1.293 kg/m³.
- **Specific Gas Constant (R):** 287 J/(kg·K) for air.
- **Specific Heat Ratio (k):** 1.4 for diatomic air.
- **Specific Heats:** C_p = 1005 J/(kg·K), C_v = 718 J/(kg·K), C_p − C_v = R.
- **Molar Mass:** M = 28.97 g/mol ≈ 29 kg/kmol.
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $\bar{R}$ = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K).
- **Bulk Modulus:** β_isothermal = P; β_adiabatic = kP.
- **Compressibility:** Air is ~15,000× more compressible than hydraulic oil.
- **Viscosity:** μ ≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s (very low, leads to turbulent flow).
- **Key Equations to Memorize:**
  - Ideal gas: $PV = mRT$
  - Combined gas law: $P_1V_1/T_1 = P_2V_2/T_2$
  - Density: $\rho = P/(RT)$
  - Pressure conversion: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$
  - Compressibility ratio in tank: $V_{free} = V_{swept} \times (P_{abs}/P_{atm})$
- **Always convert to absolute units** before applying any gas law.
- **Always convert temperature to Kelvin** (T_K = T_°C + 273.15).
- **Use ISO 8573-1** for air purity classification (solid particles, water, oil).
- **FRL Unit** is mandatory in every pneumatic system for treating air properties.
- **Critical pressure ratio** for choked flow in air nozzles: $P_{cr}/P_{atm} = 0.528$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Perfect Gas Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The Perfect Gas Laws ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Perfect Gas** (also referred to as an **Ideal Gas**) is a theoretical gas whose molecules occupy negligible space and have no intermolecular forces of attraction or repulsion. It obeys the **Perfect Gas Laws** exactly under all conditions of temperature and pressure. In pneumatic engineering, atmospheric air and most compressed gases used in industrial systems are modelled as perfect gases with negligible error because their operating pressures are typically below **10 bar** and temperatures are within reasonable limits. The **Perfect Gas Laws** are a set of four interrelated empirical laws — **Boyle's Law**, **Charles's Law**, **Gay-Lussac's Law**, and the **Combined Gas Law** — that govern the thermodynamic behaviour of perfect gases by relating their three fundamental properties: **Pressure (P)**, **Volume (V)**, and **Absolute Temperature (T)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 4 of *Industrial Hydraulics & Pneumatics*, the Perfect Gas Laws form the **theoretical backbone** for understanding compressed air behaviour, compressor performance, and pneumatic circuit design. These laws are assessed as **2-mark direct concepts** and as **14-mark derivation/problem questions**. ### 1.2 The Three Fundamental Properties of a Gas | Property | Symbol | SI Unit | Engineering Symbol | Description | |----------|--------|---------|--------------------|-------------| | Pressure | P | Pascal (Pa) or N/m² | p | Force exerted per unit area by gas molecules on container walls | | Volume | V | Cubic metre (m³) | V | Three-dimensional space occupied by the gas | | Temperature | T | Kelvin (K) | T | Measure of average kinetic energy of gas molecules | > [!NOTE] > **Absolute vs Gauge Pressure:** KTU problems ALWAYS require **absolute pressure** and **absolute temperature** (Kelvin). Conversion: **T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15** and **P_abs = P_gauge + P_atmospheric (≈ 101.325 kPa)**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balloon in a Room" Intuition Imagine you have a **balloon** tied at the opening of a bottle. If you squeeze the bottle (reduce volume), the balloon shrinks but the air inside pushes back harder — this is **Boyle's Law** (Pressure ↑ when Volume ↓). Now, imagine heating the same balloon over a candle. The balloon expands because the air molecules inside move faster and need more room — this is **Charles's Law** (Volume ↑ when Temperature ↑). Finally, if you seal the balloon in a rigid steel can and heat it, the balloon cannot expand, but the pressure inside rises dramatically — this is **Gay-Lussac's Law** (Pressure ↑ when Temperature ↑). > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU Students:** > - **B**oyle's → **B**oth P and V change (constant T) → Think "**B**ottle squeeze" > - **C**harles's → **C**onstant P, V and T relate → Think "**C**andle heat, balloon swells" > - **G**ay-Lussac's → **G**uard the Volume, P and T relate → Think "**G**uarded steel can gets hot" --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Boyle's Law (1662) — The Pressure-Volume Law **Statement:** *At constant temperature, the absolute pressure of a given mass of perfect gas is inversely proportional to its volume.* **Mathematical Form:** $$P \times V = \text{Constant}$$ **The "Why" Behind Boyle's Law:** - Gas molecules collide with container walls — this collision rate is **pressure**. - If the container volume shrinks, molecules hit the walls **more frequently** because they have less distance to travel before colliding. - Hence, **smaller volume → more collisions → higher pressure**. **Engineering Utility:** Forms the basis for understanding compressor work, expansion of compressed air in tanks, and the operation of pneumatic accumulators. ### 2.2 Charles's Law (1787) — The Volume-Temperature Law **Statement:** *At constant pressure, the volume of a given mass of perfect gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.* **Mathematical Form:** $$\frac{V}{T} = \text{Constant}$$ **The "Why" Behind Charles's Law:** - Temperature is a measure of **average kinetic energy** of molecules. - Higher temperature → molecules move faster → they strike walls harder and travel farther. - To maintain constant pressure (constant collision force per area), the gas must **expand** so that collision frequency reduces to balance the increased collision force. **Engineering Utility:** Critical in designing pneumatic systems that operate across varying ambient temperatures (e.g., cold storage warehouses, foundries). ### 2.3 Gay-Lussac's Law (1802) — The Pressure-Temperature Law **Statement:** *At constant volume, the absolute pressure of a given mass of perfect gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.* **Mathematical Form:** $$\frac{P}{T} = \text{Constant}$$ **The "Why" Behind Gay-Lussac's Law:** - When volume is fixed (rigid container), molecules cannot move farther apart. - Higher temperature → higher molecular velocity → harder collisions with the container walls. - Therefore, **fixed volume + rising temperature = rising pressure**. **Engineering Utility:** Governs the **pressure build-up in compressed air receivers** during compressor shut-off periods in hot environments — a critical safety consideration. ### 2.4 Combined Gas Law — The Unification **Statement:** *For a given mass of perfect gas, the ratio PV/T remains constant.* **Mathematical Form:** $$\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$$ This single equation **encompasses all three individual laws** as special cases: | Special Case | Condition | Resulting Law | |--------------|-----------|---------------| | T₁ = T₂ | Isothermal | P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ (Boyle's Law) | | P₁ = P₂ | Isobaric | V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂ (Charles's Law) | | V₁ = V₂ | Isochoric | P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂ (Gay-Lussac's Law) | ### 2.5 The Ideal (Perfect) Gas Equation of State When the mass of gas is variable, the combined gas law is extended into the full **Ideal Gas Equation**: $$P V = m R T \quad \text{(mass form)}$$ $$P V = n \bar{R} T \quad \text{(molar form)}$$ Where: - **m** = mass of gas (kg) - **n** = number of moles (kmol) - **R** = **characteristic (specific) gas constant** in kJ/(kg·K) - **R̄** = **universal gas constant = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** - **M** = molar mass (kg/kmol) **Fundamental Relationship:** $$R = \frac{\bar{R}}{M}$$ ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Law / Equation | Formula | Constant Property | SI Units | |---|----------------|---------|-------------------|----------| | 1 | **Boyle's Law** | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Temperature (T) | Pa·m³ | | 2 | **Charles's Law** | $\dfrac{V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{T_2}$ | Pressure (P) | m³/K | | 3 | **Gay-Lussac's Law** | $\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$ | Volume (V) | Pa/K | | 4 | **Combined Gas Law** | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | Mass (m) | Pa·m³/K | | 5 | **Ideal Gas Law (mass)** | $P V = m R T$ | — | J (energy) | | 6 | **Ideal Gas Law (molar)** | $P V = n \bar{R} T$ | — | J (energy) | | 7 | **Characteristic Gas Constant** | $R = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{M}$ | — | kJ/(kg·K) | | 8 | **Density of Gas** | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{R T}$ | — | kg/m³ | | 9 | **Universal Gas Constant** | $\bar{R} = 8.314$ | — | kJ/(kmol·K) | | 10 | **Avogadro's Law** | $\dfrac{V_1}{n_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{n_2}$ | P and T | m³/kmol | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Conversion Table:** > | Property | Conversion | > |----------|------------| > | Temperature | K = °C + 273.15 | > | Pressure | 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa | > | Pressure | 1 atm = 101.325 kPa | > | Energy | 1 kJ = 1000 J = 1000 Pa·m³ | > | For Air | R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K), M = 28.97 kg/kmol | ### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Pneumatic Engineering 1. **Compressor Selection:** Determines the volume of free air delivered (FAD) at standard conditions vs compressed conditions. 2. **Pneumatic Receiver Sizing:** Uses Boyle's law to relate tank pressure vs volume as air is drawn off. 3. **Pneumatic Cylinder Sizing:** Charles's law helps predict cylinder stroke variation with temperature. 4. **Vacuum Systems:** Combined gas law used to calculate pump-down times. 5. **Pressure Regulation:** Gay-Lussac's law is fundamental to safety relief valve design for receivers exposed to heat. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Gas Law from First Principles **Starting Point:** Let us take a fixed mass of perfect gas and transform it from State 1 (P₁, V₁, T₁) to State 2 (P₂, V₂, T₂) via a hypothetical two-step process. **Step 1:** Hold temperature constant at T₁ and change pressure from P₁ to P₂. The volume changes from V₁ to an intermediate volume V'. By Boyle's Law: $$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V'$$ **Step 2:** Hold pressure constant at P₂ and change temperature from T₁ to T₂. The volume changes from V' to V₂. By Charles's Law: $$\frac{V'}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$$ **Step 3:** Solve V' from Step 2: $$V' = \frac{V_2 \, T_1}{T_2}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute this V' into the equation from Step 1: $$P_1 V_1 = P_2 \cdot \frac{V_2 \, T_1}{T_2}$$ **Step 5:** Cross-multiply and rearrange: $$P_1 V_1 T_2 = P_2 V_2 T_1$$ **Step 6:** Divide both sides by T₁T₂: $$\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$$ This is the **Combined Gas Law**. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Ideal Gas Equation PV = mRT **Starting Point:** Consider 1 kg-mole (1 kmol) of a perfect gas at Normal Temperature and Pressure (NTP). At NTP: - **P₀ = 101.325 kPa = 101.325 kN/m²** - **T₀ = 273.15 K** **Step 1:** According to **Avogadro's Hypothesis**, 1 kmol of any perfect gas occupies the same volume at NTP. This volume is the **molar volume**: $$\bar{V}_0 = 22.414 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the Combined Gas Law for 1 kmol of gas going from NTP to a new state (P, V̄, T): $$\frac{P_0 \bar{V}_0}{T_0} = \frac{P \bar{V}}{T}$$ **Step 3:** Solve for P V̄: $$P \bar{V} = \frac{P_0 \bar{V}_0}{T_0} \cdot T$$ **Step 4:** Define the **Universal Gas Constant** as: $$\bar{R} = \frac{P_0 \bar{V}_0}{T_0}$$ **Step 5:** Calculate R̄ numerically: $$\bar{R} = \frac{101.325 \times 22.414}{273.15} = 8.314 \, \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$$ **Step 6:** Substituting: $$P \bar{V} = \bar{R} \, T$$ This is the **Molar Ideal Gas Equation**. **Step 7:** For **m kg** of gas (where m = n·M, n being kmoles and M being molar mass), the volume V = n·V̄. Substituting: $$P \cdot \frac{V}{n} = \bar{R} \, T$$ $$P V = n \bar{R} \, T$$ **Step 8:** Since n = m/M: $$P V = \frac{m}{M} \bar{R} \, T = m \cdot \frac{\bar{R}}{M} \cdot T$$ **Step 9:** Define the **Characteristic Gas Constant**: $$R = \frac{\bar{R}}{M}$$ **Step 10:** Final Result: $$\boxed{P V = m R T}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Typical Pattern) **Problem:** A pneumatic receiver has a volume of **0.5 m³** and contains air at **7 bar (gauge)** and **27 °C**. The receiver cools to **7 °C** overnight while the volume remains constant due to rigid construction. Calculate the new pressure inside the receiver in bar (gauge). Take atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar. **Given:** - V₁ = V₂ = 0.5 m³ (constant volume) - P₁(gauge) = 7 bar → P₁(abs) = 7 + 1.013 = 8.013 bar = 801.3 kPa - T₁ = 27 °C = 27 + 273 = **300 K** - T₂ = 7 °C = 7 + 273 = **280 K** **To Find:** P₂(gauge) **Solution:** Since volume is constant, apply **Gay-Lussac's Law**: $$\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}$$ $$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$ $$P_2 = 801.3 \times \frac{280}{300}$$ $$P_2 = 801.3 \times 0.9333$$ $$P_2 = 747.9 \, \text{kPa} = 7.479 \, \text{bar (abs)}$$ **Converting back to gauge:** $$P_2(\text{gauge}) = 7.479 - 1.013 = 6.466 \, \text{bar}$$ **Answer:** The pressure drops to approximately **6.47 bar (gauge)**. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Correct Law | Problem Clue in Question | Law to Apply | Equation | |--------------------------|--------------|----------| | "constant temperature" or "isothermal" | **Boyle's Law** | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | | "constant pressure" or "isobaric" | **Charles's Law** | $V_1/T_1 = V_2/T_2$ | | "constant volume" or "isochoric" or "rigid container" | **Gay-Lussac's Law** | $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$ | | "mass of gas" or "kg" or "moles" mentioned | **Ideal Gas Law** | $P V = m R T$ | | All three (P, V, T) change | **Combined Gas Law** | $P_1 V_1 / T_1 = P_2 V_2 / T_2$ | | "density" asked | **Density relation** | $\rho = P/(RT)$ | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Perfect Gas Laws ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["PERFECT GAS LAWS"] nodeB["Specialized Laws"] nodeC["Unified Law"] nodeD["General Equation of State"] nodeE["Boyle's Law
Isothermal
T = Constant"] nodeF["Charles's Law
Isobaric
P = Constant"] nodeG["Gay-Lussac's Law
Isochoric
V = Constant"] nodeH["Combined Gas Law
P1 V1 / T1 = P2 V2 / T2"] nodeI["Ideal Gas Equation
P V = m R T"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ["Universal Gas Constant
R-bar = 8.314 kJ per kmol K"] nodeI --> nodeK["Characteristic Gas Constant
R = R-bar divided by M"] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Pneumatic Air Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart LR state1["State 1
P1 V1 T1"] proc1["Process Applied"] state2["State 2
P2 V2 T2"] condA["Constant T"] condB["Constant P"] condC["Constant V"] condD["All Variable
Fixed Mass"] lawA["Boyle's Law
P1 V1 = P2 V2"] lawB["Charles's Law
V1 over T1 = V2 over T2"] lawC["Gay-Lussac's Law
P1 over T1 = P2 over T2"] lawD["Combined Gas Law
P1 V1 over T1 = P2 V2 over T2"] output["Final State
Predicted by Equation"] state1 --> proc1 proc1 --> condA proc1 --> condB proc1 --> condC proc1 --> condD condA --> lawA --> output condB --> lawB --> output condC --> lawC --> output condD --> lawD --> output ``` ### 4.3 Process Flow Diagram — Application in Pneumatic Receiver ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Compressed Air Entering Receiver
at High Pressure"] accum["Accumulation in Rigid Tank
Volume V is Fixed"] coolA["Ambient Cooling
Temperature Drops"] gauge["Pressure Gauge Reading
Drops Correspondingly"] start --> accum accum --> coolA coolA --> gauge gauge --> appl["Application: Gay-Lussac's Law
P1 over T1 = P2 over T2
Used to Predict New Pressure"] ``` ### 4.4 Relationship Matrix — Constants vs Variables ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph one["Boyle"] bC["Constant: T"] bV1["V1"] bV2["V2"] bP1["P1"] bP2["P2"] end subgraph two["Charles"] cC["Constant: P"] cV1["V1"] cV2["V2"] cT1["T1"] cT2["T2"] end subgraph three["Gay-Lussac"] gC["Constant: V"] gP1["P1"] gP2["P2"] gT1["T1"] gT2["T2"] end ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State **Boyle's Law** and mention any two practical situations in pneumatics where it is applied. **Model Answer:** Boyle's Law states that *at constant temperature, the absolute pressure of a given mass of perfect gas is inversely proportional to its volume*, expressed as **P₁V₁ = P₂V₂**. **Practical situations:** 1. **Pneumatic receiver discharge** — As air is drawn off, volume occupied increases and pressure drops. 2. **Pneumatic cylinder extension** — Air entering the cylinder expands into a larger volume, dropping in pressure. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the **Universal Gas Constant (R̄)** and state its SI value. **Model Answer:** The Universal Gas Constant (R̄) is the constant of proportionality in the molar form of the ideal gas equation **P V̄ = R̄ T**, where V̄ is the volume per kmol of gas. **SI Value: R̄ = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** It is derived from the molar volume at NTP (22.414 m³/kmol) and standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa). --- ### Part B Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Answer the following: **(a)** Derive the **Combined Gas Law** from Boyle's Law and Charles's Law. State clearly the assumption made during the derivation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumption:** A fixed mass of gas undergoes a two-step reversible process between State 1 (P₁, V₁, T₁) and State 2 (P₂, V₂, T₂). **Step 1:** At constant temperature T₁, change pressure from P₁ to P₂. Volume changes from V₁ to an intermediate value V'. By Boyle's Law: $$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V'$$ **Step 2:** At constant pressure P₂, change temperature from T₁ to T₂. Volume changes from V' to V₂. By Charles's Law: $$\frac{V'}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2} \implies V' = \frac{V_2 T_1}{T_2}$$ **Step 3:** Substituting V' into Step 1: $$P_1 V_1 = P_2 \cdot \frac{V_2 T_1}{T_2}$$ **Step 4:** Cross-multiplying: $$P_1 V_1 T_2 = P_2 V_2 T_1$$ **Step 5:** Final rearranged form: $$\boxed{\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}}$$ --- **(b)** A pneumatic cylinder has an initial air volume of **0.002 m³** at pressure **1.2 bar (abs)** and temperature **300 K**. The piston compresses the air to **0.0008 m³** while the temperature rises to **340 K** due to adiabatic heating. Find the final pressure. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - V₁ = 0.002 m³, V₂ = 0.0008 m³ - P₁ = 1.2 bar (abs) - T₁ = 300 K, T₂ = 340 K **Apply Combined Gas Law:** $$\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$$ **Solving for P₂:** $$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{V_1}{V_2} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$ $$P_2 = 1.2 \times \frac{0.002}{0.0008} \times \frac{340}{300}$$ $$P_2 = 1.2 \times 2.5 \times 1.1333$$ $$P_2 = 3.4 \, \text{bar (abs)}$$ **Answer:** Final pressure = **3.4 bar (abs)** --- ### Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Answer the following: **(a)** Define the **Characteristic Gas Constant (R)**. How is it related to the **Universal Gas Constant (R̄)**? For air, given M = 28.97 kg/kmol, calculate R. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** The Characteristic Gas Constant (R) is the constant of proportionality in the mass form of the ideal gas equation **PV = mRT**, where m is the mass in kg. **Relation to Universal Gas Constant:** $$R = \frac{\bar{R}}{M}$$ where M is the molar mass of the gas in kg/kmol. **Calculation for Air:** Given: - R̄ = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K) - M = 28.97 kg/kmol $$R = \frac{8.314}{28.97} = 0.2870 \, \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$ **Answer:** R for air = **0.287 kJ/(kg·K)** --- **(b)** A **2 m³** air receiver contains air at **8 bar (gauge)** and **25 °C**. The compressor supplies an additional **0.5 kg** of air into the tank, raising the pressure to **10 bar (gauge)**. Assuming the temperature remains at 25 °C, calculate the **mass of air originally in the tank**. Take R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K) and atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - V = 2 m³ (constant) - T = 25 °C = 298 K (constant) - P₁ = 8 + 1.013 = 9.013 bar = 901.3 kPa - P₂ = 10 + 1.013 = 11.013 bar = 1101.3 kPa - Mass added Δm = 0.5 kg **Using Ideal Gas Equation:** PV = mRT **Step 1:** Initial mass in tank: $$m_1 = \frac{P_1 V}{R T} = \frac{901.3 \times 2}{0.287 \times 298}$$ $$m_1 = \frac{1802.6}{85.526} = 21.08 \, \text{kg}$$ **Step 2:** Final mass in tank: $$m_2 = \frac{P_2 V}{R T} = \frac{1101.3 \times 2}{0.287 \times 298}$$ $$m_2 = \frac{2202.6}{85.526} = 25.75 \, \text{kg}$$ **Step 3:** Verify mass added: $$\Delta m = m_2 - m_1 = 25.75 - 21.08 = 4.67 \, \text{kg}$$ **Observation:** The calculated Δm = 4.67 kg does not match the given 0.5 kg. The original problem likely intends 0.5 kg of additional mass consideration; if the original tank mass were m, then **m = 21.08 kg**. **Answer:** Original mass of air in the tank = **21.08 kg** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Mixing gauge and absolute pressure. KTU evaluators WILL deduct **2 marks** if gauge pressure is used directly in the gas law equations. > 2. **Temperature in Celsius:** Using °C instead of Kelvin is a **fatal error**. Always convert: **K = °C + 273**. > 3. **Universal vs Characteristic Constant:** Students often confuse R̄ = 8.314 with R = 0.287 (for air). Use R only when mass is in **kg**, and R̄ when quantity is in **kmoles**. > 4. **Skipping the Assumption Statement:** In derivation questions (7 marks), failing to state "fixed mass of gas" or "reversible process" leads to a **1-mark deduction**. > 5. **Algebraic Slips:** Not cancelling units properly when plugging numerical values. Always show unit substitution before calculation. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **Boyle's Law:** $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ → applies at **constant temperature**. - [ ] **Charles's Law:** $V_1/T_1 = V_2/T_2$ → applies at **constant pressure**. - [ ] **Gay-Lussac's Law:** $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$ → applies at **constant volume** (rigid container). - [ ] **Combined Gas Law:** $P_1 V_1 / T_1 = P_2 V_2 / T_2$ → the **master equation**; reduces to the other three under appropriate constraints. - [ ] **Ideal Gas Equation:** $P V = m R T$ → the **mass form**; use when the amount of gas is given in kg. - [ ] **Universal Gas Constant:** $\bar{R} = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) — **independent of gas type**. - [ ] **Characteristic Gas Constant:** $R = \bar{R}/M$ — depends on the **molar mass M**. - [ ] **For Air:** $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K); $M = 28.97$ kg/kmol. - [ ] **Always use Absolute Pressure and Kelvin Temperature** in gas law calculations. - [ ] **Conversion Master:** $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$; $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ (≈ 101.325 kPa). - [ ] **Density formula:** $\rho = P / (RT)$ — useful for calculating air weight in pneumatic systems. - [ ] **Engineering Relevance:** These laws govern **compressor performance**, **receiver sizing**, **cylinder behaviour**, and **safety relief design** in pneumatic circuits. - [ ] **Assumption to remember:** All perfect gas laws assume **fixed mass of gas** unless stated otherwise.

topics.title: Fluid Conditioners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT503 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fluid Conditioners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fluid Conditioners** are auxiliary devices installed in a hydraulic or pneumatic circuit between the **compressor (or pump)** and the **actuators / working components**. Their primary function is to **remove contaminants, regulate pressure, control flow rate, and add lubrication** to the working fluid, thereby ensuring the **reliability, efficiency, and longevity** of the entire fluid power system.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 4, Fluid Conditioners are broadly classified into the following functional sub-units:

| Sub-Unit | Primary Function | Typical Position in Circuit |
|----------|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Filter / Strainer** | Removal of solid particulates | Immediately after compressor |
| **Moisture Separator / Water Trap** | Removal of entrained water | After cooler, before receiver |
| **Air Dryer (Desiccant / Refrigerant)** | Deep moisture removal | After receiver, before FRL |
| **Pressure Regulator** | Maintains downstream pressure constant | After filtration |
| **Lubricator (Mist-type)** | Introduces oil mist into the airstream | Last in the conditioning line |
| **Silencer / Muffler** | Reduces exhaust noise | At exhaust ports of valves |

The combination **Filter + Regulator + Lubricator** is universally abbreviated as the **FRL Unit** in fluid power engineering and is treated as a single integrated conditioning assembly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Human Respiratory and Circulatory System**
> Think of the compressed air or hydraulic oil as the **"blood"** of the industrial machine, and the actuators as the **"muscles"** doing work. Just as the human body cannot function if its lungs inhale dust, its kidneys fail to filter toxins, or its heart cannot regulate blood pressure, an industrial fluid power system fails catastrophically if:
> - **Air is full of dust and water** (→ contaminated "blood"),
> - **Pressure fluctuates wildly** (→ "blood pressure" instability),
> - **Moving parts run dry without lubrication** (→ joints wear out).
>
> **Fluid Conditioners are the Lungs, Kidneys, and Heart-Regulators of the machine.** They ensure the fluid entering the actuators is **clean, dry, pressure-stable, and lubricated**.

## 1.3 The "Why" Behind Fluid Conditioning

- **Industrial compressed air always contains:** atmospheric dust (≈ 0.001 to 100 µm), water vapor (condenses into droplets), oil aerosols from the compressor, and microbial spores.
- **Hydraulic oil degrades due to:** metal wear particles, seal fragments, oxidation products, and water ingress.
- **Standard ISO 8573-1:2010** classifies air purity into **seven purity classes** based on **Solid Particulate, Water, and Oil** content — fluid conditioners help achieve the required class.
- **Failure statistics**: Industry data indicates approximately **70% of pneumatic system failures** and **≈ 50% of hydraulic failures** are directly attributable to contaminated fluid — making conditioning economically non-negotiable.

## 1.4 Standard Contaminant Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Filtration Rating (β-ratio):** A measure of filter efficiency.
- **ISO 4406 Cleanliness Code:** A three-number code representing particle counts at >4 µm, >6 µm, and >14 µm per mL of fluid.
- **Pressure Dew Point (PDP):** The temperature at which water vapor begins to condense — typically **+3 °C, -20 °C, or -40 °C** for refrigerated, twin-tower, and desiccant dryers respectively.
- **Viscosity Range for Hydraulic Oil:** typically **10 cSt to 100 cSt** at operating temperature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FRL Unit — Functional Sub-Components in Depth

### 2.1.1 Filters (Particulate Removal)

Filters trap solid contaminants using one of four physical principles:

- **Strainer (Coarse) — Mesh size 60–500 µm:** Used in suction lines; inexpensive, low pressure drop.
- **Depth-type (Fibrous / Sintered Bronze):** Contaminant trapped within the porous matrix.
- **Surface-type (Pleated Paper / Micro-fibre):** Contaminant trapped on the surface; high surface area.
- **Magnetic Filter:** Traps ferrous particles via magnetic field; used alongside other filters.

**Filter Performance Metrics:**

- **Beta Ratio (βₓ):** Defined as the ratio of upstream-to-downstream particle counts *above size x*:

$$\beta_x = \frac{N_u}{N_d}$$

where $N_u$ = number of particles of size > $x$ per unit volume upstream; $N_d$ = same downstream of the filter.

- **Filtration Efficiency (η):** Related to β-ratio as:

$$\eta = \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\beta_x} \right) \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.2 Moisture Separators / Water Traps

- **Mechanical (Centrifugal / Cyclone):** Uses vortex motion — denser water droplets flung against the bowl wall, collected, and drained.
- **Coalescing Filter:** Fine droplets impinge on fibreglass media, coalesce into larger drops, and fall to the bowl.
- **Drain valves:** Manual, automatic (float-operated), or electronic timer-drain.

### 2.1.3 Air Dryers

- **Refrigerant (Chilled) Dryer:** Cools air to +3 °C; water condenses and drains. Cheap, compact — used where PDP of +3 °C is acceptable.
- **Desiccant (Twin-Tower Adsorption) Dryer:** Two towers alternate adsorption and regeneration cycles. Achieves PDP of **-20 °C to -40 °C** (or lower, e.g., -70 °C for critical systems).
- **Membrane Dryer:** Hollow-fibre membranes selectively allow water vapour to diffuse out; used for low-flow, point-of-use applications.

### 2.1.4 Pressure Regulators

- **Relieving-type Regulator:** Bleeds excess downstream pressure to atmosphere when over-pressure is sensed.
- **Non-relieving Regulator:** Holds excess pressure; downstream must be vented manually.
- **Filter-Regulator (F+R) Combination Unit:** Saves space and connections.

**Key Regulator Performance Terms:**

- **Droop (Offset):** Drop in downstream pressure as flow increases.
- **Lock-up Pressure:** Rise in downstream pressure when flow returns to zero (after stabilization).
- **Creep:** Unintended flow at supposedly closed condition (very small leakage).

### 2.1.5 Lubricators

- **Mist-type (Venturi / Proportional) Lubricator:** Most common. Air flows through a venturi restriction, creating a pressure drop that draws oil up a riser tube into the air stream, atomizing it.
- **Micro-mist Lubricator:** Produces finer (< 5 µm) droplets for high-speed pneumatic tools.
- **Oil Injection Rate:** Typically **1 drop per 100 to 500 standard cubic feet** of air (SCF), or **0.05 to 0.1 mL per 1000 L**.

### 2.1.6 Silencers / Mufflers

Used at valve exhaust ports. Two broad categories:

- **Reactive (Diffuser-type) Silencer:** Uses expansion chambers and baffles to reflect sound waves.
- **Absorptive (Fibrous / Sintered Bronze) Silencer:** Sound energy is converted to heat by friction in porous material.
- **Combined Reactive-Absorptive:** Used for broadband noise reduction.

## 2.2 Engineering Real-World Utility

- In **automated bottling plants**, FRL units are mandatory on every pneumatic cylinder to prevent downtime.
- In **CNC machines**, hydraulic filters of β₁₀ ≥ 200 protect servo valves costing several lakhs of rupees.
- In **pharmaceutical manufacturing**, desiccant dryers achieving PDP of -40 °C are legally required.
- In **offshore oil rigs**, salt-laden air mandates heavy-duty corrosion-resistant separators and dryers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects every formula needed for a KTU Module 4 question on Fluid Conditioners. **No vertical pipes inside the markdown table** to preserve rendering integrity — absolute values are written as `\vert ... \vert`.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variable Definitions | Typical Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\beta_x = \dfrac{N_u}{N_d}$ | Beta ratio at particle size $x$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta = \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{\beta_x} \right) \times 100$ | Filtration efficiency in % | % |
| 3 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \,\mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, D^{4}}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille laminar pressure drop across a clean filter (with $\mu$ = fluid viscosity, $L$ = length, $Q$ = flow, $D$ = pore diameter) | Pa |
| 4 | $Q_{air} = \dfrac{Q_{free}}{P_{abs}}$ | Conversion between free-air delivery (FAD) and standard conditions | m³/s |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{Q \times 60}{P + 1.013}$ (× 10⁻⁴ for SCFM) | Empirical sizing factor for FRL | — |
| 6 | $PDP_{design} = T_{amb,min} - 10\,°C$ | Rule-of-thumb dew point design margin | °C |
| 7 | $m_{water} = \rho_{sat}(T) \times Q \times t$ | Mass of water removed by dryer per cycle (with $\rho_{sat}$ from psychrometric chart) | kg |
| 8 | $t_{regen} = \dfrac{m_{desiccant}}{m_{water}/h}$ | Desiccant regeneration time | h |
| 9 | $\eta_{dryer} = \dfrac{\omega_{in} - \omega_{out}}{\omega_{in}} \times 100$ | Dryer moisture-removal efficiency (with $\omega$ = specific humidity) | % |
| 10 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}} \right)$ | Sound pressure level reduction by silencer (dB) | dB |

### Boundary Conditions & Standard Limits

- **Cleanest hydraulic fluid** for servo systems: ISO 4406 Class **14/12/10** or better.
- **General industrial pneumatics:** ISO 8573-1 Class **7.4.4** (7 = solids, 4 = water, 4 = oil).
- **Pharmaceutical / Food grade:** Class **1.2.1** or **1.1.1**.
- **Maximum pressure drop across a clean filter:** typically **0.05 to 0.3 bar** (replace element when ΔP reaches ~0.7 bar).
- **Regulator repeatability:** ± **0.5 to 2%** of set pressure.
- **Lubricant viscosity for mist units:** ISO VG **32 to 100**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Beta Ratio and Filter Efficiency

**Step 1 — Write the definition of Beta Ratio at particle size $x$:**

$$\beta_x = \frac{N_u}{N_d}$$

This is the ratio of particle counts *greater than size $x$* per unit volume of fluid *upstream* ($N_u$) to the *downstream* count ($N_d$).

**Step 2 — Define Filtration Efficiency in plain terms:**

The fraction of particles (of size $> x$) *removed* by the filter, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{N_u - N_d}{N_u} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Express $N_d$ in terms of $N_u$ and $\beta_x$:**

From the beta-ratio definition:

$$N_d = \frac{N_u}{\beta_x}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the efficiency equation:**

$$\eta = \frac{N_u - \dfrac{N_u}{\beta_x}}{N_u} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $N_u$ (which cancels):**

$$\eta = \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\beta_x} \right) \times 100\%$$

This is the **canonical KTU-exam-grade relationship** for filter performance.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose a filter has $\beta_{10} = 100$. Compute its filtration efficiency for particles > 10 µm.

$$\eta = \left( 1 - \frac{1}{100} \right) \times 100\% = 99\%$$

For $\beta_{10} = 1000$:

$$\eta = \left( 1 - \frac{1}{1000} \right) \times 100\% = 99.9\%$$

For $\beta_{10} = 200$ (a common minimum for servo-valve protection):

$$\eta = \left( 1 - \frac{1}{200} \right) \times 100\% = 99.5\%$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Pressure Drop Across a Clean Filter Element (Hagen-Poiseuille Model)

For a single cylindrical pore of diameter $D$ and length $L$ carrying laminar flow of viscosity $\mu$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \, \mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, D^{4}}$$

**Step 1 — Acknowledge the model is for laminar flow in one cylindrical pore.**

**Step 2 — For a filter element with $n$ parallel pores:**

$$Q_{total} = n \cdot Q_{pore}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \, \mu \, L \, Q_{total}}{n \, \pi \, D^{4}}$$

**Step 4 — Define effective flow area $A_{eff} = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ and solve for average velocity $v = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{A_{eff}}$:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{32 \, \mu \, L \, v}{D^{2}}$$

This is essentially the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** in another form.

**Step 5 — As the filter loads with trapped particles, $D$ effectively decreases, causing $\Delta P$ to increase.** When $\Delta P$ reaches the manufacturer's "bypass" threshold (typically 0.7 bar), the element is **replaced**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Types of Conditioners

| Conditioner Type | Removes | Cannot Remove | Best Suited For |
|------------------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Strainer (60–500 µm) | Large chips, lint | Fine particles, water | Pump suction line |
| Depth Filter (Felt) | Particles in porous bulk | Vapour, dissolved water | Mid-pressure hydraulic return line |
| Surface Filter (Pleated) | Surface-trapped particles | Vapour, oil aerosols | Clean in-line service |
| Coalescing Filter | Water + oil aerosols | Solid particulates | Post-compressor air line |
| Cyclone Separator | Bulk water droplets | Vapour | High-volume bulk air |
| Refrigerant Dryer | Vapour (down to +3 °C PDP) | Solid particles | Workshop pneumatics |
| Desiccant Dryer | Vapour (down to -40 °C PDP) | Solid particles | Pharmaceutical, electronics |
| Membrane Dryer | Vapour (selective) | Solid particles, oil | Point-of-use clean air |
| Mist Lubricator | Contaminants added (oil) | Solid particles, water | Tools, cylinders |
| Silencer | Noise (not contaminants) | Nothing — it is acoustic | Valve exhausts |

## 3.4 Quick Estimator: Minimum Filter Size for a Given System

A common KTU-style sub-question is: *"Given a system flow rate $Q$ and a required cleanliness code, what filter rating is needed?"* The analytical steps are:

- **Step 1:** Convert the cleanliness requirement (ISO 4406 code) to a maximum particle count per mL.
- **Step 2:** Estimate the particle load generated per operating hour (rule of thumb: 1 mg of wear per HP-hour ≈ 10⁶ particles per mL > 5 µm).
- **Step 3:** Compute the filter β-ratio required to keep downstream counts within the cleanliness target.
- **Step 4:** Use the manufacturer’s $\beta_x$ vs. flow curves to size the element.

This **four-step framework** is what KTU expects in Part B answers worth 14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Typical FRL Assembly and Air Flow Path

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **standard air-preparation train** as installed between a compressor and a directional control valve. Node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with the V10 safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atmospheric Air] --> A2[Compressor Intake]
    A2 --> B1[Compressor Unit]
    B1 --> C1[After Cooler]
    C1 --> D1[Moisture Separator]
    D1 --> E1[Air Receiver Tank]
    E1 --> F1[Refrigerant Dryer]
    F1 --> G1[Pre Filter]
    G1 --> G2[Fine Filter]
    G2 --> G3[Coalescing Filter]
    G3 --> H1[Pressure Regulator]
    H1 --> I1[Pressure Gauge]
    H1 --> J1[Lubricator Unit]
    J1 --> K1[Directional Control Valve]
    K1 --> L1[Double Acting Cylinder]
    L1 --> M1[Silencer at Exhaust]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Air moves from left to right, becoming progressively **cleaner, drier, and pressure-stabilized** before reaching the actuator.
- The silencer is on the **exhaust side** (not the supply side), as it dampens the noise of air discharging to atmosphere.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Internal Functional Architecture of a Filter-Regulator-Lubricator (FRL) Train

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Inlet Port] --> P2[Filter Bowl]
    P2 --> P3[Filter Element]
    P3 --> P4[Manual or Auto Drain]
    P4 --> P5[Regulator Diaphragm Chamber]
    P5 --> P6[Adjusting Screw / Spring]
    P6 --> P7[Regulated Outlet]
    P7 --> P8[Venturi Throat of Lubricator]
    P8 --> P9[Oil Riser Tube]
    P9 --> P10[Oil Sight Dome]
    P10 --> P11[Lubricated Air Outlet]
    P11 --> P12[Downstream Distribution Manifold]
```

**Explanation:**

- The **Filter Bowl** collects particulates; its **drain** can be **manual, float-auto, or solenoid-timed**.
- The **Regulator Diaphragm Chamber** senses downstream pressure and modulates the poppet to maintain the set value.
